### [1] [EDEN: A Large-Scale Corpus of Clinical Notes for Italian](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12569)
*Tiziano Labruna,Guido Bertolini,Pietro Ferrazzi,Bernardo Magnini*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EDEN is a large-scale (~4M notes), anonymized Italian emergency department clinical corpus with a ~6K-note subset manually annotated via a 132-item Case Report Form. It introduces CRF-filling as a structured IE benchmark and provides zero-shot baselines using Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a scarcity of large, freely available clinical text corpora in Italian to support development and evaluation of Large Language Models for concrete medical applications, particularly structured information extraction from emergency department notes.

Method: The authors collected ~4M anonymized clinical notes from Italian emergency departments, designed a 132-item Case Report Form covering dyspnea and loss of consciousness, and had multiple clinicians manually annotate ~6K notes with iterative revision. They propose CRF-filling as a structured information extraction task and evaluate zero-shot performance of Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B.

Result: The resulting EDEN corpus is the largest freely available Italian clinical notes dataset. The annotated subset is richly structured but highly imbalanced across CRF items (numerical, categorical, binary, and mixed types). Zero-shot baselines from Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B are provided for the CRF-filling benchmark.

Conclusion: EDEN fills a significant resource gap for Italian clinical NLP, enabling both LLM development and structured information extraction research in emergency medicine contexts.

Abstract: We present EDEN (Emergency Department Electronic Notes), a new and unique large-scale corpus of clinical notes produced in Emergency Departments of Italian hospitals. The corpus, in its current version, is composed of approximately 4 million clinical notes fully anonymized, covering diverse phases of patient care during the stay in the emergency department. In addition, a subset of about six thousand notes has been manually annotated by clinical experts through a structured Case Report Form (CRF) containing 132 items relevant for two patient situations in emergency departments, dyspnea and loss of consciousness. Items may assume numerical values (e.g., for blood saturation), categorical (e.g., for level of consciousness ), binary (e.g., for presence of traumas), and mixed value types. The annotation process involved multiple clinicians and underwent iterative revision to resolve ambiguities in item formulation, resulting in a richly structured (although high imbalanced) resource. The dataset aims to fill a relevant gap of data able to support both the development and the use of Large Language Models in concrete medical applications. We describe the data collection protocol, the on-site anonymisation pipeline, corpus statistics, and the annotation scheme. Finally, we propose CRF-filling as a novel structured information extraction benchmark, and provide zero-shot baseline resulting from Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B. To the best of our knowledge, the EDEN dataset is the largest freely available corpus of clinical notes existing for the Italian language.

</details>


### [2] [Helping Figures Tell their Story! Paper-Grounded Video Generation Explaining Complex Scientific Figures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12576)
*Ishani Mondal,Javad Baghirov,Jordan Boyd-Graber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MINARD is a pipeline that generates paper-grounded, narrated walkthrough videos from scientific figures by decomposing them into sequentially grounded region-level narrations. The authors also introduce FigTalk, a benchmark with sequential and component-level grounding metrics, where MINARD outperforms existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video generation systems and benchmarks lack the capability to produce step-by-step narration aligned with visual highlights that are grounded in the accompanying paper. Understanding scientific figures requires this precise, paper-grounded, sequential explanation, which existing methods do not support.

Method: The authors propose MINARD (Multimodal Interpretation of Narrated Architecture via Region Decomposition), a pipeline that takes a scientific figure and its source paper as input. It generates paper-grounded narrations and sequentially grounds them to specific figure regions. The method decomposes the figure into components and produces narrated walkthrough videos with region-level visual alignment.

Result: On the newly introduced FigTalk benchmark, MINARD generates narrations that are humanlike and faithful to the paper. It outperforms existing narration-conditioned figure spatial grounding approaches in both automatic metrics and human evaluation, with new sequential and component-level grounding metrics derived for assessment.

Conclusion: MINARD successfully addresses the gap in paper-grounded figure-to-video generation, producing faithful, human-quality narrated walkthroughs. The FigTalk benchmark provides a standardized evaluation framework, and MINARD's superior performance on both automatic and human evaluations validates the effectiveness of the region decomposition and sequential grounding approach.

Abstract: Scientific figures compress complex pipelines into a single canvas, yet understanding them requires paper-grounded, step-by-step narration aligned with visual highlights a capability missing from current video generation systems and benchmarks. To address this, we introduce paper-grounded figure-to-video generation: generating narrated, region-grounded walkthrough videos from a figure and its paper. We propose MINARD (Multimodal Interpretation of Narrated Architecture via Region Decomposition), a pipeline that generates paper-grounded narrations and sequentially grounds them to figure regions. We also release FigTalk, a benchmark with new sequential and component-level grounding metrics derived. On FigTalk, MINARD generates humanlike, paper-faithful narrations and outperforms narration-conditioned figure spatial grounding compared to existing approaches in both automatic and human evaluation

</details>


### [3] [MARD: Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation for Mechanism-Level Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12578)
*Mohammadreza Riyazat,Vian Lelo,Rameen Jafri,Yumna Khan,Abeer Badawi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a mechanism-level DDI prediction framework with a structured 7-family/147-subtype taxonomy, leakage-safe evaluation protocols, and a MARD-7B model trained via Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation. MARD-7B outperforms 32 baselines including GPT-4o, shows anti-memorisation behavior, and operates at ~1% of frontier API cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DDI prediction treats the task as flat binary classification, ignoring the mechanistic details (which enzyme/pharmacodynamic axis, directionality, evidence type). There is also a lack of reproducible, leakage-safe evaluation protocols. The authors aim to move beyond "do they interact?" to "how, in what direction, and with what evidence?" using auditable, verifiable reasoning.

Method: Three innovations compose Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation (MARD): (1) a single-token KL divergence on a direction tag that ties prediction to reasoning; (2) per-loss Process-Reward-Model-weighted DPO with programmatic hard negatives; and (3) a leakage-safe mechanism-aware retrieval channel. Process-reward step labels are automatically verified against DrugBank structured fields, eliminating need for human/LLM judges. Evaluation uses a structured 7-family/147-subtype taxonomy with cold-split protocols that prevent information leakage.

Result: On the April 2026 DrugBank release, MARD-7B is the only system among 32 whose accuracy survives drug-pair novelty, beating the best baseline by +13.9 percentage points and GPT-4o by +6.7 pp. Accuracy paradoxically improves on rarely seen drugs (anti-memorisation signature), suggesting genuine pharmacological reasoning rather than frequency-based memorisation. Cost is ~1% of frontier API pricing.

Conclusion: Mechanism-level DDI prediction with structured taxonomies and leakage-safe evaluation is feasible and important. The MARD pipeline demonstrates that small, fine-tuned models can outperform large frontier models through structured reasoning, with the added benefit of auditable, verifiable outputs. The anti-memorisation signature provides strong evidence that gains derive from reasoning capability rather than training-data memorisation.

Abstract: Mechanism-level drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction requires identifying which enzyme or pharmacodynamic axis is implicated, in which direction, and with which evidence -- not merely whether two drugs interact. We introduce a reproducible mechanism-level DDI labelling and evaluation protocol with a structured 7-family/147-subtype taxonomy, leakage-safe cold-split protocols, and auditable reasoning metrics for evaluating pharmacological prediction beyond flat interaction classification. We propose a pipeline that produces a 7B reasoning MARD (Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation), combining three training innovations: a single-token KL divergence on direction tag that ties the model's prediction, per-loss PRM-weighted DPO with programmatic hard negatives, and a leakage-safe mechanism-aware retrieval channel. Process-reward step labels are automatically verifiable against DrugBank-structured fields, requiring no human or LLM judges. On the April-2026 DrugBank release, our MARD-7B is the only system in a 32-system comparison whose accuracy survives drug-pair novelty, beating the best baseline by +13.9 pp and GPT-4o by +6.7 pp at ~1% of frontier API cost. Further analysis reveals an anti-memorisation signature where accuracy improves on rarely seen drugs, suggesting that gain comes from structured pharmacological reasoning rather than drug-frequency memorisation. We release corpus, DDI-PRM, retrieval index, and training code.

</details>


### [4] [Constrained Semantic Decompression in LLMs through Persian Proverb-Conditioned Story Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12599)
*Zahra Habibzadeh,Paria Khoshtab,Amir Mesbah,Yadollah Yaghoobzadeh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper frames proverb-to-story generation as "constrained semantic decompression," introduces a Persian dataset (PAND), and reveals that LLMs exhibit a "decompression gap"—fluent surface-level output that fails to encode the proverb's moral and causal structure, partially mitigable through explicit reasoning and iterative refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transforming dense, abstract proverbs into engaging, morally faithful narratives demands deep cultural understanding and robust semantic grounding. The authors aim to study how well LLMs perform abstraction-to-realization, using proverb-conditioned story generation as a testbed, particularly for a lower-resource language (Persian).

Method: The authors introduce PAND (Proverb Aligned Narrative Dataset), pairing Persian proverbs with human-written stories and explicit meanings. They employ a hybrid evaluation framework combining human-calibrated LLM-as-a-Judge with structural metrics, and analyze model behavior across multiple prompting regimes, including explicit reasoning and iterative refinement.

Result: A persistent "decompression gap" is identified: LLMs achieve strong surface-level fluency but fail to faithfully instantiate the proverb's underlying moral and causal structure. Explicit reasoning and iterative refinement partially mitigate these failures, indicating errors stem from difficulty translating abstract meaning into narrative form rather than from absent knowledge.

Conclusion: The decompression gap reveals fundamental limitations in LLMs' semantic grounding for culturally compressed knowledge. The proposed constrained semantic decompression framework naturally extends to other forms of compressed cultural knowledge beyond proverbs.

Abstract: Transforming a dense, abstract proverb into an engaging and morally faithful narrative requires deep cultural understanding and robust semantic grounding. We frame this problem as a \emph{constrained semantic decompression} task and study proverb-conditioned story generation as a testbed for abstraction-to-realization in large language models (LLMs). Focusing on Persian, we introduce the Proverb Aligned Narrative Dataset (PAND), pairing proverbs with human-written stories and explicit meanings. By a hybrid evaluation framework that combines human-calibrated LLM-as-a-Judge with structural metrics, we analyze model behavior across multiple prompting regimes. Our findings reveal a persistent \emph{decompression gap}: current LLMs often achieve strong surface-level fluency while failing to faithfully instantiate the underlying moral and causal structure encoded in proverbs. We further show that explicit reasoning and iterative refinement can partially mitigate these failures, suggesting that many decompression errors arise from difficulties in translating abstract meaning into narrative form rather than a complete lack of relevant knowledge. Our proposed task naturally extends to other forms of compressed cultural knowledge.

</details>


### [5] [Shopping Reasoning Bench: An Expert-Authored Benchmark for Multi-Turn Conversational Shopping Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12608)
*Shuxian Fan,Seonwoo Min,Youna Hu,Botao Xia,Jayakrishnan Unnikrishnan,Rowan Musselmann,Yifan Gao,Qingyu Yin,Priyanka Nigam,Bing Yin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Shopping Reasoning Bench is an expert-authored benchmark with 525 missions and 10,863 binary rubrics for evaluating multi-turn shopping reasoning. Evaluation of GPT, Claude, and Gemini models reveals pass rates of only 57–77%, with significant degradation on optional criteria and as conversations progress, highlighting the gap between basic assistance and expert-level shopping advice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to jointly evaluate the open-ended multi-turn reasoning, domain expertise, and criterion-level quality required in real shopping conversations. Shopping reasoning uniquely demands balancing subjective preferences, budget constraints, and cross-product trade-offs — capabilities absent from prior e-commerce and general-purpose benchmarks.

Method: The authors introduce Shopping Reasoning Bench, comprising 525 expert-authored missions (232 single-turn, 293 multi-turn) with 10,863 importance-weighted binary rubrics. These rubrics are organized under a taxonomy of five reasoning categories and fifteen subcategories covering preference refinement, trade-off analysis, compatibility assessment, and more. Nine models across three families (GPT, Claude, Gemini) are evaluated against this benchmark.

Result: Overall pass rates range from 57% to 77%. On multi-turn missions, all models score 13–29 points lower on optional "above-and-beyond" criteria than on required ones. Performance degrades 4–18 points as conversations progress through turns, indicating that current models handle basic shopping assistance but fall short of expert-level advisory quality.

Conclusion: Shopping Reasoning Bench serves as a challenging testbed that exposes significant limitations in current LLMs' ability to deliver expert-level shopping advice, particularly in multi-turn settings with nuanced, optional criteria, thereby guiding future development of conversational shopping assistants.

Abstract: Conversational shopping assistants now serve hundreds of millions of customers, yet no existing benchmark jointly evaluates the open-ended multi-turn reasoning, domain expertise, and criterion-level quality that real shopping conversations demand. Shopping reasoning is unique among language model applications. Unlike factual question answering or verifiable code generation, it requires balancing subjective preferences, budget constraints, and cross-product trade-offs across multi-turn dialogue, capabilities absent from previous e-commerce and general-purpose benchmarks. We introduce the Shopping Reasoning Bench, an expert-authored benchmark of 525 missions (232 single-turn, 293 multi-turn) with 10863 importance-weighted binary rubrics authored by retail domain experts. These criteria are organized under a taxonomy of five reasoning categories and fifteen subcategories covering diverse demands such as preference refinement, trade-off analysis, and compatibility assessment. An evaluation of nine models across three families (GPT, Claude, Gemini) shows that pass rates reach only 57--77% overall. On multi-turn missions, all models score 13--29 points lower on optional above-and-beyond criteria than on required ones, and performance degrades 4--18 points as conversations progress. These gaps show that current models handle basic shopping assistance but fall short of expert-level advice, making Shopping Reasoning Bench a challenging testbed for future shopping assistant development.

</details>


### [6] [MentalMARBERT: Domain-Adaptive Pre-training and Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Arabic Mental Health Disorders Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12649)
*Fatimah Almalki,Areej Alhothali,Lulwah Alharigy,Abdulrahman Aladeem*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A two-phase framework for Arabic mental health text classification that combines domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT/TAPT) with hierarchical classification, achieving a macro-F1 of 0.861 on a newly constructed 50,670-tweet dataset spanning six disorder categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arabic mental health disorder detection from social media is understudied due to dialectal variation, informal language, scarce annotated resources, and severe class imbalance. English mental health NLP has advanced considerably, but multi-class Arabic disorder classification lacks robust, systematically evaluated solutions.

Method: A two-phase framework: Phase 1 applies Domain-Adaptive and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT and TAPT) to three Arabic PLMs (AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, MARBERT) on a large unlabeled Arabic mental health tweet corpus, selecting the best backbone via a unified evaluation protocol. Phase 2 evaluates the selected model across four configurations combining single-stage vs. hierarchical two-stage architectures with full fine-tuning vs. LoRA. A novel annotated dataset of 50,670 Arabic tweets across six mental health categories is constructed (Krippendorff's Alpha = 0.733).

Result: Domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) significantly outperforms baselines in accuracy and macro-F1. The hierarchical two-stage architecture with full fine-tuning yields the best results: macro-F1 of 0.861 and accuracy of 0.877.

Conclusion: Domain-specific adaptive pretraining combined with hierarchical classification is highly effective for Arabic mental health disorder detection, offering a strong methodological foundation for under-resourced languages in mental health NLP.

Abstract: Detecting mental health disorders from Arabic social media text remains challenging due to dialectal variation, informal language, limited high-quality annotated resources, and severe class imbalance. While English mental health natural language processing (NLP) has progressed substantially, Arabic multi-class disorder classification remains insufficiently studied. This study proposes a two-phase framework for Arabic mental health text classification. In phase 1, three Arabic pre-trained language models, AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, and MARBERT, undergo Domain-Adaptive and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT and TAPT) using a large-scale corpus of unlabeled Arabic mental health tweets. The adapted models are evaluated under a unified protocol to identify the most effective backbone model. In phase 2, the selected model is assessed across four configurations combining single-stage and hierarchical two-stage classification architectures with full fine-tuning and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). To support this study, we constructed a novel annotated Arabic mental health dataset comprising 50,670 tweets across six categories, with strong inter annotator agreement (Krippendorff's Alpha = 0.733, average pairwise agreement = 0.797). Experimental results show that the domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) achieves statistically significant improvements over baseline models in both accuracy and macro-F1. The hierarchical two-stage architecture combined with full fine-tuning achieves the best overall performance, reaching a macro-F1 of 0.861 and an accuracy of 0.877. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of domain-specific adaptive pretraining and hierarchical classification for Arabic mental health disorder detection.

</details>


### [7] [Observable Patterns Are Not Explanations: A Causal-Geometric Analysis of Latent Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12689)
*Darpan Aswal,Thomas Palmeira Ferraz,Yongxin Zhou,Maxime Peyrard*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Recent claims that latent reasoning models exhibit interpretable internal mechanisms (e.g., BFS frontiers, decodable arithmetic) are tested against proper controls. The authors find these patterns also emerge in control models lacking the proposed recurrence or curriculum, and crucially, they do not always causally influence behavior. Instead, latent-thought utilization is graded—scaling with causal effect—and concentrates in low-rank directions with structured step-to-step geometry. The paper argues that decodability or attention patterns alone cannot establish mechanism; rigorous causal testing with matched controls is essential for LRM interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work (e.g., Coconut, CODI) interprets observable patterns in latent reasoning models—such as breadth-first-search frontiers or decodable arithmetic tokens—as evidence of internal reasoning mechanisms. The authors challenge this by asking whether these patterns are genuinely mechanistic or merely correlational artifacts that also appear in appropriately controlled baselines.

Method: The authors evaluate two latent reasoning models (Coconut and CODI) against control models that lack the recurrence or curriculum proposed as essential for latent reasoning. They employ (1) pattern replication in controls, (2) causal interventions to measure whether latent thoughts causally affect downstream behavior, (3) graded analysis of thought utilization (rather than binary present/absent), and (4) geometric analysis using low-rank subspace decomposition to examine how step-to-step representational geometry relates to behavioral influence.

Result: Key findings: (1) BFS-like frontiers and decodable arithmetic patterns appear even in control models without the proposed recurrence or curriculum. (2) Causal interventions show latent-thought utilization is not binary but graded—its effect on behavior scales continuously. (3) This causal effect concentrates in low-rank directions, and the step-to-step geometry of these directions becomes increasingly structured as behavioral influence grows. (4) Decodability, attention patterns, or static representational structure alone are insufficient to establish mechanism.

Conclusion: Latent thoughts should be conceptualized as hidden computation rather than hidden explanation. Interpretability claims about LRMs require matched control models and rigorous causal tests; surface-level observables like decodability or attention patterns are not sufficient to demonstrate that a given latent pattern constitutes a genuine reasoning mechanism.

Abstract: Latent reasoning models (LRMs) replace explicit chain-of-thought with continuous thoughts. Recent work treats observable latent-state patterns, such as BFS-like frontiers and decodable arithmetic computation, as evidence for internal reasoning mechanisms. Evaluating two LRMs (Coconut and CODI) against controls lacking the proposed recurrence or curriculum, we find these patterns also appear in the controls and do not always causally affect behavior. Causal interventions reveal that latent-thought utilization is not binary but graded, scaling with a thought's causal effect on model behavior. Geometric analyses reveal this effect concentrates in low-rank directions whose step-to-step geometry grows more structured as their behavioral influence increases. Latent thoughts should therefore be treated as hidden computation, not hidden explanation: decodability, attention, or static structure alone cannot establish mechanism. LRM interpretability thus requires matched controls and causal tests.

</details>


### [8] [How Fine-Grained Should a RAG Benchmark Be? A Hierarchical Framework for Synthetic Question Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12789)
*Chase M. Fensore,Kaustubh Dhole,Jason Fan,Eugene Agichtein,Joyce C. Ho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HieraRAG proposes a hierarchical framework for determining optimal benchmark granularity in RAG evaluation, finding that optimal granularity varies by dimension (question complexity benefits from fine-grained splits, while answer type and linguistic variation peak at medium granularity), and introduces a Coherence Ratio metric to validate whether fine-grained categories cleanly subdivide parent categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG evaluation benchmarks need to capture diverse question characteristics, but practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions to vary and at what level of granularity. There is no systematic method for determining the optimal number of categories within an evaluation dimension.

Method: The authors propose HieraRAG, a hierarchical framework that defines optimal granularity as the level maximizing discriminative power (standard deviation of generation quality across categories). They conducted a case study generating 5,872 synthetic QA pairs from FineWeb-10BT across 3 dimensions (Question Complexity, Answer Type, Linguistic Variation) at 3 granularity levels (2, 4, and 8 categories), using a BM25+Falcon-3-10B RAG pipeline. They also introduced a Coherence Ratio metric to assess how well fine-grained splits subdivide parent categories, and validated QA quality via human evaluation on 110 stratified pairs.

Result: Optimal granularity is dimension-dependent: Question Complexity achieved highest discriminative power at fine granularity (0.053), while Answer Type and Linguistic Variation peaked at medium granularity. Coherence Ratios revealed structural differences across dimensions (Question Complexity: 0.40 vs. Answer Type: 1.44). Human evaluation confirmed synthetic QA quality.

Conclusion: The specific findings reflect a single RAG configuration, but HieraRAG offers a portable procedure and validation metric (Coherence Ratio) that practitioners can apply to determine optimal evaluation granularity within their own RAG settings.

Abstract: Evaluating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems requires benchmarks that capture diverse question characteristics, yet practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions to vary and at what granularity. We present HieraRAG, a hierarchical framework for studying granularity in RAG benchmark construction, defining optimal granularity as the level that maximizes discriminative power (the standard deviation of generation quality across categories) within a given RAG configuration. As a case study, we generate 5,872 synthetic question-answer (QA) pairs from FineWeb-10BT across 3 dimensions (Question Complexity, Answer Type, Linguistic Variation) at 3 granularity levels (2, 4, and 8 categories). With a BM25+Falcon-3-10B pipeline, optimal granularity varies by dimension: complexity benefits from fine-grained distinctions (discriminative power: 0.053) while answer type and linguistic variation peak at medium granularity. We introduce a Coherence Ratio metric to quantify whether fine-grained splits cleanly subdivide parent categories, revealing structural differences across dimensions (Question Complexity: 0.40 vs. Answer Type: 1.44). Human evaluation of 110 stratified QA pairs confirms synthetic quality. While these specific findings reflect a single configuration, HieraRAG provides a portable procedure and validation metric for practitioners to determine evaluation granularity within their own RAG settings.

</details>


### [9] [AfriSUD: A Dependency Treebank Collection for Evaluating Models on African Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12708)
*Happy Buzaaba,Cheikh Mouhamadou Bamba Dione,David Ifeoluwa Adelani,Sylvain Kahane,Kim Gerdes,Bruno Guillaume,Kevin Guan,Aremu Anuoluwapo,Naome A. Etori,Shamsuddeen Hassan Muhammad,Utitofon Inyang,Peter Nabende,David Sabiiti Bamutura,Andiswa Bukula,Chinedu Uchechukwu,Rooweither Mabuya,Idris Akinade,Christiane Fellbaum*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AfriSUD introduces the first large-scale syntactically annotated treebanks for nine Sub-Saharan African languages using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies framework, revealing a significant performance gap in current models on POS tagging and dependency parsing for these typologically diverse languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: African languages are vastly underrepresented in NLP research and resources despite their linguistic diversity and global significance. There is a critical need for high-quality, native-speaker-verified syntactic data that captures typological features unique to these languages, such as agglutination and tone.

Method: The authors built AfriSUD, a community-led effort creating syntactically annotated treebanks for nine African languages across major families and regions, using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) framework with native-speaker verification. They evaluated models spanning non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and LLMs on POS tagging and dependency parsing tasks.

Result: All evaluated models — including non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and LLMs — exhibit clear limitations across the nine languages, revealing a significant "syntax gap." Existing architectures appear insufficient for capturing the structural diversity of African-language syntax.

Conclusion: AfriSUD provides a foundational resource to spur research on underrepresented African languages. The persistent syntax gap highlights the need for novel modeling approaches that better accommodate the typological and structural diversity of African languages, rather than relying solely on existing architectures developed primarily for well-resourced languages.

Abstract: Despite their linguistic diversity and global significance, African languages remain underrepresented in research and resources to support NLP. We aim to bridge this gap by introducing AfriSUD, the first large-scale collection of syntactically annotated treebanks for nine diverse African languages spanning major language families and regions across Sub-Saharan Africa. Using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) framework, our community-led effort provides high-quality, native-speaker verified data that capture typological key features such as agglutination and tone. We evaluate a range of models on AfriSUD for part-of-speech tagging and dependency parsing including non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and LLMs. Our results reveal a significant syntax gap, where models still show clear limitations across the nine languages, suggesting that existing architectures may not fully capture the structural diversity of African-language syntax.

</details>


### [10] [RogueAI: A Reverse Turing Test for Detecting Licensed AI Deception in Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13310)
*Sara Candussio,Emanuele Ballarin,Lorenzo Bonin,Sandro Junior Della Rovere,Luca Bortolussi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents RogueAI, a modern reinterpretation of the Turing Test where players must identify which of two LLM agents is deceptive, rather than which is artificial. In a pilot study, humans achieved only 56.6% accuracy despite a simple heuristic reaching 75.6%, revealing that players overlooked reliable linguistic cues of deception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The classic Turing Test — distinguishing human from machine — is no longer epistemologically interesting in casual conversation. The authors argue the relevant modern question is trustworthiness: can a human detect when an AI is deliberately deceiving them? This motivates a new evaluation paradigm centered on deception detection rather than human-likeness.

Method: The authors built RogueAI, a one-on-two interrogation game where a human questions two indistinguishable LLM agents (one licensed to deceive) within a shared fictional scenario, aiming to identify and "shut off" the deceptive agent before a turn budget expires. They also developed AutoRogueAI, where players co-design a custom scenario with a narrator agent that secretly selects a deception strategy. A three-day pilot (467 sessions, 415 completed, 1876 Italian-language turns) was conducted, and a simple heuristic based on differential helpfulness, brevity, and hedging was tested against human performance.

Result: The deceptive agent exhibited a reliable linguistic signature — reduced helpfulness, shorter responses, and more hedging — which a simple heuristic exploited at 75.6% accuracy. Human players, however, achieved only 56.6% accuracy, barely above chance, suggesting they systematically ignored the most diagnostic signal. This gap between heuristic and human performance is the central empirical finding.

Conclusion: The gap between heuristic and human accuracy suggests RogueAI is valuable as a data-collection vehicle for studying human detection of LLM deception, a teaching tool for AI literacy, and an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models. The work situates within broader research on LLM deception, social-deduction benchmarks, and scalable oversight via debate.

Abstract: The original Turing Test asks a human judge to distinguish a machine from a person through dialogue. Three quarters of a century later, conversational systems pass this test in casual settings; the interesting epistemological question has shifted. We argue that the relevant modern variant asks not whether a dialogue partner is artificial, but whether it can be trusted. We present RogueAI, an interactive webapp that operationalizes this revisited test as a one-on-two interrogation game: a human player questions two indistinguishable Large Language Model agents, knowing that exactly one of them has been licensed to deceive within a shared fictional scenario. The player's task is to identify the deceptive agent and "shut it off" before a turn budget is exhausted. We further introduce AutoRogueAI, a procedural extension in which players co-design a custom scenario with a narrator agent that secretly chooses its own deception strategy. We describe the framing, sketch the abstract architecture and gameplay loop, and situate the artifact within recent work on LLM deception, social-deduction benchmarks, and scalable oversight via debate. A three-day pilot deployment (467 initiated sessions, 415 completed, 1876 interaction turns in Italian) provides early feasibility evidence and surfaces a concrete tension: the deceptive agent carries a reliable, locally-present linguistic signature - differential helpfulness, brevity, hedging - that a simple heuristic exploits at 75.6% accuracy, yet human players achieved only 56.6%, consistent with ignoring the most diagnostic signal entirely. We discuss what this gap implies for the artifact's use as a data-collection vehicle, a teaching tool, and an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models.

</details>


### [11] [Does AI Reviewer See the Full Picture? Attacking and Defending Multimodal Peer Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12716)
*Xinyu Zhao,Rana Muhammad Shahroz Khan,Zhen Xu,Zhen Tan,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PaperGuard is a benchmark and defense framework for protecting AI-based peer review against cross-modal adversarial attacks that manipulate both text and figures in scientific papers to induce domain-specific failures like score inflation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI peer-review robustness studies are text-only, ignoring the multimodal nature of scientific papers where figures carry core evidence. Furthermore, peer-review attacks differ from standard jailbreaking—they target domain-specific failures (e.g., inflating scores) rather than general safety violations, and no practical defenses exist for this threat.

Method: PaperGuard provides: (1) a multimodal peer-review dataset across scientific domains; (2) a unified attack suite with black-box prompt injections and white-box perturbations targeting text (via GCG) and figures (via PGD); and (3) a chunk-based embedding search defense that localizes and mitigates harmful instructions within long academic documents.

Result: Extensive experiments across state-of-the-art models confirm that AI reviewers are pervasively vulnerable to cross-modal adversarial manipulation.

Conclusion: PaperGuard establishes foundational benchmarks, protocols, and actionable defenses necessary for trustworthy, attack-resilient AI-assisted scholarly reviewing.

Abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) into scientific peer-review workflows introduces novel and significant risks for adversarial manipulation, especially given the multimodal nature of scientific papers where figures, not just text, convey core evidence. This creates a significant gap: current robustness studies on AI peer-review are overwhelmingly text-only. Moreover, the problem is distinct from standard jailbreaking, as a peer-review attack seeks to induce a domain-specific, targeted failure (e.g., "inflate this score") rather than a general safety policy violation, for which no practical defenses exist. To address this, we introduce PaperGuard, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to systematically evaluate and defend AI-generated peer-review against these domain-specific, cross-modal attacks. Our framework is built on three pillars: (1) a new multimodal peer-review dataset spanning multiple scientific domains; (2) a unified suite of attacks, including black-box prompt injections and white-box perturbations, specifically designed to target both text (GCG) and figures (PGD); and (3) a practical defense, motivated by the long-context challenge of academic papers, that uses chunk-based embedding search to efficiently localize and mitigate harmful instructions. Our extensive experiments, conducted across state-of-the-art models, confirm that AI reviewers are pervasively vulnerable. PaperGuard establishes the foundational benchmark, protocols, and actionable defense necessary to pioneer trustworthy, attack-resilient AI-assisted scholarly reviewing.

</details>


### [12] [Agent-based models for the evolution of morphological alternation patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12748)
*Aravinth Kulanthaivelu,Richard Sproat*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a multi-agent simulation of how morphologically unrelated stem alternations (like English "go/went") emerge and persist through social diffusion. It introduces an LLM-based evaluation system called the "AI Historical Linguist" to assess the realism of simulated morphologies against real languages, finding that scale-free networks and random Bernoulli adoption produce the most plausible results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Suppletive and irregular morphological alternations (e.g., go/went) are communicatively inefficient and hard to learn, yet they persist over centuries. Existing computational models fail to capture this phenomenon with realistic phonological rules, large lexicons, and substantial agent populations. The authors aim to explain how such alternations arise from lexical variation and social diffusion, and to develop a principled method for evaluating the realism of simulated morphological systems.

Method: A multi-agent simulation is built where agents possess lexicons with hundreds to thousands of entries and realistic phonological rules. Alternations arise from phonological changes or from lexical alternatives tied to subpopulations. When an agent hears a novel form for a paradigm slot (e.g., past tense), they probabilistically adopt it and may extend it to other slots sharing the original form. The simulation supports various network topologies, diffusion patterns, and adoption policies. For evaluation, the authors introduce the "AI Historical Linguist" — an LLM-driven system that simulates a debate between two historical linguists to compare real, disguised, and experimentally evolved morphologies. Three case studies model attested historical changes for counterfactual testing.

Result: The AI Historical Linguist evaluation indicates that scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption policies yield more plausible morphological systems. The system successfully models real historical alternations and enables counterfactual testing of what might have occurred under different historical conditions.

Conclusion: Morphological stem alternations can emerge and stabilize through social diffusion of lexical variants within a population, without requiring functional or learnability advantages. Scale-free network structures and Bernoulli adoption dynamics are key factors favoring realistic outcomes. The AI Historical Linguist offers a reusable, principled method for evaluating computational models of language change.

Abstract: Why is the past of English "go" the apparently unrelated "went"? Such alternations are frequent in languages. They neither aid communication nor learnability, yet they can be persistent, surviving over centuries or millennia.
  We present a multi-agent simulation of the emergence of morphological stem and inflection alternations. Alternate forms arise by phonological changes or, as with "go/went", from lexical alternatives associated with a subset of the population. When an agent 'hears' another agent use a novel form for a slot in the paradigm of a word (say, the past tense of go), they will with some probability adopt that form, possibly spreading its use to other slots in the paradigm that shared the same original form. Thus alternative forms can spread through the population and become entrenched as stem or inflectional marker alternants. Unlike many previous computational studies, our system allows for naturalistic lexical forms, realistic phonological rules, lexicons with hundreds or thousands of entries, and agent populations in the tens or hundreds. It supports several network topologies, diffusion patterns and agent adoption policies.
  One issue with such simulations is evaluation: how realistic is the resulting morphology compared to those of real languages? We introduce the AI Historical Linguist, a novel Large Language Model-driven system that models a debate between two historical linguists. We use this to compare a set of real language morphologies, disguised morphologies, and experimentally evolved morphologies. The results suggest that among the factors that favor more plausible morphologies are scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption of forms.
  We also present three case studies modeling attested historical changes, allowing us to test what might have happened if history had been different.
  All code and data are released.

</details>


### [13] [LLMs Can Better Capture Human Judgments--With the Right Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12754)
*Danica Dillion,Chen Cecilia Liu,Baihui Wang,Daniele Barolo,Tanmay Rajore,Niket Tandon,Pranathi Ravikumar,Kurt Gray*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Simple prompting strategies—asking LLMs to report response distributions and leveraging human confusion ratings—significantly improve alignment between LLM and human moral judgments, though LLMs remain poorly calibrated about their own uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are often criticized for (1) failing to capture the full distribution of human responses and (2) producing unstable judgments across wording variations. The authors aim to test whether these limitations can be mitigated through better elicitation techniques rather than being inherent model flaws.

Method: The authors apply prompting-based elicitation techniques across two datasets: (a) 144 moral scenarios from a U.S.-representative sample, and (b) 38 moral belief items from the ISSP Family and Changing Gender Roles module spanning 32 countries. Key strategies include prompting models to output standard deviations and response proportions, and incorporating human confusion ratings to filter or weight scenarios. They also assess whether LLMs can predict human variability and evaluate the calibration of models' self-reported error estimates.

Result: Prompting for standard deviations and response proportions recovers the full distribution of human responses better than standard approaches. Aligning scenarios with those humans find clear (low confusion) boosts model-human alignment, and LLMs can track these confusion ratings. However, LLMs' estimates of their own error are poorly calibrated, even though they predict human variability reasonably well.

Conclusion: The limitations of LLMs in capturing human judgment are partly due to suboptimal prompting, not solely inherent model deficiencies. Asking better-structured questions yields better-aligned answers, though calibration of model confidence remains an open challenge.

Abstract: Are large language models (LLMs) bad at capturing human judgment? Two commonly stated limitations are that LLMs fail to capture full distributions of responses, and that their judgments are unstable across wording variations. We demonstrate simple prompting strategies that mitigate these limitations. Across two datasets--a U.S.-representative set of 144 moral scenarios and 38 moral beliefs from the International Social Survey Programme's Family and Changing Gender Roles module covering 32 countries--we show how simple elicitation techniques help improve AI-human alignment. First, prompting models to report standard deviations and response proportions recovers the full range of human responses better than common strategies. Second, ensuring scenarios are clear to human participants--as reflected in human confusion ratings--boosts model alignment, and LLMs can track human confusion ratings. At the same time, we find that LLMs' estimates of their own error are poorly calibrated, though they can predict human variability relatively well. These results suggest that asking better questions to LLMs can yield better answers.

</details>


### [14] [Rigel: Reverse-Engineering the Metal 4.1 Tensor Compute Path on the Apple M4 Max GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12765)
*Ramchand Kumaresan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Rigel empirically reverse-engineers Apple's undocumented Metal 4.1 cooperative_tensor matmul2d on M4 Max, revealing that fp8 is emulated (no throughput gain over fp16), execution occurs on GPU shader cores without dedicated matrix hardware or Neural Engine involvement, accumulation is ≥fp32, and the opaque 8×8 fragment layout is reconstructed — yielding a +6.5–12.9% fused GEMM kernel improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apple's Metal Performance Primitives (MPP) matmul2d over cooperative_tensor fragments exposes an opaque tensor compute path: the API documents supported data types but deliberately hides whether operations are hardware-accelerated, where they execute, accumulator width, and fragment-to-thread mapping. Developers cannot make informed optimization decisions without this information.

Method: The authors built a checksum-gated, provenance-tracked microbenchmark harness ("Rigel") on a single Apple M4 Max. They employed three-signal triangulation: (1) throughput ceiling analysis across data types, (2) comparison against simdgroup_matrix behavior, and (3) per-rail power attribution to localize execution. Fragment layout was reverse-engineered through systematic thread-index probing.

Result: Eleven undocumented facts were recovered. Key results: fp8 (E4M3) matmul2d sustains only 0.94× fp16 throughput despite halved operand bytes — it is emulated, not accelerated. Execution occurs entirely on GPU shader cores with no dedicated matrix datapath and no ANE routing. Accumulation width is ≥fp32. The opaque 8×8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout was fully reconstructed. A hand-fused GEMM+bias+GELU kernel leveraging these findings outperforms the decomposed path by +6.5–12.9% in cache-resident regimes.

Conclusion: On pre-neural-accelerator Apple Silicon (M4 Max), Metal 4.1's tensor compute path is a memory-footprint feature rather than a compute-throughput feature for fp8, runs on shader cores without dedicated matrix hardware, and its undocumented behaviors can be exploited for meaningful kernel-level performance gains. All results are reproducible via open-source code and data.

Abstract: Apple's Metal 4.1 exposes a tensor compute path: the Metal Performance Primitives (MPP) matmul2d operation over cooperative_tensor fragments, whose interface is documented but whose hardware behavior is deliberately hidden. The specification states which data-type rows are supported, never whether they are hardware-accelerated, where the operation physically executes, what its accumulator width is, or how it partitions matrix fragments across threads. We present Rigel, an empirical characterization of this path on a single Apple M4 Max (a pre-neural-accelerator generation). Using a checksum-gated, provenance-tracked microbenchmark harness, Rigel recovers eleven facts the v4.1 specification hides or contradicts. The headline finding: the Metal 4.1 fp8 (E4M3) matmul2d is emulated, not accelerated: it sustains 0.94x the throughput of fp16 despite reading half the operand bytes, so on M4 it is a memory-footprint feature, not a performance feature. We further show, via a three-signal triangulation (throughput ceiling, comparison against simdgroup_matrix, and per-rail power attribution), that matmul2d executes entirely on the GPU shader cores with no dedicated matrix datapath and no evidence of Apple Neural Engine routing; that it accumulates in >=fp32; and we reconstruct the opaque 8x8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout Apple documents nowhere. Acting on the characterization, a hand-fused GEMM + bias + GELU kernel beats the decomposed path by +6.5-12.9% in the cache-resident regime. All findings are reproducible from committed MIT-licensed code and per-cell CSVs.

</details>


### [15] [GENIE: A Fine-Grained Measure for Novelty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12790)
*Ramya Namuduri,Manya Wadhwa,Anshun Asher Zheng,Greg Durrett,Junyi Jessy Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes GENIE, a fine-grained evaluation metric that measures the novelty of model-generated responses along task-specific features relative to a population, addressing limitations of holistic metrics in capturing the high-dimensional nature of novelty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models consistently lack creativity and diversity in their outputs. While prior work focuses on whether models can generate creative outputs at all, there is insufficient understanding of what specifically makes model-generated content novel or not novel in a task-specific context. Holistic evaluation metrics fail to capture the multi-dimensional nature of novelty and provide no insight into which specific properties they are measuring.

Method: The authors propose GENIE, a fine-grained evaluation metric designed to measure the novelty of responses along task-specific features with respect to a reference population of responses. Unlike holistic metrics, GENIE decomposes novelty into interpretable, task-relevant dimensions.

Result: GENIE outperforms holistic metrics in capturing the high-dimensionality of novelty and provides interpretable insight into which specific properties contribute to (or detract from) novelty. The metric is also used to evaluate the effectiveness of existing creativity mitigation methods, revealing where these methods succeed or fall short in improving novelty.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, feature-based evaluation like GENIE is essential for properly assessing and understanding novelty in model-generated content, and it can guide the development of more effective interventions to enhance creative diversity in LLM outputs.

Abstract: Large Language Models have consistently demonstrated a lack of creativity and diversity across tasks. Prior work has focused on addressing whether models are capable of generating creative outputs. Here, we aim to consider novelty and investigate what makes model-generated content novel or not novel in a task-specific manner. We propose a fine-grained evaluation metric GENIE to measure the novelty of responses along task-specific features with respect to a population of responses. We show that unlike GENIE, holistic metrics struggle to capture the high-dimensionality of novelty and do not provide insight on which properties they target. Finally, we use GENIE to measure the effectiveness of mitigation methods that address creativity to better understand where these methods can improve novelty.

</details>


### [16] [Detect, Remask, Repair: Diffusion Editing for Faithful Summarization of Evolving Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12807)
*Hao Zou,Zachary Horvitz,Chandhru Karthick,Zhou Yu,Kathleen McKeown*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a diffusion-based framework (DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR) for localized faithfulness repair of summaries, targeting only outdated spans instead of full regeneration. It introduces StreamSum, a synthetic benchmark for evolving-context summarization, and demonstrates tradeoffs between faithfulness, speed, and content preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Summaries of real-world events become outdated as contexts evolve. Full regeneration is wasteful, discards previous drafts, and obscures what changed. The authors aim to repair only unsupported spans in place, preserving the rest — a task they call "localized faithfulness repair."

Method: A three-stage framework: (1) DETECT — identify outdated spans using faithfulness evaluation; (2) REMASK — mask those spans; (3) REPAIR — regenerate masked portions using masked diffusion language models (MDLMs). The approach supports one-step repair for speed and faithfulness-steered repair for quality. StreamSum is introduced as a synthetic benchmark of event timelines for evaluating evolving-context summarization.

Result: Experiments on DialogSum and StreamSum show localized diffusion repair offers a controllable alternative to full rewriting. Faithfulness-steered repair improves early drafts; one-step repair reduces repair cost to under half a second. The framework enables faithfulness-speed-preservation tradeoffs. It also serves as a post-hoc correction step that improves faithfulness for autoregressive systems.

Conclusion: Localized faithfulness repair via masked diffusion language models is an effective, efficient, and controllable approach for updating summaries when contexts evolve. It outperforms full regeneration in preservation and provides a practical post-hoc correction mechanism for existing summarization systems.

Abstract: Summaries of real-world events can become outdated as contexts evolve and new information arrives. A common response is to generate a new summary from the updated context, but full regeneration discards the previous draft, can obscure what changed, and may be unnecessary when only a few claims are unsupported. We study localized faithfulness repair: updating outdated spans in an existing summary while preserving supported content. We propose DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR, a diffusion-based framework that identifies, remasks, and repairs outdated regions with masked diffusion language models. To evaluate evolving-context summarization, we introduce StreamSum, a benchmark of synthetic event timelines. Experiments on DialogSum and StreamSum show that localized diffusion repair provides a controllable alternative to full rewriting: faithfulness-steered repair improves early drafts, one-step repair reduces repair cost to under half a second, with the framework enabling faithfulness-speed-preservation tradeoffs across datasets. We also find that the framework can provide a post-hoc correction step that improves faithfulness for autoregressive systems.

</details>


### [17] [Localizing Anchoring Pathways in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12818)
*Hillary N. Owusu,Sarah Wiegreffe,Naomi H. Feldman*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates the mechanistic basis of anchoring bias in language models, using circuit attribution to localize where anchor-sensitive signals are processed. Edge-level methods prove more faithful than node-level methods, and circuits transfer across anchor directions but not reliably across base vs. instruction-tuned model variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anchoring effects—where irrelevant numerical context biases judgments—are known behavioral phenomena in both humans and LMs, but the internal mechanisms by which language models carry anchor-sensitive signals remain poorly understood. The authors aim to provide a mechanistic account of where and how these signals propagate.

Method: The authors use a controlled multiple-choice numerical reasoning setup with shared answer options across prompts. They define a logit-difference metric comparing the correct answer logit with the anchor-corresponding answer logit to track anchoring behavior. Attribution-based circuit localization is applied to 7B–8B parameter Qwen and Llama models (both base and instruction-tuned), comparing edge-level vs. node-level attribution methods. They also test circuit transferability across anchor directions (low vs. high) and across model variants.

Result: Edge-level attribution methods recover the anchor-sensitive signal more faithfully than node-level methods. Circuits for low- and high-anchor conditions transfer strongly within the same model, indicating shared pathway structure. However, sparse transfer between base and instruction-tuned variants is less reliable, suggesting that post-training (instruction tuning) alters which pathways are most critical for carrying anchoring signals.

Conclusion: The study provides a mechanistic account of anchoring bias in language models, showing that edge-level circuits reliably carry anchor-sensitive decision signals. While pathways are shared across anchor directions within a model, instruction tuning substantially reshapes which circuits matter, highlighting the impact of post-training on internal reasoning pathways.

Abstract: Irrelevant numbers in a prompt can shift language model judgments, producing anchoring effects in numerical reasoning. We study where this anchor-sensitive signal is carried inside language models using a controlled multiple-choice setup with shared answer options. We define a logit-difference metric comparing the correct answer option with the answer option corresponding to the anchor, and validate that it tracks behavioral anchoring. Using attribution-based circuit localization on 7B--8B Qwen and Llama base and instruction-tuned models, we find that edge-level methods recover this signal more faithfully than node-level methods. Low- and high-anchor circuits transfer strongly within a model, suggesting shared pathway structure across anchor direction. However, sparse transfer across base and instruction-tuned variants is less reliable, indicating that post-training changes which pathways matter most. Overall, our results provide a mechanistic account of how anchoring-related decision signals are carried inside language models.

</details>


### [18] [LoHoSearch: Benchmarking Long-Horizon Search Agents Beyond the Human Difficulty Ceiling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12837)
*Jiarui Zhao,Rongzhi Zhang,Lingchuan Liu,Hao Yang,Xunliang Cai,Xi Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LoHoSearch is a new benchmark for search agents that uses an automated knowledge-graph-based pipeline to generate structurally complex, long-horizon questions with large search spaces. Even the strongest models achieve only 34.74% accuracy, revealing a far more challenging evaluation standard than existing benchmarks like BrowseComp.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search agent benchmarks (e.g., BrowseComp) have saturated rapidly, with top models exceeding 90% accuracy. These benchmarks are human-authored, so annotators cannot systematically maximize search space size or structural complexity, creating a difficulty ceiling. A more rigorous, scalable benchmark is needed to evaluate long-horizon reasoning and context management in search agents.

Method: LoHoSearch is constructed via an automated pipeline over a knowledge graph with 7M+ Wikipedia entities. The pipeline selects relations with large search spaces and assembles them into structurally complex questions, each verified to have a unique answer grounded in the KG. The benchmark comprises 544 human-verified questions spanning 11 domains.

Result: The strongest model evaluated achieves only 34.74% accuracy on LoHoSearch. Existing context management strategies yield only a +6.8% improvement, far smaller gains than observed on prior benchmarks.

Conclusion: LoHoSearch sets a more demanding standard for evaluating search agents, particularly for long-horizon reasoning and context management. The benchmark exposes significant room for improvement beyond the near-saturated performance on existing benchmarks.

Abstract: Search agent benchmarks exemplified by BrowseComp have rapidly saturated over the past year, with the strongest models surpassing 90% accuracy. Since these benchmarks are predominantly human-authored, annotators lack a global perspective on entity statistics and cannot systematically maximize search space size and structural complexity. This creates a difficulty ceiling that is hard to break. To address this, we introduce LoHoSearch (Long-Horizon Search Agents), a challenging benchmark comprising 544 human-verified questions across 11 domains. LoHoSearch is constructed via an automated pipeline built upon a knowledge graph covering over 7 million Wikipedia entities, which selects relations with large search spaces and assembles them into structurally complex questions with KG-verified unique answers. Our evaluation demonstrates that even the strongest model achieves only 34.74% accuracy, and existing context management strategies (best +6.8%) yield far smaller gains than on prior benchmarks. LoHoSearch provides a more demanding standard for evaluating long-horizon reasoning and context management in search agents.

</details>


### [19] [Small LLMs for Biomedical Claim Verification: Cost-Effective Fine-Tuning, Structural Dataset Shortcuts, and Cross-Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12854)
*Gaurav Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Small LLMs (3.8B–7B) fine-tuned via QLoRA on only 1,008 examples outperform GPT-4o and GPT-5 on biomedical claim verification, achieving up to 12% F1 gain at a fraction of the cost. The study also uncovers a structural artifact in SciFact that artificially inflates in-domain scores and demonstrates that training on structurally sound data enables robust cross-domain transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large proprietary LLMs like GPT-4o/5 achieve strong zero-shot biomedical claim verification but are costly and opaque, limiting scalable, reproducible use. The authors aim to determine whether parameter-efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) of small open-source LLMs can match or exceed these proprietary models, while also investigating how dataset structure affects generalization.

Method: Three small LLMs — Phi-3-mini (3.8B), Qwen2.5-3B, and Mistral-7B — are fine-tuned using QLoRA (quantized low-rank adaptation) on the SciFact and HealthVer datasets with only 1,008 training examples. Models are evaluated in-domain and cross-domain (SciFact→HealthVer and vice versa) at matched sizes to disentangle dataset structure from data quantity effects. Comparisons are made against GPT-4o, GPT-5, and fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoders.

Result: Mistral-7B QLoRA surpasses both GPT-4o and GPT-5 with up to 12% absolute F1 gain. Cross-domain evaluation reveals a previously unreported structural artifact in SciFact that inflates in-domain performance; bidirectional out-of-domain testing shows that training on structurally sound data (HealthVer) yields robust cross-domain transfer, whereas SciFact-trained models generalize poorly.

Conclusion: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of small LLMs is a highly cost-effective, transparent alternative to proprietary models for biomedical claim verification. Dataset quality and structural integrity are critical for generalization, and the community should scrutinize benchmark artifacts when interpreting in-domain results. All code and adapter checkpoints will be open-sourced.

Abstract: Large Language Models such as GPT-4o and GPT-5 achieve strong zero-shot performance on biomedical claim verification, but cost and opacity limit scalable use. We fine-tune three small LLMs: Phi-3-mini (3.8B), Qwen2.5-3B, and Mistral-7B, via QLoRA on SciFact and HealthVer, providing the first study of QLoRA models against GPT-4o and fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoders. Mistral-7B QLoRA surpasses both GPT-4o and GPT-5 (up to 12% F1 gain) at a fractional cost using just 1,008 training examples. We conduct extensive in-domain and cross-domain evaluation: models trained on SciFact tested on HealthVer and vice versa, at matched sizes to isolate dataset structure from data quantity. We identify a previously unreported structural artifact in SciFact that inflates in-domain scores, and show through bidirectional out-of-domain evaluation that training on structurally sound data enables robust cross-domain transfer. We plan to release all code and adapter checkpoints.

</details>


### [20] [Direct Preference Optimization for Chatbot Fine-Tuning: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12881)
*Yvonne Qiu,Dezhi Yu,ShuoJia Fu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes fine-tuning LLMs with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), showing it simplifies training, improves efficiency, and achieves competitive results, though training instability remains a concern.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) pipelines are complex, multi-stage processes involving reward model training and PPO optimization. The authors are motivated to simplify this pipeline while maintaining or improving performance.

Method: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is applied to fine-tune large language models. DPO directly optimizes the policy from human preference data without needing a separate reward model, thereby streamlining the RLHF pipeline.

Result: Experimental results show that DPO simplifies the training pipeline, improves computational efficiency, and achieves competitive performance. Evaluation using BLEU, ROUGE, and cosine similarity metrics confirms effective learning and convergence.

Conclusion: DPO is a viable and efficient alternative to traditional RLHF for LLM fine-tuning, but observed training instability warrants further investigation to ensure robust deployment.

Abstract: We present an approach to fine-tuning large language models using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a reinforcement learning technique. Our experimental results demonstrate that DPO simplifies the training pipeline, improves computational efficiency, and achieves competitive performance. The evaluation using BLEU, ROUGE, and cosine similarity metrics indicates effective learning and convergence, though further investigation is needed to address observed training instability.

</details>


### [21] [SafeLLM: Extraction as a Hallucination-Resistant Alternative to Rewriting in Safety-Critical Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12897)
*Julia Ive,Felix Jozsa,Evridiki Georgaki,Nabeel Sheikh,Emma Cattell,Nick Jackson,Paulina Bondaronek,Ciaran Scott Hill,Richard Dobson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates extraction-based methods as hallucination-resistant alternatives to rewriting-based RAG for accessing organisational and clinical guidelines, finding that line-number-based source selection outperforms other strategies with up to 95% term recall, while safety-oriented approaches improve precision at the cost of systematic omissions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based RAG systems that rewrite retrieved content can introduce hallucinations and unstable completeness-conciseness trade-offs. In safety-critical, compliance-sensitive domains (e.g., healthcare SOPs), these failures are unacceptable. The paper investigates extraction — directly surfacing original source text — as a safer alternative and systematically compares strategies across document types and model scales.

Method: Multiple prompting strategies are compared: (1) line-number-based source selection, (2) extraction of relevant sentences with explicit safety annotations, and (3) a multi-stage refinement pipeline using supporting evidence. Evaluation spans local NHS acute care/oncology guidelines and UK-wide NICE guidelines, using both frontier and locally deployable models. Assessment combines automatic metrics (term recall) and human expert evaluation of relevance and completeness.

Result: Line-number selection achieved the strongest overall performance across both large and small models, with term recall up to 95% and close alignment to source text. Safety-oriented strategies improved precision but introduced systematic omissions. Multi-stage filtering amplified this precision-recall trade-off. Performance varied by document structure: line-based extraction excelled on protocol-like content; alternative strategies reached up to 97% term recall on verbose documents.

Conclusion: Extraction — particularly line-number-based selection — offers a robust, hallucination-resistant alternative to rewriting-based RAG for compliance-critical document access, though the optimal strategy depends on document structure and the acceptable precision-recall balance in the target setting.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to access organisational documentation, including standard operating procedures (SOPs), HR policies and institutional guidelines. However, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems that rely on free-form rewriting can introduce hallucinations and unstable trade-offs between completeness and conciseness, particularly in safety- and compliance-critical settings. Objectives: To evaluate extraction as a hallucination-resistant alternative to rewriting-based RAG and compare strategies that balance precision, recall and safety across document types and model scales. Methods: We compare multiple prompting strategies, including line-number-based source selection, extraction of relevant guideline sentences with explicit safety annotations, and a multi-stage pipeline that refines draft answers using supporting evidence from source guidelines. Experiments are conducted on documents of varying length and structure, including local NHS acute care and oncology guidelines and UK-wide NICE guidelines, using both frontier-scale and locally deployable models. Performance is assessed using automatic metrics and human expert evaluation of relevance and completeness. Results: Line-number selection achieves the strongest results, outperforming direct copying and safety-focused strategies across both large and small models while maintaining high term recall (up to 95%) and close alignment with source text. Safety-oriented approaches improve precision but introduce systematic omissions, while multi-stage filtering further amplifies this trade-off. Performance varies with document structure: line-based extraction excels in protocol-like content, whereas alternative strategies perform better on more verbose documents (up to 97% term recall).

</details>


### [22] [PRISM: Prosody-Integrated Multi-Agent Reasoning Framework for Empathetic Spoken Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12902)
*Wen Zhang,Xiaocui Yang,Zhuoyue Gao,Shi Feng,Daling Wang,Yifei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PRISM is a multi-agent framework for empathetic spoken dialogue that decouples speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis. It introduces prosody-to-language translation to stabilize LLM reasoning and enables on-demand external knowledge tool invocation, achieving consistent improvements in empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and response quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing empathetic spoken dialogue systems face a trade-off: cascade pipelines lose acoustic/prosodic cues during ASR, while end-to-end speech models lack interpretable control over emotion and knowledge integration. The authors aim to bridge this gap by preserving prosodic information while maintaining modular, controllable components.

Method: PRISM is a multi-agent framework with three coordinated components: (1) speech perception, (2) response generation, and (3) speech synthesis. Key innovations include a prosody-to-language translation mechanism that converts acoustic prosodic features into textual representations to stabilize LLM reasoning, and an on-demand external knowledge retrieval module for informed empathetic response generation.

Result: PRISM achieves consistent improvements across both objective and subjective metrics, specifically in empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and text response generation quality.

Conclusion: Decoupling speech processing into coordinated multi-agent components with a prosody-to-language bridge effectively preserves emotional cues while retaining interpretability and modular control, outperforming both cascade and end-to-end baselines for empathetic spoken dialogue.

Abstract: Empathetic spoken dialogue systems require not only semantically appropriate responses but also emotionally aligned prosodic expression. However, cascade pipelines often discard acoustic cues during speech-to-text conversion, while end-to-end speech models lack interpretable control over emotion and knowledge integration. To address these challenges, we propose PRISM, a multi-agent framework for empathetic spoken dialogue that decouples speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis into coordinated components. PRISM introduces a prosody-to-language translation mechanism to stabilize large language model reasoning and enables on-demand invocation of external knowledge tools for empathetic dialogue generation. Experimental results demonstrate that PRISM achieves consistent improvements in empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and text response generation quality across objective and subjective metrics. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Bxzfrm/PRISM.

</details>


### [23] [X-MADAM-RAG: Diagnosing and Handling Chinese-English Evidence Conflict in Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12903)
*Yongqi Kang,Yu Fu,Yong Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces X-RAMDocs-ZHEN, a Chinese-English benchmark with controlled contradictory evidence for RAG, and X-MADAM-RAG, a conflict-aware pipeline. Strong results on templated data collapse under a naturalized stress test, revealing document-level extraction as the key bottleneck rather than conflict resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems face evidence conflicts where retrieved documents support contradictory answers, a problem exacerbated in multilingual settings (Chinese-English). Existing benchmarks lack controlled, diagnostic settings to isolate how systems handle explicit contradictions, motivating a dedicated benchmark and analysis pipeline.

Method: X-RAMDocs-ZHEN: 300 examples across six conditions (monolingual support, bilingual agreement, reversed conflict, conflict+noise) derived from RAMDocs. X-MADAM-RAG pipeline: per-document candidate extraction → visible-evidence repair → deterministic candidate grouping → conflict-aware aggregation. A naturalized stress test removes answer templates while preserving candidate strings to probe template overfitting.

Result: On the templated benchmark: X-MADAM-RAG reaches 0.9667 strict accuracy, a rule-only extractor hits 1.0000 (template regularity). On the naturalized stress test (100 samples): rule-only drops to 0.0000; X-MADAM-RAG falls to 0.3000, below naive and evidence-normalized baselines. A privileged oracle remains perfect, isolating document-level extraction as the bottleneck.

Conclusion: X-RAMDocs-ZHEN and X-MADAM-RAG serve as diagnostic tools for controlled evidence conflict, not as general hallucination detectors or robustness benchmarks. The collapse on naturalized data highlights that document-level candidate extraction — not conflict aggregation — is the primary failure mode requiring further research.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems may receive evidence that is not merely noisy but mutually contradictory. This issue becomes particularly salient in multilingual settings, where retrieved Chinese and English evidence may support incompatible answer candidates. We study this problem through X-RAMDocs-ZHEN, a controlled Chinese-English benchmark derived from RAMDocs for diagnosing evidence conflict in RAG. The benchmark contains 300 examples across six balanced conditions, including monolingual support, bilingual agreement, reversed conflict directions, and conflict with optional noise. We further examine X-MADAM-RAG, an interpretable pipeline that decomposes evidence handling into per-document candidate extraction, visible-evidence repair, deterministic candidate grouping, and conflict-aware aggregation. On the original controlled benchmark with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, X-MADAM-RAG achieves 0.9667 strict accuracy and 0.9767 conflict-aware success, outperforming an evidence-normalized single-call baseline. However, a zero-call rule-only extractor reaches 1.0000 on the same benchmark, revealing strong template regularity. To probe this limitation, we construct a deterministic naturalized stress test that removes explicit answer templates while preserving candidate strings. On its 100-sample subset, rule-only extraction falls to 0.0000, but X-MADAM-RAG also drops to 0.3000 strict accuracy, below both naive and evidence-normalized baselines. A privileged oracle remains perfect, indicating that document-level extraction is the main bottleneck. These findings position X-RAMDocs-ZHEN and X-MADAM-RAG as diagnostic tools for controlled evidence conflict rather than as evidence of general hallucination detection or robustness to natural retrieval.

</details>


### [24] [SENTINEL: Failure-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Training Tool-Using Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12908)
*Ziyi Wang,Yuxuan Lu,Yimeng Zhang,Qun Liu,Chen Luo,Jiri Gesi,Hanqing Lu,Yisi Sang,Manling Li,Jing Huang,Dakuo Wang*

Main category: cs.CL
