TL;DR: SENTINEL is a failure-driven RL framework that converts a solver agent's failed trajectories into targeted training tasks via a Controller-Proposer-Solver loop, improving tool-using LM agents by training on their own weaknesses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL for tool-using agents suffers from distributional mismatch: fixed pre-training tasks become increasingly mismatched with the policy's evolving capabilities, wasting rollouts on uninformative tasks. The key insight is that model failures themselves provide a rich, adaptive signal for targeted improvement.

Method: SENTINEL uses a three-component loop: (1) the Controller analyzes failed trajectories to identify recurring error patterns and summarize weaknesses; (2) the Proposer generates new executable tasks explicitly designed to stress those identified weaknesses; (3) the Solver is trained via RL on these targeted tasks. The cycle repeats, creating a self-improving feedback loop grounded in real failure modes.

Result: On Tau2-Bench Retail with Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, SENTINEL improved Pass@1 from 66.4 to 74.9 and outperformed RL trained on general synthetic tasks across all Pass@k metrics.

Conclusion: Model failures serve as an effective, scalable source of targeted training signal. Failure-driven task generation adapts to the policy's current weaknesses, yielding more efficient and effective RL training for tool-using LM agents compared to static task distributions.

Abstract: Language model agents are increasingly effective in solving realistic tasks through multi-turn tool use. However, training reliable tool-using agents remains challenging in practice. While reinforcement learning provides an on-policy paradigm for improving agents from their own environment interactions, its effectiveness depends heavily on the training task distribution. When tasks are fixed before training, the task distribution can become increasingly mismatched with the policy's evolving capabilities, causing many rollouts to be spent on uninformative tasks. We propose SENTINEL, a failure-driven reinforcement learning framework that turns the Solver's rollout failures into targeted training tasks. SENTINEL follows a Controller--Proposer--Solver loop: the Controller analyzes failed trajectories and summarizes recurring error patterns, the Proposer generates executable tasks that stress these weaknesses, and the Solver is trained on the targeted tasks. On Tau2-Bench Retail with Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, SENTINEL improves Pass\^{}1 from 66.4 to 74.9 and outperforms RL on general synthetic tasks across Pass\^{}k metrics. These results demonstrate that model failures provide an effective and scalable source of targeted training signal for improving tool-using language model agents.

</details>


### [25] [PiDA: Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation for Robust Vietnamese Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12911)
*Giang Son Nguyen,Tung X. Nguyen,Hieu Minh Truong,Nhu Vo,Wray Buntine,Dung D. Le*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper systematically categorizes ASR phonetic errors in Vietnamese speech translation, quantifies their impact on NMT, and proposes Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA) — a method that generates synthetic ASR-like errors using phonetic embeddings — which improves BLEU by up to +2.04 on erroneous ASR outputs while maintaining clean-text performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cascaded ST systems suffer from error propagation when ASR produces incorrect transcripts. While this is known, no prior work systematically categorizes ASR errors for Vietnamese ST by phonetic cause or quantifies their specific impact on downstream NMT. The authors aim to fill this gap and leverage the insights to design a targeted mitigation strategy.

Method: The authors first classify ASR substitution errors in Vietnamese by phonetic cause (vowel, tone, consonant, or combination). They use Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling to quantify how each phonetic error type affects downstream NMT performance. Building on the finding that phonetic confusions dominate, they propose PiDA: a data augmentation technique that replaces words in clean training transcripts with phonetically similar alternatives using phonetic word embeddings, simulating realistic ASR errors. An NMT model is then fine-tuned on this PiDA-augmented version of FLEURS Vietnamese-English.

Result: Phonetic confusions account for the majority of ASR substitution errors, not random noise. These phonetic errors significantly degrade ST quality. Fine-tuning on PiDA-augmented data improves translation of erroneous ASR outputs by up to +2.04 BLEU over standard fine-tuning, while also yielding a slight improvement on clean-text translation.

Conclusion: Phonetic confusions are the dominant source of ASR errors in Vietnamese and are particularly harmful to downstream ST. PiDA effectively mitigates this by exposing the NMT model to realistic, phonetically-motivated corruptions during training, offering a simple yet effective strategy for robust speech translation without sacrificing clean-input performance.

Abstract: Cascaded speech translation (ST) systems suffer from error propagation when Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) outputs incorrect transcripts. We present the first systematic categorization of ASR errors for Vietnamese ST, classifying substitution errors by phonetic cause and quantifying their impact on downstream Neural Machine Translation (NMT) performance using Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling. We confirm that most ASR substitution errors arise from phonetic confusions rather than random noise, and that these phonetic errors significantly degrade ST quality. Motivated by this finding, we propose Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA), which generates ASR-like corruptions by substituting words with phonetically similar alternatives using phonetic word embeddings. Fine-tuning on a PiDA-augmented version of FLEURS Vietnamese-English improves translation of erroneous ASR outputs (up to +2.04 BLEU over standard fine-tuning) while also slightly improving clean-text performance.

</details>


### [26] [Polar: A Benchmark for Evaluating Political Bias in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12922)
*Sangho Kim,Heejin Kim,Yoonhee Park,Hyunggeun Jeon,Jaejin Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Polar is a multiple-choice benchmark for measuring political bias in LLMs via option-level likelihoods, covering 4,026 instances across two ideological axes and eight issue categories. Evaluated on 38 LLMs in U.S. and South Korean contexts, models lean left-progressive on U.S. content but are more centered on Korean content; presentation language alone can shift bias measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Political bias in LLMs is increasingly important but difficult to measure reproducibly across different political and linguistic contexts. Existing approaches relying on prompt-based generation lack consistency, motivating a likelihood-based, cross-contextual evaluation framework.

Method: Polar is a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark that measures political bias using option-level likelihoods rather than generation. It covers two ideological axes (economic and social) and eight issue categories from the Manifesto Project. The benchmark evaluates models in parallel across U.S. and South Korean political contexts, with translation experiments isolating the effect of presentation language. 38 LLMs are assessed.

Result: All 38 LLMs lean left-progressive on U.S. political content. On South Korean content, models display more centered and ideologically mixed patterns. Translation experiments reveal that presentation language alone can shift measured political bias, demonstrating that bias is not invariant to linguistic framing.

Conclusion: Political bias in LLMs is context-dependent, varying with political setting, issue category, model family, and language of presentation. Reliable bias assessment requires multilingual and cross-contextual evaluation frameworks rather than monolingual, single-context benchmarks.

Abstract: Political bias in large language models (LLMs) is increasingly significant, but difficult to measure reproducibly across political and linguistic contexts. We introduce Polar, a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark that measures political bias through option-level likelihoods rather than prompt-based generation. Polar covers two ideological axes and eight issue categories derived from the Manifesto Project, and evaluates models in parallel across U.S. and South Korean political contexts. Across 38 LLMs, measured bias varies systematically with political context, issue category, model group, and presentation language. All models lean left-progressive on U.S. political content, but show more centered and mixed patterns on South Korean content. Translation experiments further show that presentation language alone can shift measured bias. These findings highlight the need for multilingual and cross-contextual evaluation of political bias in LLMs.

</details>


### [27] [Multi-Turn Reasoning When Context Arrives in Pieces: Scalable Sharding and Memory-Augmented RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12941)
*Shu Tong Luo,Wenqin Liu,Rui Liu,Mingming Gong,Jiaxian Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLMs suffer up to 65% accuracy degradation when task-critical information is fragmented across multiple conversation turns ("Lost in Conversation"). The authors propose training models with a compact rolling memory instead of full history attention, using a scalable sharding pipeline that converts single-turn QA datasets into multi-turn episodes. Memory-augmented models trained only on sharded GSM8K show strong multi-turn improvements and zero-shot generalization, outperforming full-history baselines even at test time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit significant performance degradation when users reveal task-critical information incrementally across multiple conversation turns, even though the full context remains available. This "Lost in Conversation" phenomenon lacks scalable solutions, as creating multi-turn training data typically requires costly manual annotation.

Method: The authors introduce a low-cost sharding pipeline that automatically converts single-turn QA datasets into multi-turn fragmented-information episodes. They then train models to maintain a compact rolling memory that compresses prior turns, rather than attending to the full growing conversation history. The approach is validated by training solely on sharded GSM8K data.

Result: The memory-augmented policy significantly improves multi-turn accuracy compared to baselines. Remarkably, the model generalizes zero-shot to harder math problems and out-of-domain long-context QA tasks. Memory-trained models even outperform full-history baselines when given the full history at test time, indicating that learned compression fosters more robust incremental reasoning.

Conclusion: Learning to maintain a compact rolling memory, rather than relying on full context, effectively mitigates the Lost in Conversation problem. The proposed sharding pipeline makes this scalable, and the approach induces more robust reasoning capabilities that generalize beyond the training distribution.

Abstract: When a user reveals task-critical information across several conversation turns, LLM accuracy drops by up to 65% despite full context availability. We show that this Lost in Conversation degradation can be substantially mitigated by training models to maintain a compact rolling memory instead of attending to a growing history. To make such training scalable, we introduce a low-cost sharding pipeline that converts single-turn QA datasets into multi-turn fragmented-information episodes, eliminating the need for hours of manual annotation. Training only on sharded GSM8K, our memory-augmented policy significantly improves multi-turn accuracy and generalises zero-shot to harder math and out-of-domain long-context QA. Moreover, memory-trained models outperform full-history baselines even when given the full history at test time, suggesting that learning to compress induces more robust incremental reasoning than full-context exposure alone.

</details>


### [28] [SkillChain: Closing the Loop on Skill Evolution for Image-Based E-Commerce AI Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12984)
*Yimin Hu,Mengtao Xu,Hao Guo,Yuheng Song,Xiaoyong Zhu,Bo Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillChain is a production-deployed framework that automates the lifecycle of Skills for image-based AI assistants on e-commerce platforms, using three stages—Skill Creator, Route Optimizer, and Body Refiner—to handle heterogeneous user intents and significantly improve response quality, user engagement, and retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image-based AI assistants on e-commerce platforms face a critical challenge: a single uploaded image can map to diverse user intents (product search, style recommendation, visual encyclopedia, utility tools), each requiring distinct response formats, tool invocations, and domain knowledge. LLM-based systems without per-intent behavioral constraints conflate these modes and fail to meet domain quality standards. Manual engineering cannot scale given the breadth and dynamic nature of the intent space.

Method: SkillChain automates the Skill lifecycle through three stages: (1) Skill Creator bootstraps skills from task specifications and trajectories; (2) Route Optimizer aligns routing decisions to ensure queries are directed to the appropriate Skill; and (3) Body Refiner iteratively improves Skill Bodies using a dual-path LLM-Judge evaluation mechanism. Together, these close the production feedback loop on Skill evolution.

Result: Deployed on a production-scale e-commerce image assistant, SkillChain substantially improved aggregate response quality, with the strongest gains in structural compliance and content quality. A one-week online A/B experiment confirmed significant improvements in user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention.

Conclusion: SkillChain provides a scalable, automated alternative to manual engineering for managing heterogeneous user intents in production image-based AI assistants, demonstrating that closing the feedback loop on Skill evolution yields measurable gains in both offline quality metrics and online user behavior metrics.

Abstract: Image-based AI assistants are now deployed at production scale on e-commerce platforms, where a single uploaded image can trigger fundamentally different user intents: product search, style recommendation, visual encyclopedia, or utility tool calls, each demanding its own response format, tool invocation, and domain knowledge. Without per-intent behavioral constraints, LLM-based systems conflate these heterogeneous modes and fall short of domain quality standards, while the breadth and dynamism of the intent space render manual engineering infeasible. To address this, we present SkillChain, which closes the production feedback loop on Skill evolution, automating the lifecycle of Skills through three stages: Skill Creator for bootstrapping from task specs and trajectories, Route Optimizer for routing alignment, and Body Refiner for iterative Skill Body refinement via dual-path LLM-Judge evaluation. Deployed on a production-scale e-commerce image assistant, SkillChain substantially improves aggregate response quality, with the strongest gains on structural compliance and content quality; a one-week online A/B experiment further confirms significant gains in user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention.

</details>


### [29] [No Hidden Prompts Needed! You Can Game AI Peer Review with Presentation-Only Revisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13044)
*Xu Yang,Zhizhou Sha,Junbo Li,Jian Yu,Yifan Sun,Matthew Zhao,Jinrui Fang,Xinyue Guo,Yining Wu,Xu Hu,Yifu Luo,Qiang Liu,Zhangyang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Adversarial repackaging is a presentation-only attack on AI peer reviewers that achieves 75.1% success and +1.21/10 score gain by optimizing narrative framing, related-work positioning, and discussion without altering scientific evidence. It exposes structural failures where reviewers confuse appearance of rigor with actual rigor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robustness research on AI reviewers focuses on explicit attacks (hidden text, prompt injection), but a more policy-relevant threat exists: manipulating review outcomes solely through presentation-level changes (abstract, framing, discussion) while leaving all scientific evidence intact. This reflects a real deployment risk where the paper presentation itself becomes an optimization surface.

Method: The authors introduce "adversarial repackaging," a closed-loop attack pipeline that iteratively revises presentation-level content (abstract, contribution framing, related work, discussion, narrative structure) guided by AI-reviewer feedback, while strictly preserving all methods, experiments, figures, equations, proofs, and numerical results. Three mainstream AI reviewers are tested. Ablations compare strategies: related-work repositioning, analytical discussion expansion versus surface edits like local polishing, table formatting, and algorithm boxes.

Result: Adversarial repackaging achieves 75.1% attack success rate and a mean score gain of +1.21/10 across three AI reviewers. The effect is not attributable to ordinary prose polishing. Two structural failure modes emerge: (1) AI reviewers are easier to impress than to convince — highlighting strengths reliably boosts scores while attempting to dissolve weaknesses often backfires; (2) AI reviewers confuse the appearance of addressing a limitation with actually resolving it, reinterpreting unchanged evidence as stronger contribution.

Conclusion: Deployment risk for AI reviewers extends beyond hidden-instruction attacks: paper presentation itself is a viable optimization surface. The authors release a contamination-free rolling benchmark and attack framework for testing whether AI reviewers remain anchored to scientific content under presentation-only edits.

Abstract: As AI-generated reviews move from experimental tools into peer-review infrastructure, most robustness concerns have focused on explicit attacks such as hidden instructions and prompt injection. We study a harder and more policy-relevant failure mode: no hidden text, no prompt injection, and no changes to methods, experiments, figures, equations, proofs, or numerical results. The attacker modifies only presentation-level content, such as the abstract, contribution framing, related work, discussion, and narrative structure. We introduce adversarial repackaging: a closed-loop attack that uses AI-reviewer feedback to search for presentation-level revisions while keeping the scientific evidence fixed. Across three mainstream AI reviewers, adversarial repackaging achieves a 75.1% attack success rate and a mean score gain of +1.21/10. The effect is not explained by ordinary prose polishing. We also reveal that strategies that change how the reviewer interprets the paper, such as related-work repositioning and analytical discussion expansion, substantially outperform surface edits such as local polishing, table formatting, and algorithm boxes.
  Our analysis reveals two deeper structural failure modes. First, AI reviewers are easier to impress than to convince: highlighting strengths reliably increases perceived merit, while attempts to dissolve weaknesses frequently backfire. Second, AI reviewers can confuse the appearance of addressing a limitation with actually resolving it, allowing unchanged evidence to be reinterpreted as stronger scientific contribution. These results show that the deployment risk is not only malicious hidden instructions, but the emergence of paper presentation itself as an optimization surface. We release a contamination-free rolling benchmark and attack framework for testing whether AI reviewers remain anchored to scientific content under presentation-only edits.

</details>


### [30] [sebis at CRF Filling 2026: A Two-Stage Local LLM Pipeline for Medical CRF Filling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13082)
*Katharina Sommer,Tristan Till,Florian Matthes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A fully local, domain-adapted pipeline using MedGemma-27B with a two-stage architecture (binary presence classification + value extraction) achieves second place among open-source, locally-hosted submissions on the CL4Health 2026 CRF filling task (macro-F1: 0.55), demonstrating that privacy-preserving LLMs can approach proprietary model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extracting structured clinical data from unstructured EHR notes remains a bottleneck. LLMs perform well but face deployment barriers: privacy risks, high inference costs, and hallucination beyond textual evidence. The authors aim to address these challenges with a fully local, privacy-preserving pipeline.

Method: A two-stage architecture using the MedGemma-27B model: Stage 1 performs binary presence classification (present vs. negated/uncertain/unknown); Stage 2 performs value extraction only for positively classified items. The pipeline employs item-specific, few-shot in-context learning without external API calls or fine-tuning, enforcing strict textual grounding and deterministic handling of negated/uncertain states.

Result: The pipeline achieved a macro-F1 score of 0.55 on the official English test track of CL4Health 2026, securing second place among all locally-hosted, open-source submissions.

Conclusion: Privacy-preserving, on-premise LLM pipelines can achieve near-competitive performance with proprietary frontier models, offering a practical, data-sovereign framework for clinical NLP tasks such as CRF filling from EHR notes.

Abstract: The extraction of structured clinical information from unstructured EHR notes is a persistent bottleneck in healthcare informatics. While large language models (LLMs) offer high performance, their deployment in clinical settings is hindered by privacy risks, inference costs, and the tendency to hallucinate beyond textual evidence. We address these challenges for the CL4Health 2026 Case Report Form (CRF) filling task by proposing a fully local, domain-adapted pipeline using the MedGemma-27B model. Our two-stage architecture, which separates binary presence classification from value extraction, enforces strict adherence to textual evidence and ensures deterministic outputs for negated, uncertain, or unknown states. By leveraging item-specific, few-shot in-context learning without external API calls or fine-tuning, our approach achieves a macro-F1 score of 0.55 on the official English test track. This result secures second place among all locally-hosted, open-source submissions. Our work demonstrates that privacy-preserving, on-premise LLM pipelines can achieve near-competitive performance with proprietary frontier models, providing a practical, data-sovereign framework for clinical NLP.

</details>


### [31] [LEDGER: A Long-Context Benchmark of Corporate Annual Reports for Grounded Financial Retrieval and Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13100)
*Charles Moslonka,Amaury de Vitry,Arthur Garnier,Hicham Randrianarivo,Emmanuel Malherbe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LEDGER is a benchmark dataset of ~5,000 digitized corporate annual reports with 31 annotated financial KPIs, offering three evaluation tasks (page-level retrieval, needle-in-a-haystack lookup, full KPI extraction) for assessing long-context capabilities of language models in the finance domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing financial NLP benchmarks oversimplify by using plain-text SEC 10-K filings with limited QA pairs, failing to test models on real-world long-document understanding that includes figures, tables, and narrative. The authors aim to address the pressing need for rigorous long-context evaluation in finance.

Method: The authors construct LEDGER from 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports, annotating each with 31 consolidated financial KPIs linked to market reactions. They derive three benchmarks: (1) a page-level KPI retrieval task with 118,048 natural language questions using TREC-style relevance judgments, (2) a conversational needle-in-a-haystack single-value lookup, and (3) a full KPI extraction task from numerically dense long documents. Human OCR-quality annotations with inter-annotator agreement and a complete toolchain (extraction, validation, scoring) are provided.

Result: The paper releases the LEDGER corpus and demonstrates its research utility through a case study linking CEO-letter rhetoric to post-publication market impact.

Conclusion: LEDGER fills a critical gap in long-context financial NLP evaluation by providing a realistic, multi-modal, long-document benchmark that goes beyond simplified regulatory filings, enabling more rigorous assessment of LLMs' capabilities in financial information extraction and retrieval.

Abstract: Finance reporting is a natural proving ground for large language models, and the very-long-context capabilities of recent models across all sizes make rigorous evaluation in this domain an increasingly pressing need. Yet most public financial resources reduce the task to plain-text SEC 10-K filings paired with a handful of question-answer items. We release LEDGER (Long-context Evaluation of Documents for Grounded Extraction and Retrieval), a corpus of 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports - full documents with figures, tables, and narrative, not just regulatory filings. Each report is labeled with 31 consolidated financial KPIs to be extracted and linked to the market's reaction at the earnings date. From this data we derive three evaluation benchmarks spanning the difficulty spectrum: a pure page-level KPI retrieval task with TREC-style relevance judgments over 118,048 questions in natural language, a conversational "needle-in-a-haystack" single-value lookup, and a full KPI extraction task, both from long, numerically dense reports. We additionally provide human OCR-quality annotations with inter-annotator agreement and the complete extraction, validation, and scoring toolchain. We further demonstrate the dataset's research utility with a case study linking CEO-letter rhetoric to post-publication market impact.

</details>


### [32] [MÖVE: A Holistic LLM Benchmark for the German Public Sector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13111)
*Camilla Dalerci,Thilo Michael,Robin Schaefer,Daniel Weinland*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MÖVE is a holistic benchmark for evaluating LLMs in the German public sector, assessing 39 models across both performance (summarization, QA, topic extraction) and governance criteria (hallucination, energy use, transparency, constitutional alignment). No single model excels across all tasks, and model size poorly predicts quality. Results are publicly available as a living benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM adoption in public administration is increasing, but model selection remains ad hoc due to a lack of suitable benchmarks — existing ones are English/US-centric and focus solely on task performance, ignoring governance concerns critical to the public sector.

Method: The benchmark evaluates 39 LLMs on ten German-language datasets (including gold- and silver-standard datasets built for public-administration domains) using classical NLP metrics, embedding-based methods, and LLM-as-a-judge. It covers three performance tasks (summarization, question answering, topic extraction) and four governance criteria (hallucination tendencies, energy consumption, provider transparency, alignment with German constitutional values and political party knowledge). The benchmark itself is analyzed for statistical precision, judge reliability, dataset impact, prompt sensitivity, and energy estimate validity.

Result: No single model dominates across all criteria; top performers vary by task. Model size alone is a poor predictor of quality. The benchmark analysis demonstrates adequate statistical precision and judge reliability, while revealing the influence of private datasets on rankings and sensitivity to prompt formulation.

Conclusion: MÖVE fills a critical gap by providing a publicly available, living benchmark tailored to the German public sector that jointly evaluates performance and governance dimensions, enabling more informed and responsible LLM selection in public administration contexts.

Abstract: We present MÖVE (Modelle für die Öffentliche Verwaltung Evaluieren), a holistic benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in the context of the German public sector. While LLMs are increasingly adopted in public administration, model selection remains largely ad hoc, and existing benchmarks offer limited guidance: they are predominantly English-centric, US-centric in content, and focus exclusively on task performance. MÖVE addresses these gaps by evaluating 39 models across two complementary dimensions. Performance criteria cover summarization, question answering, and topic extraction. Governance criteria assess hallucination tendencies, energy consumption, provider transparency, and alignment with German constitutional values and knowledge about positions by German political parties. In total, we utilize ten German-language datasets, including gold- and silverstandard datasets that we constructed to reflect public-administration domains. We employ a multi-metric evaluation strategy combining classical NLP metrics, embedding-based methods, and LLM-as-a-judge approaches. Our results show that no single model dominates across all criteria: top performers differ between tasks, and model size alone is a poor predictor of quality. We further evaluate the benchmark itself, analyzing its statistical precision, LLM judge reliability, the impact of our private datasets on model rankings, the sensitivity of our results to prompt formulation, and the validity of our energy consumption estimates. MÖVE is designed as a living benchmark under active development; results are publicly available at https://moeve.bundesdruckerei.de/.

</details>


### [33] [G-Long: Graph-Enhanced Memory Management for Efficient Long-Term Dialogue Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13115)
*Minjun Choi,Yoonjin Jang,Sangwon Youn,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: G-Long uses a graph-based framework with a fine-tuned small LM for structured triplet extraction and an attention-aware scoring mechanism (via T5 cross-attention) to improve long-term consistency in dialogue while reducing computational costs, achieving SOTA on response generation and memory retrieval benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with long-term consistency in open-domain dialogue due to limited long-context reasoning and inefficient raw-text processing. Existing solutions either use unstructured memory (lossy) or expensive LLMs (high latency). A structured, efficient, and cost-effective approach is needed.

Method: G-Long framework: (1) fine-tuned small LM (sLM) for structured triplet extraction and associative retrieval from conversational history; (2) attention-aware importance scoring mechanism leveraging T5 summarizer's intrinsic cross-attention signals to identify salient memories; (3) graph-enhanced storage for structured memory representation.

Result: State-of-the-art performance: up to 9.8% improvement in response quality on MSC benchmark and 40.8% improvement in retrieval recall on LME benchmark, with significantly reduced computational overhead.

Conclusion: G-Long effectively addresses long-term dialogue consistency through graph-structured memory and attention-based salience scoring, delivering superior performance and efficiency over existing methods.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced open-domain dialogue systems, maintaining long-term consistency remains a challenge due to inherent limitations in long-context reasoning and the inefficiency of processing extensive raw text. Existing approaches typically rely on either unstructured memory storage, which is prone to information loss, or computationally expensive LLMs that incur high latency. To address these limitations, we propose G-Long, a graph-enhanced framework that utilizes a fine-tuned small Language Model (sLM) for structured triplet extraction and associative retrieval, significantly reducing operational costs. Furthermore, we introduce the novel attention-aware importance scoring mechanism that leverages the intrinsic cross-attention signals of a T5 summarizer to identify salient memories. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that G-Long achieves state-of-the-art performance in both response generation and memory retrieval, yielding performance gains of up to 9.8% in response quality on MSC and 40.8% in retrieval recall on LME, while significantly minimizing computational overhead.

</details>


### [34] [EvoBrowseComp: Benchmarking Search Agents on Evolving Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13120)
*Yunhan Wang,Jiaan Wang,Lianzhe Huang,Xianfeng Zeng,Fandong Meng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoBrowseComp introduces an evolving, contamination-free benchmark for evaluating search-augmented LLMs, synthesized via a three-agent framework that ensures temporal freshness and high difficulty through live-web traversal, supporting regular auto-updates in both English and Chinese.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search-agent benchmarks (e.g., BrowseComp) rely on static knowledge, making them susceptible to test-set contamination and parametric memorization. Models can exploit fact recall rather than genuine retrieval, obscuring true browsing competence. A future-proof, dynamically updated benchmark is needed to assess authentic search and reasoning abilities.

Method: A three-agent collaborative framework: (1) a QA synthesis agent retrieves fresh knowledge from the live web to create QA pairs; (2) an information filtering agent screens retrieved knowledge by credibility and popularity to block parametric shortcuts; (3) a high-level guidance agent structures questions into reasoning graphs to reduce logical redundancy and shortcuts. The fully automated pipeline produces EvoBrowseComp (400 English, 400 Chinese questions) and supports regular updates.

Result: Extensive experiments confirm EvoBrowseComp's high difficulty, requiring broad horizontal search across diverse sources. The benchmark resists contamination and memorization-based shortcuts, effectively distinguishing genuine retrieval capability from parametric recall.

Conclusion: EvoBrowseComp establishes a scalable paradigm for auto-updatable, high-difficulty benchmarking that keeps pace with evolving world knowledge and advancing agent capabilities, addressing the shortcomings of static evaluation suites.

Abstract: Search Agents -- large language models augmented with search tools -- have intensified the need for future-proof evaluation benchmarks. Existing benchmarks such as BrowseComp rely on static knowledge, making them vulnerable to test-set contamination and parametric memorization. Consequently, models can achieve high scores through fact recall rather than genuine retrieval, obscuring true browsing competence via reasoning shortcuts.
  In this paper, we introduce EvoBrowseComp, an evolving benchmark of 400 English and 400 Chinese contamination-free complex questions synthesized via live-web traversal. To collect these questions, we design a three-agent collaborative framework: (1) a QA synthesis agent that retrieves fresh knowledge from the live web to synthesize QA pairs; (2) an information filtering agent that filters retrieved knowledge in terms of credibility and popularity to block parametric shortcuts; and (3) a high-level guidance agent that formalizes questions into reasoning graphs to reduce logical redundancy and shortcuts in synthesized QA pairs. Because the framework supports fully automated synthesis, EvoBrowseComp can be regularly updated to prevent data contamination and maintain temporal freshness. Extensive experiments confirm its great difficulty, requiring broad horizontal search. It establishes a scalable paradigm for auto-updatable, high-difficulty benchmarking that keeps pace with both evolving world knowledge and advancing agent capabilities.

</details>


### [35] [NaturalFlow: Reducing Disruptive Pauses for Natural Speech Flow in Simultaneous Speech-to-Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13121)
*Dongwook Lee,Youngho Cho,Sangkwon Park,Heeseung Kim,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A fluency-aware optimization framework for simultaneous speech-to-speech translation that reduces unnatural inter-chunk silences by leveraging internal model signals, achieving natural speech flow without sacrificing latency or translation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simultaneous speech-to-speech translation minimizes latency for near-real-time communication, but aggressive low-latency chunking introduces frequent pauses that disrupt acoustic flow, creating an unnatural listening experience and increasing cognitive load. The goal is to find an optimal balance between low latency and natural speech fluency.

Method: The authors propose a fluency-aware optimization framework that minimizes inter-chunk silences. The method leverages two model-internal signals: (1) linguistic diversity of the generated output, and (2) induced temporal variability in speech durations. These signals guide the optimization to discover a sweet spot between simultaneous translation latency and consecutive translation fluency.

Result: Experiments on both short-form and long-form benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed framework produces natural speech flow while maintaining competitive latency and translation quality compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Optimizing for fluency alongside latency and quality in simultaneous speech-to-speech translation is feasible and effective. The framework successfully bridges the gap between low-latency and natural-sounding speech output.

Abstract: Simultaneous speech-to-speech translation aims to enable near-real-time communication by minimizing latency, offering a compelling, real-time alternative to the high latency of consecutive translation. However, the excessive pursuit of low latency often results in fragmented chunk-wise speech. Consequently, listeners are subjected to an unnatural acoustic flow punctuated by frequent pauses, which could increase their cognitive load. To bridge this gap, we introduce a fluency-aware optimization framework designed to discover the sweet spot between the low-latency benefits of simultaneous translation and the natural flow of consecutive translation. Our framework minimizes inter-chunk silences by leveraging model-internal signals, including linguistic diversity and induced temporal variability in speech durations. Experiments on short- and long-form benchmarks show that our framework produces natural speech flow while maintaining competitive latency and translation quality.

</details>


### [36] [HyPE: Category-Aware Hypergraph Encoding with Persistent Edge Embeddings for Persona-Grounded Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13142)
*Sangwon Youn,Yoonjin Jang,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HyPE is a hypergraph-based persona encoder that models high-order relations among persona attributes (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) using HyperGCN with Persistent Edge Embeddings, outperforming flat sentence pooling across multiple LLM backbones on PersonaChat.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing persona-grounded dialogue systems treat personas as flat sentence sets and fail to capture high-order relations among persona attributes, such as shared topical categories across multiple persona sentences.

Method: HyPE parses each persona-bearing text into (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruples and organizes them into a hypergraph where hyperedges represent shared category labels. An HyperGCN hypergraph neural network propagates structural information into a persona summary vector and soft-memory bank for conditioning the response generator. Persistent Edge Embeddings (PEE) are introduced as lightweight per-category learnable priors fused into the HyperGCN message-passing step.

Result: Under greedy decoding on PersonaChat, HyPE consistently outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines across GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, and Qwen2.5-3B backbones, demonstrating that structured hyperedge-level persona encoding provides a transferable advantage across model scales.

Conclusion: Structured hyperedge-level persona encoding via hypergraph neural networks offers a scalable, transferable improvement over flat persona representations for persona-consistent dialogue generation.

Abstract: Persona-grounded dialogue systems aim to produce responses consistent with a speaker's persona, yet existing methods treat personas as a flat set of sentences and fail to model the high-order relations among persona attributes-e.g., that several persona sentences share a topical category. We propose HyPE (Hypergraph Persona Encoder), a framework that (i) analyzes each persona-bearing text as a (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruple, and (ii) organizes persona elements into a hypergraph whose hyperedges are induced by shared category labels. An HyperGCN hypergraph neural network propagates this structure into a persona summary vector and a soft-memory bank that condition the response generator. We further propose Persistent Edge Embeddings (PEE), lightweight per-category learnable priors fused into the HyperGCN message-passing step. On PersonaChat under greedy decoding, HyPE consistently outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines across GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, and Qwen2.5-3B backbones by demonstrating that structured hyperedge-level persona encoding provides a transferable advantage across model scales.

</details>


### [37] [NTS-CoT: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLM-based News Timeline Summarization with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13171)
*Feng Lyu,Huiqin Yan,Sijing Duan,Hao Wu,Shuang Gu,Xue Qiao,Weixu Zhang,Haolun Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NTS-CoT proposes a Chain-of-Thought framework with three modules (Element-CoT, Date Selection, Causal-CoT) to mitigate hallucinations in LLM-based timeline summarization, addressing unfaithful content generation and information omission.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Timeline summarization (TLS) is crucial for tracking event developments in rapidly updating online news, but LLM-based approaches suffer from hallucinations — specifically unfaithful content during news summarization and information omission during date-event summarization — which are underexplored in existing work.

Method: The authors propose NTS-CoT, a three-module framework: (i) Element-CoT captures essential news elements (who, what, when, where, why, how) via Chain-of-Thought reasoning to ensure faithful summarization; (ii) Date Selection combines temporal saliency and event prominence to select appropriate timestamps; (iii) Causal-CoT infers causal relationships between events to reduce omissions in date-event summarization.

Result: Extensive experiments on three TLS benchmarks, along with human evaluation, show NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively mitigating both types of hallucinations and improving overall TLS performance.

Conclusion: NTS-CoT demonstrates that structured Chain-of-Thought reasoning can effectively reduce hallucinations in LLM-based timeline summarization by enforcing faithfulness to source material and capturing causal event relationships.

Abstract: The rapid updates of online news make tracking event developments challenging, highlighting the need for timeline summarization (TLS). Hallucinations, where LLM-generated content deviates from source news, still remain a critical issue in LLM-based TLS and are not well studied in existing works. To bridge this gap, we identify two primary types of hallucinations: unfaithful content during news summarization and information omission in date-event summarization. Then, we propose NTS-CoT, a novel framework that leverages Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to mitigate hallucinations in TLS. The framework consists of three key modules: i) Element-CoT to capture essential news elements for faithful summarization, ii) Date Selection to combine temporal saliency and event prominence for timestamp selection, and iii) Causal-CoT to infer causal relationships and reduce omissions in date-event summarization. Extensive experiments, including quantitative analysis on three TLS benchmarks and human evaluation, demonstrate that NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively mitigating hallucinations and improving LLM-based TLS performance. Our source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NTS-CoT .

</details>


### [38] [MemRefine: LLM-Guided Compression for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13177)
*Minjae Kim,Jinheon Baek,Soyeong Jeong,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MemRefine is an LLM-guided memory management framework that keeps a long-term agent's memory store within a fixed storage budget by using similarity-based candidate pairing and LLM-judged delete/merge/preserve decisions, outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budgets while maintaining downstream task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents operating over long-term interactions accumulate unbounded memory stores filled with redundant entries, inflating storage costs and degrading retrieval quality. This is especially problematic on resource-constrained platforms with hard memory budgets. Existing approaches lack principled methods to compress an already-constructed memory store while preserving information useful for future tasks.

Method: MemRefine uses surface similarity (e.g., embeddings) to propose candidate pairs from the memory store, then defers delete, merge, and preserve decisions to an LLM judge that evaluates pairs based on factual content rather than surface similarity alone. The process iterates until the target storage budget is met.

Result: Across multiple memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, MemRefine consistently meets target storage budgets while preserving downstream task performance. It outperforms rule-based baselines, particularly under tight budget constraints.

Conclusion: LLM-guided memory refinement based on factual content assessment is an effective strategy for storage-budgeted memory management, enabling resource-constrained deployment of long-term conversational agents without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly expected to operate over long-term interactions, where information from past dialogues must be preserved and recalled to support future tasks. However, as interactions accumulate, the memory store grows without bound and fills with redundant entries that inflate storage cost and degrade retrieval by crowding out the most useful evidence. Furthermore, this is especially limiting on resource-constrained platforms with hard memory budgets, motivating us to formulate storage-budgeted memory management, the task of keeping an already constructed memory store within a fixed budget while preserving information useful for future interactions. To this end, we then propose MemRefine, an LLM-guided framework that, since surface similarity poorly reflects factual value, uses similarity only to propose candidate pairs and defers delete, merge, and preserve decisions to an LLM judge based on factual content, iterating until the budget is met. Across multiple memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, MemRefine consistently meets target budgets while preserving downstream performance and outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budgets.

</details>


### [39] [LAUKIN: A Multi-jurisdictional Common Law Contract Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13184)
*Amrita Singh,Aditya Joshi,Jiaojiao Jiang,Hye-young Paik,May Fong Cheong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LAUKIN is a cross-jurisdictional legal equivalence dataset spanning Australia, UK, and India, comprising 14,727 clause pairs. It introduces a multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline for construction and includes 3,000 expert-annotated pairs. Benchmarking 12 models yields a best macro-F1 of 65.11%, highlighting the difficulty of cross-jurisdictional legal equivalence tasks despite shared common-law heritage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multinational companies need cross-jurisdictional contract review, but existing legal NLP datasets are limited to single jurisdictions. There is a gap in resources for studying legal equivalence across different common-law systems.

Method: A multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline is developed to construct initial clause pair mappings across Australia-UK, UK-India, and India-Australia jurisdiction pairs. Legal experts then manually annotate a subset of clause pairs as "Equivalent" or "Not Equivalent." The final dataset includes 14,727 clause pairs from 204 contracts spanning 8 agreement types, with 3,000 manually labelled (900 train, 600 dev, 1,500 test). Twelve models across four techniques are evaluated on the benchmark.

Result: The best-performing model achieves a macro-F1 of 65.11%, indicating that cross-jurisdictional legal equivalence classification is a challenging task. Results show that drafting conventions diverge significantly across Australia, UK, and India despite their shared legal heritage.

Conclusion: LAUKIN establishes a challenging new benchmark for cross-jurisdictional legal NLP. The significant performance gap demonstrates that shared legal heritage does not simplify equivalence classification. The dataset's 11,727 unlabelled training pairs further support future semi-supervised learning research.

Abstract: Multinational companies increasingly require cross-jurisdictional contract review, yet existing legal NLP datasets are largely restricted to a single jurisdiction. We introduce LAUKIN (Legal equivalence dataset of Australia, UK, and INdia), a dataset of clause pairs (AU-UK, UK-IN, IN-AU) labelled for boolean legal equivalence. We develop a novel multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline to construct the initial clause pair mapping, with a subset of clause pairs subsequently annotated by legal experts as Equivalent or Not Equivalent. The dataset comprises 14,727 clause pairs from 204 contracts across 8 agreement types, of which 3,000 are manually labelled: 900 train, 600 dev, and 1,500 test. We evaluate 12 models across 4 techniques, achieving a best macro-F1 of 65.11%, establishing LAUKIN as a challenging benchmark. Results reveal that, despite shared legal heritage, drafting conventions diverge significantly across jurisdictions, making cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification non-trivial. LAUKIN also includes 11,727 unlabelled training pairs to support future semi-supervised learning research in legal NLP.

</details>


### [40] [A Context-Aware Dataset for Stance Detection in Bioethical Controversies on Reddit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13187)
*Hu Huang,Genan Dai,Fuqiang Niu,Yi Yang,Zhaoya Gong,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BioStance is a new dataset of 39,600 annotated Reddit post-comment pairs for stance detection in bioethical discourse, covering six targets across three ethical dimensions with high inter-annotator reliability (α=0.82).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bioethical debates increasingly occur on social media, but stance detection research lacks large-scale, domain-specific resources that capture the context-dependent nature of such discourse.

Method: The authors constructed BioStance by collecting Reddit discussions, preserving hierarchical conversational context. The dataset covers six controversial bioethical targets across three dimensions: fundamental value conflicts, individual liberty vs. collective responsibility, and technological uncertainty. Each instance is a post-comment pair labeled by three independent annotators using a three-class stance scheme (Favor, Against, None).

Result: BioStance contains 39,600 annotated post-comment pairs. Annotations achieved a mean Krippendorff's α of 0.82, indicating substantial inter-annotator reliability.

Conclusion: By combining thematic diversity, conversational structure, and high-quality annotation, BioStance provides a valuable resource for advancing context-aware stance detection, argument mining, and computational analysis of bioethical discourse.

Abstract: Bioethical debates increasingly unfold on social media, yet stance detection research lacks large-scale, domain-specific resources for modeling such context-dependent discourse. We present BioStance, a context-aware dataset of 39,600 annotated Post-Comment pairs from Reddit bioethical discussions. BioStance covers six controversial targets across three dimensions of bioethical controversy: fundamental value conflicts, individual liberty versus collective responsibility, and technological uncertainty. Each instance preserves hierarchical conversational context and is labeled by three independent annotators using a three-class stance scheme: Favor, Against, and None. The annotations achieve a mean Krippendorff's $α$ of 0.82, indicating substantial reliability. By combining thematic diversity, conversational structure, and high-quality human annotation, BioStance supports research on context-aware stance detection, argument mining, and computational analysis of bioethical discourse.

</details>


### [41] [SICI: A Semantic-Pragmatic Complexity Index Reveals Regime Shifts in LLM Stance Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13189)
*Fuqiang Niu,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SICI is a 7D diagnostic metric quantifying semantic-pragmatic burden in stance detection. It predicts LLM accuracy better than surface features, reveals a phase-transition pattern in errors (over-attribution → boundary instability → None concentration), and shows that prompting/retrieval/debate interventions shift models along the attribution-abstention axis rather than resolving high-complexity failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current prompt-based LLMs struggle with harder stance detection examples, and existing remedies (clearer instructions, reasoning prompts, retrieval, debate) fail to reliably repair these errors. There is no systematic framework for diagnosing why or when failures occur, motivating a multidimensional complexity measure that captures the underlying semantic-pragmatic difficulty.

Method: The authors construct SICI, a seven-dimensional index measuring semantic-pragmatic burden in target-text pairs. They validate it on SemEval-2016 and VAST benchmarks, assess cross-scorer reliability (Krippendorff's α=0.771), compare its predictive power against surface-level proxies, analyze error regime shifts across SICI levels, test the phase-transition pattern across four LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, GPT-4o), and conduct a 15-method intervention study covering prompting, retrieval, and debate strategies.

Result: SICI predicts LLM accuracy substantially better than surface proxies. As SICI increases, errors shift through distinct regimes: low-complexity → over-attribution (excess Against predictions); intermediate → unstable classification boundary; high-complexity → mass concentration on None. This phase-transition-like structure holds across all four tested LLMs, with stronger models shifting boundaries but not eliminating the pattern. The 15-method intervention study reveals that prompting, retrieval, and debate primarily move models along the attribution-abstention axis, failing to resolve the high-complexity bottleneck.

Conclusion: The high-complexity bottleneck in stance detection is a fundamental limitation not remedied by current intervention techniques. SICI provides a reliable diagnostic tool for characterizing example difficulty and forecasting LLM behavior, and the phase-transition pattern suggests deeper architectural or representational constraints rather than superficial prompt-engineering problems.

Abstract: Prompt-based LLMs are increasingly used for stance detection, but harder examples are not always repaired by clearer instructions, reasoning prompts, retrieval, or debate. We introduce SICI (Stance Inference Complexity Index), a seven-dimensional diagnostic measure of the semantic-pragmatic burden imposed by a target--text pair. Across SemEval-2016 and VAST, SICI predicts LLM accuracy better than surface proxies and shows substantial cross-scorer reliability ($α=0.771$). More importantly, LLM errors change regime as SICI increases: low-complexity examples invite over-attribution, especially Against predictions; intermediate examples form an unstable boundary; and high-complexity examples rapidly concentrate on None. This phase-transition-like structure persists across GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, and GPT-4o, although stronger models move the boundaries. A 15-method intervention study further shows that prompting, retrieval, and debate often shift models along the attribution--abstention axis rather than removing the high-complexity bottleneck.

</details>


### [42] [Layer-Resolved Optimal Transport for Hallucination Detection in NMT and Abstractive Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13216)
*Mariia Onyshchuk,Maksym-Vasyl Tarnavskyi,Marta Sumyk*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper extends optimal transport (OT) analysis of cross-attention distributions from NMT hallucination detection to abstractive summarization faithfulness. It finds OT is a reliable detector for source-disengagement hallucinations but fundamentally limited when unfaithfulness occurs downstream of attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work showed OT on cross-attention detects NMT hallucinations without supervision. The authors aim to (1) provide a finer-grained layer-wise analysis of this signal across all decoder layers, and (2) test whether the same geometric approach transfers to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection, a more complex failure mode.

Method: The authors compute Wasserstein distances (Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data) between cross-attention distributions and reference distributions across all six decoder layers of Fairseq DE-EN (N=3,414). For summarization, they apply the same unsupervised OT detector to the AggreFact benchmark (N=1,116) on CNN/DM and XSum, comparing against supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L. Structural layer-ablation experiments on T5-base probe which layers are critical for generation quality.

Result: In NMT: Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary, specializing across hallucination types; detection is strongest in layers L1-L4, while L5 is anti-predictive for subtler hallucinations; correct translations show an exploratory attention phase absent in hallucinations from the first decoding step. In summarization: OT achieves 57.2%/57.6% balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum, well above chance but substantially below supervised baselines (69.9%/74.3%). The gap is principled—unfaithful summaries can attend correctly while misrepresenting content. T5-base shows consistent decoder organization: Layer 3 has peak concentration, Layer 12 is most critical for generation quality.

Conclusion: OT on cross-attention is a reliable, unsupervised hallucination detector specifically for source-disengagement failures, serves as a principled interpretability tool across tasks, but is fundamentally limited when faithfulness violations occur after the attention stage (e.g., misrepresenting correctly-attended content in summarization).

Abstract: Optimal transport (OT) has been shown to detect hallucinations in neural machine translation (NMT) by measuring the geometric distance between cross-attention distributions and a reference distribution, without any supervision. We extend this analysis to all six decoder layers of the Fairseq DE-EN model ($N=3{,}414$), showing that Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary detectors specialised across hallucination types, that detection is concentrated in layers L1--L4 with L5 anti-predictive for subtler types, and that hallucinated translations lack the exploratory attention phase present in correct translations from the first decoding step. We further evaluate whether the geometric signal transfers to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection: our unsupervised OT detector on AggreFact ($N=1{,}116$) achieves $57.2\%$/$57.6\%$ balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum -- above chance but substantially below supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L($69.9\%$/$74.3\%$). This gap is principled: unlike NMT hallucinations, unfaithful summaries can attend correctly to source tokens while misrepresenting their content, a failure mode invisible to concentration-based OT metrics by construction. Structural experiments on T5-base confirm consistent decoder organisation across depth, with Layer~3 showing peak concentration and Layer~12 being most critical for generation quality. Together, the results establish OT on cross-attention as a reliable detector when the failure mode is source disengagement, a principled interpretability tool regardless of task, and fundamentally limited when faithfulness failures occur downstream of attention.

</details>


### [43] [When Similar Means Different: Evaluating LLMs on Arabic--Hebrew Cognates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13218)
*Junhong Liang,Noor Abo Mokh,Bashar Alhafni*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SemCog Bench, a 1,858-pair Arabic-Hebrew benchmark, reveals that LLMs rely heavily on surface-form similarity, excelling on true cognates but failing on false friends and loanwords—contextual cues provide only marginal improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arabic and Hebrew share true cognates, false friends, and loanwords due to their Semitic kinship, creating a challenging testbed for cross-lingual semantic reasoning. Current LLMs lack rigorous evaluation on form-meaning conflict resolution between closely related languages.

Method: The authors construct SemCog Bench: 1,858 Arabic-Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation. They evaluate open-source and commercial LLMs across four input representations (raw, diacritized, Romanized, phonetic) and analyze performance patterns across word-pair categories.

Result: LLMs achieve high accuracy on true cognates but suffer sharp performance drops on false friends and loanwords. Sentence-level context provides only modest gains, indicating models over-rely on surface-form similarity and cannot effectively leverage contextual cues to override misleading orthographic signals.

Conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit a fundamental limitation in resolving cross-lingual form-meaning conflicts. SemCog Bench serves as a rigorous diagnostic benchmark for multilingual semantic reasoning, highlighting the need for architectures that go beyond surface-form pattern matching.

Abstract: Arabic and Hebrew, as closely related Semitic languages, share a substantial lexicon of true cognates, misleading false friends, and modern loanwords. This overlap poses a challenge for cross-lingual semantic understanding in large language models (LLMs). To evaluate this capability, we introduce SemCog Bench, a curated benchmark of 1,858 Arabic--Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation. We evaluate open-source and commercial LLMs across multiple input representations (raw, diacritized, Romanized, and phonetic) and reveal a critical gap in cross-lingual reasoning. While models achieve high accuracy on true cognates, performance drops sharply on false friends and loanwords, reflecting a strong reliance on surface-form similarity. Furthermore, sentence-level context yields only modest improvements, suggesting that contextual cues alone are insufficient to overcome misleading form-based signals. These findings reveal a fundamental limitation of current LLMs in resolving cross-lingual form--meaning conflicts and establish SemCog Bench as a rigorous benchmark for multilingual semantic reasoning. Our code and data are publicly available.

</details>


### [44] [PolyAlign: Conditional Human-Distribution Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13227)
*L. D. M. S. Sai Teja,Ufaq Khan,Sathira Silva,Xiao Wu,Muhammad Haris Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PolyAlign is a distribution-aware alignment framework that moves beyond global alignment to match human response distributions conditioned on interaction context (language, task, response style, length), using Bucket-Aware SFT and Human-Distribution Preference Optimization to improve conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness in bilingual settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard post-training methods (SFT, preference optimization) align models toward a single global assistant behavior, which suppresses the natural variation of human responses across different languages, tasks, and dialogue settings. The authors frame this as a conditional human-distribution alignment problem: models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the specific interaction context rather than converging to a uniform response style.

Method: PolyAlign organizes bilingual interaction data into buckets defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length, creating bucket-specific human reference distributions. It employs two components: (1) Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets to prevent dominant distributions from overshadowing minority ones; and (2) Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning by incorporating a critic-estimated distance to the bucket-specific human support, steering the model toward context-appropriate responses.

Result: Evaluated across a bilingual suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while maintaining competitive task utility compared to global alignment baselines.

Conclusion: Post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment with human response distributions. Conditioning on interaction context yields models that better reflect the diversity of human communication without sacrificing task performance.

Abstract: Post-training methods such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference optimization typically align language models toward a single global assistant behavior. While effective for improving average helpfulness, this can suppress the natural variation of human responses across languages, tasks, and dialogue settings. We study this problem as conditional human-distribution alignment: models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the current interaction context, rather than a universal response style. We introduce PolyAlign, a distribution-aware alignment framework that organizes bilingual interaction data into bucket-specific human reference distributions defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length. PolyAlign combines Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets, with Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning using critic-estimated distance to bucket-specific human support. Across a bilingual evaluation suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while preserving competitive task utility. The results suggest that post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment with human response distributions.

</details>


### [45] [Evaluating Pluralism in LLMs through Latent Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13254)
*Laura Majer,Jan Šnajder,Martin Tutek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a domain-agnostic, multi-layered unsupervised framework for extracting perspectives from text, applies it to book reviews, and finds that while LLMs approach broad perspective coverage, rarer perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented compared to human-written text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work demonstrates LLMs homogenize training data diversity primarily via multiple-choice questionnaires or high-level text features. A systematic method for extracting and quantifying perspectives from free-form text is needed to operationalize pluralistic alignment and clearly articulate the "pluralistic gap" in LLM generation.

Method: The authors design a domain-agnostic, multi-layered unsupervised framework for perspective extraction. They evaluate it on book reviews — a highly opinionated, diverse-perspective dataset — and compare multiple LLMs and prompting strategies against human-written text distributions.

Result: Some models and prompting techniques nearly achieve broad perspective coverage, but rarer perspectives remain consistently and disproportionately underrepresented. The resulting perspective distributions diverge significantly from those observed in human-authored text.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively surfaces the pluralistic gap: LLMs struggle to faithfully reproduce the long tail of perspective diversity, even when prompting strategies improve overall coverage. This provides actionable guidance for pluralistic alignment efforts.

Abstract: The growing need to represent diverse perspectives has increased interest in pluralistic LLM generation. Although difficult to operationalize, identifying perspectives expressed in text would provide clear guidance on pluralistic alignment and more clearly articulate the pluralistic gap in LLM generation. While models have been shown to reduce the diversity of training data and generate homogeneously, this has been demonstrated primarily on multiple-choice questionnaires or using high-level characteristics of free-form text. In this paper, we introduce and implement a domain-agnostic multi-layered framework for unsupervised extraction of perspectives suitable for identifying the pluralistic gap in LLM-generated text. We evaluate our framework on book reviews, a highly opinionated dataset representing diverse perspectives, and compare various prompts and models. Our results show that while some models and prompting techniques come close to covering a broad spectrum of perspectives, rarer perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented, resulting in distributions that diverge from human text.

</details>


### [46] [SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13317)
*Kunfeng Chen,Qihuang Zhong,Juhua Liu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillCAT is a training-free framework for LLM agent skill self-evolution that introduces three novel stages: Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE) uses success/failure trajectory pairs to identify outcome-relevant evidence; Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE) validates candidate patches before merging; and Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE) builds a routable sub-skill topology for efficient inference. It achieves up to 40.40% improvement over baselines on SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill self-evolution pipelines for LLM agents suffer from three key limitations: (1) they learn from only one trajectory per task, missing richer causal signals; (2) they merge candidate skill patches before checking them, risking degradation; and (3) they load the entire skill corpus at inference, which is inefficient. SkillCAT addresses all three gaps in a training-free manner.

Method: SkillCAT is a three-stage, training-free framework. Stage 1 — Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE): samples multiple trajectories per task and contrasts same-task success vs. failure pairs to extract evidence explaining outcome differences. Stage 2 — Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE): replays each candidate skill patch on source-task clones, retaining only patches that improve or preserve outcomes, then performs hierarchical merging. Stage 3 — Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE): compiles evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology so that inference loads only task-relevant capability nodes.

Result: SkillCAT was evaluated on SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, with additional tests on cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization. It raises the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%, demonstrating reliable skill evolution without any model training.

Conclusion: SkillCAT demonstrates that separating skill evolution into contrastive extraction, assessment-gated merging, and topology-aware execution yields substantial performance gains without requiring model training. Its strong cross-model and OOD generalization further underscore the robustness of the approach.

Abstract: Skill self-evolution methods for LLM agents aim to turn execution trajectories into reusable skill documents, but current pipelines typically learn from one trajectory per task, merge candidate skill patches before checking them, and load the full skill corpus before inference. We propose SkillCAT, a training-free framework that separates this process into three stages. Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE) samples multiple trajectories for each task and compares same-task success/failure pairs to identify evidence that explains outcome differences. Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE) replays each candidate patch on source-task clones and keeps only patches that improve or preserve task outcomes before hierarchical skill patch merging. Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE) compiles the evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology, so inference loads only the capability nodes relevant to the task. We evaluate SkillCAT on common agent benchmarks, including SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, and further test cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization. Across these settings, SkillCAT raises the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%, demonstrating reliable skill evolution without model training.

</details>


### [47] [Low-Latency Real-Time Audio Game Commentary System via LLM-Based Parallel Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13322)
*Ryota Kawamatsu,Anum Afzal,Yuki Saito,Shinnosuke Takamichi,Graham Neubig,Katsuhito Sudoh,Hiroya Takamura,Tatsuya Ishigaki*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A real-time audio game commentary system that parallelizes text generation with speech playback and buffers candidate utterances, reducing inter-utterance silence from 9.6s to 0.3s and improving perceived speaking rhythm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional end-to-end commentary pipelines process utterances strictly sequentially — capture, generate text, synthesize speech, play back, then repeat — causing accumulated waiting time and unnaturally long silences between utterances. This latency bottleneck degrades the naturalness of real-time commentary.

Method: The proposed system runs text generation in parallel with speech playback and pre-buffers multiple candidate utterances during ongoing playback. This allows immediate speech synthesis at playback boundaries rather than waiting for generation to complete after each utterance finishes.

Result: The parallel design reduces mean inter-utterance silence from 9.6 seconds to 0.3 seconds compared to sequential baselines on fast-paced game videos. Silence timing pattern similarity to professional commentary improves by over 40%, and a user study with 120 experienced game players confirms significantly improved perceived speaking rhythm.

Conclusion: Parallelizing generation with playback and buffering candidate utterances effectively eliminates the latency bottleneck in real-time commentary systems, yielding near-professional silence patterns and rhythm perception.

Abstract: We present a low-latency real-time audio game commentary system that generates spoken commentary directly from live gameplay video. In this end-to-end setting, a key bottleneck is accumulated waiting time; conventional pipelines capture frames, generate text, and synthesize speech sequentially for each utterance, and do not request the next generation until speech playback has completed. This strict sequentiality causes long and unnatural silence between utterances. To address this latency bottleneck, our system runs text generation in parallel with speech playback and buffers multiple candidate utterances ahead of time, enabling immediate synthesis at playback boundaries. Experiments on fast-paced game videos show that our parallel design reduces the mean inter-utterance silence from 9.6 seconds to 0.3 seconds compared to sequential baselines. It also improves similarity to professional speaking--silence timing patterns by over 40 %, and a user study with 120 experienced game players confirms significantly improved perceived speaking rhythm. Our demo video is available at: https://youtu.be/pmrRUlvav8M.

</details>


### [48] [IVIE: A Neuro-symbolic Approach to Incremental and Validated Generation of Interactive Fiction Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13348)
*Micaela Vaucher,Santiago Silveira,Santiago Góngora,Luis Chiruzzo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IVIE is a neuro-symbolic system that generates complete interactive fiction worlds by combining LLM-driven creative generation with symbolic validation, achieving immersive and coherent gameplay while identifying remaining challenges in puzzle consistency and goal validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computational creativity in Interactive Fiction faces a trade-off: LLMs produce creative narratives but lack world coherence, while symbolic systems ensure consistency but are creatively rigid. The authors aim to reconcile these opposing strengths through a neuro-symbolic architecture.

Method: IVIE extends the PAYADOR neuro-symbolic framework with a four-stage incremental generation pipeline. Creative tasks (setting, character creation, puzzle design) are delegated to LLMs, while world-state integrity is enforced through symbolic validation. The output includes interconnected locations, functional items, NPCs, and coherent puzzles centered on a goal-oriented architecture.

Result: Human evaluation shows IVIE produces immersive, thematically coherent worlds with high player engagement, demonstrating that symbolic validation can ground LLM generation without stifling creative freedom. However, two failure modes persist: LLM inconsistencies can bypass puzzle constraints, and gaps in objective validation allow structurally impossible goals.

Conclusion: The neuro-symbolic approach successfully balances narrative flexibility with world coherence, but challenges remain. The authors distill key design considerations for future neuro-symbolic interactive storytelling systems, particularly around leveraging LLM capabilities while mitigating their limitations.
