Abstract: Computational creativity in Interactive Fiction faces a fundamental tension: Large Language Models (LLM) may produce creative narratives but struggle with world coherence, while symbolic systems ensure consistency but lack creative flexibility. We present IVIE (Incremental & Validated Interactive Experiences), a neuro-symbolic approach to generating complete and playable interactive fiction worlds from scratch. Building upon PAYADOR's neuro-symbolic framework, IVIE implements a four-stage incremental generation pipeline that delegates creative decisions--setting and character creation, puzzle design--to LLMs while grounding the world state through symbolic validation. The system generates worlds with interconnected locations, functional items, non-player characters, and coherent puzzles, all structured around a central goal-oriented architecture. Human evaluation shows the approach generates immersive, thematically coherent worlds with high player engagement. Results seem to indicate that the neuro-symbolic approach successfully balances flexibility with narrative coherence: symbolic validation grounds LLM generation without eliminating generative freedom. However, challenges remain: LLM inconsistencies occasionally bypass puzzle constraints, and objective validation gaps allow some structurally impossible goals. We identify key design considerations for future neurosymbolic interactive storytelling systems, particularly regarding LLM capabilities and their limitations.

</details>


### [49] [From Passive Generation to Investigation: A Proactive Scientific Peer Review Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13349)
*Haishuo Fang,Yue Feng,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProReviewer frames scientific peer review as a Markov Decision Process, using a structured review log as a workspace for an LLM agent to accumulate evidence and proactively investigate suspicious parts of a paper, achieving state-of-the-art review quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based peer review systems lack the human-like ability to flexibly and proactively investigate suspicious paper sections based on accumulated evidence, resulting in shallow reviews without concrete support.

Method: ProReviewer formulates proactive investigation as an MDP and maintains a structured review log as an agent workspace. An 8B-parameter LLM backbone is trained via supervised fine-tuning and then optimized with reinforcement learning to iteratively gather evidence and generate in-depth reviews.

Result: ProReviewer achieves the highest average score across five quality dimensions, outperforming prompt-based frontier LLMs by up to 39% relatively and the strongest fine-tuned baseline by 16% relatively, while also attaining the highest win rates in human evaluation.

Conclusion: Formulating peer review as an MDP with a structured review log enables proactive, evidence-driven investigation, significantly improving automated review quality even with smaller model backbones.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating scientific peer review. However, existing approaches often struggle to generate in-depth reviews supported by concrete evidence. We argue that a key limitation is the lack of flexibility to proactively investigate suspicious parts of a paper based on accumulated evidence, as human reviewers do. In this paper, we explore how to enable an LLM-based review agent to perform such proactive investigation. We find that this can be naturally formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and propose ProReviewer, a scientific peer review agent that proactively reviews a paper guided by a maintained, structured review log. The structured review log serves as a workspace for the agent to track evidence and intermediate findings collected during review. Experiments show that ProReviewer with an 8B backbone, trained by supervised fine-tuning and optimized by reinforcement learning, achieves the highest average score across five quality dimensions, outperforming prompt-based methods with much larger frontier LLMs by up to 39% and the strongest fine-tuned baseline by 16% relatively. It also attains the highest win rates against baselines in human evaluation.

</details>


### [50] [An End-to-End Hybrid Framework for Rumour Detection in Low-Resources Algerian Dialect](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13411)
*Dihia Lanasri,Fatima Benbarek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A hybrid rumour detection framework for Algerian dialect social media, combining transformer embeddings with classical classifiers, achieves an F1-score of 0.84. The work includes a purpose-built annotated dataset, a transliteration pipeline for Arabic script and Arabizi, and demonstrates that domain-specific pre-training outweighs model size in low-resource dialect settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rumour spread on social media is exacerbated in the Algerian context due to three factors: (1) the informal, code-switched nature of dialectal content, (2) scarcity of annotated resources for Algerian dialect, and (3) the ineffectiveness of standard Arabic NLP tools on dialect text. These challenges necessitate a dedicated framework tailored to Algerian dialectal social media.

Method: The authors propose an end-to-end hybrid framework with four components: (1) a domain-specific annotated dataset built from real social media posts, synthetic data, and the FASSILA corpus, using a similarity-based automatic labelling process; (2) a transliteration pipeline generating parallel datasets in Arabic script and Arabizi; (3) evaluation of multiple paradigms including classical ML, deep learning, transformers, and hybrid models; (4) a hybrid approach that couples transformer embeddings with a classical classifier for final prediction.

Result: The hybrid model (transformer embeddings + classical classifier) achieves the best F1-score of 0.84. A key finding is that domain-specific pre-training (social media data) proves more impactful than model scale — social media-trained models consistently outperform larger models trained on formal Arabic corpora.

Conclusion: Rumour detection in low-resource Algerian dialect settings is feasible. The hybrid framework, supported by tailored dataset construction and transliteration, offers a viable solution. Domain-adapted pre-training should be prioritised over simply using larger, generic Arabic models.

Abstract: The rapid growth of social media has intensified the spread of rumours. This issue is more challenging in the Algerian context due to the informal and code-switched nature of dialectal content, the scarcity of annotated resources, and the limited effectiveness of standard Arabic NLP tools on dialect text.
  This paper presents an end-to-end rumour detection hybrid framework for Algerian dialect social media content. We build a domain-specific annotated dataset by combining real social media posts, synthetic data, and the FASSILA corpus, with automatic labeling based on a similarity-based annotation process. A transliteration pipeline is also introduced to generate parallel datasets in Arabic script and Arabizi.
  We evaluate multiple approaches, including classical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and hybrid models. Experimental results show that a hybrid approach combining transformer embeddings with a classical classifier achieves the best performance, reaching an F1-score of 0.84. We also find that domain-specific pre-training is more important than model size, with social media-trained models outperforming larger models trained on formal Arabic corpora.
  These results demonstrate the feasibility of rumour detection in low-resource Algerian dialect settings.

</details>


### [51] [S-GBT: Smooth Growth Bound Tensor for Certified Robustness Against Word Substitution Attacks in NLP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13439)
*Mohammed Bouri,Mohammed Erradi,Adnane Saoud*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces S-GBT, a second-order defense method that bounds the Hessian element-wise to control both gradient magnitude and curvature, achieving up to 23.4% improvement in certified robust accuracy against word substitution attacks over prior first-order defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NLP defenses against word substitution attacks focus only on first-order sensitivity (gradient magnitude), ignoring curvature — how sensitivity itself changes. When gradients vary sharply (high curvature), models can still be fooled despite small first-order sensitivity. This gap motivates a second-order approach.

Method: The authors propose the Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), which bounds the Hessian element-wise. Output change under word substitution is bounded by both a linear and a quadratic term. A regularization term minimizing these bounds is integrated directly into the training objective. The method is derived for both LSTM and CNN architectures.

Result: Combining first- and second-order regularization improves certified robust accuracy by up to 23.4% over prior methods, while clean accuracy remains competitive. Evaluated across multiple benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: Controlling both the gradient and its variation (curvature) is a promising direction for building more robust NLP models. Second-order regularization yields tighter certified robustness bounds.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in Natural Language Processing (NLP), models remain vulnerable to word substitution attacks. Most existing defenses focus on first order sensitivity and measure how much the output changes when the input is slightly perturbed. However, they ignore how this sensitivity evolves, which is described by curvature. When gradients vary sharply, models can still fail. This paper introduces the Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second order method that bounds the Hessian element-wise, for which we provide formal theoretical proofs on the resulting robustness bounds. A regularization term is added during training to minimize these bounds. This yields tighter certified robustness against word substitution attacks. The change in the output under word substitution is bounded by both a linear term and a quadratic term. S-GBT is derived for two architectures: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). The method is integrated directly into the training objective. Its effectiveness is evaluated on multiple benchmark datasets. The results show that combining first and second order regularization improves certified robust accuracy by up to 23.4% compared to prior methods, while clean accuracy remains competitive. These findings indicate that controlling both the gradient and its variation is a promising direction for building more robust models.

</details>


### [52] [Ontology Memory-Augmented ASR Correction for Long Text-Speech Interleaved Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13464)
*Xinxin Li,Huiyao Chen,Meishan Zhang,Yunxin Li,Zulong Chen,Zhibo Ren,Xiaoqing Dong Baotian Hu,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes an ontology memory framework for ASR correction in long, text-speech interleaved conversations, where interaction history is structured into retrievable nodes (entities, terminology, variants, confusions, relations). Introduces RAMC-Corr dataset and demonstrates improvements in 9/10 backbone-setting combinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ASR correction focuses on isolated utterances, but long text-speech interleaved conversations demand conversation-level context. Existing methods concatenate raw dialogue history, making it difficult to locate sparse correction evidence amid redundancy and noise.

Method: An ontology memory-augmented ASR correction framework that dynamically organizes preceding interaction history into an updatable ontology memory. Nodes store entities, terminology, surface variants, potential ASR confusions, and semantic relations as retrievable evidence for context-grounded correction. The RAMC-Corr dataset is constructed from MAGIC-RAMC for evaluation.

Result: The proposed method improves over direct correction in 9 out of 10 paired backbone-setting combinations on RAMC-Corr, yielding more selective and evidence-grounded corrections for context-dependent ASR errors.

Conclusion: Structuring dialogue history as an ontology memory provides an effective mechanism for context-grounded ASR correction in long-form interleaved conversations, outperforming direct correction approaches and enabling more selective, evidence-based error recovery.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) correction has traditionally focused on isolated utterances or short local contexts. However, as text and speech become increasingly interleaved in long interactions, ASR correction requires conversation-level contextual evidence. Existing ASR correction methods often rely on the current hypothesis or concatenate raw dialogue history. In such contexts, sparse correction evidence can be difficult to locate amid redundancy and noise. Addressing these challenges, we propose an ontology memory-augmented ASR correction framework for long text-speech interleaved conversations. The framework organizes preceding interaction history into a dynamically updatable ontology memory, where entities, terminology, surface variants, potential ASR confusions, and semantic relations are stored as retrievable nodes for context-grounded correction. To evaluate this setting, we construct RAMC-Corr, a dataset derived from MAGIC-RAMC for long-range ASR correction with grounded context. Experiments on RAMC-Corr show that our method improves over direct correction in 9 out of 10 paired backbone-setting combinations and encourages more selective and evidence-grounded corrections for context-dependent ASR errors.

</details>


### [53] [Leveraging Audio-LLMs to Filter Speech-to-Speech Training Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13507)
*Qixu Chen,Satoshi Nakamura*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes a two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy using an audio-language model for filtering noisy speech-to-speech translation data, achieving up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale mined speech corpora for S2ST contain noise, misalignment, and semantic errors that degrade translation performance. Manual labeling is infeasible at scale, necessitating an automated, scalable filtering method that can assess both acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency directly from raw audio.

Method: A two-stage Rank-to-Distill framework: (1) a lightweight ranker generates keep/drop pseudo-labels from noisy speech pairs, providing scalable supervision; (2) these pseudo-labels then train an audio large language model to directly predict keep/drop decisions from raw paired speech, jointly capturing acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency.

Result: Experiments on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over unfiltered training, yielding up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU for end-to-end S2ST systems.

Conclusion: The Rank-to-Distill approach provides an effective, scalable solution for noise filtering in S2ST training data without manual annotation, and the resulting audio-language model successfully captures both acoustic and semantic quality signals.

Abstract: Large-scale mined corpora provide abundant training data for end-to-end speech-to-speech translation (S2ST) but may contain noise, misalignment, and semantic errors. Filtering noisy data is crucial to maintain robust speech translation performance. We study how to train an audio-language model to make keep/drop decisions on paired speech directly from audio. To obtain reliable supervision without manual labels, we adopt a scalable two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy. A lightweight ranker generates keep/drop pseudo-labels from noisy speech pairs, then trains an audio large language model to predict keep/drop directly from raw paired speech. The resulting model jointly captures acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency for the selection of speech-conditioned data. Experiments on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix show consistent improvements over unfiltered training, yielding up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU for end-to-end S2ST.

</details>


### [54] [When Does Mixing Help? Analyzing Query Embedding Interpolation in Multilingual Dense Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13537)
*Tongyao Zhu,Chao-Ming Huang,Min-Yen Kan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A controlled study on mMARCO reveals that mixed-language query embeddings (via interpolation) can outperform monolingual queries in dense retrieval, but gains are asymmetric: mixing helps non-English indices, while English indices perform best with pure English queries. English dominates as a mixing partner, and gains correlate negatively with typological distance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixed-language querying is widespread in multilingual communities, yet dense retrievers' sensitivity to such queries is poorly understood. The authors aim to systematically characterize when and why embedding-level query mixing helps or hurts retrieval performance.

Method: Ratio-controlled experiments on mMARCO using BGE-M3 embeddings. Mixed queries are constructed via embedding-level interpolation of parallel monolingual query translations at varying mixing ratios. Retrieval performance is evaluated across language pairs and document indices, with additional validation across model families and scales.

Result: An optimal mixing ratio outperforms the best monolingual endpoint in 88/105 cases. Mixing uniformly benefits retrieval from non-English indices, while English-containing indices are best served by pure English queries. English is the strongest mixing partner for every non-English document language. Gains from mixing correlate negatively with typological distance when controlling for English dominance. Patterns are robust across models.

Conclusion: Language-mix sensitivity in dense retrieval is structured and predictable. The asymmetry driven by English dominance and the negative correlation with typological distance provide a framework for optimizing mixed-language query strategies in multilingual IR systems.

Abstract: While mixed-language querying is ubiquitous in multilingual communities, the sensitivity of dense retrievers to such queries remains poorly understood. We present a ratio-controlled study on mMARCO that systematically evaluates retrieval performance by varying the mixing proportion of parallel query translations via embedding-level mixing -- constructing mixed queries as an interpolation of monolingual embeddings. Experiments with BGE-M3 demonstrate that an optimal mixing ratio outperforms the best monolingual endpoint in 88/105 cases. We uncover a distinct asymmetry driven by English dominance: mixing is uniformly beneficial when retrieving from non-English document indices, whereas indices containing English are best served by pure English queries. Furthermore, English acts as the strongest mixing partner for every non-English document language. Finally, when controlling for English dominance, mixing gains correlate negatively with typological distance. We conclude that language-mix sensitivity is structured and predictable, and we validate the robustness of these patterns across model families and scales.

</details>


### [55] [ArogyaSutra: A Multi-Agent Framework for Multimodal Medical Reasoning in Indic Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13572)
*Tanmoy Kanti Halder,Akash Ghosh,Subhadip Baidya,Arijit Roy,Sriparna Saha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ArogyaBodha is a large-scale multilingual multimodal medical QA dataset covering 8 languages and diverse clinical domains. ArogyaSutra, an actor-critic multi-agent framework with dual-memory and tool grounding, improves medical reasoning in low-resource Indic language settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs perform poorly in specialized, multilingual, low-resource healthcare settings — particularly in rural India, where patients use native Indic languages and multimodal inputs. This limits equitable AI-driven healthcare access.

Method: ArogyaBodha dataset: constructed from 8 heterogeneous sources, spanning 31 body systems, 6 imaging modalities, 21 clinical domains, in English + 7 Indic languages. ArogyaSutra framework: actor-critic-based multi-agent system with tool grounding, dual-memory mechanisms for step-wise reasoning, and distillation from stored actor-critic simulation trajectories.

Result: The dataset and framework improve multilingual medical reasoning accuracy across all Indic languages. Ablation studies validate each component's contribution.

Conclusion: ArogyaBodha and ArogyaSutra together address the critical gap in multilingual multimodal medical reasoning, advancing equitable AI-driven healthcare for low-resource Indic language communities.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promising reasoning capabilities in general domains, yet their performance remains limited in specialized settings such as healthcare, especially in multilingual and low-resource scenarios. This gap is critical in regions like rural India, where patients often express complex medical queries in native Indic languages and rely on multimodal inputs such as medical images. Existing English-centric MLLMs struggle to support such use cases, limiting equitable access to AI-driven healthcare assistance. To address this challenge, we introduce ArogyaBodha, a large-scale multilingual multimodal medical question-answer dataset constructed from eight heterogeneous sources, covering 31 body systems, six imaging modalities, and 21 clinical domains across English and seven major Indian languages. We further propose ArogyaSutra, an actor-critic-based multi-agent framework that integrates tool grounding with dual-memory mechanisms for step-wise, reasoning-aware decision making, and uses stored actor-critic simulation trajectories for distillation. Experiments show that our dataset and framework improve multilingual medical reasoning accuracy across all Indic languages, with ablations validating the contribution of each component. The source code and dataset are available at: https://iitp-cse.github.io/ ArogyaSutra/

</details>


### [56] [LabVLA: Grounding Vision-Language-Action Models in Scientific Laboratories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13578)
*Baochang Ren,Xinjie Liu,Xi Chen,Yanshuo Liu,Chenxi Li,Daqi Gao,Zeqin Su,Jintao Xing,Zirui Xue,Rui Li,Xiangyu Zhao,Shuofei Qiao,Minting Pan,Wangmeng Zuo,Lei Bai,Dongzhan Zhou,Ningyu Zhang,Huajun Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses the gap between AI reasoning and physical experiment execution in scientific labs by introducing RoboGenesis (a simulation-based data engine for lab workflows) and LabVLA (a vision-language-action model trained via FAST pretraining and flow matching). LabVLA achieves state-of-the-art success rates on the LabUtopia benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems can reason about experiments (read literature, generate hypotheses, plan protocols) but cannot physically execute them—human operators are still needed at the bench. Existing VLA models are trained on household/tabletop tasks and cannot handle lab-specific instruments, transparent liquids, or fixed protocol workflows. Two central bottlenecks are identified: lack of lab-specific training data and the challenge of supporting diverse robot embodiments.

Method: (1) RoboGenesis: a simulation-based data engine that composes configured lab workflows from atomic skills, validates and filters rollouts, and exports structured demonstrations across multiple robot profiles. (2) LabVLA: a two-stage training approach—first, FAST action token pretraining on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct to make the backbone action-aware; second, flow matching posttraining to attach a DiT (Diffusion Transformer) action expert under knowledge insulation to prevent catastrophic forgetting of the pretrained representations.

Result: LabVLA achieves the highest average success rate among all evaluated baselines on the LabUtopia benchmark, under both in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) settings.

Conclusion: Addressing data and embodiment bottlenecks via RoboGenesis and LabVLA enables effective grounding of VLA models in laboratory settings. The two-stage training (FAST + flow matching with knowledge insulation) proves effective, and LabVLA sets a new state-of-the-art on LabUtopia.

Abstract: Scientific laboratories increasingly rely on AI systems to reason about experiments, but the physical act of doing science remains largely outside their reach. AI can help read literature, generate hypotheses, and plan protocols, yet the execution of those protocols at the bench still requires a human operator. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models provide one possible interface between written protocols and robot execution, but existing policies are trained mostly on household and tabletop demonstrations and rarely encounter the instruments, transparent liquids, or fixed protocol workflows found in scientific laboratories. Closing this gap requires both laboratory-specific supervision and a unified learning framework that can accommodate the diverse robot embodiments used to execute experimental protocols. We therefore identify data and embodiment as central bottlenecks alongside model design. To address the data side, we build RoboGenesis, a simulation-based workflow and data engine that composes configured laboratory workflows from atomic skills, validates and filters rollouts, and exports structured demonstrations across supported robot profiles. On the policy side, we present LabVLA, trained with a two-stage recipe: FAST action token pretraining first makes the Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct backbone action aware before any continuous control is learned, and flow matching posttraining then attaches a DiT action expert under knowledge insulation. On the LabUtopia benchmark, LabVLA achieves the highest average success rate among all evaluated baselines under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

</details>


### [57] [One Polluted Page Is Enough: Evaluating Web Content Pollution in Generative Recommenders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13610)
*Minghao Luo,Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FORGE benchmark reveals that search-augmented LLMs are highly vulnerable to recommending fake products when search results are polluted, with fooled rates reaching 73.8% under full top-3 replacement. Reasoning often backfires by fabricating social proof, and standard defenses like skepticism prompting can worsen the problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Search-augmented LLMs increasingly mediate consumer recommendations by retrieving live web content, but this creates a vulnerability: polluted web content (fake reviews, promotional pages) may manipulate the LLM into unwittingly promoting fake products. The authors aim to systematically measure this risk.

Method: The authors introduce FORGE, a benchmark with 225 real-world products across 15 categories and 5 consumer scenarios. FORGE simulates web-content pollution by locally rewriting real products in retrieved web pages into fake ones, then measures how often the LLM recommends the fake product. They test 12 commercial and open-weights LLMs under varying pollution levels (single page to top-3 replacement). Three defenses are evaluated: skepticism prompting, consensus filtering over model priors, and cross-document evidence filtering.

Result: All 12 LLMs are vulnerable. A single polluted page yields fooled rates up to 27%; full top-3 replacement reaches 73.8%. Vulnerability varies by category and is higher when models lack stable prior knowledge. Reasoning does not mitigate the issue—it often generates spurious social proof justifying false recommendations. Skepticism prompting can exacerbate vulnerability, while consensus filtering risks suppressing legitimate products.

Conclusion: Search-augmented LLMs are systematically vulnerable to web-content pollution in recommendation contexts. Current defenses are insufficient: skepticism backfires, and filtering introduces trade-offs. The benchmark and findings highlight an urgent need for robust mitigation strategies in generative recommenders.

Abstract: Search-augmented LLMs increasingly mediate everyday consumer recommendations by retrieving live web content. This creates a new risk: generative recommenders may consume polluted web content, such as fake reviews and promotional pages crafted to mislead recommendations. We ask: to what extent do search-augmented LLMs become unwitting promoters of fake products when consuming polluted retrieval results? To answer this, we introduce FORGE (Fake Online Recommendations in Generative Environments), a benchmark for measuring fake-product promotion under controlled web-content pollution. Given an upstream search result, FORGE locally rewrites real products in retrieved web pages into fake ones to simulate web-content pollution, and measures how often the LLM recommends the fake product. FORGE covers 225 real-world products across 15 categories and 5 consumer scenarios. Across 12 commercial and open-weights LLMs, all models are vulnerable: a single polluted page yields fooled rates of up to 27%, while the full top-3 replacement raises this to 73.8%. Vulnerability varies substantially across categories, increasing when models lack stable prior knowledge of the relevant products. Reasoning does not mitigate this vulnerability; instead, it often generates spurious social proof to justify false recommendations. We evaluate three defenses: skepticism prompting and consensus filtering (over model priors or cross-document evidence). Skepticism can exacerbate vulnerability, much like reasoning, while filtering risks suppressing legitimate products. We release FORGE at https://github.com/leoluolol/forge-benchmark.

</details>


### [58] [Beyond Uniform Tokens: Adaptive Compression for Time Series Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13624)
*Jialin Gan,Xin Qiu,Guangzhe Chen,Xue Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies inefficiencies in how LLMs process time series (TS) tokens versus prompt tokens, revealing that TS tokens are spectrally redundant and prompt-token influence decays with model depth. It proposes an adaptive token budgeting framework that compresses TS tokens via frequency-domain structure and progressively reduces prompt tokens across layers, achieving up to 7.68× inference acceleration with performance gains in 78% of evaluated settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform token processing in LLMs for time series analysis is inefficient because TS tokens (numerical observations) and prompt tokens (textual context) have fundamentally different information structures. The authors aim to improve token efficiency by exploiting these structural asymmetries, addressing both the spectral redundancy of TS tokens and the diminishing influence of prompt tokens across transformer layers.

Method: The paper proposes an adaptive token budgeting framework with two components: (1) frequency-domain compression of TS tokens, which prunes tokens with redundant frequency patterns while preserving those carrying critical temporal evidence; (2) progressive reduction of prompt tokens across model layers, justified by the observed attenuation of prompt-token influence with depth. These asymmetric compression strategies are integrated into a unified budgeting mechanism.

Result: Experiments across forecasting, classification, imputation, and anomaly detection demonstrate up to 7.68× inference acceleration. Performance gains are achieved in 78% of evaluated settings, showing that asymmetric token compression improves both efficiency and effectiveness for scalable time series foundation models.

Conclusion: Asymmetric token compression — treating TS tokens and prompt tokens differently based on their distinct information structures — is an effective strategy for building scalable and efficient time series foundation models. The proposed adaptive token budgeting framework significantly reduces computational cost while maintaining or improving task performance across diverse TS analysis tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled time series (TS) analysis by jointly modeling numerical observations and textual context through a shared token interface. However, TS tokens and prompt tokens exhibit fundamentally different information structures, making uniform token processing inefficient. In this paper, we study token efficiency in TS language modeling from an asymmetric-token perspective. We show that TS tokens have highly uneven spectral contributions, where many tokens share redundant frequency patterns while a small subset preserves critical temporal evidence. We also observe that prompt-token influence attenuates with model depth, suggesting that full prompt retention across all layers is unnecessary. Based on these findings, we develop an adaptive token budgeting framework that compresses TS tokens via frequency-domain structure and progressively reduces prompt tokens across layers. Experiments across forecasting, classification, imputation, and anomaly detection demonstrate up to \textit{\textbf{7.68$\times$}} inference acceleration and performance gains in \textit{\textbf{78\%}} of evaluated settings, showing the effectiveness of asymmetric token compression for scalable TS foundation models.

</details>


### [59] [From Tokens to Faces: Investigating Discrete Speech Representations for 3D Facial Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13630)
*Pedro Correa,Olivier Perrotin,Samir Sadok,Paula Costa,Thomas Hueber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Evaluates four speech representation families for 3D facial animation, finding that phonetic-class encoding benefits prediction accuracy. Introduces an AVTTS pipeline using discrete representations as a shared space for decoding both speech and facial motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The choice of speech representation is critical for speech-driven 3D facial animation, yet it is unclear which type of representation — SSL features, neural codecs, ASR-style label spaces — is most effective. A systematic comparison is needed to understand what each representation encodes and how it impacts facial reconstruction quality.

Method: Four speech representation families are evaluated across two facial decoders using objective metrics and perceptual evaluation. Probing analyses relate tokenized representations to phonetic units and articulatory deformations. An Audio Visual Text-to-Speech (AVTTS) pipeline is introduced, leveraging discrete representations as a shared latent space.

Result: Encoding phonetic classes proves beneficial for accurate facial animation prediction. Both semantic (SSL-based) and label-based (ASR-style) representations achieve comparable facial animation quality. Discrete representations can serve as an effective shared space for joint speech and facial motion decoding.

Conclusion: Phonetically-informed representations yield strong performance for speech-driven 3D facial animation. The proposed AVTTS pipeline demonstrates that discrete representations can unify audio and visual synthesis in a shared latent space.

Abstract: The choice of speech representation is critical in speech-driven 3D facial animation. Representations differ in what they encode: SSL features emphasize segmental and semantic cues, neural codecs yield latents optimized for acoustic reconstruction, and ASR-style objectives produce label-based spaces. We evaluate four speech representation families for 3D facial synthesis, comparing their facial reconstruction quality across two facial decoders using objective metrics and a perceptual evaluation. We additionally conduct probing analyses that relate tokenized representations to phonetic units and to articulatory deformations. We found that encoding phonetic classes is beneficial for accurate facial animation prediction on both semantic and label-based representations with comparable facial animation quality. From the latter, we introduce an Audio Visual Text-to-Speech (AVTTS) pipeline that leverages, as a shared space, discrete representations to decode speech and 3D facial motion.

</details>


### [60] [Operads for compositional reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13634)
*Nathaniel Bottman,Kyle Richardson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes operads as a rigorous mathematical framework for question decomposition in LLM reasoning, defining the questions operad Q and the concept of operadic consistency to measure answer reliability across decomposition trees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question decomposition is widely used to improve LLM reasoning but lacks a rigorous mathematical foundation. The authors seek to formalize this practice mathematically using operad theory.

Method: The authors define the questions operad Q, where operations correspond to question templates and composition corresponds to substitution of sub-answers. QA models are interpreted as algebras over Q. They introduce operadic consistency, which measures whether a QA model's answers agree across partial collapses of a question decomposition tree.

Result: The companion paper (Bottman, Liu, and Richardson, 2026) empirically demonstrates that operadic consistency strongly correlates with accuracy across twelve LLMs and four multi-hop QA datasets, outperforming standard temperature-based self-consistency baselines.

Conclusion: Operads provide the natural mathematical home for question decomposition, and invariants like operadic consistency open new directions for analyzing and improving multi-step reasoning reliability in LLMs.

Abstract: Question decomposition, i.e. breaking a complex query into simpler sub-queries whose answers are composed to produce a final answer, is a widely used strategy for improving LLM reasoning, yet it currently lacks a rigorous mathematical foundation. In this paper, we propose operads, mathematical structures that model many-in, one-out operations and compositions thereof, as a natural framework for describing question decomposition. We define the questions operad $Q$, in which operations correspond to question templates and composition corresponds to substitution of sub-answers, and show how QA models can be interpreted as algebras over $Q$. Beyond reframing existing practice, this operadic perspective points toward new methods, in particular a notion of operadic consistency, which measures whether a QA model's answers agree across the partial collapses of a question decomposition tree. Empirical evaluation of operadic consistency is reported in our companion paper (Bottman, Liu, and Richardson, 2026), which finds it strongly correlated with accuracy across twelve LLMs and four multi-hop QA datasets and outperforming standard temperature-based self-consistency baselines. We argue that operads are the natural mathematical home for question decomposition, and that invariants such as operadic consistency open new directions for analyzing and improving the reliability of multi-step reasoning.

</details>


### [61] [Recursive Agent Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13643)
*Elias Lumer,Sahil Sen,Kevin Paul,Vamse Kumar Subbiah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Recursive Agent Harness (RAH), a code-first pattern where a parent agent spawns subagent harnesses (with filesystem tools, code execution, and planning) via executable scripts. RAH extends recursive language models from model-level recursion to harness-level recursion. Evaluated on long-context reasoning benchmarks, RAH significantly outperforms Codex baselines (71.75% → 81.36% with GPT-5; 89.77% with Claude Sonnet 4.5) on Oolong-Synthetic across context lengths up to 4M tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two converging trends motivate this work: (1) recursive language models (RLMs) demonstrate that recursion over model calls improves long-context reasoning, and (2) production coding agents increasingly spawn subagents at scale (e.g., Anthropic's dynamic workflows). The authors identify a gap: existing recursion operates at the model-call level without tools, whereas practical agents need full harness recursion—integrating filesystems, code execution, and planning. RAH formalizes and evaluates this underexplored intermediate pattern.

Method: RAH frames recursion at the harness level rather than the model level. A parent agent generates and executes a script that spawns multiple subagent harnesses in parallel for fine-grained workloads, while using structured function calls for smaller subtasks. Each subagent harness is a full agent equipped with filesystem tools, code execution capabilities, and planning modules. For evaluation, the authors use the Oolong-Synthetic benchmark (199 samples, 13 context-length buckets up to 4M tokens), comparing RAH against published Codex and RLM baselines with GPT-5 as a controlled backbone, and also testing with Claude Sonnet 4.5.

Result: With GPT-5 as the backbone (matching published baselines), RAH improves the Codex coding-agent baseline from 71.75% to 81.36% on Oolong-Synthetic—a gain the authors attribute to the harness design rather than model capability. With a stronger backbone (Claude Sonnet 4.5), RAH reaches 89.77%, demonstrating that the architecture benefits from but is not dependent on frontier model improvements.

Conclusion: Harness recursion—recursion at the level of full agent harnesses rather than bare model calls—is a powerful, code-first paradigm for long-context reasoning. RAH bridges the gap between model-level recursion (RLMs) and production subagent spawning, showing substantial gains that are attributable to architecture rather than model scaling alone. The approach scales well with stronger backbones and across extreme context lengths.

Abstract: Recursive language models (RLMs) showed that recursion over model calls is an effective strategy for long-context reasoning, and production coding agents have begun to write code that spawns subagents at scale, most recently in Anthropic's dynamic workflows. We name and study the pattern between these two lines of work, where the recursive unit is a full agent harness with filesystem tools, code execution, and planning rather than a model call with no tools. We call this the Recursive Agent Harness (RAH) and frame it as harness recursion, the code-first extension to the model recursion of RLMs. A parent agent generates and runs an executable script that spawns subagent harnesses in parallel for fine-grained workloads and uses structured function calls for small subtasks. We provide a controlled evaluation on long-context reasoning. With the backbone held fixed at GPT-5 to match the published Codex and RLM baselines, RAH improves the Codex coding-agent baseline from 71.75% to 81.36% on Oolong-Synthetic (199 samples, 13 context-length buckets up to 4M tokens), a gain attributable to the harness rather than the model. With a stronger backbone, Claude Sonnet 4.5, the same design reaches 89.77%.

</details>


### [62] [SkMTEB: Slovak Massive Text Embedding Benchmark and Model Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13647)
*Marek Šuppa,Andrej Ridzik,Daniel Hládek,Natália Kňažeková,Viktória Ondrejová*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkMTEB introduces a comprehensive Slovak text embedding benchmark (31 datasets, 7 task types). Evaluation of 31 models shows large multilingual models outperform Slovak-specific NLU models. The authors release e5-sk-small and e5-sk-large — vocabulary-trimmed, fine-tuned Multilingual E5 variants — achieving competitive performance with proprietary APIs while being locally deployable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Slovak, as a low-resource West Slavic language, lacks a comprehensive text embedding benchmark. Existing multilingual benchmarks offer sparse Slovak coverage (only ~8 datasets). There is also a need for efficient, locally-deployable Slovak embedding models suitable for semantic search and RAG without relying on proprietary APIs.

Method: The authors construct SkMTEB, an MTEB-style benchmark with 31 datasets spanning 7 task types (e.g., classification, clustering, pair classification, reranking, retrieval, STS, summarization). They evaluate 31 embedding models. They then apply vocabulary trimming (reducing tokenizer size to Slovak-relevant tokens) and fine-tuning to Multilingual E5 models to produce two efficient models: e5-sk-small (45M) and e5-sk-large (365M), with up to 62% size reduction.

Result: Large instruction-tuned multilingual models achieve the strongest performance on SkMTEB. Slovak-specific NLU models transfer poorly to embedding tasks. The proposed e5-sk-small and e5-sk-large achieve competitive performance with proprietary APIs despite significant parameter reduction, and remain fully locally deployable.

Conclusion: A dedicated, comprehensive embedding benchmark is essential for low-resource languages. Vocabulary trimming plus fine-tuning offers an effective, replicable pipeline for building efficient, locally-deployable embedding models for under-resourced languages. All artifacts (benchmark, models, datasets, code) are released openly.

Abstract: We introduce SkMTEB, the first comprehensive MTEB-style text embedding benchmark for Slovak, a low-resource West Slavic language, comprising 31 datasets across 7 task types -- nearly 4$\times$ the depth of existing multilingual benchmark coverage for Slovak. Our evaluation of 31 embedding models reveals that large instruction-tuned multilingual models achieve the strongest performance, while existing Slovak-specific models trained for NLU tasks transfer poorly to embedding tasks. To address the need for efficient, locally-deployable Slovak embeddings, we develop \texttt{e5-sk-small} (45M parameters) and \texttt{e5-sk-large} (365M) by applying vocabulary trimming and fine-tuning to Multilingual E5 models. Despite size reductions of up to 62\%, our open-source models achieve competitive performance with proprietary APIs while remaining locally deployable for semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). We release the benchmark, models, datasets, and code openly, hoping our approach offers a replicable path for other under-resourced languages.

</details>


### [63] [Operadic consistency: a label-free signal for compositional reasoning failures in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13649)
*Nathaniel Bottman,Yinhong Liu,Kyle Richardson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Operadic consistency (OC) checks whether an LLM's direct answer to a compositional query matches the answer it derives by composing answers to sub-questions. Across 12 LLMs and 4 multi-hop QA datasets, OC shows uniformly strong correlation with accuracy (r ∈ [0.86, 0.94]), outperforming baselines like CoT self-consistency and semantic entropy, and yields robust selective-prediction gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting LLM reasoning failures at inference time without ground-truth labels is critical for trustworthy deployment. Existing confidence methods (self-consistency, semantic entropy, P(True)) rely on within-question sampling and self-evaluation, but lack a principled diagnostic based on compositional structure. Operad theory—the mathematics of iterated substitution—suggests that a model's direct answer should agree with the answer obtained by composing answers to explicitly decomposed sub-questions. This motivates a complementary, compositionality-based failure detector.

Method: The authors propose operadic consistency (OC): for a given multi-hop query, the model produces (1) a direct answer, and (2) a decomposition of the query into sub-questions, answers each sub-question, then composes the sub-answers into a final answer. OC measures agreement between these two paths. The method is evaluated across 12 instruction-tuned LLMs (4B–671B parameters, both open and closed) on HotpotQA, DROP, MuSiQue, and StrategyQA. OC is compared against baselines including CoT self-consistency (CoT-SC), semantic entropy, and P(True). For frontier thinking models, the decomposition is extracted from the model's own chain of thought. Selective-prediction performance is measured via AUARC and AUROC at equal sampling budget (K=3).

Result: OC achieves Pearson r ∈ [0.86, 0.94] with accuracy across all four datasets (all p ≤ 0.0004) and is the only signal with r ≥ 0.85 uniformly. CoT-SC matches OC on HotpotQA (r=0.93) and DROP (r=0.87) but drops to r ≈ 0.45 on MuSiQue and StrategyQA. OC contributes unique information beyond CoT-SC and semantic entropy (cluster-robust p ≤ 10⁻¹⁶), robust to decomposition-aware baselines (p ≤ 10⁻¹³). At equal K=3 budget, OC yields selective-prediction AUARC lifts of +0.086 to +0.096 and AUROC lifts of +0.092 to +0.164 over tuned CoT-SC, with 95% CIs excluding zero. On five frontier thinking models with auto-extracted decompositions, OC gives positive lift on all 16 (dataset, budget, metric) cells, with 95% CIs excluding zero on 12 of 16.

Conclusion: Operadic consistency provides a principled, compositionality-grounded signal for detecting LLM reasoning failures without labels. It is uniformly strong across diverse multi-hop QA datasets, captures information orthogonal to existing methods, and enables practical selective-prediction improvements at equal compute budget, even when decompositions are extracted from the model's own reasoning traces.

Abstract: Detecting LLM reasoning failures at inference time without ground-truth labels has motivated a wide range of confidence baselines, including self-consistency, semantic entropy, and P(True), built on within-question sampling and self-evaluation. Operad theory, the formalism for systems built by iterated substitution, suggests a complementary diagnostic: a model's direct answer to a compositional query should agree with the answer it produces by composing a stated decomposition of the same query. We instantiate this idea as operadic consistency (OC), a per-question signal. Across twelve instruction-tuned LLMs (4B to 671B parameters, open-weights and closed-source) on four multi-hop QA datasets, OC is strongly correlated with accuracy on every dataset (Pearson $r \in [0.86, 0.94]$, all $p \leq 0.0004$), and is the only signal we evaluate with $r \geq 0.85$ uniformly across all four datasets. Chain-of-thought self-consistency (CoT-SC; Wang et al., 2023) matches OC on HotpotQA and DROP ($r = 0.93, 0.87$) but drops to $r \approx 0.45$ on MuSiQue and StrategyQA. At the per-question level, OC contributes information beyond CoT-SC and semantic entropy on every dataset (cluster-robust $p \leq 10^{-16}$ for the OC coefficient), and the conclusion is robust to additionally controlling for constructed decomposition-aware baselines ($p \leq 10^{-13}$). The same signal yields selective-prediction improvements (accuracy at fixed coverage) over a tuned CoT-SC baseline at the equal-cost $K = 3$ budget (AUARC lifts of +0.086 to +0.096 and AUROC lifts of +0.092 to +0.164; 95% CIs exclude zero on every cell). On five frontier thinking models, where the decomposition is extracted from the model's own chain of thought, the same equal-cost comparison gives positive selective-prediction point-estimate lift on all 16 (dataset, budget, metric) cells tested, with 95% CIs excluding zero on 12 of the 16.

</details>


### [64] [HyperTool: Beyond Step-Wise Tool Calls for Tool-Augmented Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13663)
*Yaxin Du,Yifan Zhou,Yujie Ge,Jiajun Wang,Xianghe Pang,Shuo Tang,Tuney Zheng,Bryan Dai,Jian Yang,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HyperTool introduces an MCP-style tool interface that lets LLM agents execute multi-step tool workflows within a single code block, reducing context consumption and eliminating the execution-granularity mismatch caused by step-wise atomic tool calls. Trained on synthesized cross-tool trajectories, HyperTool substantially boosts multi-step tool-use accuracy on MCP-Universe.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current tool-augmented LLM agents rely on step-wise atomic tool calls, where every invocation, observation, and value transfer is exposed in the main reasoning trace. This creates an execution-granularity mismatch: locally deterministic workflows are needlessly unfolded into repeated model-visible decisions, wasting context length and forcing the model to manage low-level dataflow. The authors seek to fold deterministic tool subroutines into a single call, making the model-visible unit of execution coarser and more efficient.

Method: The authors propose HyperTool, a unified executable MCP-style tool interface. Instead of step-wise calls, the model invokes HyperTool with a code block that can (1) call existing tools through their original schemas, (2) manipulate returned values, and (3) pass intermediate results locally — all within a single outer call. To train models for this interface, they synthesize HyperTool-format trajectories from cross-tool compositional tasks and verify these trajectories in real MCP environments, producing supervised fine-tuning data.

Result: On the MCP-Universe benchmark, HyperTool raises average accuracy from 15.69% to 35.29% on Qwen3-32B and from 9.93% to 33.33% on Qwen3-8B. It also surpasses GPT-OSS and Kimi-k2.5 on average accuracy, demonstrating substantial improvements in multi-step tool use.

Conclusion: HyperTool effectively addresses the execution-granularity mismatch by consolidating deterministic tool workflows into single invocations. This reduces context overhead and simplifies dataflow management, leading to significant accuracy gains in compositional multi-tool tasks across different model scales.

Abstract: Tool-augmented LLM agents commonly rely on step-wise atomic tool calls, where each invocation, observation, and value transfer is exposed in the main reasoning trace. This creates an \emph{execution-granularity mismatch}: locally deterministic tool workflows are unfolded into repeated model-visible decisions, consuming context and forcing the model to manage low-level dataflow in the trace. We introduce \textbf{HyperTool}, a unified executable MCP-style tool interface that changes the model-visible unit of tool execution. A model invokes HyperTool with a code block that can call existing tools through their original schemas, manipulate returned values, and pass intermediate results locally, folding deterministic tool subroutines into a single outer call. To train models to use this interface, we synthesize HyperTool-format trajectories from cross-tool compositional tasks and verify them in real MCP environments. On MCP-Universe, HyperTool improves average accuracy from 15.69\% to 35.29\% on Qwen3-32B and from 9.93\% to 33.33\% on Qwen3-8B, and surpass GPT-OSS and Kimi-k2.5 on average accuracy, showing that our HyperTool can substantially improve multi-step tool use.

</details>


### [65] [Influcoder: Distilling Decoders' Gradient Influence Rankings into an Encoder for Data Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13668)
*Dimitri Kachler,Damien Sileo,Pascal Denis*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Influcoder proposes a fast, cost-effective method for influence-based Data Attribution at scale, addressing the speed and storage limitations of existing influence function methods when applied to large datasets and LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs grow more capable, there is a pressing need to curate high-quality training datasets by identifying harmful or influential samples. Existing Data Attribution methods based on influence functions are effective but suffer from prohibitive computational speed and storage requirements, making them impractical for large-scale deployment.

Method: The paper introduces Influcoder, a method that enables quick and cost-effective influence-based Data Attribution at scale. While specific architectural details are not provided in the abstract, the approach is positioned as a practical alternative to classical influence function methods, presumably optimizing both processing speed and storage compactness for large datasets.

Result: The abstract does not report specific experimental results or quantitative benchmarks, focusing instead on positioning Influcoder as a scalable solution to the identified efficiency problems in influence-based Data Attribution.

Conclusion: Influcoder offers a practical pathway to implement influence-based Data Attribution at scale, enabling efficient identification of training samples that condition undesirable model outputs (e.g., toxic behavior), thereby facilitating large-scale dataset curation for LLMs.

Abstract: With the growth of LLMs' (Large Language Models) capabilities, there has been an increasing push to curate high quality datasets by filtering samples in the training data. In general, Data Attribution (DA) methods aim to estimate how individual samples in a training dataset can precondition a model to generate certain outputs. As an example, one might be interested in which samples in the data could be the source of toxic behavior after training the LLM. Many methods quantify this conditioning through the paradigm of influence functions. While methods of this family are effective in its function, they lack the necessary processing speed and storage compactness to be practically implemented on large datasets. We propose a method, Influcoder, as a quick and cost-effective approach to influence-based Data Attribution at scale.

</details>


### [66] [Learning to Reason by Analogy via Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13680)
*Zilin Xiao,Qi Ma,Chun-cheng Jason Chen,Xintao Chen,Avinash Atreya,Hanjie Chen,Vicente Ordonez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RA-RFT is a post-training framework that combines a reasoning-aware retriever (trained via gold-relevance distillation) with reinforcement fine-tuning to teach LMs to reason by analogy, outperforming standard RL fine-tuning on mathematical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RAG retrieves by semantic/lexical similarity, which fails for complex reasoning tasks — similar problems may need different strategies, while different problems may share reasoning patterns. A retrieval mechanism that ranks contexts by reasoning benefit is needed.

Method: RA-RFT has two stages: (1) Gold-relevance distillation trains a retriever to rank contexts by expected reasoning benefit rather than semantic overlap. (2) The policy model is fine-tuned via reinforcement fine-tuning (e.g., GRPO) using retrieved analogous demonstrations with verifiable outcome rewards.

Result: RA-RFT improves AIME 2025 average@32 accuracy by 7.1 points (Qwen3-1.7B) and 2.8 points (Qwen3-4B) over GRPO. Reasoning-aware retrieval surfaces complementary solution strategies that provide distinct reasoning scaffolds.

Conclusion: Reasoning-aware retrieval is a complementary axis of improvement, orthogonal to advances in reward design or training curricula. RA-RFT consistently outperforms standard RL fine-tuning across challenging math reasoning benchmarks.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has become a standard mechanism for grounding language models in external knowledge, yet conventional retrieval based on lexical or semantic similarity is poorly suited for complex reasoning tasks: a semantically similar problem may demand an entirely different solution strategy, while a superficially different problem may share the same underlying reasoning pattern. We propose Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RA-RFT), a post-training framework that teaches language models to reason by analogy. RA-RFT uses gold-relevance distillation to train a retriever that ranks contexts by expected reasoning benefit rather than semantic overlap, and then fine-tunes the policy model via reinforcement fine-tuning methods with retrieved analogous demonstrations, so the model learns to leverage reasoning traces under verifiable outcome rewards. We further analyze the diversity of retrieved contexts and find that reasoning-aware retrieval surfaces complementary solution strategies that provide distinct reasoning scaffolds for individual problems. Across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, RA-RFT consistently outperforms standard reinforcement fine-tuning methods. For example, it improves AIME 2025 average@32 accuracy by 7.1 and 2.8 points over GRPO for Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B respectively -- suggesting that reasoning-aware retrieval is a complementary axis of improvement and orthogonal to advances in reward design or training curricula.

</details>


### [67] [EvoArena: Tracking Memory Evolution for Robust LLM Agents in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13681)
*Jundong Xu,Qingchuan Li,Jiaying Wu,Yihuai Lan,Shuyue Stella Li,Huichi Zhou,Bowen Jiang,Lei Wang,Jun Wang,Anh Tuan Luu,Caiming Xiong,Hae Won Park,Bryan Hooi,Zhiyuan Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoArena introduces a dynamic benchmark where environments evolve over time, and EvoMem is a patch-based memory system that helps LLM agents track these changes. Current agents struggle on EvoArena (39.6% avg accuracy), and EvoMem provides consistent gains on both the evolving benchmark and standard static benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most LLM agent evaluations assume static environments, but real-world deployment involves continuous environmental change. Agents must continually adapt their knowledge, skills, and behavior to evolving conditions. No existing benchmark systematically models this dynamic evolution.

Method: EvoArena models environmental changes as progressive update sequences across three domains: terminal (command-line tools), software (GUI/app interfaces), and social (preference-based interactions). EvoMem is a patch-based memory paradigm that records memory evolution as structured update histories, allowing agents to reason about environmental changes through diffs/patches rather than storing full snapshots.

Result: Current agents achieve only 39.6% average accuracy across evolving domains on EvoArena. EvoMem yields a 1.5% average gain on EvoArena and improves standard benchmarks GAIA (+6.1%) and LoCoMo (+4.8%). Chain-level accuracy on EvoArena improves by 3.7%. Mechanistic analysis indicates EvoMem improves evidence capture, preserving more complete evolving environment states.

Conclusion: Modeling environmental evolution in both evaluation benchmarks and agent memory is critical for reliable real-world deployment. EvoMem's patch-based approach effectively captures dynamic state changes and generalizes to improve performance on static benchmarks as well.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have achieved strong performance on a wide range of benchmarks, yet most evaluations assume static environments. In contrast, real-world deployment is inherently dynamic, requiring agents to continually align their knowledge, skills, and behavior with changing environments and updated task conditions. To address this gap, we introduce EvoArena, a benchmark suite that models environment changes as sequences of progressive updates across terminal, software, and social domains. We further propose EvoMem, a patch-based memory paradigm that records memory evolution as structured update histories, enabling agents to reason about environmental evolution through changes in their memory. Experiments show that current agents struggle on EvoArena, achieving an average accuracy of 39.6% across evolving terminal, software, and social-preference domains. EvoMem consistently improves performance, yielding an average gain of 1.5% on EvoArena and also improving standard benchmarks such as GAIA and LoCoMo by 6.1% and 4.8%. Beyond individual tasks, EvoMem further improves chain-level accuracy by 3.7% on EvoArena, where success requires completing a consecutive sequence of related evolutionary subtasks. Mechanistic analysis shows that EvoMem improves evidence capture in the memory, indicating better preservation of complete evolving environment states. Our results highlight the importance of modeling evolution in both evaluation and memory for reliable agent deployment.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [68] [Influence Factors on RAG Poisoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12469)
*Pedro Pereira,Eva Maia,Isabel Praça,Adrien Bécue*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A comprehensive factorial study (432 configurations) examining how dataset, retriever type, retrieval depth, database composition, chunking, and generator model affect poisoning robustness in RAG systems. Key finding: vulnerability arises from interactions across all components, not any single factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems improve LLM outputs by integrating external knowledge at inference time, but this dependency introduces poisoning attack vulnerabilities where adversarial documents can corrupt both retrieval and generation. Prior work lacked a systematic, multi-factor understanding of what drives these vulnerabilities.

Method: Full factorial experimental design spanning 432 configurations, systematically varying six factors: dataset, retriever type (BM25, dense, graph-based), retrieval depth, database composition (poison/clean mix), chunking strategy, and generator model. Both retrieval-level and generation-level metrics were evaluated.

Result: Retriever architecture, dataset, and retrieval depth are the strongest factors for poisoning exposure at the retrieval level. Generator choice and database composition most influence downstream attack success. Dense and graph-based retrievers improve robustness over BM25; larger retrieval depth increases poisoned passage retrieval. Replicating poisoned content across databases amplifies adversarial impact, while adding clean sources mitigates it.

Conclusion: Poisoning vulnerability in RAG is an emergent property of system-wide interactions among retrieval, generation, and knowledge-base configuration, rather than being attributable to any single component. Holistic defense strategies are necessary.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems enhance large language models by grounding responses in retrieved documents from external knowledge sources at inference time. However, this reliance on retrieved content introduces vulnerabilities to poisoning attacks, in which adversarial documents can manipulate both the retrieval process and the generated outputs. This paper investigates poisoning robustness in RAG through a full factorial experimental study covering 432 configurations. We analyze the impacts of dataset, retriever type, retrieval depth, database composition, chunking strategy, and generator model on retrieval-level and generation-level metrics. The results show that retriever architecture, dataset, and retrieval depth are the strongest factors affecting poisoning exposure, while generator choice and database composition have a major impact on downstream attack success. Dense and graph-based retrievers generally improve robustness relative to BM25, whereas larger retrieval depth increases the likelihood of retrieving poisoned passages. We further show that replicating poisoned content across multiple databases amplifies adversarial influence, while additional clean sources can mitigate it. These findings highlight that poisoning vulnerability in RAG is not attributable to a single component, but instead arises from the interaction of retrieval, generation, and knowledge-base configuration.

</details>


### [69] [From Parameters to Feature Space: Task Arithmetic for Backdoor Mitigation in Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12498)
*Zhenqian Zhu,Yamin Hu,Yiya Diao,Weixiang Li,Haodong Li,Wenjian Luo*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LFPM is a backdoor mitigation method for model merging that optimizes an anti-backdoor task vector from a feature-space perspective, using gradient accumulation and loss path-integral to suppress backdoors while preserving clean-task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model merging is vulnerable to backdoor attacks, and existing defenses (e.g., task arithmetic) degrade clean-task performance because they operate via direct parameter-space editing. A method is needed that can neutralize backdoors without harming benign task accuracy.

Method: LFPM leverages the Cross-Task Linearity (CTL) framework to formulate backdoor robustness from a feature-space perspective. It introduces an anti-backdoor task vector optimized via gradient accumulation and loss path-integral along the interpolation path between the clean and backdoored merged models, ensuring consistent backdoor suppression.

Result: Extensive experiments show LFPM achieves strong robustness against backdoor attacks in both full fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) settings.

Conclusion: LFPM provides an effective, feature-space-based defense against backdoor attacks in model merging, outperforming parameter-space editing methods by better preserving clean-task performance while robustly suppressing backdoors.

Abstract: Model merging (MM) has gained significant attention as a cost-effective approach to integrate multiple task-specific models into a unified model. However, recent work reveals that MM is highly susceptible to backdoor attacks. Existing defenses based on task arithmetic often fail to eliminate backdoors without substantially degrading clean-task performance, owing to their reliance on direct parameter-space editing. To address this gap, we propose Linear Feature Path Minimization (LFPM), a backdoor mitigation framework for model merging, which introduces an anti-backdoor task vector into the backdoored merged model. Unlike prior approaches, LFPM formulates the backdoor robustness of the merged model from a unified feature-space perspective under the Cross-Task Linearity (CTL) framework, which leverages the approximate linearity of features across tasks. This perspective guides the optimization of the anti-backdoor task to suppress backdoors while preserving clean-task performance. Furthermore, we introduce an effective optimization mechanism based on gradient accumulation and loss path-integral, ensuring robust backdoor suppression along the interpolation path. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LFPM consistently exhibits strong robustness against backdoor attacks in both full fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) settings.

</details>


### [70] [Beyond Attack Success Rate: Examining Trigger Leakage in Vision-Language Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12586)
*Jiamin Chang,Salil Kanhere,Piotr Koniusz,Jason,Xue,Hammond Pearce*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper identifies "trigger leakage" as a critical but overlooked failure mode in visual backdoor attacks on Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS), where inputs visually/semantically similar to the intended trigger inadvertently activate malicious behavior. They introduce Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR) to quantify this, demonstrate severe leakage under standard poisoning, and show that hard-negative sampling during training substantially mitigates it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor evaluations use clean accuracy and attack success rate (ASR), which measure only whether a trigger works, not whether it is "precise" — i.e., activating hidden behavior only when intended. In VLAS, where triggers propagate through decision pipelines into executable actions, imprecise triggers pose a severe system-level threat. The authors aim to formalize, quantify, and mitigate this imprecision.

Method: The authors formalize trigger imprecision as "trigger leakage" and propose Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR) as a metric: the proportion of neighbor inputs (visually or semantically close to the intended trigger) that inadvertently activate the backdoor. They evaluate icon and text triggers at a 3% poisoning ratio under common visual transformations. Using textual triggers as a controlled probe, they analyze standard fine-tuning's activation boundaries and test a mitigation: injecting edit-distance-one hard-negative samples during training. They validate this across image-editing and embodied-manipulation workflows.

Result: At 3% poisoning, icon and text triggers maintain robustness to visual transformations but exhibit severe leakage: NLR reaches 0.996 for icon triggers and 0.944 for text triggers. Standard fine-tuning learns a broad activation region, causing neighboring strings to invoke malicious behavior even without the exact trigger. Adding edit-distance-one hard-negative samples during training substantially narrows the activation region and reduces leakage in both image-editing and embodied-manipulation settings.

Conclusion: Trigger leakage is a pervasive and underappreciated vulnerability in backdoor attacks on VLAS. Standard ASR metrics are insufficient; NLR provides a necessary complementary measure of attack precision. Hard-negative training offers an effective mitigation by sharpening the model's trigger decision boundary.

Abstract: Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS) connect visual perception to planning, tool use, and physical actions. This means backdoor-type triggers can propagate through both decision pipelines and their connected interfaces, thus making visual backdoors a system-level threat. Current evaluations on such backdoors focus on clean accuracy and attack success rate (ASR), metrics that capture whether a trigger works, but not whether an attack is actually "precise" -- i.e. whether it triggers hidden behaviors only when intended. In this work, we formalize the failure of trigger precision as "trigger leakage": inputs that are visually or semantically close to the intended trigger and therefore inadvertently activate the attacker-specified behavior. To quantify this leakage, we introduce Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR). Our experiments show that at a 3% poisoning ratio, icon and text triggers remain robust to common visual transformations, but their neighboring variants leak heavily, with NLR reaching 0.996 (icon) and 0.944 (text). Using textual triggers as a controlled probe, we show that standard fine-tuning learns a broad activation region rather than an exact trigger condition, causing neighboring strings to invoke the malicious behavior even when the exact trigger is absent. Adding edit-distance-one hard-negative samples during training substantially narrows this activation region and reduces leakage, including in image-editing and embodied-manipulation workflows, where leaked triggers can propagate into executable programs and action sequences.

</details>


### [71] [Amnesia: A Stealthy Replay Attack on Continual Learning Dreams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12655)
*Ahmed Sharshar,Naveen Kumar Kummari,Mohsen Guizani*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Amnesia," a replay composition attack against continual learning models where an insider controls only replay index selection. Under auditable visibility and mass budgets, Amnesia tilts the replay class histogram toward harmful classes via lightweight utility scores and projects back into a divergence ball. Experiments show significant degradation in accuracy and backward transfer, with a KL variant being stealthier and a TV variant being more damaging but detectable, exposing a practical threat surface in index-only replay control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning models rely on experience replay to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, but the robustness of replay sampling to adversarial interference is underexplored. Existing attacks lack explicit auditable constraints, limiting their practical realism. This work aims to define and study a limited-privilege threat model where an insider controls only replay index selection under monitor-verifiable audit bounds.

Method: Amnesia operates in two steps: (1) Compute lightweight per-class utilities (e.g., EMA loss or confidence) to tilt a nominal class histogram p0 toward harmful classes. (2) Project the tilted distribution back into a δ-ball (TV or KL divergence budget) using efficient optimizers — exponential tilt for KL, balanced mass redistribution for TV. A windowed scheduler ensures compliance with rolling-window audits. The attacker only manipulates replay indices within a fixed replay rate f and visibility budget δ.

Result: Across challenging CL benchmarks and strong replay baselines, Amnesia consistently reduces final accuracy (ACC) and worsens backward transfer (−BWT). The KL variant achieves high impact while remaining largely undetected under per-batch and rolling-window audits. The TV variant inflicts greater damage but is easier to detect, especially under tight per-class constraints.

Conclusion: Index-only replay control constitutes a practical, auditable threat surface in continual learning systems. The paper establishes a principled impact–visibility trade-off: stealthier attacks (KL) remain under audit radar with moderate damage, while aggressive attacks (TV) maximize degradation at the cost of detectability.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) models often use experience replay to reduce catastrophic forgetting, but their robustness to replay sampling interference remains underexplored. Existing CL attacks alter inputs or training pipelines (poisoning/backdoors) and rarely include explicit auditable constraints, limiting realism. Here, auditability means a monitor can verify compliance from sampler-visible telemetry - e.g., logged replay index/label statistics - by checking that the realized replay class histogram stays close to a nominal baseline and that replay rate is unchanged per batch and/or over a rolling window. We study a limited-privilege insider who controls only replay index selection, not pixels, labels, or model parameters, while staying within auditable limits such as queue priorities. We introduce Amnesia, a replay composition attack that maximizes degradation under two budgets: a visibility budget delta bounding the TV/KL divergence from a nominal class histogram p0, and a mass budget f fixing the replay rate. Amnesia has two steps: (i) compute lightweight class utilities, such as EMA loss or confidence, to tilt p0 toward harmful classes; and (ii) project the tilt back into the delta-ball using efficient KL (exponential tilt) or TV (balanced mass redistribution) optimizers. A windowed scheduler enforces rolling audits. Across challenging CL benchmarks and strong replay baselines, Amnesia consistently lowers final accuracy (ACC) and worsens backward transfer (-BWT). The KL variant delivers high impact while remaining largely undetected under multiple audit schemes, including per-batch and rolling-window checks. The TV variant is more damaging but easier to detect, especially under tight per-class constraints. These results expose index-only replay control as a practical, auditable threat surface in CL systems and establish a principled impact-visibility trade-off.

</details>


### [72] [CAPED: Context-Aware Privacy Exposure Defense for Mobile GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12666)
*Siyu Shen,Fenghao Xu,Wenrui Diao,Kehuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR
