TL;DR: CAPED is a phone-side, context-aware pre-upload filter for mobile GUI agents that selectively masks incidental private content in screenshots before transmission to remote multimodal models, reducing privacy leakage while preserving task utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile GUI agents that send screenshots to cloud-based multimodal models create "incidental visual privacy exposure," where unrelated sensitive content (contacts, messages, photos, health cues) is leaked during task execution. Existing defenses like text anonymization miss visual/inferential cues, and generic masking destroys task-relevant UI evidence and controls.

Method: CAPED operates on-device before upload. It extracts the user's task requirements, uses screen context as a privacy prior, parses visible UI elements, and selectively exposes only content necessary for the current task while masking incidental private content. Evaluated on AndroidWorld for utility and via a controlled 28-task seeded privacy evaluation measuring trajectory-level incidental leakage.

Result: In the seeded evaluation, Full CAPED reduces success-conditioned weighted seeded leakage from 0.766 (raw screenshots) to 0.268 while preserving high task utility. Broader AndroidWorld runs show a remaining prototype-level utility cost but support the core claim.

Conclusion: Screenshot upload should be treated as an explicit device-cloud boundary decision governed by task-driven selective exposure rather than all-or-nothing screen sharing. CAPED demonstrates this is feasible with acceptable trade-offs.

Abstract: Screenshot-based mobile GUI agents can operate ordinary smartphone apps through the same visual interface as a human user, but this capability also turns every screen observation into a privacy boundary. During normal task execution, screenshots may expose contacts, messages, photos, files, recommendations, health cues, and other sensitive context that is unrelated to the user's request. We call this problem incidental visual privacy exposure. It is difficult to address with existing defenses: text anonymization misses many visual and inferential cues, while generic privacy masking can remove the evidence and controls that a GUI agent needs to complete the task.
  This paper presents CAPED, a context-aware pre-upload exposure control layer for mobile GUI agents. CAPED is designed as a phone-side protection layer: before screenshots are released to a remote multimodal agent, it extracts task requirements, uses screen context as a privacy prior, parses visible UI elements, and selectively exposes only content needed for the current task while masking incidental private content. We evaluate CAPED on AndroidWorld for broad task utility and with a controlled 28-task seeded privacy evaluation used as a measurement instrument for trajectory-level incidental leakage. In this seeded evaluation, Full CAPED reduces success-conditioned weighted seeded leakage from 0.766 under raw screenshots to 0.268 while preserving high task utility. A broader AndroidWorld run shows a remaining prototype-level utility cost, but the results support the central claim that screenshot upload should be treated as an explicit device--cloud boundary decision, governed by task-driven selective exposure rather than all-or-nothing screen sharing.

</details>


### [73] [SMSR: Certified Defence Against Runtime Memory Poisoning in Persistent LLM Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12703)
*Tarun Sharma*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: RAG agents with persistent multi-session memory are vulnerable to memory poisoning attacks. The authors formalize this as Multi-Session Memory Poisoning (MSMP), show no existing defense certifies against it, and propose Signed Memory with Smoothed Retrieval (SMSR) — the first defense with certified robustness bounds, combining HMAC-SHA256 provenance signing and randomized memory ablation with verdict-based voting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persistent memory in RAG agents creates a new attack surface where adversaries can inject poisoned memories through normal channels that later steer agent responses for other users. Existing defenses either assume a static corpus (inapplicable to dynamic memory) or use heuristic filters easily bypassed by fluent text. No prior defense provides certified robustness for this setting.

Method: SMSR has two components: (1) HMAC-SHA256 provenance signing at write time to block unsigned injection, and (2) randomized memory ablation with verdict-based majority voting at query time to bound authenticated adversary influence. The authors formally prove no provenance-free retrieval-time filter can certify against adaptive injection, derive a hypergeometric certificate for Component 2, and formalize the Consistent Minority Effect where verdict-based voting defeats adversaries that win under string-based voting.

Result: Across 15 enterprise scenarios (3,150 trials), Component 1 reduced attack success from 93-100% to 0% for unsigned variants. For authenticated single-injection adversaries, Component 2 held success to 8.0% (95% CI [5.8, 10.9], n=450), below the certified worst case. In end-to-end query-only attacks where the agent itself writes poison, SMSR reduced success from 65.3% to 5.3% (n=150). Clean-query utility was 90% (Component 1 alone) and 85% (combined).

Conclusion: SMSR provides the first certified defense against multi-session memory poisoning in RAG agents, effectively neutralizing unsigned attacks and bounding authenticated ones while maintaining high clean-query utility.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) agents increasingly run with persistent memory that accumulates across user sessions. This creates a new attack surface: an adversary interacting only through normal channels can inject crafted memories that, once retrieved, steer the agent's responses for future users, without touching model weights or code. We call this Multi-Session Memory Poisoning (MSMP) and show that no existing defence certifies against it; static-corpus defences (RobustRAG, ReliabilityRAG) assume a fixed knowledge base, and heuristic filters are bypassed by fluent enterprise-style text. We present Signed Memory with Smoothed Retrieval (SMSR), the first defence with a certified robustness bound for this setting. Component 1 adds HMAC-SHA256 provenance at write time, blocking unsigned injection. Component 2 applies randomised memory ablation with verdict-based majority voting at query time, bounding the influence of authenticated adversaries. We prove that no provenance-free retrieval-time filter can certify against adaptive injection, derive a hypergeometric certificate for Component 2, and formalise the Consistent Minority Effect, whereby a consistent adversarial answer wins string-based voting as a numerical minority while verdict-based voting removes it. Across 15 enterprise scenarios (3,150 repeated trials), Component 1 cuts attack success from 93-100% to 0% for all unsigned variants. For an authenticated adversary with a single injection, Component 2 holds success to 8.0% (95% CI [5.8, 10.9], n=450), below the certified worst case. In an end-to-end query-only attack where the agent itself writes the poison rather than it being pre-seeded, SMSR reduces success from 65.3% to 5.3% (n=150, non-overlapping CIs) on a live agent stack. Clean-query utility is 90% (Component 1) and 85% (combined).

</details>


### [74] [DIG: Oracle-Guided Directed Input Generation for One-Day Vulnerabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13037)
*Andrew Bao,Haochen Zeng,Peng Chen,Stephen McCamant,Pen-Chung Yew*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: DIG leverages LLM-based patch analysis to synthesize oracles that explicitly encode vulnerability-triggering constraints, enabling a two-level PoC generation strategy (oracle-guided generator evolution + instrumented fuzzing). It triggers 80/138 CVEs, outperforming the best baseline by 40%, and discovers 6 new zero-days.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-day vulnerabilities are dangerous due to patch adoption delays, but generating PoC inputs to assess their impact is hard. Existing directed fuzzing lacks explicit constraint identification and solving; agentic approaches suffer from goal drift in long-horizon reasoning. Patches inherently encode the preconditions needed to trigger the vulnerability — exploiting this insight is the core motivation.

Method: DIG operates in two stages: (1) An LLM analyzes the patch diff to synthesize an oracle that explicitly captures necessary triggering conditions. (2) At the high level, an agent performs oracle-guided generator evolution — inferring and solving constraints to produce inputs satisfying the oracle. At the low level, DIG instruments the oracle into the target binary and uses branch-distance feedback to steer random mutation in directed fuzzing toward satisfying those conditions.

Result: On 138 real-world CVEs, DIG triggers 80 vulnerabilities — 40% more than the best baseline (57). It exclusively triggers 9 CVEs no other tool can. DIG is faster than the average tool in 92.9% of cases, achieving >100x speedup in 48.8% (max 3,664x). It also discovers 6 previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities in widely deployed libraries.

Conclusion: DIG demonstrates that patch-derived oracles bridge the gap between static patch understanding and dynamic constraint solving for PoC generation. The two-level approach (agentic constraint solving + instrumented fuzzing) synergistically overcomes limitations of both directed fuzzing and autonomous agents, establishing a new state-of-the-art for one-day PoC generation with zero-day discovery capabilities.

Abstract: One-day vulnerabilities pose significant risks due to delayed or incomplete patch adoption. Generating proof-of-concept (PoC) inputs is therefore essential for assessing real-world impact. The key challenge is identifying necessary constraints for triggering the vulnerability and solving them effectively. Existing directed fuzzing approaches prioritize inputs toward target locations, but neither explicitly identify necessary constraints nor solve them effectively, relying instead on target-distance feedback and random mutation. Agentic approaches show strong potential through code reasoning and structured input generation, but goal drift in long-horizon reasoning limits their effectiveness.
  DIG addresses this challenge by exploiting a key property of one-day vulnerabilities: patches often reveal necessary preconditions for triggering. DIG uses an LLM to analyze the patch and synthesize an oracle making these conditions explicit. The oracle supports effective PoC generation at two levels. At the high level, DIG performs oracle-guided generator evolution, where an agent infers and solves constraints to satisfy the oracle. At the low level, DIG instruments the oracle into the target program and uses branch-distance feedback to guide random mutation in directed fuzzing. Evaluation shows DIG outperforms 2 state-of-the-art agents and 10 fuzzers across 138 real-world CVEs. DIG triggers 80 vulnerabilities, surpassing prior results and outperforming the best baseline by 40% (57 vs. 80 CVEs). Notably, DIG exclusively triggers 9 vulnerabilities no existing technique can trigger. Compared to the average of other tools, DIG triggers vulnerabilities faster in 92.9% of cases, achieving over 100x speedup in 48.8% of cases, with a maximum speedup of 3,664x. Beyond one-day PoC generation, DIG uncovers 6 previously unknown vulnerabilities in widely deployed libraries, enabling zero-day discovery.

</details>


### [75] [PI-Hunter: Automated Red-Teaming for Exposing and Localizing Prompt Injections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12737)
*Pengfei He,Lesly Miculicich,Vishesh Sharma,Ash Fox,George Lee,Jiliang Tang,Tomas Pfister,Long T. Le*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: PI-Hunter is an automated agentic auditing framework that proactively exposes prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents by constructing source-aware test cases and iteratively evolving them through feedback-driven exploration to induce retrieval and revelation of embedded malicious instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents interacting with external tools face indirect prompt injection risks from untrusted sources. Current defenses focus on inference-time blocking, and red-teaming methods optimize for attack success rather than understanding how latent injections propagate. Developers lack visibility into vulnerability emergence, creating a need for proactive auditing.

Method: PI-Hunter constructs realistic source-aware test cases that simulate external environments with embedded malicious instructions. It uses an iterative, feedback-driven exploration process where an automated auditing agent evolves test cases to induce the target agent to retrieve and reveal latent injections, maximizing vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks, agent architectures, attack types, and defenses show PI-Hunter substantially outperforms strong automated red-teaming baselines in vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage, while remaining effective against existing prompt injection defenses.

Conclusion: PI-Hunter provides a systematic, proactive approach for auditing and exposing prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents, offering developers better visibility into latent risks and demonstrating superior performance over existing red-teaming methods.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly evolving into agentic systems that interact with external tools and environments, introducing new security risks such as indirect prompt injection attacks through untrusted external sources. Existing defenses mainly focus on blocking malicious content at inference time, and current red-teaming methods primarily optimize attack success. As a result, developers have limited visibility into how latent prompt injections emerge and propagate through agents. We propose PI-Hunter, an automated agentic auditing framework for proactive vulnerability exposure in LLM agents. PI-Hunter constructs realistic source-aware test cases and iteratively evolves them through feedback-driven exploration to induce agents to retrieve and reveal latent malicious instructions embedded within external environments. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks, agent architectures, attacks, and defenses demonstrate that PI-Hunter substantially improves vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage over strong automated red-teaming baselines, while remaining effective under existing prompt injection defenses.

</details>


### [76] [Semantic Identification of IoT Devices from Behavioral Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12793)
*Samuel Witt,Hassan Habibi Gharakheili*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes IoT device identification using semantic matching of Manufacturer Usage Description (MUD) profile ACEs rather than raw traffic patterns. Using 28 MUD profiles with 1,023 ACEs, the authors construct semantic embeddings, analyze their geometry, and demonstrate that semantic ACE matching outperforms exact matching when runtime traffic overlaps sparsely with canonical profiles, validated on 800K+ real IoT flows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing IoT device identification methods rely on low-level packet/flow features whose traffic patterns are brittle across deployments, software versions, and user interactions. MUD profiles offer a standardized, policy-level behavioral description, but exact ACE matching fails under sparse or absent overlap with canonical profiles at runtime. A semantically-aware matching approach is needed to maintain identification robustness under realistic runtime variability.

Method: (1) Construct ACE-level semantic representations by encoding compact behavioral text (protocol, endpoint, direction, port semantics) from 28 MUD profiles containing 1,023 ACE instances; analyze geometric properties and apply whitening calibration. (2) Evaluate semantic vs. exact ACE matching under controlled variations: unseen ACEs, drifted hostnames, and partial runtime observation. (3) Validate on real IoT traffic traces (>800K flows), comparing exact overlap against semantic ACE matching for device identification, especially under early-stage and sparse-overlap conditions.

Result: ACE-level embeddings preserve device behavioral distinctions better than whole-profile embeddings and benefit from whitening. Exact ACE matching performs well under high canonical overlap but degrades sharply with sparse/missing overlap. Semantic ACE matching retains useful identification evidence across all tested runtime variations. On real traffic, semantic matching provides stronger identification during early observation, frequently retains the correct device among top-ranked candidates, and remains effective under sparse-overlap conditions.

Conclusion: Semantic ACE matching on MUD profiles provides a robust complement to exact matching for IoT device identification, particularly valuable when runtime traffic exhibits sparse or incomplete overlap with canonical device profiles. The approach leverages standardized MUD semantics to maintain identification fidelity where traditional exact-match methods fail.

Abstract: Accurate identification of IoT devices is important for security management and policy enforcement. Existing approaches typically learn device signatures from packets or flow records. These methods operate on low-level communication observations whose traffic patterns may vary across deployments, software versions, and user interactions. This paper studies device identification using Manufacturer Usage Description (MUD) profiles. MUD profiles describe device behavior using Access Control Entries (ACEs), where each ACE represents a behavioral primitive consisting of protocol, endpoint, direction, and port semantics derived from device communication policy. Our contributions are threefold. First, using 28 publicly available MUD profiles containing 1,023 ACE instances, we construct ACE-level semantic representations from compact behavioral text and analyze their geometric properties. ACE-level representations preserve device-level behavioral distinctions more effectively than whole-profile embeddings and remain effective after whitening calibration. Second, we evaluate semantic ACE matching under controlled runtime variations, including unseen ACEs, drifted hostnames, and partial runtime observation. Exact ACE matching performs well when the overlap with the canonical MUD profile remains high, but degrades sharply when the overlap becomes sparse or disappears. In contrast, semantic ACE matching preserves useful identification evidence across these conditions. Third, we evaluate the same approach on real IoT traffic traces comprising more than 800,000 observed flows. Exact overlap remains the strongest signal when stable overlap exists, while semantic ACE matching provides stronger identification evidence during the early stages of observation, frequently retains the correct device among the highest-ranked candidates, and remains effective under sparse-overlap runtime traffic.

</details>


### [77] [A Privacy-Preserving Framework Using Remote Data Science for Inter-Institutional Student Retention Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12845)
*John Fields,K M Sajjadul Islam,Ruchitha Thota,Victor Chen,Praveen Madiraju*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A study demonstrating privacy-preserving machine learning for inter-institutional student retention prediction using PySyft's remote data science framework, achieving consistent F1 scores (~0.69) across three universities while maintaining FERPA compliance, and introducing Data-Type-Aware Templates for synthetic data generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable collaborative predictive modeling of student retention across multiple institutions without exposing sensitive student data, addressing FERPA constraints that typically prevent direct data sharing between universities.

Method: A semi-air-gapped remote data science (RDS) architecture using PySyft with high-side and low-side servers. Three synthetic data generation approaches were evaluated on historical data (N=720) from a private university. Introduced Data-Type-Aware Templates, a novel synthetic data method prioritizing privacy over distributional fidelity. Validated through actual inter-institutional collaboration among three universities.

Result: Consistent classification performance across institutions with Macro F1 scores ranging from 0.690 to 0.695, demonstrating that RDS-based PPML achieves reliable predictive accuracy while preserving strict FERPA compliance.

Conclusion: RDS-based PPML is technically feasible for educational settings and offers a practical alternative to federated learning, particularly suited for small-scale inter-institutional collaborations where data privacy regulations would otherwise preclude joint modeling efforts.

Abstract: This study explores privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML) techniques using the PySyft platform to enable collaborative prediction of student retention between institutions. We developed a remote data science (RDS) framework with a semi-air-gapped architecture consisting of high-side and low-side servers, allowing researchers from three universities to build predictive models on sensitive student data without direct data access. Using historical data from a small private university (N=720), we evaluated three synthetic data generation approaches and validated the framework through inter-institutional collaboration. The results demonstrate consistent classification performance across institutions (Macro F1: 0.690--0.695) while maintaining strict Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) compliance. We also propose Data-Type-Aware Templates, a novel synthetic data method that prioritizes privacy over distributional fidelity. Our findings confirm that RDS-based PPML is technically feasible for educational settings and offers a practical alternative to federated learning for small-scale inter-institutional collaborations. The code is available at https://github.com/jtfields/NAIRR240195-Privacy-Preserving-Machine-Learning.

</details>


### [78] [LNTest: A Testbed for Evaluating Bitcoin Lightning Network-Based Botnets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12887)
*Thomas Bakaysa,Ahmet Kurt,Abdul-Salem Beibitkhan,Jesus Maria Romo Diaz de Leon,Tag Kalat,Joshua Kramer,Estela Rodriguez,Abraham Watkins,Abdullah Aydeger*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LNTest is an open-source testbed for evaluating Lightning Network-based botnet topologies, revealing that the D-LNBot autonomous formation creates fragile clustered chains, command propagation scales linearly (Θ(n)) rather than sublinearly, and overlay topology critically determines resilience to takedown strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior LN-based botnet designs (LNBot and D-LNBot) were only simulated proofs-of-concept, leaving unanswered questions about real-world topology formation behavior, accurate propagation complexity, and resilience to different takedown strategies.

Method: LNTest: a reusable, containerized testbed using Core Lightning nodes over a shared Bitcoin Core regtest chain, supporting three overlay topology modes (deterministic chain, autonomous peer discovery, and user-supplied graphs). Experiments compare three topology types under command propagation benchmarks and node-removal takedown strategies (targeted vs. random).

Result: (1) D-LNBot's autonomous formation yields a clustered chain with bridging nodes, not a uniform chain; removal of bridges fragments the network. (2) Command propagation is Θ(n), not O(m log n), with no benefit from higher neighbor degree. (3) Uniform-degree chains resist targeted removal but fail under random failure; scale-free topologies show the opposite; the autonomous clustered chain is fragile under both.

Conclusion: LNTest enables reproducible, empirical evaluation of LN-based botnet behaviors under realistic conditions, demonstrating that actual topology formation and propagation costs differ significantly from prior theoretical claims, and that topology choice governs takedown resilience. The testbed is released open-source to support further defensive research.

Abstract: Bitcoin's Lightning Network (LN) can be exploited as a covert, low-cost command-and-control (C&C) channel for botnets, as demonstrated by the LNBot and D-LNBot designs. However, both remain proof-of-concept prototypes evaluated only through simulation, leaving key questions about real-world topology formation, propagation complexity, and resilience to takedowns unanswered. We present LNTest, the first reusable testbed for LN-based botnets, built from Core Lightning nodes containerized with Docker over a shared Bitcoin Core regtest chain. LNTest supports three overlay topology modes (a deterministic chain, autonomous peer discovery, and user-supplied graphs), enabling controlled experiments across different botnet structures. Using LNTest, we report three main findings. First, D-LNBot's autonomous formation protocol does not produce the uniform chain from its design; instead, it creates a clustered chain in which cliques are linked by bridge nodes whose removal fragments the network. Second, command propagation scales linearly with botnet size ($Θ(n)$), not the $O(m \log n)$ previously claimed, and gains nothing from higher neighbor connectivity. Third, the overlay topology determines the effectiveness of takedown strategies: uniform-degree chains resist targeted removal but fragment under random failure, scale-free topologies show the opposite pattern, and the autonomous clustered chain is fragile under both, making it the most vulnerable of the three. LNTest is released as open source, with a script that reproduces all our experiments, to support reproducible research on LN-based botnet defenses.

</details>


### [79] [Who Pays the Price? Stakeholder-Centric Prompt Injection Benchmarking for Real-world Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13385)
*Zihao Wang,Yiming Li,Yutong Wu,Zheyu Liu,Kangjie Chen,Fok Kar Wai,Pin-Yu Chen,Vrizlynn L. L. Thing,Bo Li,Dacheng Tao,Tianwei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Introduces StakeBench, a stakeholder-centric benchmark for evaluating prompt-injection attacks on LLM-based web agents. Unlike prior attack-centric evaluations, it categorizes harm by affected entities (user, seller, platform) and identifies distinct failure modes: stealthy parasitism, misaligned disruption, and compounded failure. Results show no current agent reliably resists any attack objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt-injection security benchmarks for LLM web agents are attack-centric, focusing only on technical feasibility while ignoring the asymmetric, victim-dependent distribution of harms. A single exploit can affect different stakeholders differently, and the same attack pattern varies in effectiveness by target. This gap necessitates stakeholder-aware evaluation.

Method: Proposes StakeBench, a stakeholder-centric benchmark that: (1) distinguishes affected entities (user, seller, platform); (2) decomposes attacks into concrete objectives; (3) evaluates using complementary outcome-level and process-level metrics. The framework captures nuanced harm attribution and categorizes failure into three modes: stealthy parasitism, misaligned disruption, and compounded failure.

Result: No single attack objective is reliably resisted by current LLM-based web agents. Failures distribute across three qualitatively distinct modes: stealthy parasitism (attack succeeds without disrupting the user's task), misaligned disruption (task disrupted without attack success), and compounded failure (both adversarial objective and task integrity simultaneously violated). These patterns are missed by conventional evaluation methods.

Conclusion: Conventional attack-centric benchmarks overlook the stakeholder-dependent nature of prompt-injection harm. Stakeholder-aware assessment is essential for real-world deployment of LLM-based agents, and StakeBench provides a systematic framework for this evaluation.

Abstract: Web agents driven by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world environments, where they operate over untrusted web content and execute actions with direct consequences. This makes them vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks, in which seemingly benign content embeds adversarial instructions that manipulate agent behaviour. Existing security benchmarks adopt an \textit{attack-centric} perspective, focusing on the technical feasibility of injections while overlooking the nuanced distribution of resulting harms. In practice, however, prompt-injection risk is victim-dependent: a single exploit can produce asymmetric consequences for different stakeholders, and the same attack pattern may exhibit substantially different effectiveness depending on whom it targets. To capture these properties, we introduce \textbf{\sysname}, a \textit{stakeholder-centric} benchmark to systematically categorize and attribute harm in real-world web agent systems. It distinguishes between affected entities (e.g., user, seller, platform), decomposes the attacks into concrete objectives, and evaluates each case with complementary outcome- and process-level metrics. Our results reveal substantial and heterogeneous vulnerabilities: not a single attack objective is reliably resisted by current agents, and failures distribute across qualitatively distinct modes ranging from \emph{stealthy parasitism} (attack succeeds without disrupting the user's delegated task) to \emph{misaligned disruption} (task disrupted without attack success) and \emph{compounded failure} (both adversarial objective and task integrity simultaneously violated). These patterns are missed by conventional evaluation, highlighting the need for stakeholder-aware assessment of LLM-based agents in real-world deployments. Benchmark is available at https://github.com/StakeBench/SBC.

</details>


### [80] [MAStrike: Shapley-Guided Collusive Red-Teaming on Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12918)
*Chejian Xu,Zhaorun Chen,Jingyang Zhang,Freddy Lecue,Avni Kothari,Sarah Tan,Wenbo Guo,Bo Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: MAStrike is a closed-loop red-teaming framework for hierarchical multi-agent systems that uses agent-level Shapley values to identify vulnerable coalitions and generate coordinated, role-aware adversarial attacks, outperforming heuristic baselines across domains like finance, software engineering, and CRM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing red-teaming approaches for multi-agent systems rely on heuristic target selection and perturb isolated message streams, failing to identify which agents are most responsible for system safety or how compromised agents coordinate to bypass defenses. The attack surface in hierarchical MAS is distributed across specialized agents, making collusive adversarial behaviors like privilege escalation and cross-agent collusion a critical but underexplored threat.

Method: MAStrike introduces: (1) the first agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS to quantify each agent's marginal contribution to system robustness under task-specific distributions; (2) coalition identification guided by Shapley attributions to discover vulnerable agent groups; (3) structured causal diagnosis that iteratively refines attacks by attributing failures to uncompromised agents blocking adversarial attempts; and (4) a comprehensive benchmark with controllable hierarchical topologies across finance, software engineering, and CRM domains.

Result: MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines across MAS built on multiple frontier models. The analysis reveals non-trivial Shapley value distributions and higher-order interaction structures among agents, exposing critical vulnerabilities and coordination patterns overlooked by single-agent or template-based methods.

Conclusion: The Shapley-based attribution approach effectively identifies safety-critical agents and vulnerable coalitions in hierarchical MAS, demonstrating that coordinated, role-aware red-teaming is essential for uncovering systemic vulnerabilities that isolated or heuristic methods miss.

Abstract: Hierarchical multi-agent systems (MAS) are rapidly being deployed in high-stakes workflows across domains such as finance and software engineering. In these systems, safety and security are inherently distributed across role-specialized agents, significantly expanding the attack surface, particularly under coordinated adversarial behaviors such as privilege escalation and cross-agent collusion. Existing red-teaming approaches for MAS remain limited: they rely on heuristic selection of target agents and perturb isolated message streams, leaving critical questions unanswered as which agents are most responsible for system safety, and how compromised agents can coordinate to bypass defenses. We propose MAStrike, a closed-loop framework for collusive red-teaming in hierarchical MAS. We propose the first agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS, quantifying each agent's marginal contribution to system robustness under task-specific distributions. GGuided by this attribution, MAStrike identifies vulnerable agent coalitions and generates coordinated, role-aware adversarial manipulations. These attacks are iteratively refined through structured causal diagnosis, attributing failure cases to uncompromised agents that block adversarial attempts. We further build a comprehensive MAS red-teaming benchmark and controllable environments spanning diverse hierarchical topologies and domains, including finance, software engineering, and CRM. Extensive experiments across MAS built on multiple frontier models show that MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines. Our analysis further uncovers non-trivial Shapley value distributions and higher-order interaction structures among agents, revealing critical vulnerabilities and coordination patterns that are overlooked by prior single-agent or template-based methods.

</details>


### [81] [ViPER: Vision-based Packing-Aware Encoder for Robust Malware Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12949)
*Fatima Qaiser,Bisma Tahir,Muhammad Abid Mughal,Nauman Shamim*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: ViPER is a vision-based malware detection system using a ViT backbone with dual-head architecture that jointly learns malware classification and packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism enables distinct decision boundaries for packed vs. unpacked binaries, addressing a key failure mode in visualization-based malware detection. It achieves 0.8521 balanced accuracy and 0.9949 packing detection AUC on 200,000 Windows PE byteplot images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visualization-based malware detectors fail on packed executables because packing produces high-entropy byteplot images that obscure structural patterns. Since packing is common in both benign and malicious software, packing state alone is not a reliable indicator. Current approaches lack a unified supervised framework that jointly handles packing detection and malware classification.

Method: ViPER uses a LoRA-adapted ViT-B/14 backbone with a dual-head architecture: one head for malware classification and one for packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism conditions malware predictions on the inferred packing state, creating separate decision boundaries. Training employs frequency-weighted losses with stratified sampling over joint class-packing strata to address packing label skew.

Result: On 200,000 Windows PE byteplot images, ViPER achieves: balanced accuracy 0.8521, ROC-AUC 0.9260, AUPR 0.9279 for malware detection; packing detection AUC of 0.9949. It outperforms representative state-of-the-art baselines across all primary metrics.

Conclusion: ViPER demonstrates that jointly modeling packing state and malware class within a unified vision-based framework significantly improves robustness against packed binaries, a long-standing vulnerability in visualization-based malware detection pipelines.

Abstract: Visualization-based malware detection maps raw binary bytes to grayscale images and applies learned visual classifiers, providing an evasion-resistant and disassembly-free alternative to conventional analysis pipelines. However, executable packing remains a critical failure mode: packed binaries produce high-entropy images that obscure the structural patterns these models rely on. Because packing is also prevalent in benign software (e.g., for compression or copy protection), packing state alone is not a reliable indicator of maliciousness, and existing approaches do not address this challenge within a unified supervised framework. We present ViPER, a Vision-based Packing-Aware Encoder for Robust malware detection. ViPER builds on a LoRA-adapted ViT-B/14 backbone with a dual-head architecture that jointly learns malware classification and packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism conditions malware predictions on the inferred packing state, enabling distinct decision boundaries for packed and unpacked inputs. To address packing label skew during training, we employ frequency-weighted losses with stratified sampling over joint class-packing strata. Evaluated on 200,000 Windows PE byteplot images, ViPER achieves a balanced accuracy of 0.8521, ROC-AUC of 0.9260, and AUPR of 0.9279, outperforming representative state-of-the-art baselines across all primary metrics, while attaining a packing detection AUC of 0.9949.

</details>


### [82] [SoK: The Constant Time Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13000)
*Billy Bob Brumley*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper systematizes constant-time programming models, identifies gaps between theoretical protections and practical specifications, and develops an offensive methodology to discover timing vulnerabilities. Applying it reveals a private key loading leak in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL, where BoringSSL paradoxically exhibits a far stronger side-channel signal despite its stricter threat model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constant-time programming is the primary defense against timing side-channel attacks on cryptographic implementations, yet the definition of "constant time" is inconsistent across academia and industry. This ambiguity creates security gaps where implementations believed to be protected may still leak sensitive information through timing channels.

Method: The authors systematize existing constant-time models and their evolution, identify recurring mismatches between model protections and specification assumptions, and synthesize an offensive methodology for finding timing leaks that arise outside the cryptographic primitive boundary. They then apply this methodology to real-world libraries (OpenSSL and BoringSSL).

Result: A specification-level timing vulnerability was discovered in the private key loading process. The leak was confirmed in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL. Counterintuitively, BoringSSL's per-observation leakage signal is orders of magnitude stronger than OpenSSL's, despite BoringSSL having an explicitly stricter threat model.

Conclusion: Constant-time guarantees must extend beyond cryptographic primitives to encompass surrounding infrastructure such as key loading. The gap between model assumptions and real-world specifications represents a significant, under-explored attack surface. Stricter threat models do not automatically translate to better protection and may paradoxically amplify leakage.

Abstract: Constant time programming patterns is the primary defense against timing attacks on cryptographic implementations, yet what "constant time" means varies across academia and industry. This work systematizes constant time models and their evolution, identifies a recurring gap between what models protect and what specifications assume, and distills an offensive methodology for discovering timing vulnerabilities that originate outside the cryptographic primitive boundary. Applying this methodology, we locate a specification-level vulnerability related to private key loading, and confirm the leak in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL. Counterintuitively, BoringSSL's per-observation signal is several orders of magnitude stronger than OpenSSL's, despite an explicitly stricter threat model.

</details>


### [83] [The Emergence of Autonomous Penetration Capabilities in Large Language Model-Powered AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13079)
*Jiaqi Luo,Jiarun Dai,Zhile Chen,Jia Xu,Weibing Wang,Yawen Duan,Brian Tse,Geng Hong,Xudong Pan,Yuan Zhang,Min Yang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper constructs a new evaluation framework for autonomous penetration testing by LLM-powered AI agents, featuring realistic multi-service target servers (Tier 1 and Tier 2, 300 total) and general-purpose agent scaffolding without target-specific prior knowledge. Evaluating 19 open-weight and proprietary LLMs reveals penetration success rates of 10.7%–69.3%, with capability scaling alongside general model improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of autonomous penetration capabilities in AI systems suffer from opaque methodologies, overly simplistic or unrealistic scenarios, and excessive provision of prior knowledge or task-specific guidance. These shortcomings prevent accurate assessment of whether frontier AI systems can autonomously execute penetration — a core subtask within the broader red-line scenario of autonomous cyberattacks causing real-world harm.

Method: The authors construct a two-component evaluation framework: (1) Target servers with two difficulty tiers — Tier 1 (one secure service alongside a vulnerable service) and Tier 2 (three secure services), totaling 300 target servers. (2) A general-purpose agent scaffolding equipped with standard cybersecurity tools, deliberately withholding any target-specific prior knowledge. They evaluate 19 open-weight and proprietary LLMs under this framework.

Result: Current LLMs achieve autonomous penetration success rates ranging from 10.7% to 69.3% across the evaluated models. Autonomous penetration capability shows a positive correlation with overall model capability improvements.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that modern LLMs possess non-trivial autonomous penetration capabilities, with top models achieving nearly 70% success rates. The strong correlation between general model capability and penetration performance suggests that as frontier models continue to improve, the risk of crossing the autonomous cyberattack red line will increase, underscoring the need for robust evaluation frameworks and safety measures.

Abstract: Nowadays, the autonomous execution of cyberattacks capable of causing substantial real-world harm is widely regarded as one of the critical red lines that frontier AI systems must not cross. Within this broader red-line scenario, autonomous penetration represents a core enabling capability and subtask: the ability of LLM-powered AI systems to independently conduct adversarial operations against a target server without human intervention, identify and exploit vulnerabilities, and obtain unauthorized access or control. A growing body of work has sought to assess the autonomous penetration capabilities of AI systems. However, existing evaluations often employ opaque methodologies, rely on unrealistic or overly simplified penetration-testing scenarios, or provide LLMs with excessive prior knowledge and task-specific guidance, and cannot accurately capture the extent to which modern AI systems can autonomously perform this core capability within broader high-impact cyberattack scenarios.
  To address these limitations, we construct a new autonomous penetration evaluation framework consisting of two components: target servers and agent scaffolding. Specifically, on the target-server side, we design two levels of target environments based on the number of secure services without known vulnerabilities deployed alongside a vulnerable service: Tier~1 (one secure service) and Tier~2 (three secure services), resulting in a total of 300 target servers. Meanwhile, the agent scaffolding adopts a general-purpose agent architecture equipped with a set of general-purpose cybersecurity tools, without any target-specific prior knowledge. We evaluate 19 open-weight and proprietary LLMs, and find that current models achieve penetration success rates ranging from 10.7% to 69.3%. Moreover, we observe that autonomous penetration capability continues to improve alongside advances in overall model capability.

</details>


### [84] [The Invisible Ink of the Android Malware World: A Longitudinal Study on the Usage of Covert Communication Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13107)
*Zeya Umayya,Manan Aggarwal,Manan Chugh,Mann Nariya,Yogesh Kaushik,Sambuddho Chakravarty*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: First longitudinal study of covert channel (CC) usage in Android malware (2009–2025), analyzing 3.5M APKs. Reveals exponential growth from 0.30% to 50% CC adoption, identifies 288K malicious APKs across 511 families using CCs, and validates 59 CC-linked IPs across 17 countries via combined static/dynamic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy tools like proxies, VPNs, and Tor are dual-use: essential for circumventing censorship but also exploited by malware to hide C2 communications. Despite growing malware prevalence, no longitudinal study had systematically analyzed how Android malware leverages covert channels for evasion.

Method: A multistage pipeline combining static analysis (rule-based validation) and dynamic analysis (system- and network-level feature extraction). Applied to a 16-year corpus of 3.5M Android malware samples (2009–July 2025). Static rules identify CC usage; dynamic analysis validates by observing actual IP contacts and CC behavior.

Result: Static analysis flagged 288K APKs using CCs across 511 malware families; CC prevalence grew from 0.30% (2012) to 50% (2025). Dynamic analysis revealed 19,308 unique IPs across 85 countries, with 59 IPs in 17 countries explicitly confirmed as CC endpoints. Some malware families use multiple CCs; one family switched CCs 40 times between 2019–2025.

Conclusion: Covert channel usage in Android malware has become pervasive and increasingly sophisticated, evolving toward multi-CC strategies and frequent rotation. This highlights an urgent need for detection mechanisms that account for CC-based evasion at both the network and application layers.

Abstract: Proxies, VPNs and Tor have long helped the privacy community and users in censored regions to fight censorship. However, the same tools can be maliciously exploited by malware and botnets to conceal their communication to external command and control servers. Despite being a critical concern fueled by the proliferation of malware based attacks, no longitudinal studies have analyzed how malware applications use covert channels (CC) to evade detection.
  We fill this gap by performing the first study of the usage of covert channels in the Android malware ecosystem. To that end, we develop a multistage pipeline that combines static and dynamic analysis to investigate both system and network-level features. We applied this pipeline on a corpus of 3.5M Android malware spanning 2009 to July 2025. Our carefully crafted static validation rules uncovered 288K APKs that used CCs spanning 511 malware families and CC usage growing exponentially from 0.30\% (2012) to 50\% (2025). Overall, in dynamic analysis, we identified 19,308 unique IP addresses being contacted in 85 countries, out of which we were able to explicitly validate the presence of CCs for 59 IP addresses across 17 countries. Further, we performed a longitudinal dataset study spanning over 16 years for CC based malware and found that CC usage has evolved, \textit{e.g.,} some malware adopted by using more than one CCs; others switched between them periodically (one family switched CC usage 40 times from 2019 to 2025).

</details>


### [85] [An Assessment Framework for Application-Level Cryptographic Agility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13425)
*Navaneeth Rameshan,Gregoire Messmer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Introduces a seven-dimension framework to systematically assess cryptographic agility in APIs, evaluates six major cryptographic APIs against it, and identifies three critical, pervasive gaps that collectively explain why migration to post-quantum cryptography remains a significant software engineering challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The post-quantum cryptographic transition demands wholesale algorithm replacement, but existing APIs lack systematic agility assessment. The term "cryptographic agility" conflates multiple distinct capabilities (algorithm replacement, policy-driven selection, implementation substitution), and without structured decomposition, neither evaluation nor development of agile APIs is possible.

Method: A component-based assessment framework with seven orthogonal dimensions: three coupling dimensions (measuring application knowledge of algorithms/providers), one cross-cutting decoupling mechanism, one governance authority dimension, and two agility enablers (measuring actual migration capability). The framework produces non-hierarchical, non-linear profiles. Six APIs are evaluated: PKCS#11, OpenSSL 3.0, JCA, Google Tink, AWS KMS, and HashiCorp Vault Transit.

Result: Three pervasive, independent gaps were identified across all evaluated APIs: (1) no system supports intent-based key creation, (2) none provides policy-driven algorithm selection distinct from access control, and (3) none offers first-class operations for algorithm transformation of existing keys. Each gap alone is sufficient to block agile migration.

Conclusion: Despite decades of cryptographic API evolution, the post-quantum transition remains an unresolved software engineering problem because no current API adequately addresses the fundamental dimensions of cryptographic agility. The three identified gaps represent concrete, actionable targets for next-generation API design.

Abstract: The impending post-quantum transition to new cryptography will require complete replacement of algorithms within all software. The cryptographic APIs used today make this transition challenging because they were not designed with agility as a concern. There is no method for systematically assessing cryptographic agility as an overall ability. In addition to this, the term itself refers to multiple independent capabilities. Specifically, it includes replacing algorithms, selecting by policy, and substituting implementations. This lack of structured decomposition limits both the evaluation of systems and the development of cryptographically agile APIs.
  We introduce a component-based assessment framework that characterizes application-level cryptographic agility along seven orthogonal dimensions: three coupling dimensions that measure what the application code knows about algorithms and providers, a cross-cutting decoupling mechanism, a governance authority dimension, and two agility enablers that measure actual migration capability. The framework is non-linear and captures non-hierarchical profiles: a system may achieve high operation decoupling yet low creation decoupling, or strong versioning without externalized configuration.
  We evaluate six representative APIs (PKCS#11, OpenSSL~3.0, JCA, Google Tink, AWS KMS, and HashiCorp Vault Transit) against the framework, revealing three pervasive and independent gaps: no system supports intent-based key creation, none provides policy-driven algorithm selection (as distinct from access control), and none offers dedicated/first-class operations for algorithm transformation of existing keys. These gaps are individually sufficient to prevent agile migration, explaining why the post-quantum transition remains a software engineering problem despite decades of API progress.

</details>


### [86] [Intent-Based Cryptographic API Design for Cryptographic Agility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13445)
*Navaneeth Rameshan,Gregoire Messmer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Current cryptographic APIs are algorithm-specific and lack support for policy-driven selection and key migration, making post-quantum transitions difficult. This paper proposes design principles for cryptographically agile APIs — abstraction, stability, temporal flexibility, separation, and extensibility — and demonstrates their implementation via Protocol Buffers patterns, including an intent vocabulary based on scopes, abstract policy APIs, stable key identifiers, and key evolution operations (rotation, transformation, migration), enabling algorithmic updates as operational processes without application code changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations migrating to post-quantum cryptography face a significant software engineering challenge: existing cryptographic APIs are tightly coupled to specific algorithms, lack policy-based algorithm selection, and provide no mechanisms for migrating existing keys to new algorithms. This makes large-scale cryptographic transitions across complex software portfolios extremely difficult.

Method: The authors identify five fundamental architectural characteristics required for cryptographic agility — Abstraction, Stability, Temporal Flexibility, Separation, and Extensibility — from which they derive concrete design principles. They then demonstrate how these principles can be realized through Protocol Buffers API design patterns, introducing: (1) an intent vocabulary based on scopes that decouples key creation from algorithm identities, enabling transparent algorithm substitution; (2) an abstract policy API for cryptographic governance that is agnostic to policy format; and (3) stable key identifiers with support for key evolution operations (rotation, transformation, migration) that preserve identity lineage and history.

Result: The proposed API design enables cryptographic transitions to become operational processes that do not require rewriting application code. Algorithm substitutions can occur transparently within defined scopes, keys can be migrated between algorithms and providers while tracking their evolution history, and governance policies can be enforced without prescribing specific policy formats.

Conclusion: Cryptographic agility is fundamentally a software engineering problem, not merely a cryptographic one. By embedding the five architectural characteristics into API design, organizations can build systems where post-quantum cryptographic transitions are manageable operational tasks rather than costly, error-prone redevelopment efforts.

Abstract: As organizations move toward post-quantum cryptography, they face the major challenge of updating cryptographic algorithms across large, complex software portfolios. However, most cryptographic APIs in use today were designed around specific algorithms. These APIs expect explicit use of specific algorithms, provide little or no support for policy-based algorithm selection, and offer no straightforward way to migrate existing keys to newer algorithms. This makes the transition to post-quantum cryptography challenging. The companion assessment framework identifies the barriers to cryptographic agility and explains why algorithm transition is largely a software engineering problem.
  To address the limitations of current cryptographic APIs, we identify the principles necessary to design a cryptographically agile API. The design principles are derived from five fundamental architectural characteristics (Abstraction, Stability, Temporal Flexibility, Separation, and Extensibility). We also show how the design principles can be implemented using several examples of Protocol Buffers API design patterns. In particular, we present an intent vocabulary that is based on scopes which allows for decoupling key creation from algorithm identities. It also supports transparent substitutions of algorithms in the applicable scope. Cryptographic governance is enabled by an abstract policy API that does not prescribe the policy format. Keys are represented by stable identifiers and support key evolution operations (rotation, transformation, migration), facilitating migration between algorithms and providers while tracking both the original key identity and its evolution history. With this approach, updating cryptography becomes an operational process without the need to rewrite application code.

</details>


### [87] [Differentially Private Hierarchical Heavy Hitters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13563)
*Ari Biswas,Graham Cormode,Yaron Kanza,Divesh Srivastava,Zhengyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper studies the problem of releasing Hierarchical Heavy Hitters (HHH) under differential privacy, in both streaming and non-streaming settings. Key findings: in the non-streaming case, relative error for residual count estimation is independent of hierarchy height and number of heavy hitters; in the streaming case, absolute frequency estimation error is independent of available space, despite high global sensitivity of streaming approximation functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While HHH has been extensively studied in data streams, the problem of privately releasing HHH when the underlying data is sensitive remains unexplored. This gap motivates the authors to investigate differentially private mechanisms for HHH release.

Method: The authors analyze differential privacy mechanisms for HHH in two settings: (1) a non-streaming (offline) setting, where they bound the relative error for residual count estimation; (2) a streaming setting, where they address the challenge of high global sensitivity in streaming approximation functions and bound the absolute error for frequency estimation.

Result: In the non-streaming setting, the relative error in estimating residual counts for any prefix is independent of the hierarchy height and the number of heavy hitters. In the streaming setting, despite exponential (linear in space) global sensitivity of streaming approximations, the absolute error for frequency estimation remains independent of available space.

Conclusion: Differentially private HHH release is feasible in both streaming and non-streaming settings, with error guarantees that are surprisingly robust to key parameters (hierarchy height, number of heavy hitters, and available space), overcoming the sensitivity challenges inherent to streaming approximations.

Abstract: The task of finding _Hierarchical_ Heavy Hitters (HHH) was introduced by Cormode et al. [VLDB 2003] as a generalisation of the heavy hitter problem. While finding HHH in data streams has been studied extensively, the question of releasing HHH when the underlying data is private remains unexplored. In this paper, we study differentially private HHH release in both the streaming and non-streaming setting. In the non-streaming setting, we show the surprising result that the relative error in estimating the residual count for any prefix is independent of the height of the hierarchy and the number of heavy hitters in the stream. Meanwhile, in the streaming setting, although the exact version of HHH has low global sensitivity (as counting queries are 1-sensitive), the approximation functions due to streaming have high global sensitivity, linear in the available space. Despite this obstacle, we show that the absolute error for estimating frequencies in the steaming setting is independent of the available space.

</details>


### [88] [Beyond the IT Checklist: Engineering a Reasonable Standard of Care for Cyber Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13612)
*Matthew E. Jablonski,Linton Wells,Kathryn B. Laskey,F. Brett Berlin*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: U.S. cyber policy for critical infrastructure over-relies on IT-centric administrative compliance rather than addressing physics-based kinetic risks. Analysis of 292 policy documents (2000-2025) reveals gaps concentrated in withstand/recovery phases, where delegated standards fail to align with cyber-physical hazards. The paper proposes hazard-specific traceability, structured assurance cases, and resiliency engineering as a modernized standard of care, paired with economic incentives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current U.S. cyber policy treats documentation of IT controls as a proxy for safety in the built environment, which is fundamentally inadequate for cyber-physical systems (CPS) where software failures can cause physical/kinetic harm (e.g., infrastructure damage, injury, loss of life). The authors aim to empirically diagnose the gap between policy obligations and the engineering demands of CPS resilience.

Method: The authors construct a corpus of 292 U.S. critical infrastructure policy documents spanning 2000–2025 and systematically code them against the NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2 resilience lifecycle framework (Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt). This produces obligation maps showing how "reasonable care" is distributed across lifecycle phases and what types of compliance mechanisms (administrative vs. engineering) are mandated.

Result: Three major disconnects were identified: (1) delegated standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-53) are miscalibrated for physics-based hazards; (2) recovery is operationalized as notification/paperwork rather than engineered navigation back to safe states; (3) adaptation requirements vary unevenly across sectors with no coherent rationale. Obligations concentrate heavily in the Anticipate phase, while Withstand and Recover phases rely on delegated IT-focused control catalogs.

Conclusion: A modernized standard of care is needed, grounded in hazard-specific traceability, structured assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering. Federal policy should pair these engineering obligations with targeted economic incentives so that resilient architectures become viable business decisions rather than unfunded mandates.

Abstract: Current U.S. cyber policy, centered on security, often treats documentation of controls and incident reports as a proxy for safety in the built environment. This paper argues that such an approach is inadequate for cyber-physical systems, where digital failures can produce kinetic harm. We construct and code a corpus of critical infrastructure policy documents (N=292, 2000-2025) to examine how "reasonable care" is operationalized across the NIST SP 800-160 Vol.~2 resilience lifecycle. The resulting maps show that obligations are concentrated in the Anticipate phase and emphasize administrative compliance, while Withstand and Recover phases rely heavily on delegated references to IT-focused control catalogs that are poorly aligned with physics-based hazards. We identify three major disconnects: miscalibrated delegated standards, recovery defined as notification rather than engineered navigation, and uneven adaptation requirements across sectors. We then propose a modernized standard of care anchored in hazard-specific traceability, structured assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering. Finally, we recommend that federal policy pair these engineering obligations with targeted incentives so that resilient architectures for critical infrastructure become a viable business decision rather than an unfunded expectation.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [89] [ToolSense: A Diagnostic Framework for Auditing Parametric Tool Knowledge in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12451)
*Ashutosh Hathidara,Sai Shruthi Sistla,Sebastian Schreiber,Sahil Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Parametric tool retrieval models perform well on standard benchmarks but exhibit a knowledge-retrieval dissociation when tested with ToolSense, a diagnostic framework that generates realistic, ambiguous queries and probing benchmarks, revealing up to 50–64 pp performance collapse and near-random factual understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard ToolBench retrieval benchmarks use verbose, fully-specified queries and constrained decoding, which mask whether parametric tool retrieval models genuinely understand tool semantics. The authors aim to diagnose this gap between retrieval performance and true tool comprehension.

Method: The authors introduce ToolSense, an open-source LLM-powered framework that automatically generates three diagnostic benchmarks from any tool catalog: (1) a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries at three ambiguity tiers, (2) an MCQ probing benchmark, and (3) a QA probing benchmark. They apply it to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluate five parametric model training configurations against embedding-based baselines.

Result: Several parametric configurations collapse by ~50–64 percentage points on RRB compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-model baseline. Additionally, some models score near-random on factual probes despite strong retrieval performance, demonstrating a knowledge-retrieval dissociation.

Conclusion: Current parametric tool retrieval models suffer from a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: they can retrieve tools effectively under constrained evaluation but lack genuine semantic understanding. ToolSense provides a rigorous diagnostic framework to expose these limitations, and the authors open-source both the framework and benchmarks to facilitate more robust evaluation.

Abstract: Large language models deployed as agents over large tool catalogs face a critical tool-retrieval bottleneck. As embedding-based retrieval approaches rely on compact encoders that may under-capture specialized tool semantics, parametric tool retrieval addresses this by encoding each tool as a virtual token appended to the LLM vocabulary, fine-tuned in two stages (memorization then retrieval SFT) to use the LLM as a retriever, achieving strong performance on standard ToolBench retrieval benchmarks. Yet these benchmarks use verbose, fully-specified queries, and their evaluation applies constrained decoding that restricts outputs to valid token paths, neither reveals whether the model actually understands its tools. We introduce \textbf{ToolSense}, an open-source LLM-powered diagnostic framework that takes any tool catalog as input and automatically generates three benchmarks: a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries at three ambiguity tiers, an MCQ probing benchmark, and a QA probing benchmark. Applying ToolSense to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluating five parametric model training configurations reveals a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: on RRB queries, several configurations collapse by ~50-64 percentage points compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-model baseline. Additionally, despite strong retrieval performance, some models score near-random on factual probes, suggesting a knowledge-retrieval dissociation. We open-source the ToolSense framework and the ToolBench diagnostic benchmarks at https://github.com/SAP/toolsense.

</details>


### [90] [Arbor: Tree Search as a Cognition Layer for Autonomous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12563)
*Neha Prakriya,Chaojun Hou,Zheng Gong,Huasha Zhao,Xi Zhao,Mou Li,Zhenyu Gu,Emad Barsoum*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Arbor is a multi-agent framework that uses structured tree search as a shared cognitive layer for autonomous optimization across large, stateful action spaces. Tested on full-stack LLM inference optimization, it achieves up to 193% throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor baselines, significantly outperforming single-agent approaches while maintaining stability and reproducibility across hardware generations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation, limiting their effectiveness in complex, stateful domains like full-stack LLM inference optimization — a problem that traditionally requires coordinated engineering teams across application, framework, compiler, kernel, and hardware layers. There is a need for an architecture that can maintain state, learn from failures, and coordinate multiple specialized agents over extended multi-day campaigns.

Method: Arbor employs a structured tree search as an explicit cognition layer, maintaining a search tree of scored hypotheses serving as shared working memory across agents. It uses a dual-agent checks-and-balances architecture: an Orchestrator agent drives optimization by delegating to Domain Specialists across the inference stack, while a Critic agent provides root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation. Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols). Failures are treated as diagnostic signals that reshape subsequent exploration, and prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution.

Result: Arbor achieves up to 193% improvement on the throughput-latency Pareto frontier over vendor-optimized baselines. In contrast, a single agent without the harness plateaus at +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. The method demonstrates hardware-agnostic generalization across multiple hardware platform generations, with run-to-run variance within 2 percentage points.

Conclusion: The structured tree search cognition layer, combined with the checks-and-balances multi-agent architecture, enables robust, reproducible, and highly effective autonomous optimization over extended campaigns. Arbor's decomposition of agent capabilities into hard and soft skills, along with failure-aware exploration, proves critical for tackling complex, stateful optimization problems like full-stack LLM inference.

Abstract: Arbor is a multi-agent framework that introduces structured tree search as a cognition layer for autonomous agents operating in large, stateful action spaces. Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation. Arbor instead maintains an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses that serves as the shared working memory across agents, evolving with every measurement, treating failures as diagnostic signal that reshapes subsequent exploration, and expanding as prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution.
  We validate Arbor on full-stack LLM inference optimization, a domain where achieving peak performance has historically required coordinated effort from engineering teams across the application, framework, compiler, kernel, and hardware stack. Arbor pairs an Orchestrator agent, which drives optimization by delegating to Domain Specialists across the inference stack, with a Critic agent that safeguards stability through root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation -- a checks-and-balances architecture where neither agent can unilaterally drive the system. Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols that determine how contributions compose), enabling fully autonomous multi-day campaigns. Arbor achieves up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines, while a single agent without the harness plateaus at +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. Arbor generalizes to multiple generations of hardware platform, and run-to-run variance is within 2 percentage points demonstrating that the method is hardware-agnostic and reproducible.

</details>


### [91] [Strategic Decision Support for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12587)
*Shayan Kiyani,Sima Noorani,George Pappas,Hamed Hassani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a framework for strategic decision support where AI agents are the primary actors, flipping the traditional human-centered decision support paradigm. It formulates an optimization problem that minimizes support usage while controlling a counterfactual missed-support error, derives an optimal threshold policy, and develops an online algorithm with calibration-on-the-fly that reliably reduces support calls across diverse agentic settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In modern agentic systems, AI agents act on behalf of users while humans and tools serve as support. This role reversal raises reliability concerns: agentic errors can be consequential, and agent behavior must align with human goals. Classical decision support principles—the cost–value tradeoff and uncertainty quantification—need to be revisited for this new paradigm where agents are central actors rather than human decision-makers.

Method: The authors frame strategic decision support for AI agents as an optimization problem minimizing support usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error (the probability that the agent acts alone when support would have materially improved output). At the population level, the optimal policy is shown to be a threshold rule on the value of support. They develop an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds a support-value score using randomized exploration to control missed-support error without distributional assumptions, plus a calibration-on-the-fly method to reduce unnecessary support calls online. The framework is instantiated across information gathering, human–AI collaboration, and tool use scenarios.

Result: Experiments across information gathering, human–AI collaboration, and tool use settings demonstrate that the proposed method reliably controls the target missed-support error while substantially reducing support usage in practice.

Conclusion: The classical decision support paradigm must be rethought for agentic AI systems where roles are reversed. The proposed optimization-based framework, with its threshold policy structure and adaptive online algorithm, provides a principled way to balance autonomy with reliability, ensuring agents seek support only when necessary while maintaining alignment with human goals.

Abstract: Traditionally, decision support studies how humans use machine learning models to make better decisions. In modern agentic systems, this division of roles is increasingly reversed: AI agents act on behalf of users, while humans and tools becomes support mechanisms around them. This role reversal brings reliability concerns to the forefront, since agentic errors can be consequential and agent behavior must remain aligned with human goals and constraints. Departing from the classical view of decision support, we revisit its two basic principles, the cost--value tradeoff of seeking support and the role of uncertainty quantification, in a setting where AI agents are the central actors. We propose a framework for strategic decision support for AI agents through an optimization problem that minimizes support usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error: the probability that the agent acts alone on instances where support would have materially improved its output. At the population level, we show that the optimal policy is a threshold rule on the value of support. Building on this structure, we develop an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds such a score and uses randomized exploration to control missed-support error without distributional assumptions. We further introduce a calibration-on-the-fly method that reduces unnecessary support calls online. We instantiate this framework across diverse scenarios, including information gathering, human--AI collaboration, and tool use, showing how each can be modeled through the same strategic decision-support lens. Experiments across these settings show that our method reliably controls the target error while substantially reducing support usage in practice.

</details>


### [92] [Pythagoras-Prover: Advancing Efficient Formal Proving via Augmented Lean Formalisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12594)
*Joshua Ong Jun Leang,Zheng Zhao,Mihaela Cătălina Stoian,Qiyuan Xu,Haonan Li,Wenda Li,Shay B. Cohen,Eleonora Giunchiglia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Pythagoras-Prover introduces compute-efficient open-source Lean theorem provers (4B, 32B autoregressive; 4B diffusion-based) that achieve state-of-the-art formal proof performance through curriculum SFT, dynamic proof filtering, and a novel data augmentation method (ALF). The 4B model surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B on MiniF2F with ~167x fewer parameters, while the 32B model achieves 93.0% on MiniF2F and solves 93/672 PutnamBench problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern Lean provers demand substantial training and inference compute due to scarce verified proof data and long reasoning traces, making both SFT and sampling expensive. The authors aim to build a compute-efficient, open-source prover family that achieves strong performance under practical compute budgets.

Method: (1) Curriculum SFT with a Lean-verified corpus stratified into easy/medium/hard problems for progressive skill acquisition. (2) Dynamic proof-reasoning filtering that preserves informative traces within an 8k-token context budget. (3) Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF), which expands scarce verified corpora by mutating formal statements and populating them via self-distillation without requiring formal verification of every variant. (4) A diffusion-based prover (4B) as proof-of-concept that iteratively refines Lean proofs at inference time, complementing standard autoregressive models (4B, 32B).

Result: Pythagoras-Prover-4B achieves 86.1% pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test, surpassing DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B (82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters. Pythagoras-Prover-32B sets the open-source state of the art at 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test and solves 93/672 PutnamBench problems. On MiniF2F-ALF (a contamination-sensitive mutated benchmark), the 32B model remains strongest and the 4B model matches prior SOTA Goedel-Prover-V2-32B, with all models losing accuracy relative to clean benchmarks.

Conclusion: Compute-efficient theorem proving is achievable through curriculum training, dynamic context filtering, and self-distilled data augmentation (ALF). The Pythagoras-Prover family demonstrates that smaller models can match or exceed much larger models by training on higher-quality, stratified data, and that diffusion-based proof generation is a viable alternative paradigm worth further exploration.

Abstract: Modern Lean theorem provers achieve strong performance only with substantial training and inference compute, driven in part by scarce verified proof data and the long reasoning traces of formal proof search, making both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and sampling expensive. We introduce Pythagoras-Prover, a compute-efficient open-source family of Lean theorem provers built for practical compute budgets. The family spans two generation paradigms: autoregressive models at 4B and 32B parameters, and a first proof-of-concept diffusion-based prover (4B) that iteratively refines Lean proofs at inference time. For training efficiency, we build a Lean-verified corpus stratified into easy, medium, and hard problems for curriculum SFT, so models acquire proof skills progressively from shorter, simpler proofs to longer, harder ones.
