During SFT, a dynamic proof-reasoning filtering scheme preserves informative proof traces while keeping each instance within an 8k-token context budget. We also introduce Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF), which expands scarce verified corpora into variants of formal statements, populated via self-distillation for extra training signal without formally verifying every mutated instance. By perturbing known problems while preserving their formal character, ALF reduces reliance on any statement's surface form. Empirically, Pythagoras-Prover-4B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B at pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test (86.1% vs 82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters, while Pythagoras-Prover-32B sets the open-source state of the art at 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test and solves 93 of 672 PutnamBench problems. We release MiniF2F-ALF, an ALF-mutated contamination-sensitive benchmark on which every evaluated model loses accuracy; here our 32B remains strongest and our 4B matches the prior state of the art, Goedel-Prover-V2-32B.

</details>


### [93] [PersonaDrive: Human-Style Retrieval-Augmented VLA Agents for Closed-Loop Driving Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12616)
*Mahmoud Srewa,Praneetsai Iddamsetty,Mohammad Abdullah Al Faruque,Salma Elmalaki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PersonaDrive conditions a vision-language-action driving agent on retrieved human demonstrations from a style-instructed dataset (aggressive, neutral, conservative), enabling diverse non-ego traffic agents without per-style retraining and achieving state-of-the-art performance on Bench2Drive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Closed-loop driving simulators lack realistic behavioral diversity in non-ego agents, as existing approaches rely on single-mode rule-based or learned models. Prior style-variation methods use proxy signals (post-hoc labels, LLM-inferred rewards) rather than explicit human demonstrations of instructed driving styles, limiting authenticity and controllability.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) offline triplet mining over per-style human driving data using combined image-text similarity; (2) training a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder, operating over per-style databases; (3) fine-tuning a single VLA backbone to use retrieved context points as in-context behavioral demonstrations for waypoint prediction. At inference, style is selected by swapping the per-style retrieval database — no retraining required.

Result: On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (style-agnostic) outperforms SimLingo by 4.6% and HiP-AD by 2.5% in driving score. Under style conditioning, it achieves the highest driving score across all styles within a ~2% band; its weakest style surpasses the strongest baseline (DMW) by 5.4%. Average speed and acceleration increase by 18% and 25% from conservative to aggressive instructions.

Conclusion: PersonaDrive demonstrates that conditioning a single VLA model on retrieved human demonstrations from style-explicit driving data yields realistic, controllable behavioral diversity for simulation agents, outperforming prior methods while eliminating per-style retraining overhead.

Abstract: Closed-loop driving simulators typically populate their environments with non-ego traffic agents that behave largely the same way, produced either by rule-based traffic managers or by learned models trained toward a single behavioral mode. Recent work introduces style variation through post-hoc labels on observational data or LLM-inferred reward weights, but these signals act as proxies for what a style should reward rather than demonstrations of humans explicitly asked to drive in that style. We introduce PersonaDrive, a pipeline that conditions a vision-language-action (VLA) driving agent on retrieved demonstrations from a style-instructed human driving dataset, in which participants drive CARLA leaderboard routes under aggressive, neutral, and conservative instructions on a driver-in-the-loop rig. The pipeline has three stages: (i) offline triplet mining over per-style human driving data using a combined image-text similarity score; (ii) training a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder over per-style databases; and (iii) fine-tuning a single VLA backbone to treat retrieved context points as in-context behavioral demonstrations during waypoint prediction. At inference, the same backbone is conditioned on any style by swapping which per-style database the retrieval head queries, so selecting a style requires no per-style retraining while enabling human-style, style-diverse non-ego agents for closed-loop simulation. On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (no style) improves the driving score by 4.6% over SimLingo and 2.5% over HiP-AD, and under style conditioning attains the highest driving score in every style within a roughly 2% band (its weakest style surpassing the strongest baseline, DMW, by 5.4%), while average speed and acceleration rise by 18% and 25% from the conservative to the aggressive instruction.

</details>


### [94] ["Did you lie?" Evaluating Lie Detectors across Model Scale and Belief-Verified Model Organisms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12618)
*Alan Cooney,David Africa,Geoffrey Irving*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates lie detectors for language models, finding that current detection methods (logprob classifiers, activation probes) fail on model organisms with verified hidden beliefs, while chain-of-thought judging remains effective (0.82 balanced accuracy). The authors contribute 13 reasoning model organisms with verified CoT beliefs and a diverse prompted-lying testbed, demonstrating that detectors scale with model size on prompted lies but collapse on trained deception, challenging the reliability of high-confidence belief claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lie detection benchmarks lack model organisms where researchers can verifiably confirm that models believe the opposite of what they say. Without verified ground-truth beliefs, prior positive and negative detection results are uninterpretable. The authors aim to establish rigorous testbeds that enable trustworthy evaluation of lie detectors.

Method: The authors create two testbeds: (1) 13 reasoning model organisms with chain-of-thought-verified hidden beliefs that generalize to held-out tasks; (2) Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed spanning diverse lie-inducing motivations. They evaluate four detector types — a CoT judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes including their novel Did-You-Lie (DYL) follow-up probe — across 31 open-weight models (2B–1T parameters). Detectors are assessed on both prompted lying and trained model organisms.

Result: All four detectors show positive scaling with model capability on prompted lying. However, on trained model organisms, every activation- and logprob-based detector drops sharply; DYL retains the most signal but still degrades. Only the CoT judge remains robust (0.82 balanced accuracy), partly due to the verification process favoring CoT-readable beliefs. This reveals a fundamental gap: current lie detectors cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs.

Conclusion: Current lie detection methods are unreliable for making high-confidence claims about model beliefs, especially when models have been trained to deceive. The CoT judge's apparent success is partly an artifact of verification methodology. The authors call for new research directions to address these limitations and release all datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors to facilitate further work.

Abstract: Robust lie detectors for language models could enable powerful techniques for auditing, monitoring, and post-hoc investigation of model behaviour, but evaluating them requires testbeds where models verifiably believe the opposite of what they say. We show that existing trained model organisms often fail this requirement, leaving prior positive and negative detection results difficult to interpret. We address this with 13 reasoning model organisms whose hidden beliefs are verified in chain-of-thought and shown to generalise to held-out tasks, alongside Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed covering a broad range of lie-inducing motivations. On these testbeds we evaluate four detectors: a chain-of-thought judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes, including Did-You-Lie (DYL), a new method for training follow-up probes. On prompted lying, across 31 open-weight models spanning 2B to 1T parameters, all four detectors show positive scaling with model capability. However, every activation- and logprob-based detector drops sharply on our trained model organisms, with DYL retaining the most signal; only the chain-of-thought judge remains strong, with 0.82 balanced accuracy, partly as an artefact of our verification process favouring CoT-readable beliefs. Current lie detectors therefore cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs, and we suggest research directions that may address some of their current limitations. We release our datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors.

</details>


### [95] [TrajGenAgent: A Hierarchical LLM Agent for Human Mobility Trajectory Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12657)
*Siyu Li,Toan Tran,Lingyi Zhao,Khurram Shafique,Li Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TrajGenAgent is a fine-tuning-free, hierarchical LLM-agent framework that generates realistic human mobility trajectories using a two-stage orchestrator-worker design: an LLM synthesizes activity chains via in-context learning, followed by a deterministic grounding workflow. It introduces an anomaly-detection-based evaluation framework and outperforms neural and LLM baselines in spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and behavioral realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale human trajectory data is crucial for transportation, urban planning, and epidemic control but is expensive and privacy-constrained to collect. Existing LLM-based generators either use prompt engineering (preserving reasoning but lacking spatiotemporal grounding) or trajectory-level fine-tuning (improving precision but costly and potentially weakening general reasoning). A method is needed that achieves high realism without model fine-tuning.

Method: TrajGenAgent employs a two-stage orchestrator-worker architecture: (1) An LLM orchestrator synthesizes an individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chain from historical evidence using in-context learning. (2) A deterministic worker workflow grounds each activity into a complete visit via personalized POI retrieval, distance-aware location selection, kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and LLM-based duration estimation. For evaluation, an anomaly-detection framework uses two complementary detectors to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility beyond aggregate statistics.

Result: Experiments on benchmark and large-scale simulation datasets demonstrate that TrajGenAgent improves spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific behavioral realism over representative neural and LLM-based baselines, all while avoiding parameter updates (no fine-tuning).

Conclusion: A semantic-aware, hierarchical LLM-agent framework can generate realistic synthetic mobility trajectories without fine-tuning, combining the reasoning strengths of LLMs with deterministic spatiotemporal grounding. The anomaly-detection-based evaluation provides a more meaningful realism assessment than aggregate statistics alone.

Abstract: Human mobility data is important for transportation, urban planning, and epidemic control, but large-scale trajectory collection is often costly and privacy-constrained, motivating realistic synthetic trajectory generation. Existing LLM-based generators typically rely on either prompt engineering, which preserves zero-shot reasoning but lacks fine-grained spatiotemporal grounding, or trajectory-level fine-tuning, which improves statistical precision but incurs substantial computational cost and may weaken general reasoning. We propose TrajGenAgent, a semantic-aware hierarchical LLM-agent framework for human mobility trajectory generation without model fine-tuning. TrajGenAgent uses a two-stage orchestrator-worker design: an LLM first synthesizes an individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chain from historical evidence via in-context learning, and a deterministic workflow then grounds each activity into a complete visit using personalized POI retrieval, distance-aware location selection, kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and LLM-based duration estimation. To evaluate realism beyond aggregate spatiotemporal statistics, we introduce an anomaly-detection-based evaluation framework using two complementary detectors to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility. Experiments on benchmark and large-scale simulation datasets show that TrajGenAgent improves spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific behavioral realism over representative neural and LLM-based baselines, while avoiding parameter updates.

</details>


### [96] [The Illusion of Multi-Agent Advantage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13003)
*Prathyusha Jwalapuram,Hehai Lin,Chuyuan Li,Fangkai Jiao,Sudong Wang,Yifei Ming,Zixuan Ke,Chengwei Qin,Giuseppe Carenini,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automatic Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) underperform Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC) on reasoning benchmarks while being up to 10x more expensive. A diagnostic synthetic dataset reveals that automatically generated MAS suffer from architectural bloat — superficial complexity misaligned with core multi-agent principles — which existing evaluation frameworks fail to capture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevailing wisdom holds that MAS outperform SAS due to context protection, parallelism, and distributed decision-making. However, empirical evidence relies on benchmarks favoring isolated reasoning, which do not test these advantages. The authors aim to rigorously evaluate whether automatically generated MAS — designed for generalizability — genuinely outperform SAS baselines when subjected to tasks requiring interactive, multi-step workflows and explicit decomposition.

Method: The authors systematically compare automatically generated MAS against CoT-SC across (1) traditional reasoning datasets and (2) interactive multi-step workflow tasks (BrowseComp-Plus). They introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset specifically designed to test MAS capabilities: explicit task decomposition, context separation, and parallelization potential. They then compare expert-architected MAS against automatically generated architectures on this dataset, and perform systematic architectural deconstruction of generated MAS to identify structural deficiencies.

Result: Automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC across all evaluated benchmarks despite up to 10x higher computational cost. On the diagnostic synthetic dataset, expert-architected MAS outperform automatically generated ones in both raw performance and cost-efficiency. Deconstruction reveals that automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat: superficial complexity that fails to translate into functional utility, exposing misalignment with multi-agent principles.

Conclusion: Existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps in complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Current automated MAS design paradigms prioritize superficial complexity over functional multi-agent advantages, and benchmarks must evolve to properly assess distributed, parallel, and context-separated reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Prevailing wisdom posits that Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) are superior to Single-Agent Systems (SAS), citing advantages like context protection, parallel processing and distributed decision-making. However, empirical support for this claim relies primarily on comparisons with SAS baselines using benchmarks that prioritize isolated reasoning tasks, which do not adequately assess these advantages. Focusing on automatically generated MAS that are designed for enhanced generalizability over manually-designed counterparts, we perform a rigorous, systematic evaluation against SAS, specifically Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC). Across traditional reasoning datasets and tasks with interactive multi-step workflows (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus), we demonstrate that automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC despite being up to 10x more expensive. To isolate these failures from limitations inherent to task structure, we introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset tailored for MAS featuring explicit task decomposition, context separation and parallelization potential. We show that expert-architected MAS consistently outperforms automatically generated architectures in both raw performance and cost-efficiency on this dataset, demonstrating that existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and inefficiencies of complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Critically, systematic deconstruction of the generated MAS architectures reveals that current automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat that prioritizes superficial complexity which does not translate into functional utility, exposing a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.

</details>


### [97] [Evoflux: Inference-Time Evolution of Executable Tool Workflows for Compact Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12674)
*Kushal Raj Bhandari,Ling Yue,Ching-Yun Ko,Dhaval Patel,Shaowu Pan,Pin-Yu Chen,Jianxi Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evoflux is an inference-time evolutionary search method that repairs broken tool-use workflows for small language models, boosting execution feasibility from ~3% to 17-24% on MCP-Bench, outperforming distillation-based approaches (SFT/DPO) under limited teacher traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small language models are attractive for tool-agent deployment due to lower cost and latency, but they struggle with MCP-style tool use — discovering tools, satisfying schemas, tracking dependencies, and grounding responses. Small-corpus distillation teaches workflow format but fails to cover recovery from failed plans, especially as tool catalogs change.

Method: Evoflux operates at inference time as an evolutionary search over typed workflow graphs. It applies structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive mutation intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning to iteratively repair non-executable workflows into executable ones.

Result: On held-out MCP-Bench tasks with live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux raises execution feasibility from ~3% to 17-24% across small planners. SFT and SFT+DPO on search-mined data match or underperform zero-shot baselines. ReAct achieves higher peaks but with greater variance and token cost.

Conclusion: Execution-grounded evolutionary search is more reliable than distillation-based fine-tuning for teaching small LMs robust tool use when teacher-trace budgets are scarce.

Abstract: Compact language models (LMs) reduce cost, latency, and deployment risk for tool agents. Yet MCP-style tool use requires more than isolated function calling: an agent must discover tools from live catalogs, satisfy schemas, preserve dependencies across intermediate outputs, and ground final responses in executed evidence. Small planners often generate plausible workflow graphs that fail under tool resolution, parameter validation, dependency tracking, or execution. We argue that this failure mode is poorly handled by small-corpus distillation. A few hundred teacher traces can teach workflow format, but rarely cover the recovery behavior needed to repair failed plans over changing tool catalogs. We introduce Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search method that treats compact tool use as the repair of executable tool workflows. It evolves typed workflow graphs through structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. On held-out MCP-Bench tasks spanning live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux raises execution feasibility from roughly 3% to 17-24% across small planners. In contrast, SFT and SFT+DPO on the same search-mined data match, underperform, or collapse below zero-shot performance; ReAct reaches higher peaks, but with higher variance and token cost. These results show that execution-grounded search is more reliable under scarce teacher-trace budgets.

</details>


### [98] [LLM-as-an-Investigator: Evidence-First Reasoning for Robust Interactive Problem Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13220)
*Fabrizio Marozzo,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces "LLM-as-an-Investigator," an evidence-first agentic methodology that combats user-driven sycophancy — where LLMs prematurely reinforce user hypotheses — by having an agent iteratively generate candidate hypotheses, ask targeted clarification questions, and update probabilities until one explanation dominates. Evaluated on a benchmark of solved mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic forum threads using a three-agent pipeline, the approach outperforms standard and reasoning-only baselines in diagnostic accuracy while reducing conversational bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs used as technical assistants tend to exhibit "user-driven sycophancy" — prematurely aligning with user-provided hypotheses (especially when descriptions are incomplete or explanations are plausible but unverified) rather than systematically testing alternative explanations before proposing solutions. This leads to incorrect diagnoses and biased problem-solving.

Method: The authors propose an "LLM-as-an-Investigator" framework implemented via a Solution Investigator Agent that: (1) estimates the ambiguity of the initial problem description, (2) generates multiple candidate hypotheses, (3) asks targeted clarification questions, (4) updates hypothesis probabilities after each user answer, and (5) continues investigation until one hypothesis is sufficiently stronger than alternatives. For evaluation, they construct a benchmark from solved technical forum threads across mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains using a three-agent pipeline: a Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts threads into structured cases, a Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while hiding the known solution, and the tested assistant attempts to recover the solution through dialogue. Comparisons include standard assistants, reasoning-oriented LLMs, and the investigator model across multiple LLM backbones.

Result: The investigator-based approach achieves higher diagnostic accuracy than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines. The evidence-first protocol demonstrably reduces user-induced conversational bias. Analysis also confirms that standard assistants are prone to following misleading user hypotheses in diagnostic scenarios.

Conclusion: An evidence-first, hypothesis-testing agentic protocol significantly improves LLM diagnostic accuracy and mitigates user-driven sycophancy in technical problem-solving contexts, demonstrating the value of structured investigation over immediate response generation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive assistants for technical problem solving. However, when users provide incomplete descriptions or plausible but unverified explanations, LLMs may prematurely align with these assumptions and propose solutions before collecting sufficient evidence. We refer to this behavior as user-driven sycophancy: the tendency of an LLM to reinforce a user-provided hypothesis instead of testing alternative explanations. This paper introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator, an evidence-first agentic AI methodology for robust problem diagnosis. The approach is implemented through a Solution Investigator Agent, which estimates the ambiguity of an initial problem description, generates candidate hypotheses, asks targeted clarification questions, and updates hypothesis probabilities after each answer. Rather than producing an immediate response, the agent continues the investigation until the evidence makes one candidate explanation stronger than the alternatives. To evaluate the approach, we build a benchmark from solved technical forum threads in mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains. We use a three-agent evaluation pipeline in which a Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved threads into structured cases, a Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while hiding the known solution, and the tested assistant attempts to recover the solution through dialogue. The experiments compare standard assistants, reasoning-oriented LLMs, and the proposed investigator-based model across LLM backbones. In addition to diagnostic accuracy, we analyze how standard assistants follow misleading user hypotheses in diagnostic cases. The results show that the proposed approach identifies the problem more accurately than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines, while its evidence-first protocol helps reduce user-induced conversational bias.

</details>


### [99] [From AGI to ASI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12683)
*Tim Genewein,Matija Franklin,Alexander Lerchner,Laurent Orseau,Samuel Albanie,Adam Bales,Cole Wyeth,Stephanie Chan,Iason Gabriel,Joel Z. Leibo,Allan Dafoe,Marcus Hutter,Thore Graepel,Shane Legg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This report examines the transition from human-level AGI to artificial superintelligence (ASI), outlining four potential pathways (scaling, paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, multi-agent collectives), associated frictions, and the prospect of a continuous series of AI-driven societal transformations rather than a single disruptive event.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how AI might develop beyond AGI along the continuum of machine intelligence toward Universal AI, providing formal grounding for understanding the AGI-to-ASI transition and its societal implications.

Method: Conceptual analysis, leveraging the theoretical framework of Universal AI as a formal endpoint, to characterize ASI and map out four distinct transition pathways, then identify frictions and bottlenecks along each, framing key open research questions.

Result: Four pathways from AGI to ASI are identified: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive self-improvement, and emergence from large-scale multi-agent collectives. The impact of frictions along these pathways is uncertain, and AI progress may continue to accelerate, suggesting a series of transformative changes rather than a single step change.

Conclusion: Due to large predictive uncertainties, the prospect of continuous, AI-enabled transformative societal changes across science and technology cannot be ruled out. Preparing for this requires a massively interdisciplinary, globally-scoped endeavor.

Abstract: Over the last decade, building human-level artificial general intelligence has moved from far-fetched speculation to being a concrete next-decade target for many of the largest AI organisations. Achieving this goal would have profound and far-reaching impacts on human society, which raises many complex questions for the decade ahead. This report investigates how AI itself might continue to develop in a post-AGI world along the continuum of machine intelligence. The endpoint of this continuum, Universal AI, is theoretically well understood, which provides some formal grounding for the main focus of this report: the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence, which, intuitively, can be understood as a system that is more intelligent and cognitively capable than large organisations of humans. After characterizing ASI, the report discusses four potential pathways from AGI to ASI: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and ASI emerging from large-scale multi-agent collectives. The report then discusses possible frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways. Determining whether the impact of these frictions will be negligible or substantial raises a number of concrete open research questions. Due to large uncertainties for predicting ASI progress, it cannot be ruled out that AI progress might continue to accelerate over the next years. This could imply that the image of a single transformative step change, caused by the introduction of human-level AGI into our society, could be inaccurate. More apt might be the prospect of a series of transformative societal changes caused by AI-enabled progress and breakthroughs across many areas of science and technology. Preparing for this prospect requires a massively interdisciplinary endeavour of global scope and interest.

</details>


### [100] [Can I Buy Your KV Cache?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13361)
*Luoyuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a "compute once, reuse everywhere" paradigm for LLM inference: publishers precompute KV caches for documents, and downstream agents load them instead of re-running prefill from scratch, achieving 9–50x compute savings with zero accuracy loss. The authors argue that provider-side hosting (analogous to prompt caching) is the only viable distribution model, since KV caches are nearly incompressible and shipping them incurs prohibitive egress costs. The economic prize is quantified: serving a single 3,774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M for re-prefill vs. ~$0.03M for reuse, framing an "agent-native prefill CDN."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Every AI agent processing the same document redundantly recomputes the identical KV cache via prefill, wasting massive compute. The authors observe this as a systemic inefficiency across the agent ecosystem and aim to eliminate it with a technically trivial but economically significant change: precompute once, reuse many times.

Method: The authors demonstrate token-exact equivalence between loading a precomputed KV cache and prefilling from scratch (verified on Qwen3-4B with 24/24 greedy tokens matching, plus logit-level verification). They benchmark compute cost ratios (9–50x cheaper for reuse vs. prefill, widening with sequence length due to O(L²) attention scaling). They evaluate two distribution models: (1) shipping the KV cache to the client (fails due to near-incompressibility making egress cost exceed prefill savings), and (2) hosting provider-side (succeeds, mirroring production prompt-caching architectures). Economic analysis compares re-prefill vs. reuse costs at scale, using current API cache-read tariff structures.

Result: KV cache reuse is token-exact with no accuracy degradation. Reuse is 9–50x cheaper than prefill on Qwen3-4B, with the gap growing with document length. Provider-side hosting eliminates egress and makes reuse economically dominant: serving one 3,774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M re-prefilling vs. ~$0.03M reusing (49.7x savings). Current 0.1x cache-read API tariffs provide a 10x consumer discount while fitting comfortably within the measured ~50x physical savings envelope, leaving the remaining ~40x as provider margin — millions of dollars per popular document.

Conclusion: A precompute-and-reuse model for KV caches is technically trivial, accuracy-preserving, and economically compelling. The architecture points toward an "agent-native prefill CDN" where publishers precompute and host KV caches provider-side. The authors identify lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment/settlement layer as the key open problems to enable a decentralized marketplace for precomputed KV caches.

Abstract: Right now, across the world, AI agents are repeating the same absurd act: to read one document, they each recompute it from scratch. Every agent re-runs prefill, the most compute-intensive step a large model takes, over identical text, only to rebuild a key-value (KV) cache identical to the one the agent before it just built. The same answer, computed a million times. We make a proposal that is almost offensively simple: compute it once. Let a publisher precompute a document's KV cache, and let every other agent buy the right to load it and skip prefill. It works, and it is token-exact: loading a precomputed KV and continuing matches prefilling from scratch (24/24 greedy tokens, and at the logits level), with no accuracy cost. On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper in compute than prefill, and the gap widens with length (prefill's attention scales with L^2), so a single reuse already pays it back. Then the part that matters: where the KV lives. Shipping it fails, because KV is nearly incompressible, so per-load egress costs more than the prefill it saves. Hosting it provider-side, exactly as production prompt-caching works, removes egress entirely. The size of the prize is set by our measured compute saving: serving one hot 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M to re-prefill but only ~$0.03M of reuse compute (49.7x less). The 0.1x cache-read tariff APIs charge passes a 10x discount to users while sitting inside this measured envelope, so the 10x is a floor that the measured ~50x compute saving clears, and the gap to the physical ~50x is provider margin: millions of dollars per popular document. We frame the resulting agent-native prefill CDN and leave lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer as the open problems.

</details>


### [101] [Deployment-Centered Evaluation: Predicting Query-Level Rejection Risk in a Clinical LLM System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12702)
*Alyssa Unell,Miguel Fuentes,Brenna Li,Bridget Lin,Meena Jagadeesan,Sanmi Koyejo,Nigam Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents a deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system in a clinical EHR setting, where a pre-response classifier predicts user rejection of LLM outputs using both query content and deployment-specific context (provider type, department, model used). Achieving an AUROC of 0.719, the model's utility is demonstrated in guardrail triggering and abstention scenarios, with the key insight that deployment context substantially improves predictive performance over query content alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static clinical NLP benchmarks measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance across queries, and require densely annotated datasets — creating blind spots for real-world clinical LLM deployment evaluation. The authors argue for evaluation methods that leverage sparse but authentic user feedback signals under actual deployment conditions.

Method: A pre-response classifier is trained to estimate the risk that a future LLM interaction will be rejected by the user. It leverages query content plus deployment-specific context (provider type, department name, language model used) available before generation. The model is evaluated prospectively over 4.5 months of real user feedback from an academic medical center's EHR-integrated LLM system, with downstream utility assessed via simulated guardrail triggering and abstention use cases.

Result: The prediction model achieved an AUROC of 0.719. Deployment-specific context features (provider type, department, language model) improved predictive ability over using query content alone. Downstream analysis demonstrated the benefit of such predictions for triggering guardrails and abstention decisions.

Conclusion: Predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context is feasible and opens the door to targeted guardrails and abstention mechanisms in clinical LLM systems, enabling more user-aligned, deployment-aware clinical AI.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into clinical systems, making it essential to evaluate the real-world utility of these systems. However, static benchmarks tend to measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance across queries, and require densely annotated datasets -- leading to major blind spots for evaluating clinical systems. In this work, we perform a deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system embedded within electronic health records at an academic medical center, where user feedback is sparse but closely reflects the deployment conditions. Specifically, we train a pre-response classifier that estimates the risk that a future interaction will result in the user rejecting the LLM response, based on query content and deployment-specific context available before generation. We conduct a prospective analysis of our model over 4.5 months of user feedback, finding that our prediction model achieves an AUROC of 0.719. Further, we estimate the benefit of such predictions in two downstream use cases (guardrail triggering and abstention). Our key conceptual insight is that making use of deployment-specific context (i.e., the provider type, department name, language model used for response), as opposed to only query content, improves the ability to predict whether the user will reject the system output. Altogether, our empirical case study demonstrates the feasibility of predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context, opening the door to targeted guardrails.

</details>


### [102] [Neuro-Symbolic Agents for Regulated Process Automation: Challenges and Research Agenda](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13405)
*Alexander Rombach,Chantale Lauer,Nijat Mehdiyev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes "compliance-by-construction," a paradigm shift in LLM-agent design for regulated industries, arguing that existing symbolic structures (regulations, process models, compliance constraints) should be integrated as core architectural components rather than treated as external guardrails. It identifies key neuro-symbolic research challenges and calls for community engagement in regulated process automation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed in regulated industries (e.g., quality management) where they automate judgment-intensive processes. Current approaches rely primarily on guardrail-based monitoring, which catches errors only after they occur. The authors argue that the symbolic structures already embedded in these domains—regulations, typed process models, and compliance constraints—remain underutilized as mere external checks rather than foundational architectural elements that could proactively shape agent behavior and prevent control-flow violations.

Method: The paper is a position paper that: (1) introduces and defines the compliance-by-construction paradigm, contrasting it with guardrail-based monitoring; (2) delineates the division of labor between structural prevention (control-flow violations) and guardrails (semantic errors); (3) identifies a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges at both foundational and capability levels that must be jointly addressed to realize compliance-by-construction; and (4) frames regulated process automation as a high-impact domain for the neuro-symbolic research community.

Result: The paper does not present empirical results. Instead, it contributes a conceptual framework: distinguishing compliance-by-construction (preventing control-flow violations through architectural integration of symbolic structures) from guardrail-based monitoring (catching semantic errors post hoc), and arguing that both are necessary and complementary. It delineates a research agenda of neuro-symbolic challenges that span foundational reasoning and applied capabilities.

Conclusion: Compliance-by-construction offers a structural foundation for building trustworthy LLM agents in regulated domains. Achieving this requires the neuro-symbolic community to tackle a set of interconnected foundational and capability-level challenges. The authors issue a call to action for the community to engage with regulated process automation as a high-impact, societally significant research domain.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are entering regulated industries where they automate judgment intensive quality management processes. We argue that symbolic structures already embedded in these domains, including regulations, typed process models, and compliance constraints, should be treated not merely as external monitoring mechanisms but as core architectural components that shape the agent's decision-making and behavior. We propose compliance-by-construction as a complementary paradigm to guardrail-based monitoring: a structural foundation that prevents control-flow violations, while guardrails remain essential for catching semantic errors. We identify a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges on foundational and capability level and show that addressing them jointly enables compliance-by-construction. We call on the neuro-symbolic community to engage with regulated process automation as a high impact research domain.

</details>


### [103] [Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13556)
*Aruna Dey,Suraj Biswas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: To solve the cold-start problem in personalized health AI—where ML models need weeks of data to distinguish baseline physiology from environmental deviations—the authors propose using genomic profiles as fixed, exogenous Bayesian priors. These "genetic anchors" initialize physiological set-point estimates and gradually decay as behavioral data accumulate, enabling immediate, person-specific interpretation of physiological signals (e.g., HRV) that would otherwise be ambiguous.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized health AI systems require extensive individual behavioral data before they can reliably separate constitutional (trait-like) physiological variation from environmentally driven (state-like) deviation. This cold-start delay renders systems useless during initial deployment. The authors aim to eliminate this lag by exploiting genomic data—available at birth, causally exogenous, and immune to reverse causation—as an immediate, personalized prior for physiological inference.

Method: The method constructs a Bayesian framework where an individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous anchor. The prior physiological set point is computed as Ĝ = μ + Σ(βᵢ · gᵢ), using GWAS-derived effect sizes (βᵢ) and risk-allele counts (gᵢ). Each incoming physiological measurement P yields a non-constitutional deviation δ = P − Ĝ, isolating environmentally attributable signal. A dynamic weighting function Ĝₜ = w(t)·Ĝ_genomic + [1−w(t)]·P̄ₜ governs the transition from genome-dominated to data-dominated inference as behavioral observations accrue. The framework is developed across six physiological domains, with priors graded by evidence strength (e.g., robust anchors: FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5; contested: SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2).

Result: The same observed physiological measurement (e.g., HRV of 55 ms) yields opposite interpretations depending on the genomic prior: a suppression hypothesis when the prior predicts 80 ms, versus an enhancement hypothesis when the prior predicts 30 ms. This interpretive reversal is impossible without a personalized anchor, demonstrating the framework's capacity to resolve baseline ambiguity from the first observation. The architecture distinguishes robustly replicated genomic anchors from contested candidate genes across six physiological domains.

Conclusion: Genomic priors can solve the cold-start problem in personalized health AI, but deployment requires four constraints: (1) evidence-graded prior strength, (2) dynamic decay from genomic to empirical dominance, (3) ancestry-matched effect sizes to avoid confounding, and (4) attributional rather than deterministic output. The authors also delineate the inferential boundary between GWAS association, Mendelian randomization, and individual-level causal claims, cautioning against conflating population-level effect sizes with token causation.

Abstract: Personalized health AI systems face a fundamental cold-start problem: machine learning models for physiological interpretation require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation. We propose a solution grounded in causal inference and Bayesian prior design. An individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous genetic anchor -- a domain-informed, personalized prior that is fixed at conception, immune to reverse causation, and available before a single behavioral observation is collected. The anchor initializes a Bayesian belief state over an individual's physiological set point G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i), where beta_i are GWAS-derived effect sizes and g_i are risk-allele counts. Each incoming physiological measurement P produces a non-constitutional deviation delta = P - G-hat that separates the signal attributable to environment and state from the constitutionally fixed baseline. As behavioral data accrue, the prior decays according to G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. The same observed HRV of 55 ms generates a suppression hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 80 ms, and an enhancement hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 30 ms -- a reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. We develop this architecture across six physiological domains, grading genomic priors by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested candidate genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2). We address the inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation, and define four constraints for deployment: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

</details>


### [104] [Definitional alignment before capability alignment: a Design-Science framework for adjudicating claims about AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12713)
*J. E. Aguilera Briones*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops DAF-AGI, a conceptual framework with five ordinal criteria and a governance audit to evaluate the fitness of AGI definitions. Demonstrated on five measurement families, it shows that a claim of current generative systems as AGI is only certifiable under performance-based operationalizations, not under capability-ontology, psychometric, or skill-acquisition approaches. It proposes "definitional sovereignty" as a governance concept.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The term "AGI" (Artificial General Intelligence) suffers from chronic under-specification: it lacks a single stable referent, and competing operationalizations yield contradictory verdicts about the same system. This ambiguity enables both premature arrival claims and perennial denialism from overlapping evidence, creating a governance gap. The paper treats this definitional confusion not as a mere semantic nuisance but as a design and governance problem requiring systematic adjudicative tools.

Method: The paper follows Design Science Research Methodology (DSRM) to construct DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact. DAF-AGI comprises two coupled components: (1) five ordinal criteria for assessing the adjudicative fitness of candidate AGI definitions (e.g., clarity, measurability, falsifiability) and (2) a structured governance audit examining authorship, interest, certification, external verification, and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent measurement families (performance-based, capability-ontology, psychometric, skill-acquisition, economic) and one deflationary boundary position, using a documented corpus. It is then stress-tested against a stylized strong arrival claim (that current generative systems constitute AGI because they outperform a well-educated adult on many cognitive tasks), drawing on 2024–2025 sources.

Result: Under DAF-AGI, the strong arrival claim was certifiable only under performance-based operationalizations. Capability-ontology, psychometric, and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify the claim. The economic family returned an indeterminate verdict, and the deflationary position refused binary adjudication altogether. This demonstrates that the plausibility of AGI arrival claims is heavily contingent on which definitional framework holds adjudicative authority.

Conclusion: The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization of definitional adjudication tools, not an empirical validation. Independent application, inter-rater reliability testing, and author-external case studies remain necessary. The paper further proposes "definitional sovereignty" as a component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify, and revise imported technological categories under conditions of public accountability, framing AGI definition as a governance choice rather than a purely technical determination.

Abstract: Claims that artificial general intelligence has already arrived and claims that it remains decades away are often defended from overlapping evidence. "AGI" lacks a single shared and stable referent and competing operationalizations can return different verdicts on the same system. This article treats that under-specification as a design and governance problem. Following Design Science Research Methodology, it develops DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two coupled components: five ordinal criteria for assessing the adjudicative fitness of candidate definitions and a structured governance audit of authorship, interest, certification, external verification and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent measurement families and one deflationary boundary position in a documented corpus and then stress-tested against a stylized strong arrival claim: that current generative systems constitute AGI because they outperform a well-educated adult on many cognitive tasks. On evidence from the cited 2024-2025 sources, the claim was certifiable only under a performance-based operationalization; capability-ontology, psychometric and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify it, the economic family remains indeterminate and the deflationary position refuses binary adjudication. The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization, not an empirical validation: independent application, inter-rater testing and author-external cases remain necessary. The paper further proposes definitional sovereignty as an enabling component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify and revise imported technological categories under public accountability.

</details>


### [105] [Multiagent Protocols with Aggregated Confidence Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13591)
*Ali Elahi,Barbara Di Eugenio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces methods to aggregate confidence signals from individual agents in multiagent debate systems into a single, system-level confidence score. Using soft voting or Bayesian fusion on calibrated confidence signals, the aggregated confidence substantially improves discriminability (AUARC) over single-agent baselines without sacrificing correctness (F1).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NLP confidence estimation methods target single models, and while prior multiagent debate (MAD) work uses confidence internally (e.g., weighting messages), no method produces or evaluates a single aggregated confidence for the multiagent system as a whole — a gap this paper aims to fill for downstream reliability and decision tasks.

Method: Three protocols are proposed: (1) transform raw confidence signals (sequence probability and self-report) to be cross-model comparable using parametric (temperature scaling, Platt scaling) or non-parametric (histogram binning, isotonic regression) calibrators; (2) aggregate via soft voting or a novel Bayesian fusion method; (3) evaluate using AUARC for discriminability and F1 for correctness. Experiments cover six homogeneous/heterogeneous debating pairs across five benchmarks, four task types, and multiple model scales.

Result: Aggregated confidence significantly outperforms the best single agent and standard debate baselines on AUARC (discriminability), while F1-score remains stable and recovers losses MAD incurs on ambiguous tasks. Calibration improves F1 for both estimators, but AUARC is less dependent on calibration quality.

Conclusion: System-level aggregated confidence for multiagent debate is both feasible and beneficial, offering reliable uncertainty estimates without degrading answer quality. Bayesian fusion and soft voting prove effective, and the framework supports both homogeneous and heterogeneous agent configurations across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Confidence is used for reliability, oversight, and a range of downstream decision tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), yet no existing method produces or evaluates a confidence for the output of a multiagent system. Prior work uses confidence within multiagent debate (MAD) to weight messages, trigger debate, or calibrate individual agents, but it never aggregates these into a single confidence for the system itself. We introduce three protocols that produce a final answer along with a single aggregated confidence by first transforming raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combining them via soft voting or a probability fusion we call Bayesian fusion. This aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (AUARC) than that of the best single agent or the standard debate baselines, while correctness (F1-score) stays stable and recovers the losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Analyzing two estimators, sequence probability and self-report, alongside parametric and non-parametric calibrators, we find that calibration improves F1 for both estimators while AUARC is less reliant on it. We evaluate six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types, spanning a range of model capabilities and sizes.

</details>


### [106] [The Theory of Mind Utility: Formal Specification of a Mentalizing Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12721)
*Nikolos Gurney,Stacy Marsella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ToM-U formalizes epistemic state inference (mentalizing) by constructing and evaluating Local Epistemic World Models — directed typed graphs capturing agents, their ordered information access histories, and epistemic relationships — using discrete candidate evaluation against observed behavior without commitment to algorithmic or neural implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing formal accounts of Theory of Mind (Bayesian ToM, simulation theory, theory-theory) either presuppose rather than derive belief states or lack formal apparatus for epistemic state inference. A computational-level specification is needed that defines what mentalizing computes and why, independent of implementation.

Method: ToM-U constructs Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) — directed typed graphs with agents, state nodes, and epistemic relations. Five formal definitions specify: (1) LEWM structure, (2) agent node properties including ordered information access history, (3) a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing, (4) three inference procedures, and (5) a residue function capturing structured traces of failed mentalizing. Candidate LEWMs are evaluated against observed behavior until sufficient confidence is reached.

Result: ToM-U generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow directly from structural properties of the model rather than auxiliary assumptions. It positions itself as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other downstream social cognitive processes.

Conclusion: ToM-U provides a computational-level formalization of epistemic state inference that derives belief states from structured representation of agents' information access histories, offering a principled alternative to existing accounts and generating structurally-grounded, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failures.

Abstract: Inferring others' beliefs requires more than reading surface signals; it requires tracking who told them what, in what order, and how credibly. The Theory of Mind Utility (ToM-U) formalizes this epistemic state inference problem at the computational level of analysis, specifying what mentalizing computes and why without commitment to algorithmic or neural implementation. ToM-U achieves this by constructing Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) -- directed typed graphs that represent agents, state nodes, and the epistemic relationships among them -- and evaluating discrete candidate LEWMs against observed behavior until one achieves sufficient confidence. Five formal definitions specify the LEWM structure, agent node properties including ordered information access history, a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing, three inference procedures, and a residue function that captures the structured trace left by failed mentalizing attempts. ToM-U differs from Bayesian Theory of Mind and adjacent formal accounts, which presuppose rather than derive belief states, and from simulation theory and theory-theory, which lack a formal apparatus for epistemic state inference. The architecture generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow from structural properties of the model rather than auxiliary assumptions, and positions ToM-U as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other downstream social cognitive processes.

</details>


### [107] [Reward Modeling for Multi-Agent Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13598)
*King Yeung Tsang,Zihao Zhao,Vishal Venkataramani,Haizhou Shi,Zixuan Ke,Semih Yavuz,Shafiq Joty,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OrchRM is a self-supervised reward modeling framework that evaluates multi-agent orchestration quality without human labels, using intermediate execution artifacts to train Bradley-Terry reward models at the orchestration level—achieving up to 10x training efficiency gains and 8% accuracy improvements in MAS test-time scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training orchestrators for LLM-based multi-agent systems is bottlenecked by scarce supervision signals and prohibitive computational costs from sub-agent rollouts. Existing methods for test-time scaling and orchestrator training rely on expensive sub-agent execution, motivating a more efficient, orchestration-level approach.

Method: OrchRM constructs win-lose training pairs from intermediate artifacts (e.g., agent outputs, interaction traces) generated during multi-agent executions, then uses these pairs to train a Bradley-Terry reward model. The resulting reward model guides both orchestrator training and test-time scaling, bypassing costly sub-agent rollouts by operating directly at the orchestration level.

Result: OrchRM improves training token efficiency by up to 10x and boosts MAS test-time scaling accuracy by up to 8%. These improvements transfer consistently across mathematical reasoning, web-based QA, and multi-hop reasoning domains.

Conclusion: Orchestration-level reward modeling represents a scalable and robust direction for multi-agent orchestration, enabling efficient training and effective test-time scaling without human annotations.

Abstract: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) require effective orchestration to coordinate specialized agents, yet training such orchestrators is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost. We propose Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations. OrchRM leverages intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to construct win-lose pairs for Bradley-Terry reward model training. Unlike existing MAS test-time scaling and orchestrator training frameworks that rely on costly sub-agent rollouts, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level, enabling efficient and high-performing reward-guided orchestrator training and MAS test-time scaling. OrchRM improves training efficiency by up to 10x in token usage while improving MAS test-time scaling performance by up to 8% in accuracy. These gains consistently transfer across multiple domains, including mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning, demonstrating orchestration-level reward modeling as a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration. Code will be available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/OrchRM.

</details>


### [108] [Rethinking Psychometric Evaluation of LLMs: When and Why Self-Reports Predict Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12730)
*Rafal Kocielnik,Pengrui Han,Peiyang Song,Myrl G. Marmarelis,Ramit Debnath,Dean Mobbs,Anima Anandkumar,R. Michael Alvarez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show selective self-report–behavior coherence: Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) achieves human-level alignment within shared conversations, while Big 5 fails. Across separate conversations, coherence persists only for training-shaped implicit biases, not context-primed behaviors like sycophancy. Persona prompting improves SR consistency but not behavioral alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work found self-report–behavior dissociation in LLMs using Big 5 traits, but Big 5 weakly predicts specific behaviors even in humans. The authors challenge whether this dissociation reflects genuine incoherence or merely inadequate measurement (weak trait–behavior mapping, isolated sessions, poor context matching).

Method: The authors compare Big 5 against the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) — a framework measuring behavior-specific intentions — across four behavioral tasks and 11 frontier LLMs, while systematically varying session context (shared vs. separate conversations) and identity/persona induction.

Result: Three key findings: (1) TPB achieves human-level SR–behavior coherence within a shared conversation; Big 5 does not. (2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored outside the immediate prompt (e.g., training-shaped implicit bias), collapsing when context strongly primes behavior (e.g., sycophancy). (3) Persona prompting increases SR consistency across conversations but fails to align behavior with self-reports.

Conclusion: Coarse trait frameworks like Big 5 are inadequate for predicting LLM deployment behavior. Task- and behavior-specific instruments (e.g., TPB) are necessary, and even these require cross-task, cross-context evaluation to establish reliable behavioral predictions.

Abstract: Anticipating LLM behavioral tendencies from low-cost psychometric probes is critical for safe deployment, but only if self-reports (SR) reliably predict behavior. Recent work documented substantial SR-behavior dissociation in LLMs, but relied on broad personality traits (Big 5) that predict specific behaviors weakly, even in humans. Furthermore, the isolation of conversational sessions combined with weak context matching left open whether LLMs truly lack coherence or whether the conditions needed to detect such coherence were not met. We contrast Big 5 with the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which measures intention targeted to a specific behavior and predicts human behavior substantially better than broad traits. We run experiments across four behavioral tasks and 11 frontier LLMs, while also varying session context and identity induction. We find that SR-behavior coherence exists but is selective. 1) Within a shared conversation, the Theory of Planned Behavior reaches human-level coherence; Big 5 does not. 2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored outside the immediate prompt, such as implicit bias shaped by training, and collapses when behavior is strongly primed by context, as with sycophancy. 3) Persona prompting makes self-reports more consistent across conversations, but does not bring behavior into alignment. These findings suggest that coarse personality frameworks, such as Big 5 may not be the best tools for testing deployment behavior. More task- and behavior-specific instruments are needed, and even these must be evaluated across tasks and contexts.

</details>


### [109] [Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning from Delayed Marketplace Feedback for Objective-Weight Adaptation in Three-Sided Dispatch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13604)
*Haochen Wu,Yi Hou,Shiguang Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DoorDash deployed a reinforcement learning system that adjusts dispatch objective weights in a food-delivery marketplace using delayed operational signals. A store-level policy selects discrete multipliers via offline RL (Double Q-learning with conservative regularization), balancing delivery quality and batching efficiency without modifying the combinatorial optimizer. A switchback experiment showed increased batching and reduced courier time costs while maintaining delivery quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Three-sided marketplaces (customers, couriers, merchants) present a challenging RL setting where dispatch decisions are evaluated by delayed, noisy, and coupled operational outcomes. The authors aim to adapt dispatch tradeoffs online using world feedback without disrupting the production combinatorial optimizer, maintaining feasibility constraints and operational safeguards.

Method: The system uses a store-level policy that selects a discrete multiplier to shift the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. A shared value function is trained via offline RL using centralized logged marketplace data with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer (a la CQL) to mitigate out-of-distribution value overestimation. Execution is decentralized at the store level. The policy is evaluated through a production switchback experiment.

Result: In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increased batching and reduced courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality metrics.

Conclusion: World feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be effectively harnessed to safely adapt decision policies online. The approach demonstrates that offline RL with conservative regularization, combined with a lightweight policy interface over an existing optimizer, enables safe, practical deployment of RL in large-scale industrial marketplaces.

Abstract: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces provides a natural setting for reinforcement learning from world feedback: decisions are evaluated by delayed operational outcomes such as delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. We present a deployed reinforcement learning system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a large-scale food-delivery marketplace using delayed signals. Rather than replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, a store-level policy learned from logged marketplace data selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. This interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility constraints and operational safeguards. We train a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation. In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality. Results illustrate how world feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be used to safely adapt decision policies online.

</details>


### [110] [Benchmarking AI Agents for Addressing Scientific Challenges Across Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12736)
*Tianyu Liu,Allen Xin Wang,Antonia Panescu,Lisa Xinyi Chen,Wenxin Long,Xinyu Wei,Yueqian Jing,Ziyao Zeng,Jihang Chen,Sihan Jiang,Ziqing Wang,Siyi Gu,Siyu Chen,Xinyang Hu,Haoran Shao,Leqi Xu,Wangjie Zheng,Zhiyuan Cao,Ada Fang,Botao Yu,Kunyang Sun,Rex Ying,Arman Cohan,Qingyu Chen,Lingzhou Xue,Kaize Ding,Yuanqi Du,Wengong Jin,Zhuoran Yang,Marinka Zitnik,James Zou,Hua Xu,Hongyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciAgentArena is a benchmark with ~200 tasks and an interactive environment for evaluating AI agents on real-world scientific research scenarios. Current agents perform well on structured data-analysis tasks but struggle with generating novel insights, self-directed exploration, and open-ended problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI agent benchmarks fail to capture the complexity and extended reasoning of real scientific work, while scientific benchmarks oversimplify research into static, direct problems with limited interactive evaluation. There is a need for a benchmark that reflects authentic scientific research demands.

Method: The authors introduce SciAgentArena, a benchmark comprising roughly 200 tasks with stepwise verification, spanning multiple scientific domains. It provides an interactive, agent-agnostic environment for systematic evaluation. The benchmark is used to assess diverse AI agents on tasks drawn from emerging, real-world research needs.

Result: Current AI agents perform effectively on well-specified data-analysis workflows with clear task structures and evaluation criteria. However, performance is uneven across contexts: agents fail to generate genuinely novel insights, sustain self-directed exploration, or formulate robust solutions for open-ended research questions. Common failure modes are characterized.

Conclusion: SciAgentArena offers a practical framework for measuring progress in AI agents for science and for guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges. Opportunities for improving agent reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning are identified.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly being developed to accelerate scientific discovery, yet their practical capabilities in real research settings remain poorly understood. Existing benchmarks for AI agents rarely capture the complexity, heterogeneity, and extended reasoning required by scientific work, whereas benchmarks for scientific tasks often reduce research to static, direct problems and provide limited support for interactive evaluation. Here, we introduce SciAgentArena, a systematic benchmark for evaluating AI agents in real-world scientific research scenarios drawn from emerging needs across multiple domains. SciAgentArena comprises approximately 200 tasks with stepwise verification and an interactive, agent-agnostic environment for assessing diverse AI agents. Using this benchmark, we find that current agents can contribute effectively to well-specified data-analysis workflows, particularly when the task structure and evaluation criteria are clear. However, their performance remains uneven across scientific contexts: agents struggle to generate genuinely novel insights, sustain self-directed exploration, and formulate robust solutions for open-ended research questions. We further characterize common failure modes across agents and identify opportunities for improving their reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning. Together, SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring progress in AI agents for science and for guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges. Full codes, tasks, and datasets can be accessed via this link: https://sciagentarena.github.io/.

</details>


### [111] [Beyond Runtime Enforcement: Shield Synthesis as Defensibility Analysis for Adversarial Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13621)
*Achraf Hsain,Sultan Almuhammadi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper repositions shielded reinforcement learning from a runtime safety mechanism to a design-time analytical tool. Using a constrained two-player safety game for network defense, the authors derive a "defensibility verdict" and fingerprint—combining formal attractor-based metrics with empirical MARL behavior—to answer architectural questions about whether and how a system can be defended.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors argue that the prevailing framing of shielded RL—as a runtime mechanism that compiles temporal-logic specifications into action-restricting automata—is misguided. The true value lies in the same automata-theoretic machinery as a design-time analytical instrument that yields structural insights about a system's defensibility, not runtime constraints on deployed agents.

Method: The approach instantiates a constrained two-player safety game for network defense with asymmetric specification enforcement: the defender specification defines unsafe regions, while the attacker specification restricts the adversary's legal actions during attractor computation. Solving the game produces a formal defensibility verdict (a certificate that a topology-specification pair is or is not defensible), a winning region, and a shield. Topology-level metrics are derived from attractor structure and combined with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial MARL to form a "defensibility fingerprint." A what-if analysis then examines the relationship between formal defensibility and operational effectiveness.

Result: Formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct aspects of security. Small architectural changes can produce large shifts in operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged. This demonstrates that shield synthesis is most valuable as a framework for answering architectural questions about defensibility rather than as a deployment mechanism for safe agents.

Conclusion: The defensibility verdict—not the safe policy—should be viewed as the primary output of shield synthesis. The framework enables answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended, reframing shielded RL as a design-time analytical instrument rather than a runtime safety mechanism.

Abstract: Shielded reinforcement learning is typically presented as a runtime safety mechanism that compiles temporal-logic specifications into automata restricting an agent's actions. We argue this is the wrong product. The same automata-theoretic machinery -- specification compilation, product game construction, attractor computation, and winning-region extraction -- is better read as a design-time analytical instrument whose outputs are structural insights about a system rather than runtime constraints on a deployed agent.
  We instantiate this through a constrained two-player safety game for network defense. The two specifications are enforced asymmetrically: the defender specification defines the unsafe region of the game, whereas the attacker specification restricts the adversary's legal actions during attractor computation. Solving the game yields a defensibility verdict -- a formal certificate that a topology-specification pair is or is not defensible -- with the associated winning region and shield.
  Beyond the binary verdict, we derive topology-level metrics from the attractor structure and combine them with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent reinforcement learning. Together these form a defensibility fingerprint capturing both a network's formal safety properties and its operational behavior under adaptive play.
  A what-if analysis shows that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct aspects of security: small architectural changes can produce large shifts in operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged. Shield synthesis is thus most valuable not as a deployment mechanism for safe agents, but as a framework for answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended. The defensibility verdict is the output, not the safe policy.

</details>


### [112] [Reducing the Complexity of Deep Learning Models for EEG Analysis on Wearable Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12742)
*Farough Shayeste Roodi,Parham Zilouchian Moghaddam,Mahdi Mohammadi-nasab,Mehdi Modarressi,Mostafa Ersali Salehi Nasab,Masoud Daneshtalab*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Investigates deploying deep neural networks (DNNs) for EEG-based epileptic seizure detection on resource-constrained wearable devices, exploring accuracy vs. computational complexity trade-offs via parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable healthcare IoT devices demand automated ECG/EEG analysis via deep learning, yet DNNs impose computational, energy, and memory bandwidth requirements far exceeding the tight resource budgets of wearable hardware, hindering practical on-device deployment.

Method: The authors apply parameter quantization (reducing numerical precision of weights/activations) and electrode reduction (decreasing the number of EEG input channels) to several state-of-the-art DNN architectures designed for epileptic seizure detection from EEG signals. They then systematically evaluate the resulting accuracy vs. computational complexity trade-offs.

Result: Both parameter quantization and electrode reduction, when applied judiciously, significantly reduce DNN computational complexity with minimal degradation in seizure detection accuracy. The study explicitly quantifies the trade-off curves between accuracy retention and complexity reduction.

Conclusion: Deploying DNN-based online EEG analysis on wearable devices is feasible through careful application of compression techniques. The work provides explicit trade-off characterizations that can guide practical design decisions for resource-constrained wearable healthcare systems.

Abstract: Wearable healthcare devices are the fastest-growing Internet of Things (IoT) sector. Many automated healthcare services rely on two crucial biological signals, namely ECG and EEG, which reflect the activity of the heart and brain, respectively. Although deep neural networks are considered the primary way to process and analyze these signals, the very tight energy and computational power constraints in wearable devices are far below the computational, energy, and memory bandwidth demands of DNN models, thereby impeding the deployment of deep learning in many practical wearable services. This paper investigates the feasibility of deploying state-of-the-art DNN models in resource-constrained wearable devices. Notably, we explore the trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity of DNNs when parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods are used. Our investigation centers on several state-of-the-art DNN models designed for EEG signal analysis, specifically for detecting epileptic seizures.
