Our findings demonstrate that, when applied judiciously, these techniques can significantly reduce the complexity of the DNNs under consideration with minimal adverse effects on accuracy. These results reveal the explicit trade-offs between accuracy and complexity reduction encountered when adapting DNN-based online EEG analysis for wearable devices.

</details>


### [113] [Prefill Awareness in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12747)
*Andy Wang,Parv Mahajan,David Demitri Africa,Alexandra Souly,Jordan Taylor,Robert Kirk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier language models can detect when their prior assistant messages have been tampered with or prefilled, a capability termed "prefill awareness." Claude Opus 4.5 detects opposing prefills in 9-35% of cases with zero false positives. Detection relies on stylistic mismatch, while behavioral reversion relies on preference mismatch. This poses a confound for alignment evaluations, jailbreaking studies, and AI control protocols that use prefilling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many safety-relevant studies (alignment evaluations, jailbreaking, AI control protocols) depend on prefilling model outputs. If models can recognize and act upon the fact that their prior assistant messages have been inserted or edited, the validity of these methods is compromised. The authors aim to systematically investigate whether frontier models possess this "prefill awareness" capability.

Method: The authors construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms (preference-opposing, preference-neutral, and preference-aligned), filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. They evaluate frontier models' ability to detect tampered vs. untampered assistant-side context, measuring both explicit detection rates and behavioral reversion. Controlled ablations disentangle the roles of stylistic mismatch vs. preference mismatch. They also examine realistic agentic settings including misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories.

Result: Claude Opus 4.5 detects preference-opposing prefills in 9-35% of cases with a 0% false positive rate. Models often revert toward baseline behavior without explicitly flagging the prefill as foreign. Stylistic mismatch primarily drives explicit detection (flagging as foreign), while preference mismatch primarily drives behavioral reversion toward baseline answers. In agentic settings, models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns, with behavior depending strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts.

Conclusion: Prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based safety methods. The authors recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems, as it threatens the validity of alignment evaluations, jailbreaking studies, and AI control protocols that rely on prefilling.

Abstract: Safety-relevant studies of language models, including alignment and jailbreaking evaluations and AI control protocols, often rely on prefilling model outputs. If AI models can recognize and act on the fact their prior assistant messages have been inserted or edited, the effectiveness and validity of these methods could be compromised. We investigate whether frontier language models can distinguish between tampered and untampered assistant-side context, a capability we call prefill awareness. To do so, we construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms, filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. We find that frontier models show substantial prefill awareness: Claude Opus 4.5 detects prefills opposing its preferences in 9-35% of cases with a 0% false positive rate when prompted; additionally, models often revert towards baseline behavior without explicitly reporting that the prefill was foreign. Controlled ablations later also show that detection and resistance rely on different cues, where stylistic mismatch mainly affects whether models flag a prefill as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly affects whether they revert toward their baseline answer. We also examine more realistic agentic settings such as misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories, where frontier models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns in ways that depend strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts. Our results indicate that prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based methods. We recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems.

</details>


### [114] [Constructing Evaluation Datasets for Procedural Reasoning: Balancing Naturalness, Grounding, and Multi-Hop Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12767)
*Sarah Elshabrawy,Rahul K. Dass,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper compares three strategies for generating question-answer datasets from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models for evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning. Strict TMK generation achieves the strongest grounding (96.5%) and usability (92.6%), while transcript-based methods produce more natural but less grounded questions. The authors introduce a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units, demonstrating that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires QA datasets that are both learner-like (natural, authentic) and grounded in the instructional knowledge the system uses. Existing generation approaches may sacrifice one quality for the other, and no systematic comparison exists for how TMK-based generation strategies affect dataset quality, particularly for multi-hop procedural reasoning.

Method: The authors compare three question-generation strategies: (1) strict generation directly from TMK models, (2) transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and (3) TMK-aware generation combining transcripts with structured TMK guidance. They introduce a grounding validation framework using closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models to measure answer support, question self-containedness, and multi-hop procedural reasoning targeting. Evaluation covers 23 instructional topics with 690 generated QA pairs.

Result: Strict TMK generation achieved 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions — the strongest overall quality. Transcript-first generation produced more learner-like, natural questions but also more context-dependent and weakly grounded items. TMK-aware generation yielded high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding scores. The trade-off demonstrates that natural phrasing and procedural richness do not inherently ensure representational grounding.

Conclusion: Procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding in QA datasets for AI-supported learning. Explicit representation-aware validation is necessary when constructing evaluation datasets, and strict TMK-based generation offers the most reliable grounding despite being less natural than transcript-based alternatives.

Abstract: Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires question-answer datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in the instructional knowledge the system is expected to use. We study how TMK-based question generation strategies affect dataset quality for procedural and multi-hop reasoning.
  We compare three strategies: strict generation from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and TMK-aware generation that combines transcripts with structured guidance. To evaluate generated items, we introduce a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models. The framework measures whether answers are supported by the underlying representation, whether questions are self-contained, and whether they target multi-hop procedural reasoning.
  Across 23 instructional topics and 690 generated question-answer pairs, strict TMK generation achieves the strongest overall quality, with 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produces more learner-like questions but more context-dependent or weakly grounded items, while TMK-aware generation yields high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding. These results show that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding, motivating explicit representation-aware validation for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning.

</details>


### [115] [A Tutorial on World Models and Physical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12783)
*Il-Seok Oh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This tutorial paper surveys world modeling paradigms for intelligent systems, distinguishing between explicit world models (structured dynamics for rollout-based planning) and implicit world models (predictive structure in learned representations). It frames these as complementary foundations for physical AI and discusses how foundation models enable unified perception-prediction-action systems, while identifying key challenges in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision-making, yet the field lacks a unified framework. The authors aim to clarify the landscape by distinguishing explicit and implicit paradigms, and to identify pathways toward artificial general intelligence by integrating these approaches with foundation models.

Method: The paper adopts a tutorial-style conceptual synthesis. It organizes diverse world modeling approaches along a single axis: shared predictive structure. The key differentiation is how that structure is represented and exploited — explicitly via structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning, or implicitly via scalable learned representations that encode predictive information.

Result: The analysis yields a coherent taxonomy where explicit and implicit world models are complementary rather than competing. Foundation models are identified as a promising pathway toward unified systems that integrate perception, prediction, and action. The paper also identifies critical open challenges: hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation, which remain bottlenecks for advancing toward AGI.

Conclusion: World modeling provides a unifying principle for physical AI across robotics and autonomous driving. Explicit and implicit approaches offer complementary strengths, and their integration with foundation models points toward more general intelligent systems. However, substantial progress is still needed in hierarchical reasoning, extended temporal planning, and self-directed goal generation to approach artificial general intelligence.

Abstract: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision making. A central distinction can be drawn between explicit world models, which learn structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning, and implicit world models, which encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations. These complementary paradigms provide a foundation for physical AI in domains such as robotics and autonomous driving, enabling intelligence beyond reactive control under real-world constraints. Recent foundation models further suggest a pathway toward unified systems integrating perception, prediction, and action. Despite rapid progress, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation, which are critical for advancing toward artificial general intelligence. This tutorial presents a coherent framework in which diverse world modeling approaches are unified through shared predictive structure and differentiated by how such structure is represented and exploited.

</details>


### [116] [The Containment Gap: How Deployed Agentic AI Frameworks Fail Public-Facing Safety Requirements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12797)
*Md Jafrin Hossain,Mohammad Arif Hossain,Weiqi Liu,Nirwan Ansari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Current agentic AI frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) lack architectural-level safety guarantees. A memory-poisoning attack on a LangChain-based government benefits agent caused 88.9% wrongful denial rates. Two proposed lightweight containment mechanisms eliminate these vulnerabilities with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic LLM systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes public-facing domains (government services, healthcare, finance), yet it is unclear whether the frameworks used to build them provide structural safety guarantees. The authors seek to audit whether dominant frameworks offer native containment against known vulnerability classes, particularly memory integrity attacks.

Method: The authors derive six containment principles from a compositional model of agentic architectures. They audit three frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) against these principles. They then empirically validate findings via a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, testing memory-poisoning attacks across all seeds and backends under both simple and complex five-factor policy scenarios. Finally, they propose and evaluate two countermeasures: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate.

Result: None of the three frameworks natively comply with any of the six containment principles. Memory integrity is absent across all frameworks. In the LangChain simulation, a single memory-poisoning write produces persistent corruption, raising the wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%. Under a complex five-factor policy, the attack increases targeted wrongful denials by 3.5× while preserving aggregate accuracy, evading standard monitoring. The proposed memory integrity validator and policy gate eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call).

Conclusion: The agentic framework ecosystem does not meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments. The authors outline priority architectural interventions needed to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications, and demonstrate that lightweight containment mechanisms can be effective without significant performance cost.

Abstract: Agentic large language model systems that autonomously invoke tools, maintain persistent memory, and execute multi-step plans are increasingly deployed in public-facing domains, including government services, healthcare triage, and financial advising. We ask whether the frameworks used to build these systems provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees. Applying six containment principles derived from a compositional model of agentic architectures, we audit three dominant frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, and OpenAI Agents SDK) and find no native compliance in any of them. Memory integrity, a defense against one of the most prevalent vulnerability classes, is not observed in any of the three evaluated frameworks. We validate these findings empirically: in a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, a single memory-poisoning write induces persistent targeted corruption across all tested seeds and backends, increasing the wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%. Under a complex five-factor policy, the same attack preserves aggregate accuracy while increasing targeted wrongful denials by 3.5x, rendering the corruption difficult to detect through standard monitoring. We then introduce two lightweight containment mechanisms: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate, which eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call). We conclude that the current agentic framework ecosystem may not yet meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments and outline priority architectural interventions to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

</details>


### [117] [MLUBench: A Benchmark for Lifelong Unlearning Evaluation in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12809)
*He Li,Haoang Chi,Qizhou Wang,Yunxin Mao,Zhiheng Zhang,Jie Tan,Tongliang Liu,Wenjing Yang,Bo Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MLUBench is a large-scale benchmark for evaluating lifelong unlearning in multimodal LLMs, covering 127 entities across 9 classes. The paper identifies that existing unlearning methods suffer cumulative degradation, largely due to the need to preserve multimodal alignment, and proposes LUMoE to mitigate this issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data owners increasingly request removal of specific content from MLLMs, and these requests arrive sequentially (lifelong unlearning). Existing benchmarks are too limited in scale and scope to capture the real-world complexities of this problem.

Method: The authors introduce MLUBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 127 entities across 9 classes for sequential unlearning evaluation. They also propose LUMoE (Lifelong Unlearning via Mixture of Experts), an effective method designed to mitigate cumulative degradation while preserving multimodal alignment during continual unlearning.

Result: Extensive experiments on MLUBench reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer severe, cumulative degradation. LUMoE significantly mitigates this degradation compared to baselines, addressing the core challenge of preserving multimodal alignment during sequential unlearning.

Conclusion: MLLM lifelong unlearning is uniquely challenging due to the multimodal alignment constraint. MLUBench provides a standardized evaluation framework, and LUMoE offers an effective solution that substantially reduces the degradation seen in prior methods.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are trained on massive multimodal data, making data unlearning increasingly important as data owners may request the removal of specific content. In practice, these requests often arrive sequentially over time, giving rise to the challenging problem of MLLM Lifelong Unlearning. However, most existing benchmarks are limited in scale and scope, failing to capture the complexities of MLLM lifelong unlearning. To fill this gap, we introduce the MLUBench, a large-scale and comprehensive benchmark featuring 127 entities across 9 classes under lifelong unlearning requests. We perform extensive experiments using MLUBench and reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer from severe, cumulative degradation. More critically, we further identify the unique challenge of this problem: unlike in unimodal models, MLLM lifelong unlearning is constrained by the need to preserve multimodal alignment. Continually unlearning from one modality could degrade the entire model. To alleviate this challenge, we propose LUMoE, an effective method. Experiments demonstrate that LUMoE significantly mitigates the degradation problem faced by baselines. The source code and the MLUBench dataset are open-sourced in https://github.com/lihe-maxsize/Lifelong_Unlearning_main.

</details>


### [118] [Teach-and-Repeat: Accurately Extracting Operational Knowledge from Mobile Screen Demonstrations to Empower GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12817)
*Yudong Zhang,Lei Hu,Daoyang Liu,Jiawei Liu,Yangfan Luo,Xingyu Liu,Zuojian Wang,Zhilin Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Teach VLM, a vision-language model that translates mobile screen demonstration videos into step-wise operational knowledge (natural-language descriptions of actions, UI elements, arguments, and execution order). It features a data flywheel for scalable training data acquisition, a Chinese benchmark for evaluation, and a Teach-and-Repeat paradigm where generated knowledge guides downstream execution agents, achieving SOTA performance on operation semantics prediction and improved task success rates in Android World.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile UI understanding needs to evolve from static perception to dynamic action comprehension — inferring the operational knowledge (action types, target elements, textual arguments, execution order) underlying visual state transitions. However, existing VLMs struggle with this due to highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications, and there is a scarcity of aligned training data for this task.

Method: The authors propose Teach VLM, a core model that extracts and analyzes operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos to produce step-wise operational knowledge. They develop a systematic data flywheel for scalable acquisition of aligned training data and introduce a novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. Building on Teach VLM, they propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm, using the generated operational knowledge as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents.

Result: Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. In Android World experiments, the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents.

Conclusion: Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm together provide a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation, bridging the gap between visual state transitions and executable operational knowledge on mobile devices.

Abstract: Understanding the digital world on mobile devices is shifting from static UI perception to dynamic action comprehension. This capability enables models to convert visual state transitions into operational knowledge, defined as short natural-language sentences that describe action types, target UI elements, textual arguments, and execution orders. However, due to the highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications, existing vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to accurately infer these underlying operations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Teach VLM, a core model designed to translate mobile screen trajectories into step-wise operational knowledge by extracting and analyzing operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos. To address the scarcity of aligned training data, we develop a systematic data flywheel for scalable data acquisition. We further introduce a novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. Building upon Teach VLM, we propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm, where the generated operational knowledge serves as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. Furthermore, experiments in Android World show that our paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents. Together, Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm offer a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation.

</details>


### [119] [GeoNatureAgent Benchmark: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Environmental Geospatial Analysis Across Frontier and Open-Weight Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12821)
*Gabriel Diaz-Ireland,Diego Prieto-Herráez,Mario García Peces,Javier Velázquez,Devika Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark for evaluating LLM-based AI agents that perform environmental analysis via structured tool calls against a real geospatial API. Tests 7 LLMs on 93 tasks across 18 categories; Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8%, structured API calling proves far more discriminative than existing GIS benchmarks, and open-weight models dominate the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate time on data wrangling rather than analysis. While AI agents could automate geospatial workflows, no benchmark exists to evaluate agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs — existing GIS benchmarks lack real API interaction and fail to capture the challenges of production-style tool use.

Method: The benchmark comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories (municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, task rejection). Evaluation uses a self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. Seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) are tested under three temperature-1.0 seeds, with capability and per-case cost reported as orthogonal axes.

Result: Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% ± 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% ± 3.1%, with no other model above 51%. The cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is dominated by open-weight models; DeepSeek V3.2 offers 93% of Claude's capability at 11× lower cost ($0.011/case). Comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons). Structured tool calling against a real API yields accuracies 25–35 points lower than general-purpose GIS benchmarks, demonstrating higher discriminative power.

Conclusion: Structured tool calling against real APIs is a more discriminative and ecologically valid evaluation paradigm than traditional GIS benchmarks. Open-weight models approach frontier closed-source performance at dramatically lower cost. Systematic weaknesses in comparison and spatial reasoning remain unsolved. The benchmark, harness, and API are publicly released, with demonstrated extensibility via BigEarthNet V2 integration.

Abstract: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate effort on data wrangling rather than analysis, and AI agents that automate geospatial workflows remain unvalidated: no benchmark evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs. We introduce the GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark for environmental analysis agents that operate via structured tool calls to a production-style geospatial API. It comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories, covering municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling and recovery, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, and task rejection. Tasks are evaluated against an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. We evaluate seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) under three temperature-1.0 seeds, reporting capability and per-case cost as orthogonal axes. We find: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% +/- 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% +/- 3.1%, with no other model above 51%; (2) the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is occupied mostly by open-weight models, with DeepSeek V3.2 offering 93% of Claude's capability at 11x lower cost ($0.011/case); (3) comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits; and (4) structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25-35 points lower. We further show extensibility by integrating BigEarthNet V2 land cover for Portugal alongside Spanish CO2 and erosion indicators. The benchmark, harness, and self-hostable API are publicly available.

</details>


### [120] [Topical Phase Transitions in Artificial Intelligence Research: Large-Scale Evidence and an Early-Warning Signature for Emerging Topics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12828)
*Rasul Khanbayov,Hasan Kurban*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analysis of 80,814 AI papers (2017–2025) from five top conferences reveals that major AI topics undergo abrupt "phase transitions" — surging from marginal to dominant within 1–3 years — rather than growing gradually. The authors characterize this phenomenon across venues, develop an early-warning signature to predict future transitions (63% recall, 27% precision), and flag reasoning, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, RAG, and world models as topics to watch for 2026–2028.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how AI research topics emerge and grow is critical for anticipating future research directions. Prior work lacks a large-scale, cross-venue characterization of whether topics grow gradually or through abrupt shifts. The authors aim to empirically identify and characterize "topical phase transitions" and test whether they can be predicted before they peak.

Method: The authors collected 80,814 accepted main-track papers from ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS (2017–2025). They performed large-scale topic modeling across venues to identify topics and track their publication dynamics over time. They define topical phase transitions as topics that remain marginal for years then surge across venues within 1–3 years. They then define an early-warning signature using four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017–2021 data, and evaluate it out-of-sample on 2023–2025 transitions. Finally, they apply the signature to 2025 data to flag candidate topics for 2026–2028.

Result: LLMs became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025, diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness, and LM methods crossed into CV via vision-language models, while reinforcement learning grew smoothly. The early-warning signature achieved 27% precision and 63% recall against a 13.5% base rate. For 2025 data, flagged topics include: reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models.

Conclusion: AI research topics advance primarily through abrupt phase transitions rather than gradual growth. These transitions exhibit a detectable early-warning signature, enabling forecast of future surges. The flagged topics for 2026–2028 suggest the next wave of AI research will center on reasoning, agency, multimodality, retrieval, and world models. The cross-venue, large-scale methodology and open-source code provide a foundation for ongoing monitoring of AI research dynamics.

Abstract: Do research topics in artificial intelligence grow gradually, or do they advance through abrupt, detectable jumps? Analyzing 80,814 accepted main-track papers from five premier AI conferences (ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS) spanning 2017 to 2025, we show major AI topics advance through topical phase transitions: remaining marginal for years, then surging across venues within one to three years. Large language models became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025, diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness, and language-model methods crossed into computer vision via vision-language models, whereas reinforcement learning compounded smoothly, distinguishing genuine phase transitions from ordinary growth. This structure is our primary contribution: a large-scale, cross-venue characterization of how AI research reorganizes. We then ask whether a transition leaves a detectable footprint before it peaks. We define an early-warning signature, four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017-2021 data, and evaluate it out of sample on 2023-2025 transitions, obtaining a precision of 27% and recall of 63% against a 13.5% base rate. Applied to 2025 data, the signature flags reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models as topics to monitor over 2026-2028. The source code is also publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/ai-phase-transitions.

</details>


### [121] [Fantastic Scientific Agents and How to Build Them: AgentBuild for Rietveld Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12834)
*Woong Shin,Craig A. Bridges,Marshall T. McDonnell,Rafael Ferreira da Silva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentBuild proposes a framework where scientists author a version-controlled "contract" (rubric, curriculum, and knowledge base) that guides an automated meta-optimizer to construct LLM-based scientific agents, preserving the scientist's judgment as a durable, re-runnable asset rather than burying it in ad-hoc tuning. The approach is demonstrated on Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-agent development paradigms (fine-tuning, RL, prompt-and-go) embed scientific judgment opaquely into model parameters or prompts, making it fragile, unreproducible, and version-inseparable. As base models evolve, scientific knowledge is lost. The authors seek to externalize and preserve the scientist's judgment as an explicit, version-controlled artifact that survives model changes.

Method: AgentBuild treats agent construction as a workflow stage. The scientist authors a contract comprising: (1) a rubric used by a judge to score agent outputs, (2) a difficulty-graded curriculum of tasks, and (3) a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the scientific agent within declared boundaries. The system compiles the agent via iterative improvement against the contract. Instantiated for Rietveld refinement using GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A protocols, tested on LLZO data across a signal-to-noise ladder culminating in a 4-hour scan frontier case.

Result: The blank-harness construction run successfully progressed through the LLZO signal-to-noise ladder, reaching the 4-hour scan as a frontier case that exposed workflow-scope limits. Critically, the rubric scores both fit quality and trajectory scope, so frontier failures are classified as contract failures rather than pattern-fitting failures. Re-running AgentBuild with updated base models constitutes a re-tune, not a rebuild.

Conclusion: AgentBuild reifies the scientist's judgment into a durable, version-controlled contract that persists across base model generations. The contract becomes the primary scientific asset, enabling reproducible, auditable, and evolvable construction of scientific AI agents. The approach separates what the scientist authors (the contract) from what the system compiles (the agent), with the frontier case analysis providing clear scope delineation.

Abstract: As scientific workflows shift from deterministic executables to LLM-based agents, the development practices on offer, such as fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and prompt-and-go, bury the scientist's judgment. We propose treating agent construction as a workflow stage and introduce AgentBuild, which builds a scientific agent from a contract the scientist authors. The contract is a version-controlled rubric, a difficulty-graded curriculum, and a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within a declared boundary, so the build compiles the agent, not the scientist's judgment. We instantiate this for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data through GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A, where a blank-harness construction run progresses through a lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) signal-to-noise ladder, reaches the 4 hour scan as a frontier case, and exposes the workflow-scope limits that remain. The same rubric that rewards credible fits also scores trajectory scope, making the frontier a contract failure rather than a pattern-fitting failure. As base models evolve, re-running AgentBuild is a re-tune, not a rebuild, and the scientist's authored contract remains the durable asset.

</details>


### [122] [(Human) Attention Is (Still) All You Need: Human oversight makes AI-assisted social science reliable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12848)
*Chen Zhu,Xiaolu Wang,Weilong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HLER, a human-in-the-loop architecture with three binding commitments (LLMs reason only, deterministic computation, human decision gates), reduced AI-assisted economic research failure rates from 72% to 16% across 280 runs, demonstrating that cognitive labour structure—not just model capability—drives reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed for research tasks traditionally performed by trained scientists, but reliability remains poorly understood. The authors argue that failure depends not only on model capability but on how cognitive labour is organized between humans and machines. No systematic framework exists for structuring this division to minimize catastrophic errors in AI-assisted research.

Method: The authors propose Human-in-the-Loop Economic Research (HLER), a decision architecture built on pre-commitment, decision sequencing, accountability, and attention allocation. They conduct a pre-specified 2×4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, comparing an unconstrained multi-agent baseline against HLER. HLER imposes three architectural commitments: (1) LLMs reason but never execute data work, (2) data and estimation are handled deterministically, and (3) three human decision gates bind the workflow. An 80-run ablation isolates the contributions of deterministic computation and human gates. Fisher's exact test is used for inference.

Result: The unconstrained baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs. HLER reduced the failure rate to 16% using the same underlying model, agent decomposition, and prompts (Fisher's exact test, p<0.001). Reliability gains were largest on the least publicly represented dataset (a Qing-dynasty population register), consistent with a task-based production model with Fréchet-distributed output quality. The ablation suggests deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently, with exploratory evidence of complementarity.

Conclusion: HLER functions as a research harness rather than an autonomous scientist: it sharply reduces failures, renders residual weaknesses more visible, and prevents unreliable claims from advancing as publication-ready outputs. The findings imply that structuring cognitive labour—not merely improving models—is essential for trustworthy AI-assisted research.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks once reserved for trained researchers, including hypothesis generation, specification choice, and drafting conclusions. We argue that the reliability of AI-assisted research depends not only on model capability, but also on how cognitive labour is structured between humans and machines. We study this problem through Human-in-the-Loop Economic Research (HLER), a decision architecture based on pre-commitment, decision sequencing, accountability, and attention allocation. In a pre-specified 2*4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, an unconstrained multi-agent baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs. Using the same underlying model, the same agent decomposition, and identical prompts for the shared reasoning agents, HLER reduced the failure rate to 16% by imposing three architectural commitments: LLMs reason but do not execute data work, data and estimation are handled deterministically, and three human decision gates bind the workflow. Fisher's exact test rejects equality of failure rates at p<0.001. Reliability gains were largest on the least publicly represented dataset, a Qing-dynasty population register, consistent with a task-based production model with Frechet-distributed output quality. An 80-run ablation suggests that deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently, with exploratory evidence of complementarity. We interpret HLER as a research harness rather than an autonomous AI scientist: it sharply reduces failures, makes residual weaknesses more visible, and prevents unreliable claims from being advanced as publication-ready outputs.

</details>


### [123] [WISE: A Long-Horizon Agent in Minecraft with Why-Which Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12852)
*Renmin Cheng,Changhao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WISE is a long-horizon embodied agent framework that enhances low-level controllers with a Causal Event Graph — augmenting episodic memory with explicit causal structure (what-where-when + which-why). It introduces an Opportunistic Task Scheduler for dynamic subtask re-prioritization and a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy, achieving improved task success and efficiency on sparse, long-horizon Minecraft tasks requiring adaptive decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-augmented hierarchical embodied agents (e.g., in Minecraft) suffer from low-level controller failures due to repeated execution errors. The authors identify two key limitations: (1) lack of episodic memory, and (2) the decoupling of spatiotemporal memory (what-where-when) from causal reasoning (which-why). Prior retrieval methods like MrSteve rely on feature similarity and fail under viewpoint changes, lacking causal understanding of why observations matter for tasks.

Method: The authors propose WISE with three core components: (1) A Causal Event Graph that enriches episodic memory with explicit causal structure, linking observations to task relevance via which-why reasoning, enabling robust recall under viewpoint shifts. (2) An Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities arise. (3) A multi-scale progressive exploration strategy providing spatially comprehensive observations for downstream reasoning and decision-making.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that WISE significantly improves task success rates and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making. The causal memory and opportunistic scheduling outperform prior feature-similarity-based retrieval approaches like MrSteve.

Conclusion: Integrating causal structure into episodic memory (which-why reasoning) is critical for robust low-level control in hierarchical embodied agents. WISE's Causal Event Graph combined with opportunistic task scheduling and multi-scale exploration provides a more adaptive, efficient solution for long-horizon sparse-reward tasks in environments like Minecraft.

Abstract: Rapid advances have been made in developing general-purpose embodied agent in environments like Minecraft through the adoption of LLM-augmented hierarchical approaches. Despite their promise, low-level controllers often become performance bottlenecks due to repeated execution failures. We argue that a key limitation is not only the lack of episodic memory, but also the decoupling of \textit{what-where-when} memory from \textit{which-why} reasoning. To address this, we propose \textbf{WISE} (Which-Why Informed Semantic Explorer), a long-horizon agent framework with an enhanced low-level controller equipped with a Causal Event Graph that augments episodic memory with explicit causal structure linking observations to task relevance. Unlike prior work such as MrSteve, which relies on feature similarity for retrieval, WISE enables robust recall under viewpoint changes and supports opportunistic task reordering through causal reasoning. Building on this memory, we propose an Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities are detected. We further equip WISE with a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy to provide spatially comprehensive observations for downstream reasoning. Experiments show that WISE largely improves task success and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making.

</details>


### [124] [DailyReport: An Open-ended Benchmark for Evaluating Search Agents on Daily Search Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12871)
*Jingxuan Han,Wei Liu,Mingyang Zhu,Youpeng Wang,Ziwen Wang,Lin Qiu,Xuezhi Cao,Xunliang Cai,Zheren Fu,Licheng Zhang,Zhendong Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DailyReport is a benchmark with 150 open-ended daily search tasks and 3,546 cascade rubrics for evaluating Search Agents (SAs). It enables fine-grained, interpretable evaluation across disentangled dimensions through subtask decomposition and user-centric aggregation, revealing that current agentic systems still underperform relative to user expectations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SA benchmarks focus on specialized, unrealistic tasks and rely on coarse task-level rubrics that lack interpretability. There is a need for evaluation that reflects real-world, daily information-seeking behaviors with fine-grained, dimension-level diagnostics.

Method: The authors introduce DailyReport, a benchmark of 150 open-ended daily search tasks derived from real-world user information demands. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and annotated with cascade rubrics across multiple disentangled evaluation dimensions (3,546 rubrics total). Cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation produce interpretable per-dimension scores and an overall user preference score. Seventeen agentic systems are evaluated under this framework.

Result: Evaluation of 17 agentic systems on DailyReport shows that current SAs consistently fall short of meeting user expectations, with performance gaps identified across multiple fine-grained dimensions.

Conclusion: DailyReport provides a more realistic, interpretable, and diagnostically useful evaluation framework for SAs. The persistent performance gap highlights the need for continued advancement in agentic search systems. The dataset and code are publicly released to spur further research.

Abstract: Search Agents (SAs) typically leverage large language models (LLMs) to support complex information-seeking tasks by autonomously exploring web sources and synthesizing information into comprehensive responses. For SAs evaluation, prior benchmarks mainly focus on specialized tasks that are unlikely to arise in real-world user scenarios. Moreover, their reliance on coarse task-level rubrics often limits evaluation interpretability. To bridge this gap, we introduce DailyReport, an open-ended benchmark to evaluate SA capabilities on daily search tasks. It contains 150 open-ended tasks with 3,546 associated rubrics, capturing widely discussed and timely information demands of real-world users. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and evaluated with cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions. Through cascade performance attribution and user-centric aggregation, we derive highly interpretable scores for each dimension, along with a user preference score. Our results on 17 agentic systems show that current systems still fall short of users' expectations. To facilitate future research, our dataset and code are made publicly available at https://github.com/AGI-Eval-Official/DailyReport.

</details>


### [125] [HarnessBridge: Learnable Bidirectional Controller for LLM Agent Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12882)
*Xiaoxuan Wang,Haixin Wang,Alexander Taylor,Jason Cong,Yizhou Sun,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HarnessBridge is a lightweight, learnable harness controller that replaces manually engineered agent-environment interfaces with end-to-end trainable bidirectional projections. It distills raw trajectories into compact states (observation projection) and converts proposed actions into executable transitions or rejections (action projection). Trained via instruction tuning, it matches or surpasses specialized harnesses on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-bench Verified while reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes across model scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing harnesses that mediate agent-environment interaction are manually engineered, making them brittle and difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. The authors aim to replace hand-crafted harness logic with a learnable, end-to-end trainable module that can adaptively optimize the agent-environment interface.

Method: The authors propose HarnessBridge, a parameterized harness controller with two bidirectional projections: (1) observation projection, which compresses raw trajectories into compact decision-relevant states, and (2) action projection, which maps proposed actions to executable transitions or issues trajectory-grounded rejections. It is trained on a harness supervision dataset using unified instruction tuning.

Result: HarnessBridge matches or outperforms strong specialized harnesses on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-bench Verified. It significantly reduces token consumption and trajectory length. Additionally, harnesses trained with smaller generators generalize effectively to larger commercial models.

Conclusion: A learnable plug-in harness controller is a viable and effective alternative to manually engineered harnesses, offering performance parity or gains alongside efficiency improvements and cross-model generalization.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as agents for long-horizon tasks, yet their performance is shaped not only by model capability and environment design, but also by the harness that mediates agent--environment interaction. Existing harnesses are largely manually engineered, making them difficult to scale as trajectories grow longer and interactions become more complex. In this work, we ask whether harness can be generated by a learnable plug-in module that can be trained in an end-to-end fashion. We introduce HarnessBridge, a lightweight learnable harness controller that parameterizes the agent--environment interface as a bidirectional projection. HarnessBridge learns two bidirectional projections: observation projection, which distills raw trajectories into compact, decision-relevant states, and action projection, which converts proposed actions into executable transitions or trajectory-grounded rejections. We train HarnessBridge on a harness supervision dataset via unified instruction tuning. On Terminal-Bench~2.0 and SWE-bench Verified, HarnessBridge matches or surpasses strong specialized harnesses while substantially reducing token usage and trajectory length, and generalizes from smaller generators to larger commercial models.

</details>


### [126] [The Hidden Power of Scaling Factor in LoRA Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12883)
*Zicheng Zhang,Haoran Li,Jiaxing Wang,Guoqiang Gong,Anqi Li,Yudong Hu,Ting Xiong,Yurong Gao,Junxing Hu,Zhida Jiang,Yifeng Zhang,Pengzhang Liu,Qixia Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LoRA's scaling factor α, often overlooked, is revealed as the dominant optimization driver distinct from the learning rate. The paper introduces a Signal-Drift framework showing α amplifies task signals without increasing drift, follows a square-root law with rank, and enables LoRA-α, a minimalist method that works with standard small learning rates while improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scaling factor α in LoRA is typically treated as a secondary hyperparameter tied to the learning rate, but its distinct role in optimization is poorly understood. The authors aim to clarify this role and exploit it for better, more efficient LoRA training.

Method: The authors combine extensive empirical analysis with a theoretical Signal-Drift framework. They analyze LoRA's spectral suppression properties, decouple the effects of α and learning rate on optimization dynamics, and derive the relationship between optimal α and rank. Based on these insights, they propose LoRA-α, a minimalist approach that sets α according to principled scaling laws rather than rank-tied heuristics.

Result: Three key findings: (1) LoRA's spectral suppression smooths optimization, making standard hyperparameters overly conservative; (2) α outperforms learning rate by amplifying task signal without increasing drift ratio; (3) optimal α follows a square-root law with rank (with a large coefficient), exposing shortcomings of existing rank-tied heuristics. LoRA-α consistently improves performance across diverse tasks while simplifying hyperparameter search.

Conclusion: The scaling factor α is not merely complementary to the learning rate but is the dominant driver of effective LoRA optimization. Restoring α to its principled regime via LoRA-α unleashes LoRA's learning potential, enabling compatibility with standard small learning rates and streamlined hyperparameter tuning.

Abstract: In Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), the scaling factor $α$ is often treated as a mere complement to the learning rate, yet its role in optimization remains poorly understood. In this paper, we reveal that the scaling factor $α$ and the learning rate function differently, with $α$ emerging as the dominant driver of effective optimization, delivering gains that cannot be replicated by learning rate scaling alone. Through the synergy of extensive empirical analysis and a theoretical Signal-Drift framework, we uncover three findings into LoRA's scaling mechanism: First, LoRA's spectral suppression smooths the optimization landscape, rendering standard hyperparameters overly conservative and creating an optimization gap. Second, when leveraging this smoothness to accelerate convergence, $α$ outperforms the learning rate by amplifying the task signal without increasing the drift ratio. Third, the optimal scaling factor follows a sublinear relationship with the rank, well characterized by a square-root law with an unexpectedly large coefficient, revealing the insufficient scaling of existing rank-tied heuristics. Based on these insights, we propose LoRA-$α$, a minimalist framework that restores $α$ to its principled regime, making LoRA compatible with standard small learning rates. Extensive evaluations across diverse tasks demonstrate that LoRA-$α$ consistently improves performance while streamlining hyperparameter search, unleashing the learning potential of LoRA.

</details>


### [127] [Zero-source LLM Hallucination Detection with Human-like Criteria Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12900)
*Jiahao Yang,Shuhai Zhang,Hailong Kang,Feng Liu,Qi Chen,Mingkui Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HCPD is a zero-source hallucination detection method that uses a reward-aligned LLM agent to adaptively decompose judgments into weighted, interpretable criteria, then aggregates multi-sampled scores for robust and explainable detection, outperforming existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate, and detecting these hallucinations is critical for safety. The zero-source constraint—where no model internals or external references are available—makes detection especially difficult, as one must rely solely on the query-answer pair. Existing methods lack the nuanced, multi-faceted reasoning that human evaluators employ.

Method: The core is the Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) mechanism: an LLM agent adaptively decomposes its judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores into a final truthfulness measure. Adaptation is achieved via a reward-based alignment scheme using only weak supervision from semantic consistency. At inference, a multi-sampling aggregation strategy ensures robust, interpretable decisions. A theoretical analysis supports the approach's reliability.

Result: Extensive experiments show that HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines for zero-source hallucination detection.

Conclusion: HCPD offers an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection, combining adaptive criteria decomposition, weak-supervision alignment, and multi-sampling aggregation to surpass existing methods while maintaining full interpretability.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often hallucinate by generating factually incorrect or unfaithful content, posing significant risks to their safe use. Detecting such hallucinations is particularly challenging under the zero-source constraint, where no model internals or external references are available, and detection must rely solely on the textual query-answer pair. In this paper, we propose Human-like Criteria Probing for Hallucination Detection (HCPD), a paradigm that emulates the multi-faceted reasoning of human evaluators. Its core is a Human-like Criteria Probing (HCP) mechanism, in which a LLM agent adaptively decomposes its judgment into a weighted set of interpretable criteria and aggregates criterion-specific scores into a final truthfulness measure. To achieve this adaptive capability, we introduce a reward-based alignment scheme using only weak supervision from semantic consistency. At inference, we employ a multi-sampling aggregation strategy to ensure robust decisions while preserving full interpretability. We further provide theoretical analysis supporting the reliability of our approach. Extensive experiments show that HCPD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering an effective and explainable solution for zero-source hallucination detection. Code is available at https://github.com/TRISKEL10N/HCPD.

</details>


### [128] [MDForge: Agentic Molecular Dynamics Pipeline Design under Sparse Simulator Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12916)
*Zehong Wang,Yijun Ma,Connor R. Schmidt,Tianyi Ma,Weixiang Sun,Ziming Li,Xiaoguang Guo,Chuxu Zhang,Matthew J. Webber,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MDForge is an LLM agent that automates molecular dynamics pipeline design through open-ended code generation, guided by multi-agent debate among physics experts as a dense verbal reward signal. It designs pipelines competitive with human experts and discovered a novel picomolar-affinity CB[7] binder validated by NMR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing MD simulation pipelines requires substantial expert knowledge and is computationally expensive, precluding trial-and-error. Existing MD agents only orchestrate predefined tools rather than generating novel pipeline code. The authors aim to automate the full expert pipeline-design process using LLM agents with open-ended code generation.

Method: MDForge employs an LLM agent that generates MD pipeline code in an open-ended fashion. The key innovation is an in-context update rule that converts sparse task rewards into dense verbal rewards through a multi-agent debate among simulated physics experts. This debate mechanism critiques and refines generated pipelines, reshaping the agent's behavior online without gradient updates.

Result: On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge-designed pipelines match human expert performance. When deployed on a library of unseen candidate guests, the CB[7] pipeline discovered a novel high-affinity binder. Wet-lab competition NMR experiments confirmed this binder exhibits picomolar affinity for CB[7].

Conclusion: LLM agents with verbal reward densification via multi-agent debate can autonomously design expert-level MD simulation pipelines and drive real-world molecular discovery. The approach moves beyond tool orchestration to genuine open-ended scientific code generation.

Abstract: Molecular dynamics (MD) is the canonical in-silico method for atomistic molecular science, simulating molecular behavior from first-principle physics. Designing an MD pipeline for a new system requires substantial expert knowledge: running it on even one molecule is expensive, ruling out trial-and-error. We automate this expert pipeline-design process with an LLM agent. Unlike existing MD agents that orchestrate a predefined tool set, we treat pipeline design as open-ended code generation in which the agent's behavior is reshaped online by verbal reward. Specifically, we build MDForge, an LLM agent whose in-context update rule densifies the sparse reward via a multi-agent debate among physics experts. On three SAMPL host-guest binding free-energy benchmarks, MDForge automatically designs MD pipelines competitive with human experts. Deployed on a library of unseen candidate guests, its CB[7] pipeline discovers a novel binder that wet-lab competition NMR confirms is a high-affinity, picomolar CB[7] binder. Our data and code are available at https://github.com/Zehong-Wang/MDForge.

</details>


### [129] [Iterating Toward Better Search: A Two-Agent Simulation Framework for Evaluating Agentic Search Architectures in E-Commerce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12924)
*Jetlir Duraj,Jayanth Yetukuri,Shuang Zhou,Dhruv Varma,Rui Kong,Ishita Khan,Qunzhi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A modular two-agent simulation framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistants, where a buyer agent interacts with interchangeable responder architectures. Key findings: rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory, targeted failure analysis yields 62% reduction in failures, LLM backbone choice matters modestly (0.16–0.45 pts), and frontier LLM judges exhibit systematic evaluation philosophy disagreements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating conversational shopping assistants is challenging due to the lack of standardized, reproducible benchmarks that isolate architectural choices from confounding variables like user behavior. The authors aim to establish a controlled simulation environment where a fixed buyer agent enables apples-to-apples comparison of different responder designs, facilitating rapid, evidence-driven iteration.

Method: A modular two-agent framework: (1) an independent buyer agent parameterized with personas, missions, and patience levels, and (2) an interchangeable responder agent connected to a real e-commerce search API. The buyer is held constant across experiments. Evaluation uses 2,011 conversations across 14 persona buckets. Both automated LLM-as-judge metrics and systematic failure analysis are employed.

Result: (1) Rolling-window memory beats intent-extraction memory across all quality metrics and is 35% faster per query. (2) Targeted fixes from systematic failure analysis reduced failure and near-failure rates by 62%. (3) Swapping Gemini 2.5 for Llama 3.3 70B as the responder backbone costs 0.16–0.45 quality points despite identical architecture. (4) Frontier LLM judges disagree systematically: Gemini emphasizes process correctness; Claude demands concrete outcomes, even under identical evaluation prompts.

Conclusion: The modular buyer-responder framework enables rigorous, reproducible evaluation of shopping assistant architectures. Memory design and systematic failure analysis drive substantial gains, while LLM backbone choice has a smaller but measurable effect. The documented judge disagreement highlights the need for multi-judge evaluation protocols and cautions against over-reliance on a single LLM evaluator.

Abstract: We present a modular two-agent simulation framework for evaluating conversational shopping assistant architectures. An independent buyer agent, configured with personas, missions, and patience levels, is paired with an interchangeable responder that integrates with a real e-commerce search API. Holding the buyer constant across experiments enables controlled comparison of responder designs on identical scenarios. Using 2011 conversations across 14 persona buckets, we establish four empirical findings. First, rolling-window memory outperforms intent-extraction memory on all quality metrics while being 35% faster per query. Second, illustrating rapid evidence-driven iteration, a systematic failure analysis of a responder version enables targeted fixes that reduce failure and near-failure rates by 62% across the full dataset. Third, swapping the responder LLM backbone from Gemini~2.5 to Llama~3.3~70B costs 0.16--0.45 points despite identical architecture. Finally, we document systematic philosophical disagreement between frontier LLM judges: Gemini rewards process correctness while Claude demands concrete outcomes, despite using the same evaluation prompt.

</details>


### [130] [MARS: Margin-Adversarial Risk-controlled Stopping for Parallel LLM Test-time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12935)
*Wenbo Chen,Puheng Li,Mengyang Liu,Weijie Su,Tianpei Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MARS is a margin-adversarial stopping rule for parallel test-time scaling that halts majority-vote reasoning early by estimating whether leading traces will switch answers, saving 25–47% of self-consistency tokens while matching full-budget accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parallel test-time scaling (e.g., self-consistency) improves LLM accuracy but requires all sampled reasoning traces to run to completion, incurring substantial computational overhead. The key insight is that intermediate checkpoints reveal evolving votes, enabling early stopping if the leading answer is safe.

Method: MARS probes partial traces at intermediate checkpoints to extract current answers, forming an evolving aggregate vote. It separates uncertainty into two sources: (1) trace-level switch probabilities (learned via a five-feature logistic model) capturing how likely a trace is to change its answer, and (2) an adversarial bound on where switching traces will land, calibrated from warmup traces. Stopping occurs once the margin of the leading answer safely exceeds a conservative estimate of future vote movement, with formal high-probability guarantees.

Result: Across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks, MARS saves 25–47% of self-consistency tokens and 14–29% over DeepConf Online (a strong confidence-weighted baseline), while matching full-budget accuracy.

Conclusion: MARS demonstrates that early stopping via margin-adversarial reasoning is both principled and practical, offering substantial compute savings without accuracy degradation in parallel test-time scaling regimes.

Abstract: Parallel test-time scaling samples many reasoning traces and majority-votes their answers, improving LLM accuracy but requiring traces to run to completion, incurring substantial computational overhead. We observe that probing partial traces at intermediate checkpoints can extract current answers without disrupting generation, revealing an evolving aggregate vote. Based on this observation, we introduce MARS, a margin-adversarial stopping rule that estimates which active traces are likely to change their answers and stops once the leader remains safe under a conservative bound on future vote movement. The rule separates two sources of uncertainty. It learns the trace-level switch probabilities that determine how much of the current margin is likely to be retained, while handling the harder question of where switching traces land through an adversarial bound calibrated from warmup traces. With true switch probabilities, MARS guarantees with high probability that the early-stopped answer matches the full-budget vote. In practice, a five-feature logistic model closely matches oracle switching behavior. Across three reasoning models and three competition-math benchmarks, MARS saves 25-47% of self-consistency tokens and 14-29% on top of DeepConf Online, a strong confidence-weighted baseline that already filters and truncates weak traces, while matching the accuracy of the corresponding full-budget baselines.

</details>


### [131] [PRISMR: Overcoming Parse Collapse in Multimodal Listwise Ranking via Parameterized Representation Internalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12942)
*Hao Jiang,Xin Li,Annan Wang,Zhi Yang,Haoxiang Zhang,Yichi Zhang,Weisi Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Generative listwise ranking with LMMs suffers from "parse collapse" — where models silently drop candidates in long contexts. PRISMR replaces in-context list processing with a hypernetwork that encodes candidates in parallel into item-specific LoRA weights, synthesizing an instance-specific adapter that internalizes list structure more robustly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative listwise ranking with Large Multimodal Models fails in long-context scenarios due to parse collapse: the decoder produces fluent but incomplete rankings by omitting candidates and terminating early. This stems from limited context utilization, and neither prompt engineering nor constrained decoding adequately address it.

Method: PRISMR (Parameterized Representation Internalization for Semantic Multimodal Ranking) uses a lightweight hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel, generating item-specific LoRA weights. These are synthesized into an instance-specific adapter for the LMM, replacing transient in-context processing with parametric structural conditioning. A large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark is also introduced.

Result: PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and instruction-tuned backbones.

Conclusion: Parametric structural conditioning via hypernetwork-generated, instance-specific LoRA adapters offers a robust alternative to in-context listwise ranking, effectively mitigating parse collapse in long-context multimodal scenarios while maintaining base model integrity and enabling strong cross-domain transfer.

Abstract: Generative listwise ranking with Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) aims to capture global list context in a single forward pass, but
  its effectiveness degrades in long-context multimodal scenarios. We identify a recurring failure mode, parse collapse, where the
  autoregressive decoder produces fluent yet incomplete rankings by silently omitting candidates and terminating early. This
  failure stems from limited context utilization rather than simple formatting mistakes, making prompt engineering and constrained
  decoding insufficient. We propose PRISMR (Parameterized Representation Internalization for Semantic Multimodal Ranking), a
  framework that replaces transient in-context list processing with parametric structural conditioning. PRISMR uses a lightweight
  hypernetwork to encode multimodal candidates in parallel and generate item-specific LoRA weights, which are synthesized into an
  instance-specific adapter for a LMM. This paradigm enables more robust internalization of list structure while preserving the
  base model. We further introduce a large-scale multimodal review-ranking benchmark for evaluation. Experiments demonstrate that
  PRISMR substantially reduces parse collapse, improves listwise ranking performance, and transfers effectively across domains and
  instruction-tuned backbones.

</details>


### [132] [Learning What to Remember: A Cognitively Grounded Multi-Factor Value Model for Agentic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12945)
*Zhibao Chen,Qian Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-factor memory value function for LLM agents facing long interaction histories, using seven interpretable factors from cognitive psychology whose weights are learned via gradient-free optimization. The learned weighting significantly outperforms uniform weights, single best factor, and recency baselines in retaining gold evidence, with interpretability confirmed through both real and synthetic experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents with long-running interactions accumulate histories exceeding context windows, requiring decisions about what to encode/forget/retrieve. Current systems rely on semantic similarity or recency, but these are mis-specified because the forgetting decision occurs at consolidation time when future queries are unknown. The work addresses this temporal mismatch by proposing a principled, learnable memory valuation framework.

Method: A linear multi-factor value function V(m)=Σᵢwᵢfᵢ(m) with seven interpretable factors (emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, usage history) is proposed. Weights are learned via gradient-free optimization (e.g., CMA-ES or similar) against a downstream objective. The scalar value uniformly controls encoding depth, forgetting risk, and retrieval rank. A controlled synthetic task with planted confounds validates the learner's ability to recover correct weightings. A neural network over the same factors serves as a non-linear comparison.

Result: On LongMemEval, scoring goal relevance against the held-out evaluation question saturates at ~0.98 gold-evidence retention (measuring retrieval, not forgetting). In the blind regime, learned multi-factor achieves 0.770±0.011 retention vs. 0.657 (uniform), 0.518 (best single factor), and 0.368 (recency); all pairwise gaps have 95% bootstrap CIs above zero. A neural network ties the linear model. Learned weights are interpretable: reliability, emotional intensity, and self/user relevance dominate; query-time goal similarity is correctly down-weighted. The synthetic task confirms the learner achieves 1.00 retention vs. 0.62 for uniform weighting.

Conclusion: Forgetting in long-context LLM agents requires a forward-looking valuation function learned from downstream objectives, not heuristics like recency or post-hoc retrieval similarity. The multi-factor linear model is both performant and interpretable, operates cheaply on CPU, and establishes that learned memory valuation significantly improves evidence retention in realistic blind settings.

Abstract: Long-running LLM agents accumulate interaction histories far larger than any context window, forcing a standing decision: what to encode deeply, what to forget, and what to retrieve under a fixed memory budget. Production systems answer with semantic similarity or recency -- both mis-specified for the forgetting decision, which is made at consolidation time before the future query is known. We propose a multi-factor memory value function V(m)=\sum_i w_i f_i(m) over seven interpretable factors (emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, and usage history) drawn from cognitive psychology, whose weights are learned from a downstream objective by a gradient-free optimiser, and whose single scalar uniformly controls encoding depth, forget risk, and retrieval rank. We make a methodological point: on LongMemEval, scoring goal relevance against the held-out evaluation question saturates gold-evidence retention at \approx 0.98 -- this measures retrieval, not forgetting. In the realistic blind regime, a learned multi-factor value retains 0.770 \pm 0.011 of gold evidence across 479 usable cases, versus 0.657 for uniform weights, 0.518 for the best single factor, and 0.368 for recency; every paired gap's 95% bootstrap CI is above zero, and a neural network over the same factors ties the linear model. The learned weights are interpretable -- reliability, emotional intensity, and self/user relevance dominate, while query-time goal similarity is correctly down-weighted for the forgetting decision. A controlled synthetic task with planted confounds confirms the learner recovers a separating weighting (1.00 retention) where uniform weighting fails (0.62). The substrate is open-source; all experiments run on a single CPU with no API calls.

</details>


### [133] [OpenMedQ: Broad Open Pretraining for Medical Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12953)
*Ibrahim Gulluk,Max Van Puyvelde,Olivier Gevaert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenMedQ is a medical vision-language model pretrained on 3.35M samples across 14 datasets (pathology, radiology, microscopy, clinical QA). It achieves SOTA on PathVQA (75.9 BLEU-1) and VQA-MED (64.5 BLEU-1), surpassing much larger models, and its vision encoder yields top macro-F1 (0.757) on 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks compared to existing biomedical VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical vision-language models are often trained on proprietary or non-public data mixtures, limiting reproducibility. The authors aim to provide a fully open, reproducible, and strong baseline for the community by pretraining on the broadest fully-open medical dataset mixture to date.

Method: Pretrain a medical vision-language model (OpenMedQ) on a curated multimodal corpus of 14 open datasets (~3.35M samples) covering pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only clinical QA. The vision encoder is evaluated on 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks using a fixed downstream recipe, and the full model is benchmarked on medical VQA tasks (PathVQA, VQA-MED).

Result: OpenMedQ achieves SOTA BLEU-1 of 75.9 on PathVQA (outperforming Med-PaLM M variants up to 562B parameters, ~80x larger) and matches the best VQA-MED BLEU-1 at 64.5. Its vision encoder attains the highest average macro-F1 (0.757) on 8 unseen classification benchmarks, exceeding BiomedCLIP (0.745), PMC-CLIP (0.745), PubMedCLIP (0.746), and a from-scratch baseline (0.616).

Conclusion: A fully-open, reproducible medical VLM can match or surpass substantially larger proprietary models on medical VQA and classification tasks. The release of code, model, and demo establishes a strong open baseline for future medical multimodal research.

Abstract: We present OpenMedQ, a medical vision-language model pretrained on the broadest fully-open medical mix to date: 14 datasets totaling ~3.35M pretraining samples spanning pathology, radiology, microscopy, and text-only clinical QA. OpenMedQ reaches state-of-the-art BLEU-1 on PathVQA (75.9), beating Med-PaLM M variants up to 562B parameters (~80x larger), and matches the best reported VQA-MED BLEU-1 (64.5). Its vision encoder, transferred to 8 unseen medical classification benchmarks under an identical downstream recipe, obtains the highest average macro-F1 (0.757) among BiomedCLIP (0.745), PMC-CLIP (0.745), PubMedCLIP (0.746), and a from-scratch baseline (0.616). We release our code and an interactive demo is publicly available as a reproducible baseline for the community.

</details>


### [134] [Multi-Modal Agents for Power Distribution Defect Detection: An Evaluation of Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12969)
*Quan Quan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Multi-Modal Agent framework for automated power distribution defect detection, systematically evaluating foundation models across perception, reasoning, and tool-use capabilities using a domain-specific benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional inspection methods for power distribution networks suffer from poor semantic understanding, limited generalization, and lack of closed-loop automation, necessitating an intelligent agent-based approach.

Method: A Multi-Modal Agent framework is proposed, where multimodal foundation models serve as unified cognitive engines evaluated across three dimensions: (1) Perception—identifying equipment and generating expert-level defect descriptions; (2) Reasoning—diagnosing causes, assessing severity, and planning maintenance using domain knowledge; (3) Tool Usage—autonomously querying knowledge bases and generating work orders for closed-loop maintenance. A domain-specific evaluation dataset and benchmark are constructed for rigorous assessment.
