Result: Experimental results reveal the strengths and limitations of current foundation models across all three dimensions (perception, reasoning, tool usage), providing empirical evidence for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes industrial settings.

Conclusion: Multimodal foundation models show promise as cognitive engines for industrial defect detection agents, but their current capabilities vary across perception, reasoning, and tool-use dimensions, highlighting areas needing improvement before reliable deployment in high-stakes environments.

Abstract: The power distribution network is critical to reliable electricity delivery, yet traditional inspection methods face limitations in semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Multi-Modal Agent framework specifically for power distribution defect detection. Central to this study is the systematic evaluation of multimodal foundation models as unified cognitive engines. We rigorously assess their integrated performance across three critical capabilities: (1) Perception, where the model must accurately identify equipment and generate expert-level descriptions of defects; (2) Reasoning, where the model interprets visual findings to diagnose causes, assess severity, and plan maintenance strategies based on domain knowledge; and (3) Tool Usage, where the model acts as an autonomous operator to execute actions -- such as querying knowledge bases or generating work orders -- to achieve closed-loop maintenance. To support this evaluation, a domain-specific evaluation dataset and a comprehensive benchmark are developed. Experimental results demonstrate the strengths and limitations of current foundation models in these three dimensions, providing empirical evidence for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes industrial environments.

</details>


### [135] [A Mathematical Forum Platform for Collaborative Problem Solving and Dataset Generation for AI Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12976)
*Akbar Erkinov,Nurmukhammad Abdurasulov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A system integrating Mathpix OCR directly into forum posting interfaces, allowing users to upload/capture images of mathematical expressions and receive live-rendered LaTeX/Markdown previews, eliminating friction between formula photography and forum posting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mathematical content sharing in online forums is hindered by three pain points: error-prone raw LaTeX writing, platform-switching required by standalone OCR tools, and the absence of any integrated photograph-to-rendered-post pipeline in current forum software.

Method: A three-layer architecture (image processing, rendering, storage) embedding the Mathpix OCR API into a forum interface. Uploaded formula images are routed through Mathpix; the system detects whether output is LaTeX or plain text with inline math, normalizes delimiters appropriately, and renders a live preview in LaTeX or Markdown mode before database commit. Supports both desktop and mobile clients.

Result: The integrated system eliminates the image-to-LaTeX-to-post friction. A provisional US patent application has been filed. The architecture is loosely coupled, cross-platform, and produces a continuously growing, community-validated dataset of mathematical problems and solutions.

Conclusion: Beyond immediate usability gains, a deployed platform constitutes a valuable, continuously growing dataset of community-validated mathematical problems with step-by-step solutions, suitable for training and benchmarking AI systems on accurate mathematical reasoning.

Abstract: Sharing mathematical content in online forums remains a significant friction point for students and educators: writing raw LATEX is error-prone, standalone optical character recognition tools require platform switching, and current forum software offers no integrated path from a photograph of a formula to a rendered post. We present a unified system that eliminates this friction by embedding an image to LATEX conversion pipeline directly inside a forum posting interface. A user uploads or captures an image of a mathematical expression; the system routes it through the Mathpix OCR API, detects whether the returned output is LATEX or plain text containing inline math, applies the appropriate delimiter normalisation, and renders a live preview in either LATEX or Markdown mode before the post is committed to the database. The architecture is organized in three loosely coupled layers: image processing, rendering, and storage, and supports both desktop and mobile clients. A provisional US patent application has been filed covering the core methods. We describe the full system design, each component in detail, the data schema, and the key technical innovations, and we position the work against existing standalone tools and forum platforms to demonstrate the practical gap it closes. Beyond immediate usability, we argue that a deployed platform of this kind constitutes a continuously growing, community-validated dataset of mathematical problems and step-by-step solutions, a resource that can be used to train and benchmark AI systems for accurate mathematical reasoning

</details>


### [136] [Structured Testbench Generation for LLM-Driven HDL Design and Verification-Oriented Data Curation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12983)
*En-Ming Huang,Yu-Hung Kao,Ren-Hao Deng,Wei-Po Hsin,Yao-Ting Hsieh,Cheng Liang,Hsiang-Yu Tsou,Mu-Chi Chen,Yu-Kai Hung,Shao-Chun Ho,Po-Hsuang Huang,Shih-Hao Hung,H. T. Kung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STG is a structured testbench generation framework that exploits hardware design structure to produce deterministic testbenches, achieving 720x faster verification than iterative LLM-based flows, higher coverage, lower false-pass rates, and enabling efficient data curation (11x faster, 127x less energy) with state-of-the-art distilled model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated testbench generation is a critical bottleneck in LLM-driven RTL workflows. Existing prompt-based methods treat testbench generation as unconstrained code synthesis, resulting in stochastic outputs, high token costs, low reproducibility, and insufficient coverage. A structured, deterministic alternative is needed.

Method: STG exploits the inherent structure of hardware designs (module interfaces, signal types, register maps, finite state machines, etc.) to generate deterministic testbenches. Instead of free-form LLM generation, it algorithmically constructs testbenches based on structural analysis of the Design Under Test (DUT).

Result: STG runs 720x faster than iterative LLM-based testbench generation; achieves higher compilation success rates and coverage; reduces false-pass verdicts on incorrect DUTs; exposes faulty benchmark testbenches; performs data curation 11x faster on a single CPU core with 127x less energy; and as a test-time scaling oracle reduces node count by 14-47%. Distilled models achieve state-of-the-art performance in multi-benchmark evaluation.

Conclusion: STG demonstrates that structured, deterministic testbench generation is a viable and superior alternative to unconstrained LLM-based synthesis, serving effectively as a verification tool, a data curation engine, and a test-time scaling oracle across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Automated testbench generation has become a critical bottleneck in large language model (LLM)-driven Register Transfer Level (RTL) workflows, where large numbers of candidate designs must be verified rapidly and reliably. Existing prompt-based approaches treat testbench generation as unconstrained code synthesis, yielding stochastic outputs with high token cost, low reproducibility, and insufficient coverage. To address this gap, we present STG, a Structured Testbench Generation framework that exploits the inherent structure of hardware designs to generate deterministic testbenches. As a direct verification tool, STG runs 720x faster than an iterative LLM-based testbench generation flow and higher rate of successful compilation, achieves higher coverage, and reduces false-pass verdicts on incorrect DUTs. STG also helps identify errors in RTL generation benchmarks by exposing faulty benchmark testbenches. As a data curation engine, it is 11x faster than LLM-based filtering on a single CPU core with 127x less energy, and the resulting distilled models provide state-of-the-art performance in our multi-benchmark evaluation. As a test-time scaling oracle, it reduces node count by 14-47\%. Our models are available at https://huggingface.co/collections/AS-SiliconMind/siliconmind-v12.

</details>


### [137] [APCyc: Property-Informed Design of Cyclic Peptides via Automated Cyclization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12991)
*Yifan Zhao,Lang Qin,Jintai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: APCyc is a target-aware generative framework for de novo cyclic peptide design that explicitly models cyclization patterns (linkage types and sites) and jointly optimizes multiple physicochemical properties using Bayesian posterior guidance, overcoming limitations of prior models trained on linear peptides.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative models for peptide design are predominantly trained on linear peptide data and fail to capture cyclization-specific constraints (e.g., pocket-adaptive cyclization patterns, linkage sites). This gap limits their utility for cyclic peptide design, which is therapeutically valuable due to enhanced stability and binding affinity.

Method: APCyc employs an expanded residue vocabulary with explicit cyclization-site and linkage-type encoding to learn cyclization-aware representations. It leverages Bayesian posterior guidance to steer sampling toward cyclic peptides that simultaneously satisfy multiple property objectives (e.g., binding affinity, drug-likeness, stability), jointly optimizing essential physicochemical properties in a target-conditioned manner.

Result: Experimental results show that APCyc learns target-dependent cyclization preferences and enables effective, controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design.

Conclusion: APCyc addresses a critical gap in cyclic peptide design by providing a principled framework that integrates cyclization modeling with multi-property Bayesian optimization, demonstrating its effectiveness for target-aware de novo generation of therapeutic cyclic peptides.

Abstract: Cyclic peptides represent a promising class of therapeutic compounds in modern drug discovery, often offering improved stability and binding affinity. However, the de novo design of cyclic peptides remains challenging because methods must identify pocket-adaptive cyclization patterns and linkage sites while simultaneously controlling drug-relevant properties. This challenge is particularly pronounced for recent generative models trained predominantly on linear peptide data, which may fail to capture cyclization-specific constraints. To address the limitation, we introduce APCyc, a target-aware de novo cyclic peptide generation framework that explicitly models cyclization and jointly optimizes multiple essential physicochemical properties. By using an expanded residue vocabulary and explicitly encoding cyclization-site and linkage-type information, APCyc learns cyclization-aware representations and leverages Bayesian posterior guidance to steer sampling toward cyclic peptides satisfying multiple property objectives. Experimental results demonstrate that our model learns target-dependent cyclization preferences, and enables effective and controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design. The source code of this paper is available at https://github.com/HKUSTGZ-ML4Health-Lab/APCyc.

</details>


### [138] [Otters++: A Time-to-first-spike Based Energy Efficient Optical Spiking Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13016)
*Zhanglu Yan,Jiayi Mao,Kaiwen Tang,Fanfan Li,Gang Pan,Tao Luo,Bowen Zhu,Qianhui Liu,Weng-Fai Wong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Otters++ exploits the natural decay of In₂O₃ optoelectronic synapses to directly implement time-to-first-spike (TTFS) computation, establishing a layer-wise equivalence to quantized neural networks for scalable Transformer training with device-aware methods, achieving 84.17% on GLUE with clear energy efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TTFS coding in SNNs is energy-efficient (each neuron fires at most once), but its practical benefit is diminished by the overhead of explicitly computing temporal decay and multiplying it by synaptic weights. The authors aim to eliminate this digital computation by co-opting a physical hardware "bug" — natural signal decay in optoelectronic devices — as the direct computational mechanism.

Method: (1) Use measured decay characteristics of custom In₂O₃ optoelectronic synapses to physically realize the TTFS temporal decay term, eliminating explicit digital computation. (2) Establish layer-wise functional equivalence between Otters++ and a quantized neural network (QNN) to scale to Transformers. (3) A hybrid training method: device-faithful SNN forward pass, with straight-through gradients routed through the equivalent QNN path in the backward pass (avoiding differentiation through discrete spike events and mitigating over-sparsity). (4) Model distillation. (5) Device-noise-aware training via sampling run-to-run variation. (6) Refined system-level energy modeling accounting for device sharing and multi-hop communication.

Result: On the GLUE benchmark, Otters++ achieves an average score of 84.17%, outperforming prior spiking Transformer baselines while maintaining a clear energy advantage.

Conclusion: Physically grounded TTFS computing using optoelectronic device dynamics is efficient, trainable, and robust under realistic hardware effects, demonstrating a viable path toward energy-efficient spiking Transformer inference.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for energy-efficient inference, and time-to-first-spike (TTFS) coding is especially attractive because each neuron fires at most once. In practice, however, this benefit is often reduced by the cost of computing a temporal decay term and multiplying it by the synaptic weight. We address this issue by turning a physical hardware "bug," the natural signal decay in optoelectronic devices, into the main computation of TTFS, named Otters++. Specifically, we use the measured decay of a custom In$_2$O$_3$ optoelectronic synapse to directly realize the TTFS temporal term, removing the need for explicit digital decay computation. To scale this idea to Transformer models, we establish a layer-wise functional equivalence between the Otters++ and a quantized neural network (QNN), and develop a hybrid training method that uses device-faithful SNN computation in the forward pass and QNN straight-through gradients through the equivalent QNN path in the backward pass, together with model distillation. This avoids differentiation through discrete first-spike events and reduces the over-sparsity problem in direct TTFS-SNN training. We further make training aware of measured device noise by sampling run-to-run variation, and refine the system-level energy model by accounting for device sharing and multi-hop communication. On GLUE dataset, Otters++ improves the average score to 84.17\% while maintaining a clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines. These results show that physically grounded TTFS computing can be efficient, trainable, and robust under realistic hardware effects.

</details>


### [139] [SciR: A Controllable Benchmark for Scientific Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13020)
*Pierre Beckmann,Marco Valentino,Andre Freitas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciR is a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on scientific reasoning that combines deduction, induction, and causal abduction. Tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction trees, inductive rules, causal graphs) to guarantee verifiable answers, then rendered into realistic multi-document scientific discourse. The benchmark independently varies extraction difficulty and inference difficulty, revealing that both axes compound to hurt model performance, and that even neurosymbolic pipelines struggle with the rendering step.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scientific reasoning benchmarks face a trade-off: human-annotated benchmarks are costly and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logical-reasoning benchmarks fail to resemble real scientific documents. There is no benchmark that combines multi-paradigm reasoning (deduction, induction, causal abduction) with controllable difficulty across both information extraction and principled inference, using verifiable ground truth in realistic scientific rendering.

Method: SciR constructs tasks from three formal objects — deduction trees, inductive rule hypotheses, and causal graphs — ensuring verifiable answers. These formal structures are then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse using per-track domain-tuned genres (e.g., experimental protocols, review articles). The construction allows independent variation along two difficulty axes: (1) extraction difficulty — how hard it is to locate key information within the rendered documents, and (2) inference difficulty — how hard the underlying principled reasoning is. Six models are evaluated, including reasoning models (e.g., deepseek-r1), instruct models, and neurosymbolic pipelines that delegate inference to a verified solver.

Result: Both extraction difficulty and inference difficulty independently degrade performance across all tested models, and their effects compound when combined. The scientific rendering step hurts even neurosymbolic pipelines, which offload inference to a verified symbolic solver but still must extract the formal structure from text. The two axes produce a per-model extraction-vs-inference profile: reasoning models like deepseek-r1 tend to outperform non-reasoning instruct models on the inference axis specifically.

Conclusion: SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark offering parametric control over both extraction and inference difficulty. It reveals that rendering formal reasoning into naturalistic scientific text poses a substantial challenge even for systems with verified reasoning components, and that reasoning-native models show relative strength on inference but remain vulnerable to extraction difficulty — pointing to key directions for future LLM development in scientific reasoning.

Abstract: Three paradigmatic forms of inference recur across scientific reasoning: deduction, induction, and causal abduction. Reliably evaluating LLMs on these in scientific settings is currently out of reach: scientific benchmarks built on human annotations are costly and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logical-reasoning benchmarks do not resemble real scientific documents. We introduce SciR, a benchmark that combines multi-paradigm reasoning with controllable scientific rendering, anchored on three paradigmatic scientific problems. Tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction tree, inductive rule hypothesis, causal graph) to guarantee verifiable answers, then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse via per-track domain-tuned genres. The construction lets us independently vary two difficulty axes: how hard it is to extract the key information needed for inference, and how hard the principled inference itself is. We test six models. Both axes hurt every model, and their effects compound. The rendering even hurts neurosymbolic pipelines, which hand inference to a verified solver. The two axes yield a per-model extraction-vs-inference profile: for instance, reasoning models like deepseek-r1 mostly surpass non-reasoning instruct models on the inference axis. To our knowledge, SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control on both extraction and inference difficulty.

</details>


### [140] [Nous: An Attempt to Extract and Inject the Cognition Behind Prediction-Market Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13038)
*Haowei Qian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Nous extracts behavioral profiles from real Polymarket traders to inject cognitive diversity into LLM agents. While extraction partially succeeds (profiles are stable and identifiable), prompt-level injection fails to transmit meaningful diversity, yielding no reduction in error correlation or improvement in forecasting accuracy. The bottleneck is identified in the profile-to-prompt translation step.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents increasingly participate in prediction markets and collective decision-making, but their reliance on shared foundation models risks a "cognitive monoculture" — correlated forecasts that undermine the wisdom-of-crowds benefit. With frontier-model errors correlated at r ≈ 0.77, the authors ask whether real human cognitive diversity (measured from trading behavior) can be harvested and transferred to LLM agents to restore independent, diverse reasoning.

Method: The pipeline has two stages. (1) Extraction: From 100 Polymarket wallets, the authors derive an eight-dimension behavioral profile covering parameters like contrarianism, overconfidence, and reaction speed, then test temporal stability (split-half ICC), identifiability (top-1 retrieval), and predictive validity (rank correlation with future profit, with behavioral-confound controls). (2) Injection: Profiles are translated via a structure-to-narrative translator into prompts given to LLM agents. Diversity transfer is evaluated using semantic embedding distance, ensemble error correlation, and Brier score, with ablations on temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty. The prompts themselves are analyzed to locate compression.

Result: Extraction is partially successful: 8 of 14 parameters show temporal stability (ICC ≥ 0.5, bootstrap CI lower bound > 0.3), contrarian score reaches ICC ~0.9; wallet identification from profiles achieves 17-22% top-1 retrieval (vs. 1% chance); two of four pre-specified dimensions rank-correlate with future profit out-of-sample, though not robust to confound controls. Injection fails: structured prompts show no significant advantage over length-matched controls on semantic embedding distance; ensemble error correlation is not reduced; Brier score does not improve. The structure-to-narrative translator emits near-uniform prompts whose spread does not track profile spread, locating the compression before the model.

Conclusion: Nous demonstrates that while human cognitive diversity can be partially extracted from behavioral data, prompt-level injection is insufficient to transmit that diversity to LLM agents. The bottleneck lies in translating structured profiles into meaningfully varied prompts. This motivates deeper interventions — fine-tuning or activation steering — rather than surface-level prompting to address cognitive monoculture in LLM-agent collectives.

Abstract: As LLM agents proliferate in prediction markets and collective decision-making, they risk a cognitive monoculture: agents built on shared foundation models produce correlated forecasts, and recent measurement finds frontier-model errors correlated at r ~ 0.77. We ask whether human cognitive diversity can be recovered from behavior and transferred to LLM agents. Nous extracts a structured eight-dimension behavioral profile from real Polymarket trading activity and injects it into agents through prompts. Our central finding is a dissociation between the two halves of that pipeline. Extraction works, partially: across 100 wallets, 8 of 14 parameters are temporally stable (split-half ICC >= 0.5, bootstrap CI lower bound > 0.3; contrarian score reaches ICC ~ 0.9); wallets are identifiable from their profiles well above chance (top-1 retrieval 17-22% vs. 1% chance); and two of four pre-specified dimensions rank-correlate with future realized profit out-of-sample, though the correlations do not survive behavioral-confound controls. Prompt-level injection does not measurably transmit it: on a semantic embedding metric, structured injection shows no significant advantage over a length-matched control on any model, and the diversity it induces neither reduces ensemble error correlation nor improves Brier score -- a null that persists across exploratory checks on sampling temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty. Measuring the prompts themselves locates the compression before the model: the structure-to-narrative translator emits near-uniform prompts whose spread does not track profile spread. We position Nous as measuring the cognitive-monoculture problem and the limits of a prompt-level remedy, motivating deeper, below-the-prompt injection (fine-tuning, activation steering). Code, frozen profiles, prompts, and model outputs: https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper

</details>


### [141] [Augmentation techniques for video surveillance in the visible and thermal spectral range](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13042)
*Vanessa Buhrmester,Ann-Kristin Grosselfinger,David Munch,Michael Arens*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates how convolutional neural networks (CNNs) learn from multimodal surveillance data (visible and thermal infrared), examining the robustness and suitability of various data augmentation techniques to understand which features (color, texture, thermal radiation, shape) most influence object detection accuracy when training on one modality and testing on another.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intelligent video surveillance systems often combine visible-spectrum and long-wave infrared (LWIR) cameras for day/night operation. However, there is no clear understanding of how variations in thermal radiation, shape, or color information influence CNN classification accuracy. Additionally, obtaining sufficient thermal infrared training datasets remains challenging, making cross-modal training (using visible data to help infrared tasks) potentially advantageous. The authors aim to uncover what CNNs actually learn from different sensor modalities.

Method: The authors investigate multispectral CNN-based object detection by systematically evaluating different data augmentation techniques applied to both visible and thermal infrared imagery. They analyze how CNNs make decisions across modalities, testing the suitability and robustness of augmentations to determine the relative importance of color, texture, thermal radiation, and shape features for classification accuracy in cross-sensor scenarios.

Result: The abstract does not provide specific quantitative results, but frames the contribution as providing deeper insight into CNN decision-making processes when learning from different sensor inputs, particularly regarding how strongly thermal radiation, shape, and color variations affect classification performance in multispectral surveillance contexts.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that understanding what CNNs learn from different sensor modalities is essential for designing effective multispectral object detection systems. The investigation of augmentation techniques provides evidence about cross-modal feature robustness, informing strategies for training when thermal infrared data is scarce and visible-spectrum data is used to supplement or substitute training.

Abstract: In intelligent video surveillance, cameras record image sequences during day and night. Commonly, this demands different sensors. To achieve a better performance it is not unusual to combine them. We focus on the case that a long-wave infrared camera records continuously and in addition to this, another camera records in the visible spectral range during daytime and an intelligent algorithm supervises the picked up imagery. More accurate, our task is multispectral CNN-based object detection. At first glance, images originating from the visible spectral range differ between thermal infrared ones in the presence of color and distinct texture information on the one hand and in not containing information about thermal radiation that emits from objects on the other hand. Although color can provide valuable information for classification tasks, effects such as varying illumination and specialties of different sensors still represent significant problems. Anyway, obtaining sufficient and practical thermal infrared datasets for training a deep neural network poses still a challenge. That is the reason why training with the help of data from the visible spectral range could be advantageous, particularly if the data, which has to be evaluated contains both visible and infrared data. However, there is no clear evidence of how strongly variations in thermal radiation, shape, or color information influence classification accuracy. To gain deeper insight into how Convolutional Neural Networks make decisions and what they learn from different sensor input data, we investigate the suitability and robustness of different augmentation techniques...

</details>


### [142] [AAbAAC: An Annotated Corpus for Autoimmunity Information Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13051)
*Fabien Maury,Solène Grosdidier,Maud de Dieuleveult,Adrien Coulet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AAbAAC is a manually annotated corpus of 115 PubMed abstracts focused on autoimmunity, covering autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies, their targets, locations, and clinical signs. The corpus was used to benchmark NER methods and fine-tune models, demonstrating that small-scale domain-specific annotation improves biomedical information extraction performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalist deep learning and large language models underperform in highly specialized biomedical domains like autoimmunity due to domain-specific complexity. There is a lack of annotated resources capturing the unique entity types (autoantibodies, molecular targets, disease associations) relevant to this field.

Method: The authors constructed AAbAAC, a corpus of 115 PubMed abstracts with manual annotation of entities (autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies, molecular targets, body locations, clinical signs) and their relationships. The corpus was then used to (1) benchmark existing NER methods and (2) fine-tune NER models to assess performance gains from domain-specific training data.

Result: Evaluation on AAbAAC confirmed performance gaps of generalist models on autoimmunity-specific NER. Fine-tuning on AAbAAC yielded expected improvements in NER performance, validating the utility of the corpus for domain adaptation.

Conclusion: Small-scale, high-quality annotation efforts like AAbAAC provide significant value for specialized biomedical domains. The corpus contributes both a benchmark resource and a fine-tuning dataset to advance computational research in autoimmunity.

Abstract: Despite advances in information extraction driven by deep learning and large language models, performance gaps remain in highly specialized biomedical fields, where domainspecific complexity poses challenges for generalist models. In this work, we focus on the domain of autoimmunity, where the main entities of interest are autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies (i.e., molecules that may mark or cause these diseases), their molecular targets, their location in the body, and their associated clinical signs. Herein, we present AAbAAC (AutoAntibodies and Autoimmunity Annotated Corpus), a corpus of 115 abstracts selected from PubMed, where we manually annotated entities and their relationships. First, AAbAAC was used to evaluate several methods on the task of named entity recognition (NER), and secondly, to fine-tune NER models. Our study demonstrates the utility of AAbAAC for information extraction in the domain of autoimmunity, showing expected improvement in NER performance after finetuning. This illustrates the value of small-scale annotation efforts for specialized domains and contributes to the computational study of autoimmunity. The AAbAAC corpus is available at https://github.com/f-maury/AAbAAC.

</details>


### [143] [Rethinking RAG in Long Videos: What to Retrieve and How to Use It?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13141)
*Yuho Lee,Jisu Shin,Nicole Hee-Yeon Kim,Jihwan Bang,Juntae Lee,Kyuwoong Hwang,Fatih Porikli,Hwanjun Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces V-RAGBench, a benchmark for faithful decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation in video RAG, and CARVE, a chunk-adaptive reranking method that selects the optimal modality-granularity configuration per chunk, outperforming eight baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two gaps limit VideoRAG progress: (1) existing benchmarks allow queries to be answered without the video, masking retrieval failures, and (2) prior methods apply a single modality-granularity configuration per query, ignoring that different chunks within a query may benefit from different configurations (e.g., text vs. visual, fine-grained vs. coarse temporal segments).

Method: The authors propose V-RAGBench, a benchmark of ⟨query, evidence chunk, answer⟩ triplets enabling decoupled evaluation. They also propose CARVE, which runs parallel retrievers across multiple modality-granularity configurations and applies chunk-adaptive reranking to select the best configuration per chunk. Each chunk enters the generator under its winning configuration, producing an interleaved evidence form where chunk-level decisions propagate through both retrieval and generation stages.

Result: CARVE outperforms eight recent VideoRAG baselines. Critically, the evidence chunks supplied to the generator interleave multiple configurations rather than sharing a single one — a behavior unattainable by query-level methods.

Conclusion: Chunk-adaptive configuration selection yields significant gains over query-level approaches in VideoRAG, and V-RAGBench provides a robust framework for decoupled, faithful evaluation of retrieval and generation.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation is moving beyond text into long, egocentric video, where systems must select query-relevant chunks across multiple modalities and temporal granularities. Yet progress in VideoRAG is limited by two gaps: existing benchmarks allow queries to be answered without the video, obscuring retrieval errors, and prior methods apply a single modality-granularity configuration per query, ignoring chunk-level variability. We address both by introducing V-RAGBench, a benchmark of $\langle$query, evidence chunk, answer$\rangle$ triplets that enables faithful, decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation, and CARVE, a simple method that runs parallel retrievers across configurations and employs chunk-adaptive reranking to identify the winning configuration for each chunk. Each chunk then enters the generator under its winning configuration selected during retrieval, yielding an interleaved evidence form where the chunk-level decision propagates across both stages. CARVE outperforms eight recent VideoRAG baselines, with the chunks supplied to the generator interleaving multiple configurations rather than sharing a single one, a behavior unattainable by query-level methods.

</details>


### [144] [TerraBench: Can Agents Reason Over Heterogeneous Earth-System Data?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13148)
*Dat Tien Nguyen,Thao Nguyen,Fadillah Adamsyah Maani,Huy M. Le,Muhammad Umer Sheikh,Numan Saeed,Muhammad Haris Khan,Salman Khan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TerraBench introduces a benchmark and agent framework (TerraAgent) for grounded Earth-science reasoning that couples LLM planning with scientific tools, spanning 403 agentic tasks across 8 domains with 24,500 verified execution steps, using process-level tool-use metrics and tolerance-aware numeric scoring to evaluate LLM-driven Earth-science workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current foundation models for weather/climate can forecast but cannot reason interactively in language, while LLMs can reason in language but cannot operate on high-dimensional Earth-system data. Real scientific workflows demand both capabilities and remain underserved by existing benchmarks, which isolate capabilities into narrow tasks rather than evaluating integrated, heterogeneous reasoning.

Method: TerraBench is built on TerraAgent, a ReAct-style executable framework that interleaves reasoning, tool calls, and observations. It couples LLM planning with scientific tools for environmental retrieval, geospatial processing, simulation, and artifact-backed computation. The benchmark features 403 agentic tasks across three tracks (Fundamentals, Simulator-Grounded, Document-Grounded Verification), eight application domains, and uses process-level tool-use metrics paired with tolerance-aware numeric scoring.

Result: The benchmark comprises 24,500 verified execution steps. Results indicate that building reliable Earth-science agents requires more than just tool access — agents must coordinate heterogeneous workflows, parameterize tools precisely, and preserve artifact provenance across multi-step reasoning chains.

Conclusion: TerraBench establishes the first unified, executable benchmark for grounded Earth-science reasoning that integrates EO imagery, gridded data, GIS reasoning, and simulation. Reliable Earth-science agents must go beyond mere tool access to master workflow coordination, precise parameterization, and provenance tracking.

Abstract: Climate and environmental decision-making increasingly requires reasoning across heterogeneous inputs, including gridded physical data, satellite imagery, geospatial context, and simulator outputs. Weather and climate foundation models can forecast well, but do not reason interactively in language, while large language models (LLMs) reason in language but cannot operate directly on high-dimensional Earth-system data. As a result, real scientific workflows in Earth-science remain underserved. We introduce TerraBench, a benchmark for grounded Earth-science reasoning, built on TerraAgent, a ReAct-style executable framework that interleaves reasoning, tool calls, and observations to couple LLM planning with scientific tools for environmental retrieval, geospatial processing, simulation, and artifact-backed computation. TerraBench unifies analysis of Earth observation imagery, gridded data, GIS reasoning and simulation in a single executable interface, whereas prior benchmarks isolate these capabilities into narrow individual tasks. It is also the first in this space to pair process-level tool-use metrics with tolerance-aware numeric scoring. The benchmark comprises 403 extensive agentic tasks across three tracks (Fundamentals, Simulator-Grounded, and Document-Grounded Verification) and eight application domains with 24,500 verified execution steps. These results indicate that reliable Earth-science agents must go beyond tool access to coordinate heterogeneous workflows, parameterize tools precisely, and preserve artifact provenance.

</details>


### [145] [Mental-R1: Aligning LLM Reasoning for Mental Health Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13176)
*Xin Wang,Boyan Gao,Yibo Yang,David A. Clifton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes CRPO (Cognitive Relative Policy Optimization), a reinforcement learning framework for mental health assessment that mimics human cognitive processes by incorporating stage-dependent uncertainty modeling. It introduces stage-wise entropy regularization to transition from exploratory to confident reasoning, guided by cognitive appraisal theory. Experiments on 8 datasets show a 10.4 percentage point average improvement in weighted F1 over the best RL baseline, with the resulting model Mental-R1 outperforming existing LLMs on reasoning-intensive mental health cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) applied to mental health assessment rely on general-purpose post-training methods that do not align with the cognitive processes underlying human clinical assessment. This mismatch can produce unreliable reasoning outcomes, which is particularly dangerous in high-stakes mental health contexts where accurate, interpretable assessment of anxiety, depression, and suicidality is critical for timely intervention.

Method: CRPO extends Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) by integrating two key innovations: (1) Stage-wise entropy regularization that encourages broad exploration in early reasoning phases and progressively enforces confident, low-entropy decision-making in later stages — mimicking the human cognitive shift from uncertainty to certainty; (2) Formalization of cognitive reasoning stages inspired by cognitive appraisal theory, providing theory-grounded, interpretable inference. The framework is instantiated through the Mental-R1 model.

Result: CRPO achieves an average improvement of 10.4 percentage points in weighted F1-score over the best reinforcement learning baseline across 8 mental health datasets. The CRPO-trained model, Mental-R1, demonstrates clear advantages over existing LLMs specifically on reasoning-intensive cases, validating that the framework enhances reasoning capabilities for mental health assessment.

Conclusion: Aligning LLM post-training with human cognitive assessment processes — specifically the transition from uncertainty to certainty across reasoning stages — significantly improves mental health assessment performance. CRPO provides an effective, theory-grounded reinforcement learning framework that enhances both accuracy and interpretability, suggesting that cognitive-process-aware training is a promising direction for clinical AI applications.

Abstract: Mental health problems such as anxiety, depression, and suicide remain urgent global challenges, where timely and accurate assessment is critical for effective intervention. Recently, large language models have been explored for mental health assessment. However, existing general-purpose post-training methods do not align with the cognitive processes of human assessment, which may lead to unreliable reasoning outcomes. To bridge this gap, we propose Cognitive Relative Policy Optimization (CRPO), a reinforcement learning framework tailored for the mental health domain. CRPO extends group relative policy optimization by integrating stage-dependent uncertainty modeling into the policy optimization process. Specifically, we introduce a stage-wise entropy regularization mechanism that encourages broad exploration in early reasoning phases and progressively enforces confident decision-making in later stages, mimicking the human cognitive shift from uncertainty to certainty. In addition, inspired by cognitive appraisal theory, we formalize cognitive reasoning stages, thereby guiding theory-grounded interpretable inference. Experiments on 8 mental health datasets show that CRPO achieves an average improvement of 10.4 percentage points in weighted F1-score over the best reinforcement learning baseline. Furthermore, the CRPO-trained model Mental-R1 demonstrates clear advantages compared with existing large language models on reasoning-intensive cases, suggesting that CRPO enhances reasoning capabilities for mental health assessment.

</details>


### [146] [Reasoning for Mobile User Experience with Multimodal LLMs: Task, Benchmark, and Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13192)
*Ruichao Mao,Zhou Fang,Teng Guo,Hao Yang,Yaping Li,Shaohua Peng,Maji Huang,Xiaoyu Lin,Shuoyang Liu,Xuepeng Li,Yuyu Zhang,Hai Rao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: UXBench is a multimodal benchmark of 2,000 VQA samples across 8 tasks for evaluating MLLMs on UI/UX reasoning from screenshots. Mainstream MLLMs perform poorly, so the authors introduce UI-UX, a Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking-based model enhanced with reinforcement learning using reward routing and asymmetric transition rewards, achieving SOTA accuracy of 0.7963 on UXBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLM evaluation in UI domains focuses on visual grounding, GUI agents, and design-to-code tasks, but UX-oriented reasoning — assessing usability, visual hierarchy, layout relationships, and content consistency from screenshots — remains underexplored and immature.

Method: First, the authors construct UXBench: a benchmark with 2,000 VQA samples derived from real-world UI screenshots, spanning 8 tasks that probe fine-grained UX issues (layout, visual hierarchy, content consistency). Second, they propose UI-UX, built on Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking and fine-tuned via reinforcement learning with two innovations: (1) a reward routing mechanism that dynamically balances perceptual understanding and logical reasoning during inference, and (2) an asymmetric transition reward that penalizes both redundant and insufficient reasoning steps.

Result: Mainstream MLLMs show fundamentally limited UI-based reasoning capability on UXBench. UI-UX achieves SOTA performance with 0.7963 accuracy, surpassing Claude-4.5-Sonnet (0.6550), while demonstrating strong cross-task generalization and low inference latency.

Conclusion: UX-centric UI evaluation is an important but neglected capability in MLLMs. The combination of UXBench and UI-UX provides both a rigorous testbed and an effective modeling approach to advance MLLM reasoning for real-world interface quality assessment.

Abstract: User experience (UX) centered on usability, perceived consistency, and functional clarity is fundamental to real-world user interfaces (UI). The application of
  multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in the field of user interfaces is evolving rapidly, such as visual element grounding, graphical user interface (GUI)
  agents, and design-to-code generation. However, research efforts on evaluating UX based on UI screenshots are still immature. To address this, we propose UXBench,
  a novel multimodal benchmark consisting of 2,000 VQA data samples designed to assess MLLMs' ability to perform UI-based reasoning. UXBench includes 8 tasks based
  on real-world UI screenshots that require fine-grained diagnosis of UX issues across layout relationships, visual hierarchy, and content consistency. Our
  extensive evaluation of mainstream MLLMs shows that they remain fundamentally limited in their capacity for UI-based reasoning. The results underscore the need
  for further advancements in this area. To bridge this gap, we propose UI-UX, an MLLM based on Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking foundation model and enhanced via reinforcement
  learning with two key innovations: a reward routing mechanism that dynamically balances perceptual understanding and logical reasoning during inference, and an
  asymmetric transition reward that suppresses redundant or insufficient reasoning steps. Experiments demonstrate that UI-UX achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA)
  performance on UXBench, attaining an accuracy of 0.7963 -- surpassing Claude-4.5-Sonnet's 0.6550 -- while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse UI tasks
  and maintaining low inference latency.

</details>


### [147] [Under What Conditions Can a Machine Become Genuinely Creative?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13196)
*Yong Zeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a rigorous framework for genuine machine creativity based on "Designics" (the science of meaning-bearing intentional change), arguing that creativity is not mere output novelty but the structural transformation of incomplete situations through recursive intervention. It specifies ten requirements organized under three laws—perception, conflict, and capability—and demonstrates tractability via case studies. It critiques current AI paradigms (foundation models, open-ended systems, agentic workflows) as insufficient, and argues that proactive ethics must be intrinsic to creative machines, not retrofitted.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems produce seemingly creative outputs, yet there is no clear, principled criterion for determining when a machine is genuinely creative versus merely generative. The paper seeks to establish conditions for authentic machine creativity while preserving human agency in shared cognitive environments, moving beyond surface metrics like novelty or benchmark performance.

Method: The paper develops a requirement framework grounded in Designics—defined as the science of meaning-bearing intentional change. It articulates three foundational laws (perception, conflict, capability) and derives ten specific requirements: environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge/environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, and human-AI co-living. Computational tractability is demonstrated through cyber-physical and cyber-biological studies (recursive element extraction, autonomous mesh generation, neurophysiological/workload analysis). The framework is then tested against pressure cases: open-ended systems, automated discovery, self-modifying agents, foundation models, and agentic workflows.

Result: The pressure cases reveal that existing AI paradigms—despite powerful generative capabilities—fail to satisfy the full set of requirements for genuine machine creativity. They lack integrated perception-conflict-capability loops, value-based scoping, and co-living dynamics. The cyber-physical/cyber-biological studies demonstrate that the ten requirements are computationally tractable, showing that the framework is not merely philosophical but implementable.

Conclusion: Genuine machine creativity requires more than generative power; it demands structural situation-transformation through recursive intervention guided by value-based scoping and human-AI co-living. Proactive AI ethics is not an external constraint but internal to the creative process itself—shaping perception, conflict identification, intervention selection, and knowledge updating. The Designics-based framework offers both a theoretical foundation and a computationally tractable path toward machines that are authentically creative while preserving human agency.

Abstract: Recent AI systems can generate texts, software architectures, hypotheses, designs, and scientific workflows that appear creative. This paper asks under what conditions a machine can become genuinely creative, and how human agency can be preserved within shared cognitive and creative environments. It develops a requirement framework derived from Designics, the science of meaning-bearing intentional change. The paper argues that genuine machine creativity should not be defined by output novelty, current performance, or transient architecture alone. Instead, creativity is understood as the structural transformation of incomplete situations through recursive intervention dynamics. On this view, it depends on ten requirements: environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge and environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, and human-AI co-living. These are organized through the three laws of Designics: perception, conflict, and capability. The paper illustrates the computational tractability of these requirements through selected cyber-physical and cyber-biological studies, including recursive element extraction, autonomous mesh generation, and neurophysiological and workload analysis. It then treats open-ended systems, automated discovery frameworks, self-modifying agents, foundation models, and agentic workflows as pressure cases: they demonstrate powerful generative means but do not by themselves establish genuine machine creativity. Finally, the paper argues that proactive AI ethics is internal to genuine machine creativity rather than an after-the-fact filter. Value-based scoping and human-AI co-living must shape how creative machines perceive environments, identify conflicts, select interventions, observe consequences, update knowledge, and rescope future action.

</details>


### [148] [ARMOR-MAD: Adaptive Routing for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate in Large Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13197)
*Fuqiang Niu,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARMOR-MAD is a training-free heterogeneous multi-agent debate framework that uses conditional computation—routing debates only when needed, stopping early upon convergence, and down-weighting outlier answers—to improve reasoning accuracy and efficiency over fixed-round debate pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-round multi-agent debate (MAD) pipelines are computationally wasteful and can amplify correlated errors among similar agents. There is a need for a more adaptive framework that selectively deploys debate and leverages genuine model heterogeneity.

Method: ARMOR-MAD introduces three components: (1) Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR): compares independently generated Round-0 answers and only triggers debate if disagreement exists; (2) Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE): monitors convergence during debate and terminates early when agents agree; (3) Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD): identifies and down-weights semantically abnormal final answers during majority-vote aggregation.

Result: On MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro, ARMOR-MAD achieves 65.5%, 96.5%, 90.0%, and 81.5% accuracy respectively, consistently outperforming fixed-round heterogeneous debate baselines using the same model pool.

Conclusion: Genuine model heterogeneity combined with agreement-based control mechanisms is critical for making multi-agent debate both more accurate and computationally efficient.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate (MAD) can improve large language model reasoning, but fixed debate pipelines often waste computation and can amplify correlated errors among similar agents. We propose ARMOR-MAD, a training-free heterogeneous MAD framework that treats debate as conditional computation. ARMOR-MAD combines three components: Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR) decides whether independently generated Round-0 answers require debate; Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE) stops debate after convergence; and Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD) down-weights abnormal final answers during aggregation. Across MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro, ARMOR-MAD consistently improves over fixed-round heterogeneous debate with the same model pool, reaching 65.5\%, 96.5\%, 90.0\%, and 81.5\% accuracy, respectively. The results suggest that genuine model heterogeneity and agreement-based control are both important for making MAD more accurate and efficient.

</details>


### [149] [A Minimal Model of Bounded Trade-Off Screening in Multi-Attribute Choice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13201)
*Manisha Dubey,Anirban Sarkar,Subramanian Ramamoorthy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a bounded trade-off reasoning framework for multi-attribute choice, where decisions are filtered through a screening process that evaluates gain-loss balance across attributes, governed by a context-sensitive trade-off tolerance parameter. Simulations demonstrate that this mechanism generates preference patterns distinct from classical compensatory models and captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical multi-attribute choice models assume fully compensatory utility aggregation, but empirical evidence shows that people often reject alternatives with poor performance on critical attributes (non-compensatory behavior). Existing models fail to adequately capture how decision-makers bound their trade-offs and how this bounding varies by context.

Method: The authors develop a computational framework centered on a screening process that evaluates the balance between gains and losses across attributes. The key innovation is a trade-off tolerance parameter that sets an acceptable imbalance threshold. They conduct simulations to compare the model's predicted preference patterns against standard utility-based models and to explore how varying the tolerance parameter generates context-dependent trade-off behaviors.

Result: The bounded trade-off screening mechanism produces preference patterns that diverge from those predicted by standard utility-based models. The trade-off tolerance parameter successfully captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior, demonstrating the model's flexibility in accounting for both compensatory and non-compensatory decision strategies.

Conclusion: Bounded trade-off screening constitutes a plausible computational mechanism for multi-attribute choice. The framework generates testable predictions for future behavioral studies, suggesting a productive avenue for bridging the gap between normative utility models and observed human decision-making heuristics.

Abstract: Human decision-making often involves choosing between multi-attribute alternatives, yet classical models assume fully compensatory utility aggregation despite evidence that people reject options with poor performance on critical attributes. We propose a bounded trade-off reasoning framework in which decisions are governed by a screening process that evaluates the balance between gains and losses across attributes. The model introduces a trade-off tolerance parameter that controls acceptable imbalance and can vary across contexts. Through simulation, we show that this mechanism produces preference patterns that differ from standard utility-based models and captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior. These results establish bounded trade-off screening as a plausible computational mechanism for multi-attribute choice and generate testable predictions for future behavioral studies.

</details>


### [150] [Hallucination in Medical Imaging AI: A Cross-Modality Analytical Framework for Taxonomy, Detection, and Mitigation under Regulatory Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13211)
*Omar Alshahrani,Muzammil Behzad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper provides a cross-modality analysis of AI hallucination in medical imaging, unifying taxonomies, comparing general-purpose vs. medical-specialized foundation models, and evaluating mitigation strategies mapped to FDA lifecycle frameworks. Key findings include that general-purpose models unexpectedly outperform medical-specialized ones on hallucination benchmarks, and that combined mitigation approaches (physics-informed constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, human-in-the-loop safeguards) are most effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems are being deployed across medical imaging faster than their failure modes — particularly hallucination — are understood. Hallucinations (fabricated anatomical structures, missed findings, incorrect laterality, invented measurements) have direct clinical consequences for biopsy decisions, staging, and treatment planning, creating urgent need for systematic understanding of taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation.

Method: Structured narrative review synthesizing peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities. The study addresses three research questions: (1) unifying existing taxonomies across modalities, (2) comparing hallucination rates between medical-specialized and general-purpose foundation models, and (3) evaluating mitigation strategies for FDA lifecycle compatibility. Findings are mapped to the FDA's Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan frameworks.

Result: Three taxonomic frameworks together cover the imaging pipeline more comprehensively than any single framework. General-purpose foundation models outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination-specific benchmarks, suggesting narrow domain fine-tuning can introduce overfitting-induced confabulation. A very high percentage of AI-generated flags required expert radiologist correction before clinical use. Physics-informed architectural constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards each address distinct failure modes and are most effective when combined.

Conclusion: Hallucination management must be treated as a lifecycle obligation rather than a pre-deployment checklist, aligned with FDA's Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan frameworks. Radiologist oversight remains essential, and combined mitigation strategies offer the most robust defense against the diverse failure modes of medical imaging AI.

Abstract: AI systems are being deployed across medical imaging faster than their failure modes are understood. At this point in time, the failure of greatest clinical concern is hallucination: clinically plausible but factually incorrect outputs, including fabricated anatomical structures, missed findings, incorrect laterality, and invented measurements in generated reports, with direct consequences, for example, for biopsy decisions, staging, and treatment planning. This structured narrative synthesizes peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities to produce a cross-modality analysis of hallucination taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation. Specifically, we address three questions in this study: (1) how can existing taxonomies be unified across modalities?, (2) how do medical-specialized foundation models hallucinate less than general-purpose ones?, and (3) which mitigation strategies are effective and compatible with FDA lifecycle oversight? We note that three taxonomic frameworks together cover the imaging pipeline in a way no single framework does alone. We also highlight that general-purpose foundation models outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination-specific benchmarks, indicating that narrow domain fine-tuning can introduce overfitting-induced confabulation. At the same time, the oversight of radiologists remains essential; for instance, a very high percentage of of AI-generated flags required expert correction before clinical use. Physics-informed architectural constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards each address different failure modes and is effective when combined. All findings are mapped to the FDA's Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan frameworks, which treat hallucination management as a lifecycle obligation rather than a pre-deployment checklist.

</details>


### [151] [Brick: Spatial Capability Routing for the Mixture-of-Models (MoM) Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13241)
*Francesco Massa,Marco Cristofanilli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Brick is a multimodal router that estimates per-query difficulty across six capability dimensions and dispatches to models using a cost-penalized geometric rule, achieving higher accuracy than any single model while reducing costs by up to 22.15×.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM routers rely on superficial features (domain labels, keywords, token count) and ignore within-domain difficulty variance that determines model success. Since frontier models cost 10–100× more than local open-weight models, even small per-request savings at production scale translate directly to reduced cloud bills.

Method: Brick scores each model on six capability dimensions and computes a per-query difficulty estimate. Dispatch decisions use a cost-penalized geometric rule. A continuous preference knob allows operators to slide between max-quality and max-saving profiles at deployment time.

Result: On 5,504 queries: max-quality mode reaches 76.98% accuracy (beating best single model at 75.02% and all tested routers); neutral cost-quality profile yields 74.11% accuracy at 4.71× lower cost; min-cost mode cuts cost 22.15× with 11.85 points accuracy loss. Median latency drops from 51.2s to 22.8s.

Conclusion: Dimension-aware difficulty estimation combined with cost-penalized routing significantly outperforms surface-feature routers and single-model baselines, enabling flexible quality–cost trade-offs at production scale.

Abstract: Defining query difficulty is one of the hardest problems in deployment engineering. Existing LLM routers rely on surface features such as domain labels, keywords, and token count, ignoring the within-domain variance that actually determines model success. Frontier models cost ten to one hundred times more than local open-weight models, so at production scale even small per-request savings become a direct cloud-bill lever. We present Brick, a multimodal router that scores each model on six capability dimensions, combines this with a per-query difficulty estimate, and dispatches via a cost-penalized geometric rule. A continuous preference knob lets operators slide between max-quality and max-saving profiles at deploy time. On a benchmark of 5,504 queries, Brick at max-quality reaches 76.98% accuracy, beating the best single model (75.02%) and all tested routers. At a neutral cost-quality profile, Brick achieves 74.11% accuracy at 4.71x lower cost than always using the strongest model. At min-cost, it cuts cost 22.15x with 11.85 points accuracy loss. Median latency drops from 51.2s to 22.8s.

</details>


### [152] [EPIG: Emotion-Based Prompting for Personalised Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13247)
*Emna Othmen,Mohamed Yassine Landolsi,Lotfi Ben Romdhane*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EPIG is a training-free, lightweight method that enriches text prompts with psychologically grounded emotion representations (valence-arousal) to improve emotional expressiveness in text-to-image diffusion models, achieving significant reductions in arousal error (14–17%) while preserving semantic consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-image diffusion models rely on generic prompting strategies that fail to capture nuanced emotional intent and affective attributes. There is a need for a method that enhances emotional expressiveness at the prompt level without modifying or retraining the underlying generative model.

Method: EPIG operates at the prompt level using psychologically informed emotion representations based on the valence-arousal model. It employs structured, role-aware prompt enrichment to augment emotion-related components of prompts. The method is training-free, lightweight, and does not alter the image generation backbone.

Result: On a benchmark of 10 diverse prompts, EPIG reduced mean arousal error by 14% compared to naive insertion and 12% compared to LLM-based prompt expansion, with statistical significance. The improvement reached 17% for prompts with explicit subjects (humans, children, animals). EPIG also preserved valence alignment and semantic consistency measured by CLIPScore, validated by ablation studies.

Conclusion: EPIG effectively enhances emotional expressiveness in text-to-image generation, particularly for arousal control, while remaining training-free and suitable for resource-constrained and personalized scenarios. Its subject-sensitive behavior suggests targeted emotional prompt enrichment is a viable path forward.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved impressive results in synthesizing high-quality images from natural language prompts. However, commonly used prompting strategies remain relatively generic, limiting the model's ability to accurately express emotional intent and nuanced affective attributes.
  This work proposes EPIG, a method that enhances emotional expressiveness at the prompt level prior to image generation. Grounded in psychologically informed emotion representations (valence-arousal) and leveraging structured, role-aware prompt enrichment, EPIG enriches emotion-related components of prompts without modifying or retraining the image generation backbone.
  The resulting emotion-aware prompts guide the generative process toward more emotionally coherent visual outputs, with particular effectiveness in controlling arousal. EPIG is lightweight, training-free, and well suited for resource-constrained and personalized image generation scenarios.
  Experimental results on a benchmark of 10 diverse prompts show that EPIG reduces mean arousal error compared to strong baselines, including naive insertion and LLM-based prompt expansion, with reductions of 14% and 12%, respectively. These improvements are statistically significant. EPIG also preserves valence alignment and semantic consistency, as measured by CLIPScore and supported by ablation studies.
  The effect is more pronounced on prompts containing explicit subjects such as humans, children, or animals, where the reduction reaches 17%, highlighting the subject-sensitive behavior of the proposed method.

</details>


### [153] [Multi-Field Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Maritime Accident Root Cause Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13249)
*Seongjin Kim,Sungil Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A field-aware hybrid RAG framework using 13,329 Korean maritime accident reports improves precedent retrieval (NormRecall@100: 0.18→0.55) and RCA generation quality (LLM-as-judge: 3.34→3.72) by indexing incident cards with Summary, Causes, and Disposition fields fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maritime accident adjudication reports are a rich source for root cause analysis, but manually retrieving relevant precedents and drafting consistent RCA reports from decades of records is labor-intensive and inefficient.

Method: The authors construct a structured knowledge base of "incident cards" from 13,329 KMST reports (1971–2025), each with three fields (Summary, Causes, Disposition) and a hierarchical L1/L2 cause taxonomy. Retrieval uses a field-aware hybrid strategy combining sparse and dense rankings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Due to the absence of large-scale expert relevance labels, proxy relevance scores derived from metadata are used for evaluation, with ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG as metrics. A generator grounded on retrieved precedents produces the final RCA.

Result: The proposed retrieval method significantly outperforms baselines: NormRecall@100 improves from 0.18 to 0.55. Grounding generation on retrieved precedents raises LLM-as-a-judge scores from 3.34 to 3.74.

Conclusion: Field-aware RAG can substantially streamline maritime safety investigation workflows by enabling faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting.

Abstract: Maritime accident adjudication reports contain critical tribunal findings for root cause analysis (RCA), yet retrieving relevant precedents and drafting consistent reports from decades of records remains labor-intensive. This paper proposes a multi-field hybrid retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for automated maritime RCA, utilizing a comprehensive dataset of 13,329 Korea Maritime Safety Tribunal (KMST) reports (1971-2025). We transform raw adjudications into a structured knowledge base of "incident cards", indexing three distinct fields-Summary, Causes, and Disposition-alongside a hierarchical L1/L2 cause taxonomy. Our retrieval strategy employs a field-aware hybrid approach, fusing sparse and dense rankings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Given the lack of large-scale expert relevance labels, we evaluate retrieval performance using ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG based on a metadata-derived proxy relevance score. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed retrieval significantly outperforms baseline methods, improving NormRecall@100 from 0.18 to 0.55. Furthermore, grounding the generator on the retrieved precedents enhances RCA generation quality over an LLM-only baseline, increasing the LLM-as-a-judge score from 3.34 to 3.72. These findings suggest that field-aware RAG can substantially streamline maritime safety investigation workflows by enabling faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting.

</details>


### [154] [MOSAIC: Modality-Specific Adaptation for Incremental Continual Learning in Parkinson's Disease Gait Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13258)
*Minlin Zeng,Zhipeng Zhou,Yang Qiu,Martin J. McKeown,Zhiqi Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a continual learning framework for modality-incremental Parkinson's disease gait assessment that addresses the Toxic Teacher phenomenon, modality-specific statistical shifts, and plasticity loss through Modality-Specific Warm-Up, statistics-decoupled MSBN, and curriculum-guided Plasticity Recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical gait assessment for Parkinson's disease increasingly uses heterogeneous sensors, but systems rarely collect all modalities simultaneously. New sensors arrive incrementally (device upgrades, protocol changes, multi-center deployment), while historical data are often inaccessible due to privacy and storage constraints. Existing continual learning methods suffer from three key issues in this modality-incremental setting: unreliable cross-modal distillation, modality-specific statistical shifts, and reduced plasticity after knowledge preservation.

Method: MOSAIC introduces three components: (1) Modality-Specific Warm-Up that counters the identified "Toxic Teacher" phenomenon by stabilizing newly learned modality representations before distillation begins; (2) a statistics-decoupled Modality-Specific Batch Normalization (MSBN) architecture that isolates per-sensor statistics while preserving a shared semantic backbone; and (3) a curriculum-guided repulsive objective for Plasticity Recovery that maintains legacy knowledge while restoring modality-specific learning capacity.

Result: Experiments on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets demonstrate that MOSAIC improves final task performance and effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: MOSAIC provides an effective and compact solution for modality-incremental continual learning in PD gait assessment, enabling robust integration of new sensor modalities without access to historical data.

Abstract: Gait-based Parkinson's disease assessment increasingly relies on heterogeneous sensors, but clinical systems rarely collect all modalities simultaneously. New sensors may arrive through device upgrades, protocol changes, or multi-center deployment, while historical patient data are often unavailable because of privacy and storage constraints. This modality-incremental setting faces three challenges: unreliable cross-modal distillation, modality-specific statistical shifts, and reduced plasticity after preservation. We propose MOSAIC, a compact continual learning framework. First, we identify the Toxic Teacher phenomenon and introduce Modality-Specific Warm-Up to stabilize newly learned modality representations before distillation. Second, we propose a statistics-decoupled MSBN architecture that isolates sensor statistics while maintaining a shared semantic backbone. Third, we design a curriculum-guided repulsive objective for Plasticity Recovery, preserving legacy knowledge while recovering modality-specific capacity. Experiments on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets show that MOSAIC improves final performance and mitigates forgetting. Project code is available at: https://github.com/minlinzeng/MOSAIC_Modality-Specific-Adaptation-for-Incremental-Continual-Learning-in-PD-Gait-Assessment.git

</details>


### [155] [From Verdict to Process: Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Stage Fact Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13262)
*Rongxin Yang,Shenghong He,Siyuan Zhu,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProFact is an agentic reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes all stages of the fact verification pipeline (claim decomposition, evidence gathering, answer generation, verdict prediction) using process-aware rewards, outperforming existing methods in both accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based fact verification systems optimize individual stages in isolation or use fixed heuristics, which prevents adaptive coordination across stages and leads to suboptimal overall outcomes. The final veracity label provides only sparse, delayed supervision, making end-to-end optimization challenging.

Method: ProFact frames multi-stage fact verification as a reinforcement learning problem, training a unified policy that jointly coordinates all stages. To overcome sparse rewards from final labels, it introduces process-aware rewards that provide stage-level learning signals throughout the verification trajectory.

Result: ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines on both verification performance and inference efficiency metrics.

Conclusion: Process-aware trajectory optimization is an effective approach for multi-stage fact verification, enabling better coordination across stages and improved overall outcomes compared to isolated or heuristic-based optimization.

Abstract: Recent approaches combining Large Language Models (LLMs) with retrieval-augmented reasoning have shown promise for automated fact verification. To process complex claims, these verification pipelines typically execute multi-stage workflows that coordinate tightly coupled modules, including claim decomposition, evidence gathering, and verdict prediction. However, existing methods optimize individual stages in isolation or rely on fixed heuristics, which limits adaptive coordination among stages and can lead to suboptimal outcomes. In this work, we propose ProFact, an agentic reinforcement learning framework for end-to-end optimization of multi-stage fact verification trajectories. ProFact trains a unified policy to coordinate claim decomposition, evidence seeking, answer generation, and verdict prediction. To address the sparse and delayed supervision provided by final veracity labels, ProFact introduces process-aware rewards that provide stage-level learning signals throughout the verification process. Empirical evaluation shows that ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines in both verification performance and inference efficiency. These results highlight the effectiveness of process-aware trajectory optimization for multi-stage fact verification.

</details>


### [156] [ERTS: Adversarial Robustness Testing of Ethical AI via Semantic Perturbation in a Bounded Consequence Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13282)
*Pratyush Chaudhari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ERTS, a formal adversarial testing framework for evaluating AI systems' ethical reasoning robustness. It encodes dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space, applies constrained semantic perturbations, and measures instability via a 4-component index. Testing across 4 baseline models and 2 LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash, Llama 3.2) on 50 scenarios revealed only 33% pass assessment, with Llama 3.2 particularly vulnerable to fairness and information degradation attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal methods for evaluating AI robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning are underdeveloped, despite deployment in high-stakes ethical contexts (healthcare, autonomous vehicles, employment screening). There is no existing framework that combines a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single adversarial pipeline.
