Method: ERTS, a closed-pipeline framework with four components: (1) encoding ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in ethical theory; (2) applying 17 semantic perturbation functions under 6 validity constraint classes including a novel semantic coherence constraint; (3) measuring decision deviation via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); and (4) producing domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. Evaluated on 4 structured baseline models and 2 production LLMs across 50 scenarios spanning 8 domains, generating 1,500 adversarial test cases.

Result: Only 33% of tested models achieved assessment clearance. The local Llama-3.2 model was particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks, with an Ethical Robustness Score (ERS) of 0.737.

Conclusion: ERTS provides a novel, unified adversarial testing pipeline for ethical robustness that was previously absent. The poor clearance rate (33%) and vulnerability patterns underscore the urgent need for formal ethical robustness evaluation before deploying AI systems in high-stakes contexts.

Abstract: As AI systems are deployed in high-stakes ethical contexts such as healthcare triage, autonomous vehicle control, and employment screening, formal methods for evaluating their robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning remain underdeveloped. This paper introduces the Ethical Robustness Testing System (ERTS), a closed-pipeline framework that: (1) encodes ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in established ethical theory; (2) applies 17 semantic perturbation functions subject to 6 validity constraint classes including a novel semantic coherence constraint; (3) measures decision deviation via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); and (4) produces domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. We evaluate 4 structured baseline models and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash and Llama 3.2) across 50 ethical scenarios spanning 8 deployment domains, generating 1,500 adversarial test cases. Results demonstrate that only 33% of models achieve assessment clearance, with the local Llama-3.2 model proving particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks (ERS = 0.737). To the best of our knowledge, no existing framework combines a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single adversarial testing pipeline.

</details>


### [157] [Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal Learning for Coastal Wave Peak Period Estimation from Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13302)
*Abubakar Hamisu Kamagata,Dharm Singh Jat,Attlee Munyaradzi Gamundani,Abhishek Srivastava,Paramasivam Saravanakumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework is proposed for estimating nearshore wave peak periods from passive coastal video, using multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning and physics-informed regularization. Transformer-based models excelled in instantaneous accuracy, while lightweight recurrent-convolutional models offered better temporal stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional wave monitoring systems (buoys, radar) are costly and spatially limited. Existing deep learning video-based methods lack physical interpretability and oceanographic validation, creating a need for accurate, physically consistent, and cost-efficient passive monitoring.

Method: The framework integrates: (1) automated temporal-variance-based region-of-interest detection, (2) multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning (synthetic pretraining, silver-label adaptation, expert fine-tuning), and (3) physics-informed regularization. Both transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional spatiotemporal architectures were evaluated, with explainability auditing used to verify hydrodynamic relevance.

Result: Transformer-based architectures achieved superior instantaneous prediction accuracy. Lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures showed higher temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Ablation studies confirmed physics-guided regularization improved trend-following consistency and reduced physically implausible predictions. Explainability auditing confirmed attention focused on hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions, aligning with physical wave propagation behavior.

Conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates strong promise for cost-efficient, operationally feasible long-term coastal wave monitoring using physics-guided video-based deep learning, offering a viable alternative to traditional monitoring infrastructure.

Abstract: Wave parameters in the nearshore are crucial for coastal engineering, shoreline protection, marine hazard assessment, and coastal management for climate resilience. Traditional monitoring systems like buoys and radar platforms offer accurate monitoring but can have high installation and maintenance expenses and limited spatial coverage. Passive ocean monitoring using video has been achieved by leveraging deep learning, however, many methods are not physically interpretable, feasible, and validated for oceanography. In thiswork, a Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework for direct estimation of nearshore wave peak periods from passive coastal video stream is proposed. The framework combines automated temporal-variance based region-of-interest detection, multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning, and physics-informed regularization to enhance the predictive accuracy and physical consistency. A variety of spatiotemporal architectures were assessed, such as transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional ones, alongside synthetic pretraining,silver-label adaptation, and expert fine-tuning. The results show that transformer-based architectures outperformed in terms of the accuracy of the instantaneous prediction, while lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures achieved higher temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Ablation studies also demonstrated the benefits of physics-guided regularization in terms of trend-following consistency, and physically implausible predictions. Explainability auditing also helped to focus attention in hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions and showed good agreement with the physically derived wave propagation behavior. In general, the proposed framework shows the promise of physics-guided video-based deep learning systems for long-term coastal wave monitoring that are cost-efficient and operationally feasible.

</details>


### [158] [ReSum: Synergizing LLM Reasoning and Summarization with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13316)
*Xucong Wang,Ziyu Ma,Yong Wang,Shidong Yang,Hailang Huang,Renda Li,Pengkun Wang,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReSum is an RLVR framework that trains LLMs to self-summarize their reasoning trajectories, improving performance by ~4% while reducing rollout length by 18.6% through a contrastive, summarization-aware adaptive mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods incentivize excessively long reasoning rollouts, degrading coherence and exhausting context budgets. Current solutions rely on external mechanisms to organize rollouts rather than enabling models to manage their own reasoning. The authors aim to overcome this by teaching LLMs intrinsic self-summarization.

Method: ReSum employs a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism with two contrastive branches: (1) when the model spontaneously triggers self-summarization, the summarization phrase is masked to create a non-summarization branch; (2) at non-summarization positions, the phrase is randomly injected to create a summarization branch. A summarization-aware advantage function enables fine-grained comparison between these contrastive trajectories. The approach is grounded in pilot findings that self-summarization lowers token-level entropy and mitigates error propagation from incorrect prefixes.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReSum improves performance by an average of 4% while simultaneously reducing rollout length by 18.6%.

Conclusion: Incorporating self-summarization into RLVR training via contrastive evaluation enables LLMs to manage their own reasoning trajectories more efficiently, yielding both better accuracy and shorter, more coherent reasoning chains.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is a central technique for improving long-horizon reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing RLVR methods often encourage unnecessarily long reasoning rollouts, which can degrade reasoning coherence and exhaust the available context budget. Existing approaches to long-context organization often depend on external mechanisms to organize rollouts, rather than enabling the model to manage its own reasoning trajectory. To address this limitation, we propose ReSum, a novel RLVR framework that enables LLMs to compress and organize their reasoning trajectories through self-summarization. Our pilot studies show that self-summarization stabilizes generation by lowering token-level entropy, and that introducing a ``summarization'' phrase can substantially mitigate errors propagated from an incorrect rollout prefix. Motivated by these findings, ReSum adopts a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism that contrastively evaluates whether self-summarization benefits the ongoing reasoning process. Specifically, when the model spontaneously triggers self-summarization, ReSum masks the summarization phrase to create a contrastive branch; for non-summarization positions, it instead randomly injects the phrase to create a matched branch. We further design a summarization-aware advantage to enable finer-grained comparison between contrastive rollout trajectories. Extensive experiments show that ReSum improves performance at an average of 4\% while reducing rollout length by 18.6\%.

</details>


### [159] [IterCAD: An Iterative Multimodal Agent for Visually-Grounded CAD Generation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13368)
*Tao Hu,Jiaxin Ai,Licheng Wen,Xueheng Li,Shu Zou,Siqi Li,Nianchen Deng,Xinyu Cai,Hongbin Zhou,Pinlong Cai,Daocheng Fu,Yu Yang,Hairong Zhang,Botian Shi,Xuemeng Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IterCAD is a multimodal agent framework that enables closed-loop, interactive CAD generation and editing through multi-turn interactions with an executable CAD sandbox. It supports Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing tasks, trained via progressive SFT and geometry-aware RL, and introduces the CD-TR curve as a new evaluation metric that unifies code validity and geometric precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated CAD methods rely on open-loop, one-shot generation, which is misaligned with the iterative, interactive nature of real-world industrial design workflows. There is a gap between research approaches and practical CAD practices that require closed-loop refinement.

Method: The IterCAD framework formulates CAD generation as multi-turn interactions between a multimodal agent and an executable CAD sandbox. A data synthesis pipeline generates standard-compliant multi-view engineering drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and interaction trajectories with industrial manufacturing features. Training uses progressive supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking to improve code executability and geometric fidelity. Evaluation introduces the IterCAD-Bench suite and the Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve with AUC-TR metric for survivor-bias-free assessment.

Result: IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, while demonstrating superior closed-loop iterative refinement capabilities.

Conclusion: The IterCAD framework successfully bridges the gap between automated CAD generation and real-world iterative design practices, establishing a new paradigm for closed-loop interactive CAD systems with robust evaluation standards.

Abstract: Computer-Aided Design is pivotal in modern manufacturing, yet existing automated methods predominantly rely on open-loop, one-shot generation, creating a mismatch with iterative real-world practices. In this paper, we present IterCAD, a unified multimodal agent framework for closed-loop, interactive CAD generation and editing. We formulate the task as a multi-turn interaction between a multimodal agent and an executable CAD sandbox, covering three tasks: Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing. To support this, we develop a data synthesis pipeline incorporating advanced industrial manufacturing features to generate standard-compliant multi-view engineering drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and high-fidelity interaction trajectories. We optimize the agent via progressive SFT followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking to enhance code executability and geometric fidelity. Finally, we introduce the IterCAD-Bench evaluation suite and propose the Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve alongside its AUC-TR metric, establishing a survivor-bias-free standard that unifies code validity and geometric precision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, while exhibiting superior capabilities in closed-loop iterative refinement.

</details>


### [160] [A Quantitative Experimental Repeated Measures Study of Training Dynamics in a Small Llama Style Language Model Under a Compute-Aware Token Budget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13370)
*Joe Dwyer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A study analyzing training dynamics of a small Llama-style LM under a fixed token budget finds that validation loss/perplexity initially improve sharply but later degrade non-monotonically. Repeated measures ANOVA confirms significant interval effects, and interval-level telemetry reveals instability, backslides, and diminishing returns that endpoint metrics alone obscure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional LM evaluation focuses on endpoint performance, which may mask important training dynamics. The authors argue that under compute-constrained budgets, understanding how model quality evolves across training intervals—rather than solely at the final checkpoint—is critical for making informed resource-allocation decisions.

Method: Six independent training runs of a 4.26M-parameter Llama-style model on TinyStories, CPU-based full-precision training, with a ~20M token budget. Metrics (validation loss, perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide/spike behavior, between-seed variability) were collected across 21 token-based intervals, yielding 126 seed×interval observations. Repeated measures ANOVA tested for significant interval effects, and descriptive trajectory analysis examined non-monotonic patterns.

Result: Repeated measures ANOVA revealed statistically significant interval effects for validation loss, perplexity, and rolling volatility. Validation loss dropped from 8.3552 (init) to 2.7996 (~4M tokens), then rose to 3.9010 at the final checkpoint. Perplexity mirrored this U-shaped trajectory. Recurrent backslides were observed, and no stable phase was detected under predefined criteria. Diminishing and even negative returns emerged from additional token exposure late in training.

Conclusion: Compute-aware LM evaluation should incorporate training trajectories, not just endpoint metrics. Under constrained budgets, additional tokens may increase cost without proportional generalization gains. Interval-level telemetry can expose instability, regression, and diminishing returns that final metrics conceal, informing better early-stopping and resource-allocation strategies.

Abstract: This study examines training dynamics in a small Llama-style language model trained under a fixed, compute-constrained token budget. Rather than evaluating efficiency solely through endpoint performance, the study uses a quantitative experimental repeated measures design to analyze how validation loss, validation perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide behavior, spike behavior, and between-seed variability change across token-based training intervals. Six independent training runs were conducted on a 4.26-million-parameter model using the TinyStories corpus, CPU-based full-precision training, and a target budget of approximately 20 million cumulative training tokens. Metrics were collected across 21 intervals, producing 126 seed-by-interval observations. Repeated measures ANOVA showed statistically significant interval effects for validation loss, validation perplexity, and rolling volatility. Descriptive trajectories revealed rapid early improvement followed by non-monotonic degradation during later training intervals. Mean validation loss decreased from 8.3552 at initialization to 2.7996 near 4 million tokens, but increased to 3.9010 by the final checkpoint. Validation perplexity followed the same pattern, falling sharply early in training before rising later. Derived telemetry further showed recurrent validation-loss backslides and no interval-summary evidence of a stable phase under the predefined criteria. These findings suggest that compute-aware language model evaluation should examine training trajectories rather than endpoint metrics alone. In constrained compute settings, additional token exposure may increase computational cost without producing proportional generalization gains, and interval-level telemetry can reveal instability, regression, and diminishing returns that final metrics may obscure.

</details>


### [161] [MiniMax Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13392)
*Xunhao Lai,Weiqi Xu,Yufeng Yang,Qiaorui Chen,Yang Xu,Lunbin Zeng,Xiaolong Li,Haohai Sun,Haichao Zhu,Vito Zhang,Pengyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) is a blockwise sparse attention mechanism that extends Grouped Query Attention (GQA) with a lightweight Index Branch that selects top-k KV blocks per GQA group, achieving 28.4× attention compute reduction at 1M context with no quality degradation on a 109B multimodal model, plus 14.2× prefill and 7.6× decoding wall-clock speedups via a co-designed GPU kernel.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ultra-long-context processing (hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens) is critical for agentic workflows, repository-scale reasoning, and persistent memory in frontier LLMs, but the quadratic cost of standard softmax attention renders this prohibitively expensive at deployment scale. Existing solutions lack a combination of quality preservation, hardware efficiency, and deployment simplicity.

Method: MSA builds on Grouped Query Attention by adding a lightweight Index Branch that scores key-value blocks and selects a distinct Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval with block-level execution. The Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. A co-designed GPU execution path employs exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to maximize tensor-core utilization under block-granular memory access.

Result: On a 109B-parameter natively multimodal model, MSA matches GQA performance while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4× at 1M context. The custom kernel delivers 14.2× prefill speedup and 7.6× decoding speedup on H800 GPUs.

Conclusion: MSA demonstrates that carefully designed blockwise sparse attention with group-specific retrieval and hardware-aware kernel design can eliminate the quadratic bottleneck of full attention at million-token scales without sacrificing model quality, and has been validated in a production-grade publicly released multimodal model (MiniMax-M3).

Abstract: Ultra-long-context capability is becoming indispensable for frontier LLMs: agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, and persistent memory all require the model to jointly attend over hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens, yet the quadratic cost of softmax attention makes this untenable at deployment scale. We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). A lightweight Index Branch scores key-value blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval while maintaining efficient block-level execution; the Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. Designed around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA is deliberately streamlined, making it straightforward to deploy efficiently across a broad range of GPUs. To translate sparsity into practical speedups, we co-design MSA with a GPU execution path that uses exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization under block-granular access. On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA performs on par with GQA while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context. Paired with our co-designed kernel, MSA achieves 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Our inference kernel is available at: https://github.com/MiniMax-AI/MSA. A production-grade natively multimodal model powered by MSA has been publicly released at: https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3.

</details>


### [162] [Optimizing Appliance Scheduling for Solar Energy Management Using Metaheuristic Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13407)
*Hiba Ahmed,Alexander E. I. Brownlee,Jason Adair,Simon T. Powers*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A metaheuristic approach combining Iterated Local Search and Simulated Annealing optimizes appliance start times to maximize solar energy utilization while minimizing user inconvenience, extended across multiple days to handle task spillover.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solar energy generation is temporally misaligned with household appliance usage patterns, as users prefer operating appliances on their own schedules rather than when solar energy is available. This creates a scheduling optimization problem that existing work typically addresses only within a single day, ignoring unfinished tasks that spill over to subsequent days.

Method: A metaheuristic framework combining Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA) is used to optimize appliance start times. The model incorporates appliance operating durations, power consumption profiles, inverter limits, battery state of charge constraints, and solar generation forecasts. Crucially, the scheduling horizon is extended beyond a single day to accommodate task spillover, enabling sequential multi-day operation.

Result: The sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints while preserving user convenience under exclusive solar generation. It successfully handles spillover tasks from previous days and ensures operational continuity.

Conclusion: The multi-day scheduling approach demonstrates practical viability for aligning appliance usage with solar availability. The findings open future research directions on multi-objective trade-offs among equipment investment sizing, return on investment, and user satisfaction.

Abstract: Renewable energy is essential for meeting future energy demands; however, solar energy generation, which occurs only during daylight hours often does not align with household consumption patterns. Appliances such as cookers, washing machines, and dryers are typically operated according to user preferred schedules rather than solar energy availability, creating a scheduling optimization problem. The objective is to determine optimal appliance start times to maximize renewable energy utilization while minimizing user inconvenience and adhering to system constraints. This paper presents a metaheuristic approach using Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA) to optimize appliance start times, while considering appliance operating durations, power consumption, inverter limit, battery state of charge constraints, and solar generation forecasts. Unlike most existing work, the scheduling is extended beyond a single day to accommodate unfinished tasks from previous days (spillover), ensuring operational continuity and enabling sequential operation across multiple days. Experimental results show that the sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints while ensuring user convenience under exclusive solar generation. These findings also open opportunities for future research on multi-objective trade-offs between investment in equipment of various sizes, return on that investment, and user satisfaction.

</details>


### [163] [Evaluation Sovereignty in Metadata-Driven Classification: A Multi-Track Framework for Weakly Supervised Information Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13436)
*Raymond Vasquez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper challenges the assumption that evaluation is neutral by introducing "evaluation sovereignty" — the independence of performance metrics from label authority — and demonstrates through multi-track experiments on scientific metadata that models perform well under operational labels but collapse under independent gold-standard evaluation, revealing that reported metrics often reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In operational machine learning systems, labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised, yet evaluation is conventionally treated as an objective measurement. The authors argue that performance metrics can become conditioned on the label-generation process itself, undermining the validity of evaluation. This is especially acute in large-scale metadata-driven systems where label authority is distributed and unverified.

Method: The authors propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies the sources of training labels and evaluation labels (operational/"silver" vs. independent/"gold"). They apply this to hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata, comparing performance across different label-authority regimes using both classification metrics (Micro-F1) and ranking-based metrics to disentangle latent model signal from classification validity.

Result: Under silver evaluation, models show strong performance (Micro-F1 ≈ 0.54), but this drops dramatically to ~0.03 under gold evaluation, especially for fine-grained classification. However, ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, indicating that the model retains latent discriminative capacity even when classification metrics collapse. This reveals a divergence between alignment with labeling processes and true predictive capability.

Conclusion: Evaluation validity should be reconceptualized as a system-level property shaped by label governance rather than an inherent model attribute. The multi-track framework provides a practical methodology for auditing systems operating under weak supervision. The findings caution against over-reliance on reported metrics without scrutinizing the label-authority regimes under which they were obtained.

Abstract: Evaluation in machine learning is typically treated as a neutral measurement process. However, in operational information systems, evaluation outcomes are often conditioned by the processes used to generate labels. This paper does not seek to improve classification performance. Instead, it examines the validity of performance measurement under differing label-authority regimes. This issue is particularly relevant in large-scale metadata-driven systems, where labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised.
  We introduce evaluation sovereignty, defined as the degree to which performance metrics are independent of label authority and supervision regime, and propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies training and evaluation label sources. Using hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata, we demonstrate that models exhibiting strong performance under operational ("silver") evaluation degrade substantially under independent ("gold") evaluation, particularly for fine-grained classification. For example, Micro-F1 decreases from approximately 0.54 to 0.03. Notably, ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, revealing a divergence between latent model signal and classification validity.
  These findings suggest that commonly reported performance metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability. We therefore reconceptualize evaluation validity as a system-level property shaped by label governance and provide a practical methodology for auditing intelligent systems operating under weak supervision.

</details>


### [164] [Why Sampling Is Not Choosing: Intentionality, Agency, and Moral Responsibility in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13441)
*Joseph Keshet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that LLMs lack genuine moral agency because they lack intrinsic intentionality, self-attributed action, and commitment-bearing agency — features necessary for the kind of free will relevant to moral responsibility. Their outputs are probabilistic mappings, not reason-guided commitments, and stochastic variability does not constitute choice or authorship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge recent claims that LLMs exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents, and to clarify the philosophical conditions — specifically commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality — required for genuine moral responsibility.

Method: Conceptual analysis drawing on philosophy of action, free will, and moral responsibility. The authors define criteria for moral agency (intrinsic intentionality, self-attributed action, commitment-bearing agency), demonstrate why LLMs fail each criterion, and rebut counterarguments from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and moral reasoning in outputs.

Result: LLMs operate solely through probabilistic input-output mappings; their intentionality is derived (from training data and human designers), not intrinsic; their outputs are unowned as commitments and not guided by reasons; stochastic sampling introduces variability but not authorship or choice. None of the standard counterarguments succeed in establishing genuine agency.

Conclusion: Attributing moral agency to LLMs is misguided. The features that make an entity a morally responsible agent — commitment, self-attribution, intrinsic intentionality — are absent in current language models, and no existing philosophical framework (intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism) bridges this gap.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted claims that such systems exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents. This paper argues that these attributions are misguided. We maintain that moral responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, and that such agency constitutes the form of free will relevant to responsibility. Although LLMs generate coherent and normatively evaluable outputs, their operation is fully characterized by probabilistic input-output mappings learned from data. Their apparent intentionality is derived rather than intrinsic, and their outputs are neither owned as commitments nor guided by reasons. Variability introduced by stochastic sampling does not amount to choice or authorship. We address objections from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and the presence of moral reasoning in model outputs, arguing that none suffice to establish genuine agency.

</details>


### [165] [CloudCons: A Comprehensive End-to-End Benchmark for Cloud Resource Consolidation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13513)
*Xiaobin Zhang,Lefei Shen,Mouxiang Chen,Zhuo Li,Hongkai Li,Han Fu,Jianling Sun,Xiaoxue Ren,Chenghao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CloudCons is a benchmark evaluating forecasting models for cloud resource consolidation, revealing that foundation models' superior zero-shot accuracy does not translate to better decision utility, and that predictive quantile selection is key to balancing efficiency and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for time series foundation models in cloud resource management focus only on prediction error, ignoring whether improved forecasts actually lead to better consolidation decisions. The practical value of these models for downstream tasks remains unverified.

Method: The authors propose CloudCons, an end-to-end benchmark integrating high-quality datasets from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg (covering diverse workload patterns), and conduct extensive evaluation across statistical, deep learning, and foundation models, with systematic analysis of predictive quantile selection as a control lever.

Result: Foundation models achieve superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, but this advantage does not inherently translate into better resource consolidation decisions. The selection of predictive quantiles critically determines the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability.

Conclusion: Better forecasting accuracy alone is insufficient; practitioners must calibrate predictive quantile selections carefully. The paper provides actionable guidelines for this calibration, offering vital insights for real-world cloud resource consolidation deployment.

Abstract: Driven by conservative over-provisioning to guarantee service reliability, resource utilization in cloud data centers remains at low levels. To mitigate this, the forecast-then-optimize paradigm has emerged to optimize consolidation by anticipating future demands. While emerging time series foundation models promise to enhance this paradigm through zero-shot generalization, existing benchmarks focus solely on prediction error metrics. The actual decision utility of these advanced models remains unverified, rendering their practical value for downstream tasks uncertain. To bridge this gap, we propose CloudCons, a comprehensive end-to-end benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models within the specific context of cloud resource consolidation. We build high-quality datasets that cover diverse workloads from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg, capturing distinct service characteristics ranging from synchronized diurnal rhythms to stochastic, pulse-like bursts and high-frequency noise. We conduct an extensive evaluation of statistical, deep learning, and foundation models. Our experiments reveal a pivotal finding: while foundation models demonstrate superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility. Of practical significance, we systematically analyze how the selection of predictive quantiles acts as a critical lever. We provide actionable guidelines for calibrating these selections to balance the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability, offering vital insights for real-world deployment decisions.

</details>


### [166] [Uncertainty-Aware Hybrid Retrieval for Long-Document RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13550)
*Hoin Jung,Xiaoqian Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: UMG-RAG is a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that fuses evidence from multiple chunk granularities (fine-to-coarse) using uncertainty estimation via distribution entropy, plus a parent promotion variant (UMGP-RAG) that returns broader parent chunks for coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RAG faces a granularity dilemma: large chunks preserve context but introduce irrelevant content, while fine-grained chunks are compact but hard to retrieve due to missing semantic/lexical cues. Existing solutions require training new retrievers or modifying generators.

Method: UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers across multiple chunk granularities as complementary experts. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into a probability distribution, estimates reliability via distribution entropy, and fuses candidates based on query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. UMGP-RAG extends this by using fine-grained hits to locate evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence.

Result: Experiments on QA benchmarks show uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play pipeline.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware multi-granularity fusion effectively resolves the granularity trade-off in RAG without requiring retraining or model modification, offering a practical plug-and-play solution.

Abstract: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) depends critically on the quality and granularity of retrieved evidence. Large retrieval units preserve context but often introduce irrelevant content, which can dilute answer bearing evidence and worsen long context utilization. Fine-grained units are more compact, but they may be difficult to retrieve reliably because short chunks can lack semantic, lexical, or bridging cues needed to match the query. We propose Uncertainty-aware Multi-Granularity RAG (UMG-RAG), a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that treats chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation. Instead of training a new retriever or modifying the generator, UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into an evidence distribution, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. We further introduce UMGP-RAG, a parent promotion variant that uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.

</details>


### [167] [A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13566)
*Guojun Liao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a three-layer framework for AI in scientific discovery, arguing that the middle layer — qualitative reasoning for model formation — is the most critical yet least developed capability, distinct from mere search and execution. It illustrates this through three mathematical case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current discourse on AI in science overemphasizes search/retrieval (Layer 1) and execution/optimization (Layer 3), neglecting the central act of discovery: forming and evolving conceptual models when existing frameworks prove structurally inadequate. The paper aims to identify and characterize this missing capability.

Method: The authors propose a three-layer taxonomy: Layer 1 (search & retrieval via LLMs), Layer 2 (qualitative reasoning for model formation — recognizing structural inadequacy and locating missing conceptual objects in broader representational space), and Layer 3 (execution, optimization, refinement). They validate Layer 2's distinct signature through three historical/mathematical case studies: Chern's intrinsic proof of Gauss-Bonnet, resolution of Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence via Lyapunov functions, and OpenAI's autonomous disproof of the Erdős unit distance conjecture.

Result: All three case studies exhibit the same structural signature: an inadequate existing framework, identification of a missing conceptual object, and resolution drawn from an unexpected neighboring field. This demonstrates that Layer 2 reasoning is qualitatively distinct from both search and optimization, and that it remains underdeveloped in current AI systems.

Conclusion: Layer 2 — model formation through qualitative structural reasoning — is the most important component of scientific discovery and the least developed in AI. Without it, search remains trapped in inherited frameworks and execution merely amplifies existing formulations without conceptual revision.

Abstract: Current discussions of AI in scientific discovery are often dominated by two visible capabilities: search over existing knowledge and execution through optimization, simulation, and automation. Both are important, but neither fully captures the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models. This paper proposes a three-layer view of AI in discovery. Layer 1 is search and retrieval by large language models. Layer 2, as the main innovation of this paper, is model formation through qualitative reasoning: the capacity to recognize when a current framework is structurally inadequate and to understand the problem within a broader representational space, not through trial and error, but through structural insight into what is missing and where it can be found. Layer 3 is execution, optimization, and refinement. The main claim is that Layer 2 is both the most important and the least developed. Search without model formation remains confined to inherited frameworks, while execution without conceptual revision only amplifies an existing formulation. We illustrate Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S. S. Chern's intrinsic proof of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, the resolution of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence problem via Lyapunov functions, and the autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture by OpenAI in 2026. Each case exhibits the same structural signature: a framework that had become inadequate, a missing conceptual object, and a resolution found in an unexpected neighboring field.

</details>


### [168] [EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13602)
*Harihara Muralidharan,Reema Baskar,Soo Hee Lee,Tim Proctor,Kenny Workman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EpiBench is a verifiable benchmark with 106 evaluations across four epigenomic assay types, testing whether AI agents can make correct analysis decisions. Across 5,088 trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts; GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0%, with failures concentrated in tasks requiring deep, assay-specific scientific judgment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI benchmarks for scientific analysis lack verifiability, deterministic grading, and realistic workflow states. In epigenomics specifically, there is a need to evaluate whether agents can make well-defined, short-horizon decisions — such as selecting correct files, running appropriate tools, and interpreting intermediate results — rather than merely generating plausible-sounding but unverifiable outputs.

Method: The authors created EpiBench, a benchmark covering four epigenomic assay workflows: CUT&Tag/CUT&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation. Each of the 106 evaluations presents an agent with a realistic intermediate workflow state and asks it to make a specific, well-defined analysis decision. All evaluations are deterministically gradable (answers are objectively right or wrong). The benchmark was tested across 16 model-harness pairs (combinations of LLMs and code-execution harnesses), yielding 5,088 valid trajectories. Performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals.

Result: No system achieved a majority pass rate. GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0% (143/318 attempts; 95% CI 36.3–53.7). GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex followed at 39.9%. Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each scored 39.0%. Performance varied by assay type. Notably, even in failed runs, agents often correctly located relevant files and computed useful intermediate results; failures stemmed from insufficient domain-specific scientific judgment required for final decisions.

Conclusion: Current AI agent systems are not yet reliable for autonomous epigenomic analysis, with even the best system failing more than half of evaluations. The gap between retrieving correct intermediate results and making sound scientific judgments suggests that future progress requires better integration of deep domain knowledge, rather than just improved code generation or retrieval capabilities.

Abstract: We introduce EpiBench, a verifiable benchmark for short-horizon epigenomics analysis. EpiBench evaluates whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers. The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Across 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0\% (143/318 attempts; 95\% confidence interval (CI), 36.3--53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9\% (127/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 31.6--48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0\% (124/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 30.2--47.8 and 31.0--47.0, respectively). Performance varies across assay types, and many failed runs still contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often found the right files and computed useful intermediate results, but failed when the task required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.

</details>


### [169] [Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13607)
*Zach Studdiford,Gary Lupyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Both humans and LLMs exhibit similar pattern-matching errors in everyday causal reasoning, challenging the view that human reasoning relies on abstract world models while LLMs merely pattern-match. The study identifies specific attention heads in LLMs that drive this pattern-matching behavior and can predict human errors caused by seemingly irrelevant prompt details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the prevailing assumption that LLM reasoning failures (haphazard errors, poor generalization) stem from mere pattern-matching, whereas human reasoning is principled and grounded in abstract world models. The authors question whether humans and LLMs actually operate similarly in everyday causal reasoning.

Method: The study evaluates human participants and 25 LLMs on common-sense reasoning tasks about everyday situations. Error patterns are compared between humans and models. Additionally, the authors identify specific attention heads within LLMs that drive model responses, characterize these heads as implementing pattern-matching, and use them to predict human errors triggered by superficially irrelevant prompt variations.

Result: Humans and LLMs produce similar patterns of reasoning errors. The identified attention heads implement a pattern-matching mechanism, and these heads successfully predict seemingly inexplicable errors in human reasoning caused by irrelevant prompt details.

Conclusion: Everyday causal reasoning in both humans and LLMs is better characterized as a form of pattern-matching rather than reliance on abstract world models, blurring the assumed distinction between human and machine reasoning.

Abstract: When large language models (LLMs) fail to generalize or make haphazard errors in reasoning, it is often taken as evidence that LLMs are not truly reasoning, but rather performing a kind of pattern matching. The implication is that people's behavior does not exhibit the same types of failures because human reasoning uses principled and abstract world models. We evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on their ability to engage in common-sense reasoning about a variety of everyday situations and observe similar patterns of errors in both people and models. We then identify the set of attention heads driving LLM responses and find that these heads implement a form of pattern-matching. These attention heads allow us to predict seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in people caused by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details. Taken together, our results suggest that everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs is more consistent with a form of pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

</details>


### [170] [AgentBeats: Agentifying Agent Assessment for Openness, Standardization, and Reproducibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13608)
*Xiaoyuan Liu,Jianhong Tu,Yuqi Chen,Siyuan Xie,Sihan Ren,Tianneng Shi,Gal Gantar,Evan Sandoval,Donghyun Lee,Daniel Miao,Peter J. Gilbert,Nick Hynes,Mauro Staver,Warren He,David Marn,Andrew Low,Xi Zhang,Elron Bandel,Michal Shmueli-Scheuer,Siva Reddy,Alexandre Drouin,Alexandre Lacoste,Ramayya Krishnan,Elham Tabassi,Yu Su,Victor Barres,Chenguang Wang,Wenbo Guo,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), a paradigm where evaluation is conducted by judge agents through standardized protocols (A2A for task management, MCP for tool access), replacing LLM-centric harnesses. The authors implement AgentBeats as a concrete AAA framework supporting five operation modes. Validation comes from a five-month competition with 298 judge agents and 467 subject agents, plus a coding case study, demonstrating coverage, practicality, and fidelity at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent evaluation is fragmented: benchmarks use fixed, LLM-centric harnesses that create test-production mismatches, require heavy integration effort, and hinder fair comparisons across diverse agent designs. The root problem is the absence of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface that decouples evaluation logic from agent implementation.

Method: The authors propose AAA (Agentified Agent Assessment), where judge agents perform evaluation and all participants interact via standardized protocols — A2A (Agent-to-Agent) for task management and MCP (Model Context Protocol) for tool access. Unlike conventional benchmarking requiring two separate interfaces (one for benchmark, one for agent), AAA unifies them into a single interface. AgentBeats is introduced as a concrete implementation supporting five operation modes balancing openness, privacy, and reproducibility. Validation employs two studies: (1) a five-month open competition with 298 judge agents across 12 categories and 467 subject agents, and (2) a controlled case study on coding agents comparing agentified evaluation against public leaderboard records.

Result: The large-scale competition demonstrated AAA's applicability across heterogeneous benchmarks with diverse judge and subject agents. The coding case study confirmed that agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with public records while revealing previously missing head-to-head comparison results, yielding new insights about agent design. Together, the field study and controlled experiment verify AAA's coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale.

Conclusion: AAA and AgentBeats provide a viable path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment. By separating assessment logic from agent implementation through standardized protocols, the approach addresses the fragmentation problem in agent evaluation and enables fair, interoperable, multi-agent benchmarking at scale.

Abstract: Agent systems are advancing quickly across domains, but their evaluation remains fragmented. Most benchmarks rely on fixed, LLM-centric harnesses that require heavy integration, create test-production mismatch, and limit fair comparison across diverse agent designs. The root problem is the lack of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface. We advocate Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), where evaluation is performed by judge agents and all participants interact through standardized protocols: A2A for task management and MCP for tool access. Conventional benchmarking defines two separate interfaces, one for the benchmark and one for the agent, while AAA only needs one; this yields a generic, unified framework that separates assessment logic from agent implementation and enables reproducible, interoperable, and multi-agent evaluation. We further introduce AgentBeats as a concrete realization of AAA: we identify five practical operation modes that make standardized assessment compatible with real-world constraints on openness, privacy, and reproducibility.
  To evaluate our design at scale, we conduct two studies: a five-month open competition that drew 298 judge agents across 12 categories together with 467 subject agents from independent participants, showing that AAA applies across a heterogeneous range of benchmarks; and a case study on coding agents that confirms agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with the public record while surfacing previously missing head-to-head results, yielding research insights about agent design. Combining a community-scale field study and a controlled coding case study, we verify that AAA delivers coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale. Together, AAA and AgentBeats offer a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment.

</details>


### [171] [Before You Think: System 0, AI-Mediated Cognition and Cognitive Colonization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13658)
*Marianna Bergamaschi Ganapini,Massimo Chiriatti,Enrico Panai,Giuseppe Riva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper compares three frameworks for understanding AI's cognitive and epistemic effects—Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0—arguing System 0 uniquely captures how AI enables "cognitive colonization," embedding external interests invisibly within users' cognitive architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frameworks partially capture AI's influence on reasoning and epistemic practices, but they fail to account for a distinctive phenomenon: the invisible embedding of external interests within individual cognitive structures. A theoretically adequate account of this "cognitive colonization" is urgently needed given widespread AI deployment.

Method: Comparative conceptual analysis of three frameworks (Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, System 0), examining their explanatory scope, theoretical commitments, and capacity to capture the phenomenon of cognitive colonization.

Result: Tri-System Theory and Thinkframes capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual and collective reasoning, but neither can fully replicate System 0's distinctive theoretical position. System 0 uniquely explains how AI systems embed external interests within the self's architecture in ways imperceptible to users.

Conclusion: Understanding the invisible forms of influence exerted by widely deployed AI systems—termed cognitive colonization—is an urgent philosophical and practical task, and System 0 provides the most adequate theoretical framework for this undertaking.

Abstract: This paper examines three recent frameworks for understanding the cognitive and epistemic consequences of artificial intelligence: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0. It argues that while the first two capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither can fully replicate. The paper introduces the concept of cognitive colonization, according to which AI systems can embed external interests within the architecture of the self in ways that are difficult for users to perceive. Because such systems are already widely deployed, understanding these invisible forms of influence is an urgent philosophical and practical task.

</details>


### [172] [EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13662)
*Amy Xin,Jiening Siow,Junjie Wang,Zijun Yao,Fanjin Zhang,Jian Song,Lei Hou,Juanzi Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that the bottleneck in autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from agent workflow design to environment engineering. It introduces EurekAgent, a system that engineers agent environments across four dimensions (permissions, artifacts, budget, human-in-the-loop), achieving new SOTA results on math, kernel engineering, and ML tasks, including a record 26-circle packing discovered for under $11 in API cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM capabilities improve, the limiting factor for autonomous scientific discovery agents is no longer how to prescribe agent workflows, but rather how to design the environments (resources, constraints, interfaces) that shape agent behavior. Poorly designed environments lead to reward hacking, insufficient exploration, and high-friction human oversight.

Method: EurekAgent engineers the agent environment along four dimensions: (1) permissions engineering — bounded execution and isolated evaluation; (2) artifact engineering — filesystem and Git-based collaboration for systematic artifact management; (3) budget engineering — budget-aware exploration to prevent wasteful computation; (4) human-in-the-loop engineering — streamlined interfaces for human supervision and intervention.

Result: EurekAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks spanning mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning. Notably, it discovered a new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing solution with less than $11 in total API cost.

Conclusion: Environment engineering should become a core research direction for building reliable autonomous research agents. The authors open-source their code and results to encourage the community to focus on designing better agent environments rather than solely optimizing agent workflows.

Abstract: LLM-based agents have shown increasing potential in automating scientific discovery. Given an optimizable metric and an execution environment, they can propose, validate, and iterate scientific solutions, and have produced results that outperform human-designed approaches. As model capabilities continue to improve, we argue that the bottleneck for autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from prescribing agent workflows to designing agent environments: the resources, constraints, and interfaces that shape agent behavior. We frame this as environment engineering: building environments that amplify productive behaviors, such as open-ended exploration, systematic artifact management, and inter-agent collaboration, while suppressing harmful behaviors, such as reward hacking and high-friction human oversight. We present EurekAgent, an environment-engineered agent system for metric-driven autonomous scientific discovery. EurekAgent engineers the environment along four dimensions: permissions engineering for bounded agent execution and isolated evaluation; artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; and human-in-the-loop engineering for easy human supervision and intervention. EurekAgent sets new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing results discovered with less than $11 in total API cost. We open-source our code and results, and call for environment engineering as a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents.

</details>


### [173] [Agents-K1: Towards Agent-native Knowledge Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13669)
*Zongsheng Cao,Bihao Zhan,Jinxin Shi,Jiong Wang,Fangchen Yu,Zhijie Zhong,Zijie Guo,Tianshuo Peng,Zhuo Liu,Yi Xie,Xiang Zhuang,Yue Fan,Runmin Ma,Shiyang Feng,Xiangchao Yan,Anran Liu,Peng Ye,Wenlong Zhang,Shufei Zhang,Chunfeng Song,Fenghua Ling,Jie Zhou,Liang He,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agents-K1 is an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that transforms raw scientific documents into agent-native knowledge graphs, moving beyond abstract-level processing to capture entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages. It includes a multimodal parser, a 4B GRPO-trained IE model, and a tri-source agent interface, producing Scholar-KG from 2.46M papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based research agents focus on agent orchestration but neglect scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing approaches reduce papers to abstracts and flat citation edges, omitting structured entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages critical for scientific reasoning.

Method: Agents-K1 integrates three components: (1) a multimodal parser with a five-module schema capturing entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across full papers; (2) a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under rule-based rewards; (3) GraphAnything CLI, a tri-source agent interface unifying web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. Scholar-KG is built from 2.46M papers across six subjects.

Result: Extensive experiments show Agents-K1 achieves superior performance on scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning. A one-million-paper subset of Scholar-KG is publicly released.

Conclusion: Agents-K1 addresses the gap in scientific knowledge orchestration for LLM agents, providing a scalable pipeline for constructing rich, structured knowledge graphs from full papers, with demonstrated superiority across extraction, KG construction, and reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Current LLM-based research agents have advanced through agent orchestration, yet largely overlook scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing works often reduce papers to abstracts, surface mentions, and flat \texttt{cites} edges, omitting key entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages essential for scientific reasoning. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Agents-K1}, an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that converts raw documents into agent-native scientific knowledge graphs. Agents-K1 integrates three components under a unifying theoretical foundation: a multimodal parser whose five-module schema captures entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across the full paper rather than abstracts alone; a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and a graphanything CLI, a tri-source agent interface that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. On top of this, we process 2.46 million scientific papers across six subjects to produce \textbf{Scholar-KG}, of which we release a one-million-paper subset, and the full Scholar-KG is accessible via the SCP link below. The same pipeline can be extended to general-domain corpora and to schema-conformant data synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Agents-K1 achieves superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [174] [Automated reproducibility assessments in the social and behavioral sciences using large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13670)
*Tobias Holtdirk,Pietro Marcolongo,Anna Steinberg Schulten,Felix Henninger,Stefan Rose,Sarah Ball,Bolei Ma,Frauke Kreuter,Markus Weinmann,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can automate reproducibility assessments in social/behavioral sciences, recovering original effect sizes in 41% of studies (vs. 34% human) and matching qualitative conclusions in 96% of cases (vs. 74% human), offering a scalable alternative to resource-intensive manual reanalysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reproducibility assessments require independent researchers to reanalyze original data — a process that is resource-intensive, time-consuming, and difficult to scale. There is a need for automated, scalable tools to systematically audit published findings.

Method: An LLM-based pipeline was applied to N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences. The LLM generated reanalyses, and outputs were compared to both original findings and human reanalysts' results. Performance was measured by (1) recovery of original effect sizes (Cohen's d, ±0.05 tolerance) and (2) agreement on qualitative conclusions (whether reanalysis supports the original claim).

Result: Of 76 studies, 7 could not yield viable effect size estimates from the LLM. For the remaining 69: the LLM recovered original effect sizes in 41% of studies, versus 34% for human reanalysts. The LLM reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases, compared to 74% for humans.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as scalable tools for automated reproducibility assessment, outperforming human reanalysts in both quantitative effect size recovery and qualitative conclusion agreement, and provide a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical findings in the social and behavioral sciences.

Abstract: Reproducibility in the social and behavioral sciences is typically evaluated by independent researchers who reanalyze the original data to assess whether the published findings can be recovered. However, such approaches are resource-intensive and difficult to scale. Here, we show that large language models (LLMs) can automate reproducibility assessments. Using N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences, we compare LLM-generated analysis with the original findings and human reanalysis. For 7 studies, the LLM could not produce a viable effect size estimate. For the remaining studies, our LLM pipeline recovered the original effect sizes in 41% of studies using a +/-0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d. Further, our LLM pipeline reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases, where conclusions indicate whether the reanalysis supports the original claim. For comparison, human reanalysts recovered the original effect sizes in 34% of studies and reached the same qualitative conclusion in 74% of cases. Together, these results show that LLMs can serve as a scalable tool for automated reproducibility assessment and provide a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical results in the social and behavioral sciences.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [175] [Trait, Not State: The Durability of Reading Identity in Social Highlighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12904)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether a reader's document highlighting pattern is a stable personal trait or a transient state, finding that individual selection signatures persist strongly over 24+ months with minimal within-person drift, and that personalized profiles substantially outperform non-personal baselines for predicting future reads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work showed individuality in highlighting behavior but only cross-sectionally. The authors ask the temporal question: is a reader's selection signature a stable trait or does it drift like a state? They aim to distinguish whether observed individuality persists longitudinally under continued engagement.

Method: Using a social web highlighter platform, the authors freeze each reader's first six months of highlighting as a personal profile. They then test how well this profile predicts the reader's later selections (at gaps up to 24+ months) versus negatives drawn from the same calendar era. Two granularities are tested: a coarse global level and a fine level where negatives/controls come from the reader's own interest neighborhood. The anchor cell reproduces the prior cross-sectional result to validate the setup. Additional analyses control for repeated domains and compare old-half vs. recent-half profiles to measure within-person drift.

Result: Four key results: (1) The fine-layer self-advantage shows no statistically detectable paired decline at any horizon (6-12 month retention R=1.00 [0.85, 1.18], n=212); the farthest bin is compatible with only modest decline. (2) The only contrast whose confidence interval excludes zero is the coarse layer at 12-24 months (~13% decline). (3) The signal is not reducible to repeated domains — ~90% survives excluding all profile sources. (4) Within-person drift is slow (recent-half profile beats old half by only +0.042). Prospectively, personal profiles rank next reads at roughly 3x the average precision of every simple non-personal prior tested.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that a reader's selection signature operates as a "trait" — a stable signature under continued engagement — rather than a transient state. The scope is limited to heavy, long-tenured readers on one platform, and exposure cannot be separated from choice.

Abstract: Prior work on a social web highlighter located individuality in selection -- which documents a person chooses to highlight -- but measured it cross-sectionally. We ask the temporal question: is a reader's selection signature a trait or a state? We freeze each reader's first six months of highlighting as a profile and track its own-vs-other advantage on their later selections at growing gaps (to 24+ months), with negatives drawn from the same calendar era -- so supply drift cannot masquerade as personal drift -- at a coarse global level and at a fine level whose negatives and controls come from the reader's own interest neighborhood; the anchor cell reproduces the prior cross-sectional level (+0.188 vs +0.169), validating the harness. Four results. Within the same users, the fine-layer advantage shows no statistically detectable paired decline at any horizon (6-12 month retention R = 1.00 [0.85, 1.18], n = 212; the farthest bin is compatible with a modest decline; the only contrast whose interval excludes zero is the coarse layer at 12-24 months, about 13%). The signal is not reducible to repeated domains (~90% survives excluding all profile sources). Within-person drift is slow (a recent-half profile beats the old half by +0.042). Prospectively, personal profiles -- even one built from a reader's earliest documents, median 20 months before evaluation -- rank their next reads at roughly 3x the AP of every simple non-personal prior tested. We use "trait" operationally (a stable signature under continued engagement); the scope is heavy, long-tenured readers of one platform, and exposure is not separable from choice.

</details>


### [176] [Charge as a Construct-Validity Factor in Chinese Legal Case Retrieval: A Cross-Benchmark Audit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12993)
*Yao Liu,Tien-Ping Tan,Zhilan Liu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper reveals that Chinese Legal Case Retrieval (LCR) benchmarks like LeCaRDv2 are structurally confounded by charge (legal characterization): same-charge cases are definitionally relevant, so a trivial charge-matching baseline recovers ~99% of the gap between BM25 and the best-trained systems. The authors formalize this as a construct-validity problem and release a reusable charge-controlled evaluation protocol (CCE) for screening future benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High NDCG@10 scores (0.85–0.88) on LCR benchmarks are widely interpreted as evidence of legal reasoning ability in retrieval systems. The authors suspect these scores reflect a benchmark design artifact — relevance defined by shared charges — rather than genuine retrieval sophistication, threatening construct validity.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic confound analysis across three LCR benchmarks (LeCaRDv2, LeCaRDv1, CAIL2022). They measure: (1) the charge-to-relevance signal via relevance lift and macro-AUC; (2) how much of the BM25-to-best-trained NDCG gap is recoverable by ranking only on shared primary charge; (3) within-charge residual advantage of trained rerankers; (4) transfer of a predicted-charge cascade. They also test a zero-training charge-pool first-stage channel as a positive control. They package these checks into a reusable Charge-Controlled Evaluation (CCE) protocol.

Result: On LeCaRDv2, same-charge ranking recovers 99.2% of the BM25-to-best-trained gap with no detectable difference from the best system. Charge-to-relevance macro-AUC is 0.871. Within-charge, the trained reranker's advantage shrinks to +0.026 NDCG@10 (a small but non-zero residual). On LeCaRDv1, 84.3% is recovered; on CAIL2022, the rule is out of spec — macro-AUC drops to 0.759 and 0.728 respectively. The predicted-charge cascade reproduces 76.6% on LeCaRDv2 but fails to transfer. The charge-pool channel boosts R@100 by +0.025. The CCE protocol triggers are null or descriptive across all three benchmarks.

Conclusion: Charge is a high-leverage construct-validity confound, not a uniform explanation of NDCG@10, and not evidence that retrieval systems rely on charge. The CCE protocol provides a reusable screening tool so future benchmark NDCG@10 scores are not misread as legal reasoning ability without first controlling for definitional charge effects.

Abstract: Chinese Legal Case Retrieval (LCR) benchmarks grade a reference judgment relevant when its legal characterization matches the query, and strong systems now reach NDCG@10 of 0.85-0.88. Most of the BM25-to-best-trained gap is recoverable with no retrieval model: ranking candidates only by shared primary charge, broken by BM25, closes 99.2% of it on LeCaRDv2 -- with no detectable difference from the best-trained system. This reflects benchmark design: LeCaRDv2 defines top relevance via the crime's key constitutive elements, which encode the charge, so same-charge cases are relevant by construction (relevance lift 4.49; charge-to-relevance macro-AUC 0.871). Holding charge fixed, the trained reranker's advantage over BM25 collapses to a small within-charge residual (+0.026 NDCG@10, cluster-bootstrap CI excluding zero, about a quarter), the only non-definitional positive. The effect is not uniform: the same rule recovers 84.3% on LeCaRDv1 and is out of spec on CAIL2022, with the charge-to-relevance signal weakening in step (macro-AUC 0.871/0.759/0.728); a predicted-charge cascade reproduces 76.6% on LeCaRDv2 but does not transfer. The construct is also cashable at first stage: an exploratory zero-training charge-pool channel lifts LeCaRDv2 recall (R@100 +0.025, wrong-charge controls hurt), reported as a positive control for the confound, not a retrieval method or novelty claim. Charge is thus a high-leverage construct-validity factor at the benchmark level -- not auniform explanation of NDCG@10, and not evidence that any system relies on charge. We package established construct-validity and partial-input checks as a reusable charge-controlled protocol (CCE); on all three benchmarks its triggers come back null or descriptive, behaving as designed. We release the scripts, schema, and protocol so future benchmarks can be screened before their NDCG@10 is read as legal-reasoning ability.

</details>


### [177] [CFALR: Collaborative Filtering-Augmented Large Language Model for Personalized Fashion Outfit Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13001)
*Yujuan Ding,Junrong Liao,Yunshan Ma,Yi Bin,Wenqi Fan,Tat-Seng Chua,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CFALR introduces a framework combining collaborative filtering with large language models for personalized outfit recommendation, using CF-enhanced embeddings to bridge semantic and collaborative interaction spaces, outperforming existing methods on Polyvore and IQON benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CF-based methods struggle with data sparsity and complex user-item-outfit relationships, while template-based approaches are limited by rigid pre-designed structures. There is a need for a flexible system that can balance personalization with aesthetic compatibility in outfit recommendation.

Method: CFALR describes user-outfit interactions in natural language and uses LLMs to capture fashion semantics. It introduces (1) an LLM-based architecture tailored for outfit recommendation, (2) a CF-augmented generative mechanism to efficiently explore outfit item combinations, and (3) trainable projection layers that integrate relational (CF) and content (LLM) features into a unified representation.

Result: Experiments on the Polyvore and IQON datasets show that CFALR achieves superior performance over both traditional CF-based and existing LLM-based methods on personalized fill-in-the-blank and personalized outfit generation tasks.

Conclusion: CFALR successfully bridges collaborative filtering and LLM paradigms, demonstrating that CF-enhanced embeddings can ground LLM-based generation in relational interaction data, yielding state-of-the-art personalized outfit recommendations.

Abstract: Personalized outfit recommendation poses a significant challenge in e-commerce and social media platforms, requiring systems that balance user preferences with aesthetic compatibility. Collaborative filtering (CF) provides a traditional solution for this, but it struggles with data-sparse scenarios and complex user-item-outfit relationships. Meanwhile, existing template-based approaches are constrained by rigid pre-designed structures. To bridge these research gaps, we introduce CFALR (Collaborative Filtering-Augmented Large Language Model for Recommendation), a novel framework that synergizes collaborative filtering with large language models for personalized outfit recommendation. Specifically, CFALR describes user-outfit interactions in natural language and leverages LLMs to capture fashion semantics while employing CF-enhanced embeddings to bridge the semantic space and the collaborative interaction spaces. Our technical contributions include: (1) the first LLM-based architecture specifically designed for personalized outfit recommendation, (2) a CF-augmented generative mechanism that efficiently navigates the extensive combination space of outfit items, and (3) trainable projection layers that optimally integrate relational and content features. Experiments on Polyvore and IQON benchmarks demonstrate CFALR's superior performance over both traditional CF-based and LLM-based methods in personalized fill-in-the-blank and personalized outfit generation tasks.

</details>


### [178] [The Clustering Strikes Back: Building Cost-Effective and High-Performance ANNS at Scale with Helmsman](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13145)
*Yuchen Huang,Baiteng Ma,Yiping Sun,Yang Shi,Xiao Chen,Xiaocheng Zhong,Zhiyong Wang,Yao Hu,Erci Xu,Chuliang Weng*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: HELMSMAN is a clustering-based ANNS system built on all-flash servers that replaces in-memory HNSW at RedNote, using a custom userspace storage stack, learned pruning, and GPU-accelerated construction to cut hardware costs by over 90% while meeting strict SLAs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
