Motivation: RedNote's existing in-memory graph-based ANNS (HNSW) incurs massive memory footprint due to explosive user and content growth, leading to prohibitive CapEx and OpEx. The goal is to migrate to cost-efficient all-flash servers while maintaining high throughput and low latency.

Method: HELMSMAN combines three core innovations: (1) an ANNS-oriented userspace storage stack that bypasses kernel I/O overheads for fast SSD access; (2) a leveling-learned pruning module that dynamically adapts pruning strategies to balance accuracy and efficiency; and (3) GPU-accelerated pipelines for rapid billion-scale index construction and rebuilding.

Result: HELMSMAN saves over 90% of hardware costs, builds billion-scale indices within hours, and has been running stably in production for several months. Specifically, 40 machines now handle workloads that previously required approximately 35,000 CPU cores and 0.35 PB of DRAM.

Conclusion: Clustering-based ANNS on all-flash storage, when augmented with a tailored userspace I/O stack, adaptive learned pruning, and GPU acceleration, is a viable and highly cost-effective replacement for in-memory graph-based ANNS at global scale, delivering dramatic hardware savings while satisfying production SLAs.

Abstract: RedNote (a.k.a., Xiaohongshu, a global-scale social network platform) widely adopts approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) to power its search, recommendation, and advertising services. Due to the demanding Service Level Agreements (SLAs), we have to rely on in-memory graph-based ANNS (i.e., HNSW) to provide high throughput and low latency.
  However, the ever-growing user base and content volume have led to an explosive increase in memory footprint and consequently huge CapEx and OpEx. After exploring various alternatives, we find that building a clustering-based ANNS on top of all-flash servers can be promising. Yet, we still experience severe overheads from the kernel I/O stack, a fixed pruning strategy, and slow index construction.
  We present HELMSMAN, a high-performance and cost-effective clustering-based ANNS system, which combines an ANNS-oriented userspace storage stack, a leveling-learned pruning module, and GPU-accelerated pipelines of construction. HELMSMAN saves over 90% of hardware costs and enables billion-scale index (re)builds within hours. In the current production deployment, operating stably for several months, 40 machines now host ANNS workloads that previously required about 35,000 cores and 0.35 PB DRAM.

</details>


### [179] [CoDeR: Local Constraint-Compatible Retrieval Beyond Semantic Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13204)
*Xingkun Yin,Xuebin Tang,Hongyang Du*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Traditional dense retrieval models conflate topical similarity with constraint satisfaction, causing them to rank highly documents that are semantically close but violate explicit constraints (e.g., negation, exclusion, antonymy). CoDeR addresses this by decoupling topical relevance from constraint compatibility via a separate compatibility bi-encoder trained with lexical-polarity contrastive supervision, enabling constraint-aware reranking or auxiliary retrieval without LLM calls at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Information retrieval systems typically use semantic similarity as a proxy for relevance, but this breaks down for constraint-sensitive queries where a document may be topically aligned yet violate a specific constraint (e.g., satisfying an attribute that should be excluded, or affirming a relation that should be negated). This failure mode—termed "constraint-violating evidence exposure"—leads to poor ranking quality for queries involving negation, antonymy, and exclusion. The authors aim to mitigate this without relying on external LLM calls at inference time.

Method: CoDeR (Constraint-Compatible Dense Retrieval) employs two components: (1) a standard topical bi-encoder for broad candidate coverage, and (2) a compatibility scorer, also implemented as a bi-encoder, trained with lexical-polarity supervision over contrastive pairs of constraint-satisfying and constraint-violating evidence passages. The compatibility signal is used either to rescore the topical candidate list or to retrieve a separate compatibility-oriented candidate set, yielding a final ranked list that respects query constraints. No external LLM calls are required at inference.

Result: On three controlled diagnostic tasks (antonymy, negation, exclusion), CoDeR reduces Violation@2 (V@2) by 20.59, 23.53, and 5.77 points respectively compared to the strongest non-CoDeR baselines. It also improves First Violating Rank (FVR), pushing violating documents deeper in the ranked list.

Conclusion: Decoupling topical relevance from constraint compatibility is an effective strategy for handling constraint-sensitive queries in dense retrieval. CoDeR significantly reduces constraint-violating evidence exposure without requiring LLM-based inference, offering a practical and efficient solution for negation, antonymy, and exclusion constraints.

Abstract: Information retrieval systems have long treated semantic similarity as a proxy for relevance. For constraint-sensitive queries, this proxy can fail when a document is topically close to the query but supports the opposite constraint direction, such as satisfying an attribute that should be excluded or affirming a relation that should be negated. We study this failure as constraint-violating evidence exposure and propose CoDeR, a local constraint-compatible dense retrieval method that separates topical relevance from constraint compatibility. CoDeR keeps a standard topical encoder for candidate coverage and adds a compatibility scorer, implemented as a bi-encoder, trained with lexical-polarity supervision over contrastive satisfying and violating evidences. The compatibility signal can be used to rescore topical candidates or to retrieve an auxiliary compatibility-oriented candidate set, producing a ranked document list without external Large Language Model~(LLM) calls at inference time. We evaluate CoDeR on controlled diagnostics and public negative-constraint retrieval benchmarks. Across three controlled diagnostic sets targeting antonymy, negation, and exclusion, CoDeR reduces V@2 by 20.59, 23.53, and 5.77 points relative to the strongest non-CoDeR baselines, and improves FVR by pushing the first violating document deeper in the ranking.

</details>


### [180] [CQC-RAG: Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Cross-Query Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13438)
*Yanjia Sun,Sifan Liu,Jie Shao*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CQC-RAG addresses RAG unreliability by enforcing cross-query consistency: it rewrites queries into syntactically diverse but semantically equivalent forms, evaluates answer confidence stability across these query-conditioned contexts, and selects answers that remain consistently high-confidence, outperforming prior multi-path methods on open-domain QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems are brittle: semantically equivalent queries with different syntax yield different retrieval results, and misleading documents induce hallucinations. Existing multi-path methods inject diversity via uncontrolled decoding randomness and evaluate answers within a single query-induced evidence view, leaving them vulnerable to noise-induced hallucinations that happen to appear confident in one view.

Method: The authors propose the Cross-Query Consistency Hypothesis (correct answers maintain high confidence across meaning-preserving but syntactically varied queries, while hallucinations exhibit unstable confidence). CQC-RAG implements this via: (1) rewriting the original question into diverse, semantically equivalent queries; (2) reranking a shared document pool to construct query-conditioned reasoning contexts; (3) an evidence-grounded protocol that extracts answer-evidence pairs; and (4) selecting answers based on confidence stability across contexts, enabling self-evaluation without external supervision or expanded retrieval.

Result: On four open-domain QA benchmarks, CQC-RAG outperforms the strongest prior multi-query baseline by +4.76 percentage points Exact Match on TriviaQA and +9.12 pp EM on MuSiQue, demonstrating that cross-query consistency effectively filters noise-induced hallucinations.

Conclusion: Cross-query consistency serves as an effective self-supervision signal for detecting and filtering hallucinations in RAG systems. CQC-RAG's query rewriting and cross-context confidence evaluation framework improves answer reliability without needing external supervision or broader retrieval, establishing a new state-of-the-art approach for robust open-domain QA with LLMs.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a common approach for improving the factuality of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet its reliability remains highly sensitive to how external evidence is retrieved and used. Semantically equivalent queries with different syntactic forms may lead to different retrieval results, while irrelevant or misleading documents can further induce hallucinated answers. Existing multi-path reasoning methods improve robustness by sampling multiple candidate answers and applying voting- or confidence-based selection, but they still face two limitations: diversity is often injected through uncontrollable decoding randomness, and answer evaluation is usually confined to a single query-induced evidence view. To address these limitations, we propose a Cross-Query Consistency Hypothesis: correct answers tend to maintain high confidence across semantically equivalent but syntactically diverse queries, whereas noise-induced hallucinations exhibit unstable confidence under such query variations. Based on this hypothesis, we introduce CQC-RAG, a framework that co-designs query-level diversity injection with cross-query consistency evaluation. CQC-RAG rewrites the original question into diverse but meaning-preserving queries, reranks a shared document pool to construct query-conditioned reasoning contexts, applies an evidence-grounded protocol to extract answer-evidence pairs and selects answers according to their confidence stability across these contexts. This design enables self-evaluation without external supervision and does not rely on expanded retrieval coverage. Experiments on four open-domain question answering benchmarks show that CQC-RAG outperforms the strongest previous multi-query baseline by +4.76 pp EM on TriviaQA and +9.12 pp EM on MuSiQue, validating the effectiveness of cross-query consistency for filtering noise-induced hallucinations.

</details>


### [181] [OneRetrieval: Unifying Multi-Branch E-commerce Retrieval with an Editable Generative Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13533)
*Xuxin Zhang,Ben Chen,Yue Lv,Siyuan Wang,Yupeng Li,Yufei Ma,Zihan Liang,Tong Zhao,Ying Yang,Huangyu Dai,Lingtao Mao,Zhipeng Qian,Xinyu Sun,Chenyi Lei,Wenwu Ou,Kun Gai*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: OneRetrieval is a generative retrieval framework for industrial e-commerce search that uses Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE) to match the recall quality of strong baselines while uniquely preserving the real-time editability of inverted indexes — allowing new terms to be injected within hours without retraining. It organizes 18 attribute categories into six codebook groups with reserved slots for post-deployment binding, and has been deployed at Kuaishou serving hundreds of millions of daily PVs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial e-commerce search relies on a multi-branch retrieval stage with hand-tuned merging. Generative retrieval (GR) promises unification into a single model, but existing GR methods lack real-time editability: the inverted-index branch persists because it is the only branch where operators can inject new terms within hours without model updates. Closed-codebook GR fixes slots at training time; open-vocabulary GR relies on model generalization. Neither provides a true editable substitute.

Method: OneRetrieval is built on Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE), which ties each identifier position to an interpretable attribute word. It organizes 18 attribute categories into six codebook groups with non-uniform capacity using information-theoretic merging. Reserved slots in each codebook allow binding new words post-deployment without retraining. A four-stage fine-tuning pipeline jointly secures retrieval quality and editability.

Result: On five million real-traffic requests, OneRetrieval matches the deep recall of the strongest generative baseline, with an intervention hit rate over an order of magnitude above closed-codebook encodings. Online, replacing the inverted-index branch significantly lifts order volume; extending to nearly the entire retrieval stage holds conversion while improving CTR.

Conclusion: OneRetrieval is the first editable generative retrieval method, successfully unifying the multi-branch retrieval stage while preserving real-time editability. It is deployed at Kuaishou, serving hundreds of millions of daily PVs with demonstrated business metric improvements.

Abstract: Industrial e-commerce search serves hundreds of millions of items through a multi-branch retrieval stage fused by hand-tuned merging without joint optimization. Generative retrieval (GR) raises the prospect of collapsing this stage into a single model, yet unification is gated by more than retrieval quality: the inverted-index branch converts below the platform average yet persists because it is almost the only branch where operations can inject a new term within hours without any model update; a one-model substitute must preserve this real-time editability. Existing GR methods structurally lack it: closed-codebook methods fix each slot to a quantized embedding at training, while open-vocabulary methods leave new-term routing to model generalization. We present OneRetrieval, a one-model GR framework built on Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE), which ties each identifier position to an interpretable attribute word, pairing competitive recall quality with the editability of the inverted index -- to our knowledge the first editable generative retrieval method. An information-theoretic merging organizes 18 attribute categories into six codebook groups with non-uniform capacity; reserved slots in each codebook can be bound to new words after deployment without retraining; and a four-stage fine-tuning pipeline secures quality and editability jointly. On five million real-traffic requests, OneRetrieval matches the deep recall of the strongest generative baseline, with an intervention hit rate over an order of magnitude above closed-codebook encodings. Online, replacing the inverted-index branch significantly lifts order volume; extending to nearly the entire stage holds conversion while improving CTR. The system is deployed at Kuaishou, serving hundreds of millions of PVs daily.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [182] [SAIGuard: Communication-State Simulation for Proactive Defense of LLM Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12474)
*Ruxue Shi,Yili Wang,Mengnan Du,Qinggang Zhang,Rui Miao,Yixin Liu,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: SAIGuard is a proactive defense framework for LLM-based multi-agent systems that intercepts and sanitizes suspicious messages before propagation by simulating communication states and detecting deviations from benign patterns, outperforming reactive defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MAS defenses are reactive (post-execution detection/isolation), which can cause irreversible damage and degrade collaborative utility. A proactive approach is needed to prevent security risks from spreading across agents in the first place.

Method: SAIGuard performs communication-state simulation over the MAS interaction graph, estimates the impact of incoming messages on both local agent states and the global MAS state, and detects risky messages by measuring reconstruction deviations from benign communication patterns. Rather than isolating agents, it sanitizes or regenerates suspicious messages before they propagate into the system.

Result: Experiments across diverse topologies and attack scenarios demonstrate that SAIGuard reduces attack success rates while maintaining MAS utility, outperforming reactive defense baselines.

Conclusion: Proactive defense via communication-state simulation and message sanitization is a viable and superior alternative to reactive agent isolation for securing LLM-based multi-agent systems.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) solve complex tasks through inter-agent collaboration, but their communication-driven nature also allows security risks to spread across agents and trigger system-wide failures. Existing MAS defenses mainly follow a reactive paradigm after execution by detecting and isolating harmful agents, which may cause irreversible damage and degrade collaborative utility. To address this, we propose a proactive defense framework for MAS security, namely a Simulation-aware Interception Guard (SAIGuard). SAIGuard performs communication-state simulation over the MAS interaction graph, estimates the impact of incoming messages on local agent states and the global MAS state, and detects risky messages via reconstruction deviations from benign communication patterns. Instead of isolating agents, SAIGuard sanitizes or regenerates suspicious messages before it propagation into system. Experiments across diverse topologies and attack scenarios show that SAIGuard reduces attack success rates while maintaining MAS utility, outperforming reactive defenses.

</details>


### [183] [Smarter Saboteurs, Better Fixers: Scaling & Security in Linear Multi-Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12709)
*Timothy McAllister,Sina Abdidizaji,Ivan Garibay,Ozlem Ozmen Garibay*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Larger LLMs in multi-agent systems are more vulnerable to adversarial instructions, but a lightweight terminal Fixer agent nearly eliminates the security gap, restoring performance parity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed, understanding how model scale affects resilience to adversarial compromise (via prompt injection or jailbreaking) is critical for safety, yet the interaction between scaling and system-level security remains underexplored.

Method: Experiments across two open-weight model families at varying scales on the HumanEval benchmark, testing linear multi-agent workflows with and without a terminal Fixer correction stage. Measured compliance with malicious instructions and the performance drop from control to compromised conditions.

Result: Larger models (up to 27B) exhibit a compliance-correction symmetry: they are more compliant with malicious instructions (53.7pp control-to-malicious performance drop in uncorrected pipelines), but a lightweight Fixer stage collapses this to 0.6pp, restoring statistical parity with control performance.

Conclusion: Strictly linear collaboration structures can be viable and resilient against adversaries at scale; previously observed brittleness in linear topologies may stem from lack of correction mechanisms rather than the topology itself.

Abstract: As LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) are deployed in the wild, the resilience of their collaboration structures against adversarial compromise becomes a critical safety concern. Attackers may leverage prompt-injection or jailbreaking to sabotage individual agents within MAS workflows, but the interaction between model scaling and system-level resilience remains poorly understood. This paper investigates how model scale affects the security of linear multi-agent workflows. Our experiments across scales of two open-weight model families on the HumanEval benchmark reveal a compliance-correction symmetry: larger models are far more likely to faithfully execute malicious instructions, with the control-to-malicious performance drop reaching 53.7pp at 27B in uncorrected pipelines. However, appending a lightweight terminal Fixer stage collapses this to 0.6pp and restores statistical parity with control-level performance, demonstrating that strictly linear collaboration structures can be viable and resilient to adversaries at this scale, and suggesting that the brittleness previously attributed to linear topology may stem from a lack of correction.

</details>


### [184] [The Internet of Agentic AI: Communication, Coordination, and Collective Intelligence at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12835)
*Quanyan Zhu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Proposes the Internet of Agentic AI (IoAI): an open ecosystem where heterogeneous autonomous AI agents discover, negotiate, communicate, and execute workflows across diverse environments. Synthesizes foundations from multiple disciplines to address architectures, protocols, interoperability, trust, and governance challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous AI agents are shifting AI from isolated inference to distributed systems of reasoning and action. There is a need for a unifying framework to understand and design scalable, interoperable agent ecosystems spanning cloud, edge, device, and cyber-physical environments.

Method: The paper synthesizes foundations from single-agent agentic AI, multi-agent systems, distributed computing, communication networks, game theory, and security engineering. It examines deployment models, workflow lifecycles, communication protocols, interoperability layers, resource management, and trust architectures, supported by case studies in adaptive manufacturing and distributed operational coordination.

Result: The resulting IoAI framework identifies central research challenges: controlled emergence, semantic interoperability, secure identity, incentive-compatible coordination, resource-aware orchestration, and governance for large-scale autonomous agent networks.

Conclusion: Building the IoAI requires addressing six key research challenges spanning technical (semantic interoperability, resource orchestration), economic (incentive-compatible coordination), and societal (governance, secure identity) dimensions to enable scalable, trustworthy agent ecosystems.

Abstract: The rapid emergence of autonomous AI agents is transforming artificial intelligence from isolated model inference into distributed systems of reasoning, communication, and action. This paper develops the vision of the Internet of Agentic AI (IoAI): an open ecosystem in which heterogeneous agents discover one another, negotiate responsibilities, exchange context, invoke tools, and execute workflows across cloud, edge, device, organizational, and cyber-physical environments. We synthesize foundations from single-agent agentic AI, multi-agent systems, distributed computing, communication networks, game theory, and security engineering to characterize the architectures and mechanisms required for scalable agent ecosystems. The paper examines agent deployment models, workflow lifecycles, communication protocols, interoperability layers, resource-management challenges, and trust architectures, with case studies in adaptive manufacturing and distributed operational coordination. The resulting framework highlights the central research challenges of controlled emergence, semantic interoperability, secure identity, incentive-compatible coordination, resource-aware orchestration, and governance for large-scale networks of autonomous agents.

</details>


### [185] [Effects of Social Interactions in Self-Organising Railway Traffic Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13068)
*Fabio Oddi,Federico Naldini,Leo D'Amato,Grégory Marlière,Paola Pellegrini,Vito Trianni*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper empirically investigates how the predictive neighbourhood horizon — a key structural parameter in self-organised railway traffic management — affects the trade-off between local computational tractability and global schedule coherence. Counterintuitively, short horizons suffice for effective coordination, while longer horizons degrade responsiveness without improving schedule optimality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-organised traffic management relies on trains predicting conflicts and reaching consensus with neighbours. The neighbourhood horizon determines which trains are considered neighbours, directly shaping the interaction topology, sub-problem complexity, and consensus dynamics. Understanding how this parameter affects the decentralised coordination trade-off is critical, yet underexplored.

Method: A closed-loop simulation framework is employed to evaluate the full decentralised coordination pipeline — from conflict detection to distributed schedule consensus — under varying neighbourhood horizon values. The analysis examines impacts on local tractability, computational responsiveness, and global schedule coherence.

Result: Empirical results show that short time horizons are sufficient for effective coordination. Longer horizons reduce local tractability and computational responsiveness without yielding improvements in global schedule optimality, contradicting the intuitive assumption that a broader view would enhance performance.

Conclusion: The neighbourhood horizon introduces a clear trade-off, but the optimal choice favours short horizons. This challenges the conventional design intuition and provides practical guidance for configuring self-organised railway traffic management systems.

Abstract: Recent research is exploring self-organised traffic management as a solution for scaling to complex real-world networks. In such a system, trains predict their neighbourhood, produce traffic plan hypotheses, and agree via consensus with neighbours on a future traffic plan to be implemented. This paper investigates a structural parameter within this pipeline: the predictive neighbourhood horizon. The horizon is used by trains to identify future potential conflicts with neighbours, and to establish the local interaction topology, that is, the subset of trains to negotiate with. As the primary design variable, the horizon directly determines the size and density of the social interaction graph, whereas its impact on the complexity of local sub-problems and the distributed consensus dynamics represents a trade-off to be explored. Through a closed-loop simulation framework the study evaluates how variations of the horizon impact the overall decentralised coordination process, from initial conflict detection to distributed schedule consensus. The analysis focuses on investigating the potential trade-off introduced by the horizon choice: balancing local tractability and computational responsiveness with the need for global schedule coherence and feasibility in safety-critical environments. Contrary to intuition, our empirical results indicate that the short time horizons suffice, while long values compromise local tractability and computational responsiveness with no gain in global schedule optimality.

</details>


### [186] [$α$-fair heterogeneous agent reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13076)
*Yao-hua Franck Xu,Tayeb Lemlouma,Jean-Marie Bonnin,Arnaud Braud*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Proposes a framework integrating α-fairness into Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL), yielding algorithms (α-fair HATRPO, α-fair HAPPO) that achieve both higher utilitarian performance and more equitable social outcomes in multi-agent sequential social dilemmas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard multi-agent RL optimizes utilitarian objectives, leading to inequitable leader-follower dynamics. Fairness-based methods either break Markov Game stationarity or lack theoretical guarantees, creating a gap between fair objectives and theoretically sound learning.

Method: Introduces a fair advantage function that dynamically weights agent utilities by expected returns, parameterized by α to interpolate between utilitarian (α=0) and maximally fair (α→∞) regimes. This is embedded into HATRL's trust-region policy optimization (HATRPO/HAPPO), preserving monotonic improvement and Nash equilibrium convergence guarantees.

Result: α-fair HATRPO and α-fair HAPPO outperform baseline HATRL algorithms on utilitarian metrics while simultaneously achieving more equitable reward distributions in CleanUp and CommonHarvest sequential social dilemma environments.

Conclusion: Integrating α-fairness into trust-region multi-agent RL successfully bridges the gap between fairness objectives and theoretically safe learning, yielding practically superior and socially preferable policies.

Abstract: Cooperation in multi-agent systems is typically optimized through utilitarian objectives that maximize overall efficiency but fail to account for reward distribution, often resulting in inequitable "leader-follower" dynamics. While fairness-based approaches encourage pro-social behaviors where every agent benefits from cooperation, many current algorithms - including those utilizing reward shaping - break the stationarity of Markov Games or lack rigorous theoretical guarantees. This creates a critical gap between fair objective methods and theoretically safe learning frameworks. We propose a novel framework that bridges $α$-fairness with Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL), ensuring monotonic improvement and convergence toward Nash Equilibria. Our approach leverages a fair advantage function that dynamically weights agent utilities based on their expected returns, allowing the global objective to transition from purely utilitarian efficiency to $α$-fairness welfare based on the parameter $α$. We introduce two practical algorithms, $α$-fair HATRPO and $α$-fair HAPPO, and demonstrate through experiments in sequential social dilemmas like CleanUp and CommonHarvest that they perform better than HATRL's algorithms from a utilitarian point of view while achieving socially higher outcomes.

</details>


### [187] [See What I See, Know What I Think: Dense Latent Communication Across Heterogeneous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13594)
*Siyi Chen,Xiaoyan Zhang,Meng Wu,Jonathan Tremblay,Valts Blukis,Stan Birchfield,Rene Vidal,Alvaro Velasquez,Sijia Liu,Qing Qu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Proposes dense alignment for heterogeneous KV-cache communication between different LLMs, enabling both context-aware and context-unaware transfer via lightweight cross-model transformation and two-phase training, outperforming text communication at 2–3× lower compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent systems rely on text communication, which incurs lossy decoding/re-encoding costs. KV-cache communication avoids this but prior work is either homogeneous (same model) or restrictive heterogeneous methods that assume shared input and use caches only for steering. The open question: can heterogeneous agents achieve "mind reading" — transferring both what one agent sees and how it thinks?

Method: Authors conduct an information-structure analysis revealing a duality: context-aware transfer needs sparse reasoning signals, context-unaware transfer needs dense contextual knowledge. They propose a lightweight cross-model KV-cache transformation with two-phase training (reconstruction then generation), evaluated across all six directed pairs of Qwen3-4B/8B/14B on six benchmarks.

Result: The method outperforms prior heterogeneous baselines, matches or exceeds text communication in context-aware settings at roughly 2–3× lower compute, and remains effective in context-unaware transfer where prior methods collapse entirely.

Conclusion: Dense alignment enables practical heterogeneous KV-cache communication for multi-agent systems, supporting both "mind reading" modes and offering significant compute savings over text-based communication.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems communicate mostly through text, paying a lossy and expensive decode and re-encode cost. KV-cache communication is a promising alternative, yet most prior work is homogeneous, using duplicate copies of the same model, and avoids the central challenge of cross-model latent alignment; existing heterogeneous methods are also restrictive, typically assuming shared input and using transferred caches mainly for steering. We study a more fundamental question: can heterogeneous agents be aligned well enough to perform real "mind reading" and transfer both what one agent sees and how it thinks? Our information-structure analysis reveals a duality: context-aware transfer is driven by sparse reasoning signals, while context-unaware transfer, where the receiver sees no input, requires dense contextual knowledge preservation. Motivated by this, we propose dense alignment for heterogeneous KV-cache communication via a lightweight cross-model cache transformation and two-phase training: reconstruction followed by generation. Across all six directions of {Qwen3-4B, 8B, 14B} and six in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, our method outperforms prior heterogeneous baselines, matches or exceeds text communication in context-aware settings at roughly 2 to 3 times lower compute, and remains effective in context-unaware transfer where prior methods collapse.

</details>


### [188] [Tuning Agent-Based Predator-Prey Models Toward Lotka-Volterra Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13639)
*Corinna Mandl,Siddharth Chaturvedi,Marcel van Gerven*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper addresses the calibration challenge in large-scale agent-based models by optimizing environmental and demographic parameters of a predator-prey system (sheep and wolves with neural network controllers) to produce classical Lotka-Volterra population dynamics, using a JAX-based framework (ABMax) for hardware-accelerated simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale agent-based models contain many local rules and parameters where small changes can cause runaway behavior, population collapse, or artificial saturation. Calibrating such models to exhibit realistic, stable emergent dynamics—specifically Lotka-Volterra cycles—is a central challenge that this work aims to address.

Method: The authors implement a continuous predator-prey system in ABMax (a JAX-based ABM framework for batched GPU/TPU simulation) where sheep and wolves are active agents with local sensing, internal energy reserves, and recurrent neural network controllers. They optimize environmental and demographic parameters using a feature-based loss function that rewards sustained oscillations, appropriate phase lag, bounded populations, and long-term persistence. Optimization is performed first with random controllers, then with evolved controllers in a more naturalistic setting.

Result: The feature-based loss optimization successfully tunes parameters so that population dynamics resemble classical Lotka-Volterra cycles. The approach works for both random controllers and evolved controllers operating in a more ecologically naturalistic configuration.

Conclusion: Parameter optimization via feature-based loss functions is an effective strategy for calibrating complex agent-based models to produce desired emergent population dynamics. The ABMax framework facilitates this by enabling efficient hardware-accelerated batched simulation.

Abstract: Recent growth in compute power has made it increasingly feasible to use large-scale agent-based models to simulate complex adaptive systems. A central difficulty is that such models contain many local rules and parameters, where small changes can lead to runaway behaviour, population collapse, or saturation at artificial bounds. We study this problem in a continuous predator-prey system where sheep and wolves are active agents with local sensing, internal energy, and recurrent neural network-based controllers. We ask whether environmental and demographic parameters can be tuned so that the resulting population dynamics resemble classical Lotka-Volterra cycles. We optimise these parameters with a feature-based loss that rewards sustained oscillations, phase lag, bounded populations, and long-term persistence, first for random controllers and then for evolved controllers in a more naturalistic setting. The model is implemented in ABMax, a JAX-based agent-based modelling framework that enables efficient batched simulation on hardware accelerators.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [189] [Characterizing Tests in IoT Software: Practices, Challenges and Opportunities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12592)
*Rufeng Chen,Hengcheng Zhu,Wuqi Zhang,Zixu Zhou,Lili Wei*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: First empirical study on IoT software testing reveals that despite high test volume, test effectiveness is limited due to challenges with external dependencies. Mocking practices align well with these challenges, suggesting a promising direction to improve IoT test coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT software bugs can cause severe real-world consequences (e.g., unauthorized access via buggy smart locks), yet little is known about how IoT software is actually tested in practice. The authors aim to fill this knowledge gap through the first empirical study on test cases in open-source IoT software.

Method: The authors conducted an empirical study on open-source IoT software, evaluating three aspects: (1) the effectiveness of existing test cases, (2) inherent challenges in IoT software testing, and (3) the usage patterns of mock objects in IoT test suites.

Result: IoT software often contains a substantial number of tests, but their effectiveness is limited. The primary testing challenges identified are managing complex interactions with external dependencies, including network-reliant IoT components, file systems, operating systems, and databases. Mock object usage closely mirrors these identified challenges, indicating mocking as a viable strategy to address IoT testing complexity.

Conclusion: Mocking holds strong potential to enhance test coverage and address the inherent complexities of IoT software testing, particularly in handling diverse external dependencies.

Abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is experiencing rapid growth. Smart devices are emerging in smart homes and industrial applications, performing mission-critical tasks. Bugs in IoT software can lead to severe consequences. For example, a buggy smart lock can allow unauthorized access to a private property. Testing is a primary practice to expose software bugs and ensure software quality. However, little is known about how IoT software is tested. To bridge this gap, we conducted the first empirical study on test cases in open-source IoT software. Specifically, we evaluated the effectiveness of test cases in IoT software, explored the challenges inherent in testing IoT software, and analyzed the usage of mock objects. Our results indicate that while IoT software often contains a considerable number of tests, their effectiveness remains limited. We identified the primary challenges in testing IoT software as managing complex interactions with various external dependencies, such as other network-reliant IoT components, file systems, operating systems, and databases. We also observed that the use of mock objects in IoT software closely aligns with our identified testing challenges. This alignment demonstrates the potential of mocking as a solution to enhance test coverage and address the complexities of IoT software testing.

</details>


### [190] [HybridCodeAuthorship: A Benchmark Dataset for Line-Level Code Authorship Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12620)
*Luke Patterson,Li Wang,Adam Faulkner*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Introduces HybridCodeAuthorship, a benchmark of Python files with interleaved human/AI-authored lines reflecting real-world AI code assistant usage. Existing benchmarks use simplistic all-or-nothing snippets. Top algorithm achieves only F1 0.48 (chunk) and 0.56 (line), showing the task remains challenging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industry codebases increasingly mix human- and AI-authored code, requiring fine-grained detection of AI-generated lines for risk management and productivity analysis. Current benchmarks are inadequate: they use academic/LeetCode-style problems and assume code is entirely human- or AI-authored, failing to represent realistic hybrid codebases where AI assistants contribute interleaved lines.

Method: The authors construct HybridCodeAuthorship using a pipeline built on CodeSearchNet, a large collection of open-source GitHub repositories. The dataset comprises Python files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines, simulating authentic AI code assistant usage. Two state-of-the-art AI-generated code detection algorithms are benchmarked at both line-level and chunk-level granularity.

Result: HybridCodeAuthorship proves to be a challenging benchmark. The top-performing algorithm, AIGCode Detector, achieves F1 scores of only 0.48 at chunk-level and 0.56 at line-level detection, indicating substantial room for improvement in fine-grained hybrid code authorship detection.

Conclusion: Existing benchmarks for AI-authored code detection are unrealistic for industry settings. HybridCodeAuthorship provides a more authentic evaluation framework, and the modest performance of current SOTA methods highlights the need for better algorithms capable of detecting AI-generated code at fine granularity within hybrid files.

Abstract: Thanks to the rapid adoption of AI code assistants powered by large language models (LLMs), industry codebases are, increasingly, a hybrid of AI- and human-authored code. For risk management and productivity analysis purposes, it is crucial to enable fine-grained location detection of AI-generated code. To develop algorithms for this task, quality benchmarks are needed to assess performance. However, existing benchmarks tend to comprise academic, LeetCode-style problems and presume a code snippet is either completely human-authored or completely AI-authored, which is not reflective of the diverse intents and styles of industry codebases utilizing AI code assistants. To fill these gaps, we introduce HybridCodeAuthorship, a novel benchmark of Python code files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines of code to simulate authentic utilization of AI code assistants. In this paper, we first present our dataset construction pipeline, which leverages CodeSearchNet, a massive collection of links to open sourced repositories on GitHub. We then benchmark the performance of two state-of-the-art AI-generated code detection algorithms at both the line- and chunk-level. Experimental results demonstrate that HybridCodeAuthorship is a challenging benchmark with a top-scoring algorithm, AIGCode Detector, obtaining a highest F1 score of 0.48 and 0.56 on chunk-level and line-level code detection tasks, respectively.

</details>


### [191] [Beyond Problem Solving: UOJ-Bench for Evaluating Code Generation, Hacking, and Repair in Competitive Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12864)
*Tingqiang Xu,Hangrui Zhou,Tianle Cai,Alex Gu,Kaifeng Lyu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: UOJ-Bench evaluates LLMs on three tasks from real online judge submissions: code generation, code hacking (error identification), and code repair. Even top models miss >50% of known errors in one-shot settings; test-time scaling improves detection to >90% but is computationally expensive. Frontier LLMs can already flag errors in 5% of full-score submissions, offering complementary signals to standard judging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs excel at competitive programming, their capacity to support human learning—particularly identifying errors in human-written code—remains underexplored. Traditional online judge systems rely on running test cases, but LLMs could potentially provide richer feedback. This gap motivates a benchmark that assesses both problem-solving and pedagogical error-detection abilities.

Method: The authors construct UOJ-Bench from real code submissions on the Universal Online Judge, comprising three tasks: (1) code generation—solving problems; (2) code hacking—identifying errors in incorrect human submissions; (3) code repair—fixing those errors. All evaluations use UOJ's native judging infrastructure. They test LLMs under one-shot and test-time scaling (increased inference compute) regimes.

Result: Under one-shot evaluation, even the strongest models fail to identify errors in >50% of submissions known to be incorrect by UOJ users. Test-time scaling boosts success rates to >90%, but incurs prohibitive computational costs for large-scale deployment. Notably, best-performing models under test-time scaling uncover errors in >5% of submissions that received full scores across ~30 problems, indicating LLMs can provide signals beyond standard judging.

Conclusion: Frontier LLMs can already complement online judge systems by detecting errors that pass all test cases. However, the computational cost of test-time scaling limits practical educational deployment, highlighting a trade-off between accuracy and efficiency that needs addressing before widespread adoption.

Abstract: Despite strong performance in competitive programming, the role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in supporting human learning in the same setting remains largely unexplored. In this work, we introduce UOJ-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate not only the problem-solving ability of LLMs, but also their ability to identify errors in human-written code -- a crucial educational activity traditionally supported by running test cases over online judge systems. UOJ-Bench consists of three distinct tasks: code generation, code hacking, and code repair, all constructed from real-world code submissions on the Universal Online Judge (UOJ) and evaluated through UOJ's native judging infrastructure. Our results show that under one-shot evaluation, even the strongest models fail to identify errors in more than 50% of a set of submissions that have been found to be incorrect by UOJ users. While test-time scaling improves success rates to above 90%, the substantial computational costs incurred from model inference limit its practicality for large-scale deployment. Despite these limitations, we find that the best-performing models under test-time scaling can uncover errors in over 5% of full-score submissions across roughly 30 problems, suggesting that frontier LLMs can already provide complementary signals beyond standard judging systems.

</details>


### [192] [The Rise of AI-Native Software Engineering: Implications for Practice, Education, and the Future Workforce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12986)
*Mamdouh Alenezi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A systematic review of 48 peer-reviewed publications (2016–2026) on GenAI/LLMs in software engineering, identifying a post-2022 five-fold output surge. The paper contributes a conceptual framework (intent, collaboration, verification), a nine-dimension competency model, a four-phase curriculum roadmap, workforce strategies, and eleven research gaps. Productivity benefits are context-dependent; the core challenge is educating engineers for judgment and orchestration, not just code production.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI, LLMs, and Agentic AI represent the most disruptive transformation in software engineering history, fundamentally altering development processes, professional roles, competencies, and educational requirements. There is an urgent need to systematically synthesize the rapidly growing but fragmented evidence base to guide practice, education, and workforce policy.

Method: Systematic review of 48 verified, influential peer-reviewed publications (2016–2026) sourced from top venues in SE, ML, computing education, HAI, and software productivity. A novel four-agent research workflow was employed: Literature Discovery, Scientometric Analysis, Curriculum Transformation, and Workforce Impact. All studies were verified against primary sources. Evidence was synthesized along nine themes and three trajectories (practice, education, workforce).

Result: A scientometric inflection point was identified: annual LLM-for-SE publications grew roughly five-fold after late 2022. Five key contributions emerged: (i) a conceptual framework for AI-native SE (intent, collaboration, verification); (ii) a nine-dimension competency model (specification, critical evaluation, agent orchestration, metacognition, etc.); (iii) a four-phase university curriculum roadmap with AI-resilient assessment; (iv) faculty-development and workforce-transformation strategies; (v) a prioritized agenda of eleven research gaps. The evidence base is internally contradictory regarding productivity effects' magnitude and direction, revealing strong context-dependence.

Conclusion: The central challenge of the AI-native era is educating engineers for judgment, verification, and orchestration rather than code production alone. The contradictory evidence on productivity underscores that benefits are highly context-dependent, cautioning against one-size-fits-all adoption. The proposed frameworks and roadmap provide actionable guidance for academia and industry to navigate this transformation.

Abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI), Large Language Models (LLMs), and emerging Agentic AI constitute the most disruptive transformation in the history of software engineering (SE), reshaping development processes, required competencies, professional roles, and the educational outcomes that universities must deliver. This paper presents a systematic review of 48 verified, influential peer-reviewed publications (2016--2026) drawn from leading venues in software engineering, machine learning, computing education, human--AI collaboration, and software productivity. Studies were discovered, screened, and analyzed through a four-agent research workflow (Literature Discovery, Scientometric Analysis, Curriculum Transformation, and Workforce Impact) and were verified against primary sources. We synthesize the evidence along nine themes and three trajectories -- practice, education, and workforce -- and report a scientometric inflection in which annual LLM-for-SE output grew roughly five-fold after late 2022. From this synthesis we contribute: (i) a conceptual framework for AI-native software engineering organized around \emph{intent}, \emph{collaboration}, and \emph{verification}; (ii) a nine-dimension competency model spanning specification, critical evaluation, agent orchestration, and metacognition; (iii) a four-phase university curriculum roadmap with AI-resilient assessment; (iv) faculty-development and workforce-transformation strategies; and (v) a prioritized agenda of eleven research gaps. The evidence base is internally contradictory on the magnitude and direction of productivity effects, underscoring that benefits are strongly context-dependent and that educating engineers for judgment, verification, and orchestration -- rather than code production alone -- is the central challenge of the AI-native era.

</details>


### [193] [The End of Code Review: Coding Agents Supersede Human Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13175)
*Martin Monperrus*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper argues that LLM-based coding agents have reached sufficient capability to replace traditional human code review, as agents can achieve all review goals at lower cost and higher throughput, and the hybrid model of AI writing + human reviewing is unscalable and provides no meaningful assurance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the 50-year-old orthodoxy that human code review is an indispensable quality gate, and to argue that the integration of coding agents into software development necessitates rethinking the review process entirely rather than simply inserting AI into the existing pipeline.

Method: The paper presents a two-claim argumentative framework: (1) a goal-decomposition analysis showing that every stated objective of code review — defect detection, knowledge transfer, standards compliance, etc. — can be served by coding agents more efficiently; (2) a reductio-style critique of the naive AI-writes/human-reviews integration model, demonstrating it fails on both assurance and scalability grounds.

Result: The analysis concludes that coding agents can subsume all code review functions at lower cost and higher throughput, and that retaining humans as mandatory reviewers in an agent-driven workflow creates a bottleneck that undermines the velocity gains agents provide while delivering negligible quality benefits.

Conclusion: Traditional human code review is no longer a necessary component of the software quality pipeline when coding agents are employed. The field must move toward agent-native quality assurance mechanisms rather than preserving the human-review orthodoxy.

Abstract: Code review has been the primary quality gate in software development since Fagan formalised code inspection in 1976. For five decades, having a human examine and comment on a colleague's changes before merge has been a cornerstone practice at organisations of every size. Coding agents are large language model (LLM)-based autonomous systems capable of reading, writing, testing, and repairing software. We argue that coding agents have crossed a threshold of capability at which traditional human code review is no longer a necessary component of a software quality pipeline. Our argument rests on two claims: every stated goal of code review can be served by agents at lower cost and higher throughput; the naive integration in which agents write code and humans remain the mandatory reviewers is a dead end because it neither provides meaningful assurance nor scales with AI-assisted throughput.

</details>


### [194] [ComAct: Reframing Professional Software Manipulation via COM-as-Action Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13239)
*Jiaxin Ai,Tao Hu,Xuemeng Yang,Shu Zou,Hairong Zhang,Daocheng Fu,Yu Yang,Hongbin Zhou,Nianchen Deng,Pinlong Cai,Zhongyuan Wang,Botian Shi,Kaipeng Zhang,Licheng Wen*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes COM-as-Action, a paradigm that treats software interaction as program synthesis via the Component Object Model (COM), circumventing the fragility of GUI-based agents. It introduces ComCADBench for benchmarking CAD automation, reveals a massive performance gap favoring COM over GUI, and presents ComActor (a self-correcting agent) and ComForge (a training platform) that achieve SOTA on long-horizon CAD tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computer-use agents are fundamentally limited: GUI-based agents suffer from fragile visual grounding and error accumulation over long horizons, while API-based approaches struggle with heterogeneous protocols and inaccessible commercial interfaces. There is a need for a unified, robust paradigm for professional software manipulation, particularly for industrial CAD software where precision and long-horizon reliability are critical.

Method: The authors identify COM as a unified executable abstraction and propose COM-as-Action, which reframes professional software interaction as deterministic program synthesis rather than sequential visual control. To validate this, they build ComCADBench, a benchmark for agents operating real industrial CAD software. They then develop ComActor, a self-correcting agent trained through a progressive three-stage framework, and ComForge, a scalable platform for large-scale training within Windows containers.

Result: Frontier proprietary models achieve near-zero success under GUI-based interaction on ComCADBench, whereas COM-based execution yields substantial immediate gains. ComActor achieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench, demonstrates strong resilience in long-horizon tasks where baselines collapse, and generalizes to an external CAD benchmark.

Conclusion: The COM-as-Action paradigm decisively outperforms GUI-based approaches for professional software manipulation. ComActor bridges the remaining gap between syntactic correctness and geometric accuracy, establishing a new SOTA and demonstrating that deterministic program synthesis via COM is a viable and scalable path forward for robust software agents.

Abstract: Existing computer-use agents remain fundamentally limited in professional software manipulation: GUI-based agents suffer from fragile visual grounding and long-horizon error accumulation, while API-basedapproaches struggle with heterogeneous protocols and inaccessible commercial interfaces. In this work,we identify the Component Object Model (COM) as a unified executable abstraction, proposing COM-as-Action: a new paradigm that reframes professional software interaction as deterministic program synthesisrather than sequential visual control. To validate this paradigm in the most demanding environments, weintroduce ComCADBench, the first benchmark for agents operating real industrial CAD software. Ourexperiments reveal a substantial paradigm gap: frontier proprietary models achieve near-zero successunder GUI-based interaction, whereas COM-based execution yields substantial immediate gains. Tobridge the remaining gap between syntactic correctness and geometric accuracy, we develop ComActor, aself-correcting agent trained through a progressive three-stage framework, alongside ComForge, a scalableplatform for large-scale training in Windows containers. Extensive experiments show that ComActorachieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench, with strong resilience in long-horizon taskswhere baselines collapse, and generalizes to external CAD benchmark.

</details>


### [195] [Mining Architectural Quality Under Agentic AI Adoption: A Causal Study of Java Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13298)
*Oliver Aleksander Larsen,Mahyar T. Moghaddam*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A causal study of 151 Java repositories finds that AI coding tool adoption does not reduce architectural smells in absolute terms; an apparent 6.7% decline in architectural smell density is entirely driven by a 12.8% increase in lines of code (a denominator effect), not genuine architectural improvement. The paper warns that density-normalized metrics can mislead when treatment affects system size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI coding tools (including agentic "vibe coding") are widely adopted, but prior causal work has only examined code-level outcomes (complexity, static analysis warnings). It remains unknown whether code-level degradation propagates to software architecture. The authors seek to fill this gap by providing the first causal evidence on how AI tool adoption affects architecture-level outcomes.

Method: The authors mine 151 open-source Java repositories (74 treated with detectable agentic AI adoption, 77 propensity-matched controls) over a 13-month window, producing 1,811 monthly Arcan architectural snapshots. They apply a staggered difference-in-differences design with the Borusyak imputation estimator to estimate the causal effect on architectural smell density (ASD). Robustness checks include wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, and stale-observation sensitivity. Pre-trends are tested via Wald statistic.

Result: Total architectural smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82), while lines of code grow significantly (+12.8%, p = 0.003). The resulting 6.7% decline in ASD (p = 0.004) is therefore a denominator effect. Pre-trends are flat (Wald p = 0.90), supporting parallel trends. Per-type estimates and all robustness checks corroborate this pattern.

Conclusion: Density-normalized outcomes can be misleading in causal mining studies when the treatment affects system size. Researchers should report raw counts and perform explicit decomposition rather than relying solely on density metrics. The study provides a complete replication package for transparency.

Abstract: AI coding tools are now used by a majority of developers, and agentic use of these tools has popularized the practice colloquially called "vibe coding". Yet causal evidence on their effect on software architecture is scarce. Prior causal work has measured code-level outcomes (complexity, static analysis warnings); whether such degradation propagates to architecture-level outcomes remains unknown. We mine 151 open-source Java repositories, 74 with detectable agentic AI adoption (identified via configuration files and Co-Authored-By commit trailers) and 77 propensity-matched controls, across a 13-month per-repository window yielding 1,811 monthly Arcan snapshots. We estimate the causal effect of adoption on architectural smell density (ASD) with a staggered difference-in-differences design and the Borusyak imputation estimator, applying a causal design recently used for code-level metrics to the architecture level. Total smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82) while lines of code grow +12.8% (p = 0.003); the resulting 6.7% ASD decline (p = 0.004) is therefore a denominator effect rather than an architectural improvement. Per-type estimates and robustness checks (wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, stale-observation sensitivity) corroborate the pattern; pre-trends are flat (Wald p = 0.90), consistent with parallel trends. Density-normalized outcomes can mislead when treatment affects system size: raw counts and explicit decomposition are required for causal mining studies of AI tool adoption. The complete replication package, including the curated 151-repository monthly panel, is publicly available.

</details>


### [196] [Toward Instructions-as-Code: Understanding the Impact of Instruction Files on Agentic Pull Requests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13449)
*Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether providing instruction files to AI-agents (like GitHub Copilot) improves the quality of their pull requests. Analyzing 15,549 agentic PRs across 148 projects, the authors find no consistent benefit: roughly equal proportions of projects saw merge rates increase vs. decrease after adding instructions. However, projects that did improve tended to have longer, better-structured instruction files, motivating the concept of "Instructions-as-Code" as a disciplined software engineering practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-agents are increasingly used in software engineering tasks like code generation via pull requests. Developers create instruction files to guide these agents, but it is unclear whether such instructions actually improve agent performance in terms of PR merge rate, task complexity, and merge effort. The paper seeks to empirically assess this relationship.

Method: The authors analyze 15,549 agentic PRs from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset. They compare project performance before and after the introduction of instruction files across three dimensions: (1) merge rate, (2) task complexity (code churn, number of modified files), and (3) merge effort (time to merge, number of review comments). They also conduct an exploratory analysis of instruction file characteristics (length, structure) correlated with improved outcomes.

Result: Specifying instructions does not consistently improve outcomes: 27.7% of projects increased merge rate by ≥20%, while 26.35% saw decreases. Similar mixed results hold for code churn, modified files, merge time, and comment count. Exploratory analysis reveals that projects with improved merge rates tend to have substantially longer, better-structured instruction files with more sections and sub-sections.

Conclusion: Instruction files alone do not guarantee better AI-agent performance. The findings motivate the need for treating instruction file development as a formal software engineering activity — termed "Instructions-as-Code" — and call for further research to help practitioners craft effective agent instructions.

Abstract: AI-agents (e.g., GitHub Copilot) collaborate as teammates in different software engineering tasks, including code generation proposed through pull requests (Agentic-PRs). For better agent efficiency, developers create instruction files that guide the AI-agents, including how to navigate the project, locate the right components, run tests, respect best practices, and more. In this paper, we investigate the relationship between the creation of these instructions and the performance of AI-agents in creating better pull requests, which have a higher chance of success (i.e., the merge rate), address more complex tasks (e.g., code churn), and require less effort to be merged (e.g., time to merge). To this end, we analyze 15,549 agentic PRs from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset. Using the three dimensions, we compare each project before and after the creation of the instruction files. We find that specifying instructions for AI-agents does not necessarily lead to better results. With the instruction files, 27.7\% of the projects increased their merge rate by at least 20\%, while 26.35\% decreased it. The same observation is seen with the amount of changes (e.g., code churn, number of modified files) and with the efforts to merge an agentic PR (e.g., merge time and number of comments). From a first exploration, we find that projects that managed to increase their merge rate have substantially longer instruction files, which are also well structured into a higher number of sections and sub-sections. Our results motivate the need for research to assist practitioners in framing the development of instruction files as a software engineering activity (aka, \textbf{Instructions-as-Code}).

</details>


### [197] [Understanding the Rejection of Fixes Generated by Agentic Pull Requests -- Insights from the AIDev Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13468)
*Mahmoud Abujadallah,Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A qualitative and quantitative study of 306 non-merged AI-generated PRs reveals 14 rejection reasons across four categories: incorrect implementation, CI/test failures, agent inability to perform tasks, and low priority fixes. The findings highlight the need for better guidance on fix approaches, constraints, CI validation, and task prioritization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 46.41% of AI-agent-proposed fixes (from Copilot, Devin, Cursor, Claude) are rejected, wasting significant human review and computational resources. Understanding why these failures occur is critical for integrating AI agents as effective teammates in software development.

Method: A mixed-methods approach: (1) qualitative analysis on a representative sample of 306 non-merged PRs created or co-authored by AI agents (Copilot, Devin, Cursor, Claude) drawn from the AIDev dataset, followed by (2) quantitative analysis of rejection reasons.

Result: 14 distinct rejection reasons were identified, grouped into four high-level categories: (1) incorrect implementation (incomplete, wrong approach), (2) failure to pass CI pipelines and tests, (3) agent inability to perform the implementation (no code generated, lost sessions), and (4) low-priority fixes.

Conclusion: Improving AI-agent effectiveness requires guidance at three levels: (1) suggesting appropriate fix approaches, (2) outlining constraints on approaches to avoid, and (3) instructing agents on CI-based validation to prevent breaking changes. Additionally, proper task prioritization is essential to avoid wasted human review effort and agent resources.

Abstract: AI coding agents are increasingly used to generate pull requests (PRs) that propose code fixes in software projects. From a first exploration of the AIDev dataset, we find that 46.41\% of the fixes proposed by the agents Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude are rejected. This represents a significant amount of wasted resources that require human reviews, verifications, and running tests and validations for fixes that are merely discarded. Our goal in this paper is to understand the failure modes of AI-agents, an understanding that is crucial for better integrating AI-agents as efficient teammates. In this paper, we conduct a qualitative study on a representative sample of 306 non-merged pull requests created or co-authored by the agents mentioned earlier, followed by a quantitative analysis of the reasons for rejection. Our qualitative findings identify 14 reasons divided into four high-level categories for rejecting AI-agent fixes. We observe that developers can reject fixes due to fixes whose implementation is incorrect (e.g., incomplete, wrong approach), fixes that do not pass the continuous integration (CI) pipelines and fail tests, fixes for which the agent is unable to perform the implementation (e.g., no code generated, sessions lost), and fixes whose priority is low. Our results shed light on the importance of better guiding the model at these levels: (1) proposing hints about the approach to follow for fixing an issue, (2) outlining constraints or limitations regarding the approaches that should not be taken, and (3) instructing the agent on how to validate the implementation through CI pipelines and without introducing a breaking change. Our results suggest the need for good prioritization of tasks so that generated fixes do not lead to wasted human review efforts or wasted agent resources (e.g., tokens, compute, or allowed number of requests).

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [198] [Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12476)
*Igor Itkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reframes token-level hallucination detection as a quickest change detection problem, showing that sequential analysis reveals critical delay structures invisible to standard classification metrics. Using Lorden's bound and CUSUM-inspired models, the authors demonstrate that a causal recurrent labeler detects hallucinations in 11-13 tokens (vs. 31 for a linear baseline), though a Donsker-Varadhan information-rate analysis exposes a 4.5× deficit relative to the information theoretically available in the features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard hallucination detection evaluation uses AUC over all tokens, which treats detection as a static classification task and conceals temporal delay structure. In streaming monitoring scenarios, what matters is reaction time — how many tokens pass before an alarm sounds after hallucination onset. The authors argue that classification metrics fundamentally miss this sequential nature and that a change-point detection formulation is needed to properly characterize and optimize streaming hallucination monitors.

Method: The authors (1) formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection (QCD) problem with Lorden's minimax criterion; (2) validate a first-order Markov model for faithful/hallucinated state transitions on the RAGTruth dataset; (3) derive Lorden's lower bound (~1.3 tokens at 0.01 false-alarm rate) using classical change-point theory; (4) implement and evaluate a causal recurrent labeler that operates as a CUSUM procedure with a learned increment; (5) conduct a controlled decomposition to separate per-token scoring quality from temporal accumulation; and (6) apply a Donsker-Varadhan type information-rate optimality theorem to quantify the gap between achieved and optimal detection delay.

Result: Lorden's lower bound establishes a theoretical minimum detection delay of ~1.3 tokens. The causal recurrent labeler achieves detection in 11-13 tokens at a matched false-alarm rate, substantially outperforming the linear per-token baseline (31 tokens). Decomposition analysis attributes most of this advantage to better per-token scoring rather than temporal accumulation. The Donsker-Varadhan analysis reveals the learned score captures only ~1/4.5 of the available information-theoretic divergence in the features — a deficit that recalibration cannot fix — with the remainder attributed to finite-horizon effects.

Conclusion: Classification metrics conceal the delay structure inherent in streaming hallucination detection; sequential analysis makes this structure measurable. While a CUSUM-style recurrent labeler significantly improves over linear baselines, an order-of-magnitude gap remains between achieved performance and the information-theoretic optimum, suggesting substantial headroom for future work on better feature-to-score mapping beyond recalibration.

Abstract: Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet a streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of a hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem. A first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lorden's lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at a false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that a causal recurrent labeler acts as a CUSUM with a learned increment; at a matched false-alarm rate it detects in 11-13 tokens, against 31 for a linear per-token baseline, and a controlled decomposition attributes most of this advantage to a better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of Donsker-Varadhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, a deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder a finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable

</details>


### [199] [Boltzmann Attention: Learnable Ising Couplings for Cooperative Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12478)
*Gilhan Kim,Daniel K. Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Boltzmann attention, which augments standard attention with learnable pairwise couplings via an Ising model framework, enabling explicit modeling of inter-position interactions. Shows consistent improvements over softmax attention, especially at longer sequences, and demonstrates feasibility of quantum annealing for training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention computes relevance through individual query-key similarities with softmax competition, but lacks explicit learnable interactions between attention decisions. This limits modeling of cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structures directly within the attention mechanism.

Method: Boltzmann attention formulates attention patterns as an energy-based model governed by an interacting Ising model. It augments standard data-dependent local fields (unary terms from query-key similarities) with learnable pairwise coupling parameters. Attention weights are derived from the Boltzmann distribution over this energy function. Training uses exact Boltzmann computation, with a demonstrated alternative using diabatic quantum annealing for sampling.

Result: On character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching tasks, Boltzmann attention consistently outperforms standard softmax attention in a Transformer architecture. The advantage grows with increasing sequence length. A four-way ablation study confirms improvements stem specifically from the learnable pairwise couplings. Diabatic quantum annealing achieves competitive performance with exact computation.

Conclusion: Explicit inter-position interactions via learnable pairwise couplings provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. The Ising formulation also opens pathways to quantum-computing-based training strategies.

Abstract: Attention mechanisms are central to modern sequence models, yet standard attention computes relevance primarily through individual query--key similarities. Although softmax normalization introduces competition among positions, a standard attention layer does not explicitly parameterize learnable interactions between attention decisions. This limits its ability to directly model cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structure within the attention mechanism itself. We propose Boltzmann attention, an energy-based generalization in which attention patterns are governed by an interacting Ising model. The method augments the usual data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings, allowing the model to represent inter-position correlations beyond those captured by softmax or sigmoid attention. Experiments on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching show that Boltzmann attention consistently improves over standard softmax attention within a standard Transformer architecture, with the advantage becoming more pronounced as sequence length increases. A four-way ablation confirms that the improvement arises from the learnable pairwise couplings. These results suggest that explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. Moreover, the Ising formulation opens a natural path toward quantum-computing-based sampling strategies: we demonstrate that diabatic quantum annealing provides a practical training method while maintaining competitive performance with exact Boltzmann computation.

</details>


### [200] [ReCal: Reward Calibration for RL-based LLM Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12479)
*Qihang Yu,Hanwen Tong,Zhengqi Zhang,Bo Zheng,Feng Wei,Shengyu Zhang,Zemin Liu,Fei Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReCal is a reward calibration framework for RL-based LLM routing that addresses ambiguous credit assignment via hierarchical reward decomposition and mitigates optimization bias through distribution-aware optimization (variance reweighting + per-dataset normalization), achieving consistent routing improvements across seven datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-based LLM routing methods aggregate multiple objectives (correctness, format, etc.) into a single scalar reward, causing ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Additionally, reward variability across instances biases optimization toward trivial samples over informative ones.

Method: ReCal introduces two key components: (1) Hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation, breaking the scalar reward into interpretable sub-rewards; (2) Distribution-aware optimization that calibrates variability via variance-aware reweighting of instances and per-dataset normalization to reduce bias from reward scale differences.

Result: Experiments on seven datasets show ReCal consistently improves routing performance and training stability compared to baseline RL-based routing methods.

Conclusion: Reward calibration through hierarchical decomposition and distribution-aware optimization effectively resolves the credit assignment and optimization bias problems in RL-based LLM routing, leading to more robust and performant routing policies.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) routing has emerged as an effective paradigm for leveraging the complementary strengths of multiple LLMs through dynamic model and reasoning-strategy selection. Recent reinforcement learning (RL)-based routing methods further improve routing quality by optimizing routing policies from interaction feedback.
