However, they still struggle to provide informative and comparable learning signals under heterogeneous tasks with varying difficulty. In practice, multiple objectives (e.g., correctness, format behavior) are aggregated into a single scalar reward, leading to ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Moreover, reward signals exhibit significant variability across instances, where some instances produce higher or more variable rewards, introducing optimization bias that favors trivial samples over informative ones. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{ReCal}, a \textbf{\underline{Re}}ward \textbf{\underline{Cal}}ibration framework for RL-based LLM routing. We first introduce a hierarchical reward decomposition mechanism with component-wise advantage estimation. We further propose a distribution-aware optimization strategy that calibrates optimization variability through variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization. Experiments on seven datasets demonstrate that ReCal consistently improves routing performance, and training stability over baselines. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ReCal.

</details>


### [201] [Representing Time Series as Structured Programs for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12481)
*Jaeho Kim,Changhun Oh,Seokhyun Lee,Irina Rish,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: T2SP is a training-free method that converts time series into structured symbolic programs (capturing trends, periods, salient events), enabling off-the-shelf LLMs to reason effectively about time-series data without modality mismatch or fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs excel at reasoning but operate on text/code, not numerical time series. Existing approaches (raw serialization, fine-tuning) create a modality mismatch, forcing LLMs to extract temporal structure from raw numbers, which degrades on long sequences and incurs high computational cost. A better representation is needed that aligns time series with LLMs' native modalities.

Method: T2SP (Time-Series-to-Structured-Program) is a deterministic, training-free representation method. It decomposes a time series into three structural components — trends, periods, and salient events — and encodes them in a program-friendly symbolic format that resembles the textual/code modalities LLMs are natively trained on. Temporal-structure extraction is shifted from the model to the representation itself.

Result: Evaluated on three time-series reasoning tasks (editing, captioning, question answering), T2SP consistently outperforms raw-string representations: it improves task performance, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates.

Conclusion: T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs, enabling off-the-shelf models to leverage their pre-trained reasoning capabilities for time-series understanding without additional training.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning and instruction-following capabilities, making them potentially powerful tools for time-series analysis. However, time series lie outside their native textual modality, raising a fundamental question: how should time series be represented so that LLMs can reason about them effectively? Existing work typically serializes raw numerical sequences or fine-tunes pre-trained LLMs on time-series data. These approaches place the burden of extracting temporal structure directly on the LLM, creating a modality mismatch that often degrades performance on long sequences and introduces substantial computational overhead. In this work, we introduce Time-Series-to-Structured-Program representation (T2SP), a deterministic, training-free method that represents a time series as a structured symbolic program. T2SP decomposes time series into trends, periods, and salient events, expressing them in a program-friendly format aligned with the textual and code-like modalities on which LLMs are natively trained. By shifting temporal-structure extraction from the model to the representation itself, T2SP enables off-the-shelf LLMs to leverage their existing reasoning capabilities for time-series understanding. We evaluate T2SP on three reasoning tasks -- editing, captioning, and question answering -- where it consistently improves performance, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates compared with raw-string representations. Our results demonstrate that T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs.

</details>


### [202] [Scalable anomaly detection via a univariate Christoffel function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12483)
*Florian Grivet,Didier Henrion,Jean-Bernard Lasserre,Louise Travé-Massuyès*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes UCF, a univariate Christoffel function for anomaly detection that overcomes the exponential dimensionality scalability issue of traditional Christoffel function methods. By using squared distance between query and support points, UCF preserves key theoretical properties while achieving superior performance (Average Precision) against 14 baselines on ADBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Christoffel function-based anomaly detection methods are theoretically appealing but suffer from intractability in moderate-to-high dimensions due to exponential growth of the matrix requiring inversion. The paper aims to eliminate this scalability bottleneck while retaining the desirable theoretical properties (on-off support dichotomy, accurate support shape capture).

Method: The authors introduce UCF (Univariate Christoffel Function), which reformulates the Christoffel function in terms of the squared distance between a query point and the support points, reducing the problem to a univariate formulation that avoids exponential dimensional scaling.

Result: Extensive experiments on the ADBench benchmark show UCF consistently outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines in terms of Average Precision, demonstrating both effectiveness and universal applicability.

Conclusion: UCF successfully resolves the scalability bottleneck of Christoffel function-based anomaly detection, expanding the toolkit with a robust, theoretically grounded, and universally applicable approach suitable for real-world moderate-to-high dimensional datasets.

Abstract: Anomaly detection plays a critical role in identifying unusual patterns across domains such as fraud detection, network intrusion, and system fault diagnosis. Recently, Christoffel function-based methods, rooted in polynomial optimization, have emerged as promising alternatives to deep learning due to their strong mathematical foundations and computational frugality. However, their practical applicability is hindered by the need to invert a matrix whose size grows exponentially with the data dimension, rendering the method intractable even for moderate-dimensional datasets. This paper addresses the dimensionality limitations of Christoffel function-based anomaly detection while preserving its key theoretical properties, i.e., the on-off support dichotomy behavior and the accurate support shape capture. We introduce UCF, a univariate Christoffel function which is based on the squared distance between the query point and the support points. Extensive experiments on the ADBench benchmark demonstrate that UCF consistently outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines in terms of Average Precision. By resolving the scalability bottleneck of the Christoffel Function, this work expands the toolkit of anomaly detection methods with a robust, theoretically grounded, and universally applicable approach.

</details>


### [203] [Speculative Rollback Correction for Quality-Diverse Web Agent Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12485)
*Longkun Hao,Hongyu Lin,Hao Li,Zhichao Yang,Haojie Hao,Dongshuo Huang,Haitao Yang,Hongyu Ge,Ming jie Xie,Yanjun Wu,Zi Hao Yin,Yan Bai,Yihang Lou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SRC introduces a branch-level imitation framework that uses fixed-horizon speculative rollouts with teacher review at the first harmful deviation, coupled with a quality-diversity archive, to train interactive web agents more effectively than step-level or trajectory-level intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing imitation learning for web agents suffers from a critical timing problem: delayed expert correction allows cascading errors that make the state irrecoverable, while premature intervention causes over-reliance on a single rigid expert trajectory, trapping the agent in local optima. The authors seek a correction strategy that balances recovery with trajectory diversity.

Method: Speculative Rollback Correction (SRC) lets the student agent execute a short fixed-horizon speculative segment without teacher input. The teacher then reviews the segment and localizes the first harmful deviation, rolling back to reset the environment to just before that error. Useful prefix states are preserved. Successful rollouts (those passing a hard verifier) are retained in a lightweight quality-diversity archive. The collected data — both localized next-action corrections and verifier-passing trajectories — support next-action supervised fine-tuning (SFT).

Result: On the WebArena-Infinity benchmark, SRC collected 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples. The fixed-horizon branch review method demonstrated an improved recovery-versus-query tradeoff compared to step-level review, while preserving diverse verifier-passing solution variants.

Conclusion: SRC effectively addresses the intervention-timing dilemma in imitation learning for web agents by combining speculative execution, localized rollback, and diversity-preserving filtering, yielding substantial improvements in both data quality and agent performance on realistic web tasks.

Abstract: Training interactive web agents through imitation learning from expert trajectories has emerged as a highly effective approach. However, determining the optimal timing for expert intervention presents a critical challenge in this context. Delayed intervention often leads to the accumulation of early-stage errors, pushing the page state into an irrecoverable regime. Conversely, premature or excessive intervention causes the agent to become overly reliant on expert policies, trapping the model in local optima characterized by a single, rigid trajectory. We propose Speculative Rollback Correction (SRC), a branch-level imitation framework for resettable agent environments. Instead of requesting teacher labels at every visited state or correcting only after a completed trajectory, SRC uses fixed-horizon branch review: the student executes a short speculative segment before teacher review, and the teacher localizes the first harmful deviation only when local progress breaks. Rollback preserves useful prefixes, while successful rollouts are filtered by a hard verifier and retained in a lightweight quality-diversity archive. The resulting data supports next-action supervised fine-tuning on both localized corrections and verifier-passing trajectories. On WebArena-Infinity, SRC collects 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples; fixed-horizon review improves the recovery-versus-query tradeoff over step-level review while retaining verifier-passing solution variants. Code is available at https://github.com/LongkunHao/SRC_gui_agent.

</details>


### [204] [An Empirical Study on Predictive Maintenance for Component X in Heavy-Duty Scania Trucks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12486)
*Valeriu Dimidov,Sasan Jafarnejad,Raphaël Frank*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A PdM methodology that models wear as a monotonic time series, uses only recent observations transformed into tabular data for ML classification, and achieves cost reduction on the Scania Component X dataset versus SOTA while leveraging AutoML for simplified modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Implementing condition-based PdM for truck fleets is difficult due to massive data volumes, complex failure detection from sensor data, and the need for cost-effective trade-offs. The authors aim to address these challenges with a simplified yet effective approach.

Method: The methodology assumes component wear is a monotonically non-decreasing time series. It selects only the most recent observations, transforms them into tabular format, and applies ML classification models (including AutoML) designed for tabular data to predict maintenance needs.

Result: The proposed methodology achieves lower costs on the Scania Component X dataset compared to current state-of-the-art approaches while simplifying the modeling process through AutoML.

Conclusion: The condition-based PdM methodology is validated as both cost-effective and simpler to implement than SOTA methods, demonstrating that tabular transformation of recent monotonic wear data combined with AutoML can effectively address fleet PdM challenges.

Abstract: Condition-based Predictive Maintenance (PdM) for truck fleets has gained momentum in recent years. This maintenance strategy aims to minimize unplanned downtimes and reduce costs by monitoring the health status of vehicles and taking proactive action based on their condition. However, the implementation of condition-based PdM systems is challenging due to the large volume of data generated by the trucks, the inherent complexity of detecting failures through sensor data and the difficulties in finding cost-effective trade-offs in the solution's implementation. In this paper, we define and validate a condition-based PdM methodology built on the assumption that the wear-and-tear state of the monitored component can be represented as a monotonically non-decreasing time series. It involves selecting only the most recent observations from the time series and transforming them into a tabular format for classification using machine learning (ML) models designed for tabular data. Our results indicate that the proposed methodology reduces costs on the Scania Component X dataset compared to current state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches, while also simplifying the modeling process through AutoML.

</details>


### [205] [DynamicPTQ: Mitigating Activation Quantization Collapse via Residual-Stream Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12487)
*Zimo Zhao,Maolin Wang,Bowen Yu,Bowen Liu,Xiao Han,Xiangyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynamicPTQ addresses 4-bit activation quantization instability in LLMs by identifying that massive activations follow a phase-wise pattern across network depth. The method dynamically assigns 8-bit precision only to quantization-sensitive layers identified via residual-stream dynamics, while keeping weights, KV caches, and other activations at 4-bit, achieving improved perplexity and throughput with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PTQ methods use static transformation-based smoothing (orthogonal rotations, affine scaling) to handle massive activations but ignore cross-layer dynamics of the residual stream. The authors identify that massive activations emerge and disappear in phase-wise patterns, causing dynamic quantization instability that static methods cannot fully resolve.

Method: The authors introduce two metrics — Jump Ratio and Historical Feature SNR — to characterize cross-layer residual dynamics and quantization instability. Based on this analysis, they propose DynamicPTQ, a phase-aware mixed-precision PTQ policy that identifies quantization-sensitive layers from residual-stream dynamics and assigns 8-bit activation precision only to those layers, while keeping weights, KV caches, and other activations at 4-bit. It integrates directly with existing PTQ baselines (QuaRot, SpinQuant, FlatQuant).

Result: Experiments on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 under W4A4KV4 quantization show that DynamicPTQ consistently improves perplexity and zero-shot QA performance, while achieving 1.05–1.07× throughput improvement with modest memory overhead compared to full 4-bit baselines.

Conclusion: DynamicPTQ provides a practical path toward robust low-bit LLM inference by addressing the dynamic, phase-wise nature of massive activations through targeted mixed-precision allocation, rather than relying solely on static smoothing transformations.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is essential for efficient large language model inference, but reliably quantizing activations remains challenging when weights, activations, and KV caches are all quantized to 4-bit precision. A key difficulty lies in massive activations, whose extreme values dominate the activation range and amplify quantization errors. State-of-the-art methods mainly mitigate massive activations through transformation-based smoothing, such as orthogonal rotations and affine scaling, but overlook the cross-layer dynamics of the residual stream. In this paper, we show that massive activations emerge and disappear in a phase-wise pattern across network depth, triggering large residual changes. These changes cause newly injected layer-wise updates to dominate the 4-bit quantization scale and weaken historical residual information. To characterize this behavior, we introduce Jump Ratio and Historical Feature SNR. This suggests that static transformation-based smoothing cannot fully resolve dynamic quantization instability caused by cross-layer residual changes. Based on this analysis, we propose DynamicPTQ, a Dynamic Post-Training Quantization policy for phase-aware mixed-precision activation quantization. DynamicPTQ identifies quantization-sensitive layers from residual-stream dynamics and assigns 8-bit activation precision only to these layers, while keeping weights, KV caches, and other activations in 4-bit precision. It can be directly integrated with strong PTQ baselines such as QuaRot, SpinQuant, and FlatQuant. Experiments on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 show that DynamicPTQ consistently improves perplexity and zero-shot QA performance under W4A4KV4 quantization, while achieving 1.05 to 1.07 times throughput improvement with modest memory overhead. These results demonstrate a practical path toward robust low-bit LLM inference.

</details>


### [206] [A Stationary (and Therefore Compatible) Representation is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12488)
*Niccolò Biondi,Federico Pernici,Simone Ricci,Alberto Del Bimbo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learning stationary representations via d-Simplex fixed classifiers enables feature compatibility across model updates. The paper shows that combining cross-entropy with contrastive loss under a d-Simplex classifier captures higher-order dependencies and is equivalent to compatibility-constrained learning, achieving SOTA in retrieval during sequential model updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When feature extractors undergo updates, existing gallery embeddings must be recomputed, which is costly. Learning compatible representations allows interchangeable use of features across model versions, enabling uninterrupted retrieval services. However, prior methods using d-Simplex fixed classifiers with cross-entropy only align first-order statistics, failing to capture higher-order dependencies between representations across updates.

Method: The authors prove that stationary representations from d-Simplex fixed classifiers imply compatibility. To address higher-order dependency capture, they propose training with a convex combination of cross-entropy loss and contrastive loss under a d-Simplex fixed classifier. They also introduce a new evaluation scenario where a pre-trained model is sequentially fine-tuned and occasionally replaced with an improved model.

Result: The proposed hybrid loss (cross-entropy + contrastive) successfully captures higher-order dependencies, is proven equivalent to learning with cross-entropy under compatibility constraints, and achieves state-of-the-art performance. Stationary representations enable uninterrupted retrieval without reprocessing gallery images during model updates and replacements.

Conclusion: Stationary representations learned via d-Simplex fixed classifiers provide a principled foundation for representation compatibility. The convex combination of cross-entropy and contrastive losses effectively captures higher-order statistics, ensuring robust compatibility across sequential model updates and replacements in practical deployment scenarios.

Abstract: Learning compatible representations aims to learn feature representations that can be used interchangeably over time whenever a model undergoes updates. In this paper, we demonstrate that stationary representations learned by d-Simplex fixed classifiers imply compatibility as in its formal definition. This result establishes a foundation for future works and can be directly exploited in practical learning scenarios. We address the challenge of learning compatibility using $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers when the model is sequentially fine-tuned. Learning according to a d-Simplex fixed classifier with the cross-entropy loss aligns feature distributions at the first-order statistics. Consequently, it may not fully capture higher-order dependencies in the representation between model updates. To address this issue, we demonstrate that training the model using a $d$-Simplex fixed classifier through a convex combination of the cross-entropy loss and a contrastive loss not only captures higher-order dependencies, but is also equivalent to learning with the cross-entropy under the compatibility constraints. We confirm our findings with extensive experiments also considering a new scenario where a pre-trained model is sequentially fine-tuned and occasionally replaced with an improved model. We show that stationary representations enable uninterrupted retrieval services (without reprocessing gallery images) while improving performance during model updates and replacements, achieving state-of-the-art. Code at https://github.com/miccunifi/iamcl2r.

</details>


### [207] [Robustness Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks with Abstraction Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12490)
*Li-Jen Lin,Chih-Duo Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents an abstraction-refinement framework for verifying certified local robustness in RNNs. By partitioning pre-activation intervals where relaxation errors dominate, the method yields exact ReLU or tighter smooth-activation bounds on refined branches. A SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy controls splitting costs, improving verification success and margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines on MNIST and CIFAR10 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear relaxation errors in RNN verification propagate through recurrent connections and accumulate over time, making linear bound propagation methods overly conservative—especially when many pre-activation intervals cross zero. Existing scalable methods fail to certify truly robust inputs, motivating a refinement approach that targets the dominant sources of approximation error.

Method: An abstraction-refinement framework that partitions pre-activation intervals to eliminate dominant relaxation error. On refined branches, ReLU activations become exact, and smooth activations (tanh, sigmoid) receive tighter linear envelopes. To manage combinatorial explosion from splitting across long sequences, a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order.

Result: Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks show consistent improvements in verification success rates and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines. The approach also reveals clear runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

Conclusion: Abstraction-refinement with SHAP-guided timestep selection effectively balances precision and scalability in RNN robustness verification, significantly reducing the conservatism of bound propagation methods at manageable computational cost.

Abstract: Certified local robustness verification for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) is challenging because approximation errors introduced by nonlinear relaxations can propagate through recurrent connections and accumulate over time. As a result, scalable linear bound propagation methods often become overly conservative and fail to certify inputs that are in fact robust, especially when many pre-activation intervals cross zero. We propose an abstraction-refinement framework for RNN verification that partitions such intervals to remove the dominant relaxation error: on each refined branch, ReLU becomes exact, and smooth activations such as tanh and sigmoid admit substantially tighter linear envelopes. To control the combinatorial cost of splitting in long sequences, we introduce a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy that ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order. Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines, while exposing clear runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

</details>


### [208] [Net-Ev$^2$: A Generative Simulator for Network Event Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12494)
*Guangyu Wang,Zhaonan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Net-Ev² is a generative simulator that models how disturbance events propagate across networks by jointly leveraging event semantics and network topology through a two-stage framework: structure-guided masked pre-training and topology-aware diffusion with U-Net-like graph denoising.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches fail to simultaneously model both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of disturbance events, and inadequately capture topological structures when simulating how event impacts propagate across real-world networks.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) structure-guided masked pre-training, and (2) topology-aware diffusion process using U-Net-like graph downsampling/upsampling during denoising. At inference, only natural-language event input is required. The authors also introduce Net-Ev²-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark across four road networks, and JL-MMD, a topology-aware evaluation metric.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability compared to existing methods, validated on the newly introduced Net-Ev²-6.5M benchmark using the JL-MMD metric.

Conclusion: Net-Ev² effectively bridges event semantics and network topology for simulating disturbance propagation, offering a flexible, language-driven generative simulator with practical utility for decision-making in real-world networks.

Abstract: Reducing real-world trial and error has long been a central goal of decision making, and generative simulators advance this goal by modeling the evolution of future states. An even more challenging yet meaningful task is simulating how disturbance events (e.g., accidents) propagate their impacts across real-world networks. The existing approaches fall short of modeling both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of events, and capturing topological structures in simulating network event evolution. Therefore, we are motivated to propose Net-Ev$^2$ ($\underline{\textbf{Net}}$work $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$ent $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$olution), a novel generative simulator that jointly leverages event cues while preserving network topology in simulations. Specifically, the framework consists of two stages, namely structure-guided masked pre-training and topology-aware diffusion process, which is achieved by U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling during denoising. At inference time, Net-Ev$^2$ can generate simulations using natural-language event input only, with greater flexibility for practical usage. Furthermore, we introduce Net-Ev$^2$-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark of aligned event and network traffic data across four large-scale road networks, as well as a new topology-aware metric, namely JL-MMD, to evaluate topological fidelity in generated network dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability of Net-Ev$^2$. Code is made available at https://github.com/Guangyu4/Net-Ev-2.

</details>


### [209] [$μ$VLA: On Recurrent Memory for Partially Observable Manipulation in VLA Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12497)
*Egor Cherepanov,Nikita Kachaev,Daniil Zelezetsky,Aydar Bulatov,Artem Pshenitsyn,Yuri Kuratov,Alexey Skrynnik,Aleksandr I. Panov,Alexey K. Kovalev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: μVLA introduces minimal recurrence into a pretrained VLA model by carrying a small set of learnable memory tokens through self-attention, isolating recurrence's contribution. It achieves large gains under partial observability (0.42→0.84 on training tasks) while preserving full-observability performance, revealing when recurrence alone suffices and when additional memory structure is needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior memory-augmented VLAs conflate recurrence with retrieval, compression, auxiliary losses, and architectural changes, obscuring recurrence's individual contribution. This work isolates recurrence to understand its capability envelope under partial observability.

Method: The authors augment a pretrained OpenVLA-OFT transformer with a small set of learnable memory tokens propagated across timesteps and updated via self-attention. Training uses end-to-end truncated backpropagation through time (TBPTT) with no auxiliary losses. Three factors are parameterized: memory width m, TBPTT length K, and the update rule (gradient-based vs. detached EMA). The resulting μVLA family ensures recurrence is the only varying factor.

Result: On MIKASA-Robo, μVLA improves average success rate on five training tasks from 0.42 to 0.84 (strongest setting) and on held-out tasks from 0.07 to 0.23 with matching memory structure. On tasks requiring different memory structure, performance stays near baseline. On LIBERO (full observability), the strongest variant reaches 96.2%, showing no regression.

Conclusion: Minimal in-backbone recurrence is sufficient for substantial gains under partial observability when memory structure matches task demands, but additional structure is needed for mismatched tasks. The study calibrates the capability envelope of recurrence alone in VLA models.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models predict chunks of future actions from the current observation, an assumption that fails under partial observability, where decisions depend on information no longer visible. Existing memory-augmented VLAs simultaneously introduce recurrence, retrieval, compression modules, auxiliary objectives, hierarchical memory, or task-specific architectural changes, so the contribution of recurrence itself remains entangled with surrounding machinery. We present a controlled isolation study of recurrence in a strong pretrained VLA backbone. Our formulation augments the transformer with a small set of learnable memory tokens carried across timesteps and updated through self-attention, trained end to end with truncated backpropagation through time, with no auxiliary losses and no architectural changes. We instantiate this as $μ$VLA, a family of OpenVLA-OFT variants parameterized by memory width m, TBPTT length K, and the memory update rule (cross-step gradients or a detached EMA), so that recurrence is the only varying factor. On MIKASA-Robo, $μ$VLA improves average success rate on five training tasks from 0.42 to 0.84 at the strongest setting and reaches 0.23 on held-out tasks with the same memory structure versus 0.07 for the memoryless baseline. On tasks requiring different memory structure, performance remains near baseline. On LIBERO, the strongest recurrent variant achieves 96.2% average success, indicating no regression under full observability. We interpret these results as a calibration of the capability envelope of minimal in-backbone recurrence, identifying the regime in which it is sufficient and the regime where additional memory structure is required. Demos and videos can be found in https://avanturist322.github.io/mu-vla/.

</details>


### [210] [Improving Crash Frequency Prediction from Simulated Traffic Conflicts Using Machine Learning Based Microsimulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12500)
*Xian Liu,Carlo G. Prato,Gustav Markkula*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether machine learning-based driving behavior models improve crash frequency prediction from microsimulation compared to traditional rule-based models. Using surrogate safety measures and Extreme Value Theory at five Leeds intersections, the ML model produced crash predictions consistent with real-world data without location-specific calibration, whereas the rule-based model failed to yield meaningful predictions. However, directly using ML-generated simulated crashes performed poorly, indicating current ML models capture conflict dynamics well but not actual crash generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional microsimulation-based safety studies rely on simplified rule-based behavior models that, while reproducing macroscopic traffic flow, fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, thereby limiting crash prediction accuracy. The emergence of ML-based behavior models trained on large-scale trajectory data offers a potential solution, but their efficacy for surrogate safety analysis remains unvalidated.

Method: Traffic microsimulation was conducted at five signalized intersections in Leeds, UK, using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML driving behavior model. Simulated vehicle trajectories were analyzed with a two-dimensional Time-to-Collision (TTC) metric to identify conflicts. Extreme Value Theory (EVT) was then applied to model these conflicts and predict crash frequency. Predictions were compared against real-world crash data. Additionally, directly using ML-generated simulated crashes for prediction was tested.

Result: The ML model's conflicts produced crash frequency predictions aligned with real-world crash data, whereas the rule-based model failed to yield meaningful predictions—likely due to lack of intersection-specific calibration. Directly using ML-generated simulated crashes to predict real-world crash frequency yielded poor results, indicating ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts but not yet crashes.

Conclusion: ML-based behavior models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts without requiring location-specific calibration. However, further development is needed before ML models can directly generate realistic crash events. The findings suggest clear future directions for ML-based traffic microsimulation in safety analysis.

Abstract: Traffic microsimulation combined with surrogate safety measures has increasingly been used as a proactive alternative to historical crash data for predicting crash frequency for current or planned road infrastructure designs. However, existing microsimulation-based safety studies have adopted simplified rule-based behaviour models, which reproduce traffic flow reasonably well but often fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, limiting crash prediction accuracy. Recent advances in machine learning (ML)-based behaviour models offer a promising opportunity to potentially improve microsimulation realism and crash frequency predictions by learning human driving behaviour directly from large-scale trajectory datasets. To investigate this possibility, traffic microsimulation was conducted for five real-world signalised intersections in Leeds, UK, using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML model. Simulated vehicle trajectories were analysed using a two-dimensional Time-to-Collision metric to identify simulated conflicts, which were then modelled using Extreme Value Theory to predict crash frequency. Results show that conflicts from the ML model yielded crash predictions in line with the real-world crash data, whereas the rule-based model did not permit meaningful predictions, presumably due to a lack of model calibration to the specific simulated intersections. Directly using ML-generated simulated crashes to predict real-world crash frequency also yielded poor results, suggesting that while current ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts, they are not yet able to generate realistic crashes. Overall, the findings demonstrate that ML-based behaviour models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts, without a need for location-specific model calibration, and suggest clear future directions for ML-based traffic microsimulation.

</details>


### [211] [Policy-driven Conformal Prediction for Trustworthy QoT Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12501)
*Kiarash Rezaei,Omran Ayoub,Paolo Monti,Carlos Natalino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal QoT integrates conformal prediction with operational policies to deliver statistically guaranteed quality-of-transmission estimates for lightpath feasibility, boosting accuracy from 92% to 99.6% even under domain shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optical network QoT estimation models degrade under domain shift (e.g., varying network conditions, equipment), and standard ML approaches lack statistical reliability guarantees, risking erroneous lightpath provisioning decisions.

Method: A policy-driven conformal prediction framework that wraps around any base QoT estimator, producing prediction sets with formal finite-sample coverage guarantees. Operational decision policies map these prediction sets to feasibility decisions, ensuring reliability under distributional shift.

Result: The framework improves lightpath-feasibility prediction accuracy from 92% to 99.6% on open datasets while maintaining the nominal statistical coverage guarantee, even under domain shift conditions.

Conclusion: Conformal QoT provides a principled and practical solution for reliable QoT estimation in optical networks, combining formal statistical guarantees with flexible operational policies to bridge the gap between ML-based estimation and real-world deployment robustness.

Abstract: We propose Conformal QoT, a policy-driven framework that combines statistically guaranteed QoT estimation with operational decision policies, enabling reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions under domain shift and improving accuracy from 92\% to 99.6\% on open datasets.

</details>


### [212] [Dolph2Vec: Self-Supervised Representations of Dolphin Vocalizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12503)
*Chiara Semenzin,Faadil Mustun,Roberto Dessi,Pierre Orhan,Alexis Emanuelli,Yair Lakretz,Gonzalo de Polavieja,German Sumbre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dolph2Vec is a species-specific self-supervised model (Wav2Vec2.0 adapted) trained on five years of dolphin vocalization recordings from five known individuals. It outperforms general-purpose baselines on signature whistle classification and whistle detection, while learned embeddings reveal interpretable acoustic units corresponding to whistle categories and sub-whistle structure, demonstrating SSL's dual role as both a model and a scientific tool for animal communication research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SSL models in bioacoustics prioritize broad cross-species generalization but fail to capture the fine-grained structure of individual species' communication systems. There is a need for species-specific SSL models that can uncover interpretable acoustic units and enable hypothesis-driven exploration of animal communication.

Method: The authors collected a novel longitudinal dataset spanning over five years from five known dolphins in a semi-naturalistic marine environment. They adapted the Wav2Vec2.0 architecture to this domain, training Dolph2Vec — the first large-scale SSL model trained exclusively on dolphin data. The model was benchmarked on two biologically relevant downstream tasks: signature whistle classification and whistle detection.

Result: Dolph2Vec significantly outperformed general-purpose baselines on both signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Analysis of learned embeddings and the codebook structure revealed interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and potentially sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained communication pattern analysis.

Conclusion: Species-specific SSL can serve a dual purpose: as a high-performing model for bioacoustic tasks and as a scientific instrument for generating and exploring hypotheses about animal communication systems. Dolph2Vec demonstrates that focusing on a single species yields richer, more interpretable representations than general-purpose models.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has opened new opportunities in bioacoustics by enabling scalable modeling of animal vocalizations without the need for expensive manual annotation. However, current SSL models in this domain prioritize broad generalization across species and are not optimized for uncovering the fine-grained structure of individual communication systems. In this work, we collect and release a novel dataset of over five years of longitudinal recordings, from five known dolphins in a semi-naturalistic marine environment, an unprecedented resource for studying dolphin communication. We adapt the Wav2Vec2.0 Baevski et al. (2020) architecture to this domain and introduce Dolph2Vec, the first large-scale, species-specific SSL model trained exclusively on this data. We benchmark our model on two biologically relevant tasks: signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general-purpose baselines in both tasks. Beyond performance, we show that learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possibly sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained analysis of communication patterns. Our findings demonstrate how SSL can serve as both a model and a scientific tool to explore hypotheses in animal communication research.

</details>


### [213] [Boosting Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12505)
*Pengwei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DPOP extends DPO with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses, activated only when the policy still prefers the rejected response over the chosen one. It achieves +5.3% and +4.4% LC win-rate gains over baselines on AlpacaEval 2.0.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DPO and its pairwise variants only use chosen and rejected responses from static datasets, ignoring a valuable signal: the response the reference model itself would generate. The authors hypothesize that penalizing these reference-greedy responses can improve alignment, particularly when the current policy has not yet learned to rank chosen above rejected responses.

Method: DPOP augments the DPO loss with a gated penalty term applied to reference-greedy responses (i.e., responses the reference model assigns highest likelihood to). Crucially, a gating mechanism activates this penalty only when the current policy assigns lower likelihood to the preferred response than to the rejected response, preventing unnecessary regularization when preference ordering is already correct. An ablation also studies a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty variant.

Result: On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled (LC) win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on both Llama-3-8b-it (5.3% relative gain) and Gemma-2-9b-it (4.4% relative gain). Ablations show that a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty outperforms NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

Conclusion: DPOP demonstrates that leveraging reference-greedy responses as an additional negative signal — with careful gating — yields consistent and meaningful improvements over existing offline preference optimization baselines, offering a simple yet effective extension to DPO.

Abstract: Offline preference optimization has become a practical substitute for reinforcement learning from human feedback, but pairwise objectives such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its variants use only the chosen and rejected responses stored in a static dataset. This leaves a useful signal unused: the response that the reference model itself would generate for the same prompt. We propose Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization (DPOP), a simple extension of DPO that augments the base preference loss with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses. DPOP activates this penalty only when the current policy still assigns a lower likelihood to the preferred response than to the rejected response. On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on both Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, achieving relative gains of 5.3\% and 4.4\% over baselines on the two models, respectively. Ablations further show that a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty is stronger than NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

</details>


### [214] [Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation: Post-Training Without Rubric Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12507)
*MohammadHossein Rezaei,Anas Mahmoud,Zihao Wang,Utkarsh Tyagi,Advait Gosai,Razvan-Gabriel Dumitru,Aakash Sabharwal,Bing Liu,Yunzhong He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGSD is a verifier-free method that distills rubric-conditioned teacher policies into unconditioned student policies token-by-token, replacing sparse LLM-judge rewards with dense per-token signals while matching judge-based GRPO performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rubric-based RLVR methods require an LLM verifier to score rollouts, which incurs high training-time overhead, introduces verifier-specific biases, and reduces rich rubric feedback to a single sparse end-of-trajectory signal. The authors aim to eliminate the verifier bottleneck entirely.

Method: RGSD conditions the base policy on a rubric (teacher) and distills its token-level distribution into an unconditioned policy (student) via self-distillation. This provides dense, per-token learning signals directly from the rubric-conditioned distribution, removing the need for any training-time LLM judge. Only one on-policy rollout per prompt is required.

Result: Across Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) on medical and science domains, RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction on par with judge-based GRPO while using far less compute. Ablations show raw rubrics outperform self-generated references as teacher enrichment, but a strong GRPO judge can surpass RGSD in some settings.

Conclusion: RGSD is a complementary, verifier-free alternative to judge-based rubric training. It is particularly suitable when verifier cost or reliability is the bottleneck, though stronger verifier-based methods may still be preferred when compute and reliable judges are available.

Abstract: Rubrics have emerged as an alternative to RLVR in open-ended domains where a single ground-truth final answer is not available. Existing rubric-based training methods rely on an LLM verifier that scores each rollout against rubrics. This introduces substantial training-time overhead, exposes optimization to verifier-specific biases, and reduces rubric feedback to a sparse end-of-trajectory signal. We propose Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD), a verifier-free training method in which the base policy, conditioned on the rubric, serves as the teacher for the unconditioned student. RGSD distills the rubric-conditioned teacher distribution into the student token-by-token, replacing sparse trajectory-level rewards with dense per-token learning signals and removing the LLM judge from the training loop entirely. Across Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) models on medical and science domains, RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based GRPO while using one on-policy rollout per prompt and no training-time verifier calls. Ablations show that raw rubrics provide a stronger teacher enrichment signal than self-generated reference responses, while a stronger GRPO judge can outperform RGSD in some settings, positioning RGSD as a complementary verifier-free alternative when verifier cost or reliability is the bottleneck.

</details>


### [215] [Crossing the Validation Crisis: Cross-Validation Reduces Benchmarking Variance Surprisingly Well](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12552)
*Célestin Eve,Gaël Varoquaux,Thomas Moreau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-validation with multiple splits substantially improves confidence in evaluating and comparing learning algorithms. The paper introduces "sample gain" to quantify virtual data augmentation from multiple splits and proposes an early-stopping procedure for cross-validation based on diminishing returns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern ML benchmarking suffers from a validation crisis: performance estimation is unreliable due to statistical variability from limited test samples and stochastic algorithms, making it difficult to discern genuine advances.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of "sample gain," which quantifies the virtual data augmentation from multiple cross-validation splits in reducing benchmarking variance. They also propose a dynamic early-stopping procedure that estimates from the first few folds whether subsequent folds will yield large sample gains, avoiding unnecessary computation.

Result: Experiments on synthetic data, histopathologic scans, and NLP fine-tuning show that multiple cross-validation splits substantially improve reliability and stability of performance estimates. Diminishing returns often set in later than expected, and the early-stopping procedure effectively identifies when further splits are no longer beneficial.

Conclusion: Cross-validation on available samples should be pushed further than common practice to achieve robust benchmarking. The sample gain framework and early-stopping procedure provide principled tools for determining when enough splits have been performed.

Abstract: Modern machine learning progresses through empirical work, benchmarking new methods to evaluate relative performance. However, the statistical variability inherent to evaluation - exacerbated by the stochastic nature of many algorithms - often makes performance estimation unreliable due to the limited test samples available, leading to a validation crisis in which genuine advances are difficult to discern. In this work, we show that cross-validation improves markedly confidence when evaluating and comparing learning algorithm performances. We introduce the concept of sample gain, which quantifies the virtual data augmentation achieved by using multiple cross-validation splits to reduce benchmarking variance. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets (histopathologic scans and NLP fine-tuning) demonstrate that multiple splits can substantially improve the reliability and stability of performance estimates, with diminishing returns often setting in later than expected. We also introduce a procedure to dynamically early-stop cross-validation by estimating from the first few folds if subsequent folds will bring large sample gains. Our findings highlight the value of pushing cross-validation on available samples to achieve robust and reliable benchmarking.

</details>


### [216] [Emerging Flexible Designs for Geospatial Multimodal Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12595)
*Philipe Dias,Waqwoya Abebe,Abhishek Potnis,Aristeidis Tsaris,Dan Lu,Xiao Wang,Dalton Lunga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An apples-to-apples comparison of foundation model architectures for geospatial multimodal reasoning, standardizing pretraining and evaluation to reveal design trade-offs in flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for Earth observation exhibit substantial architectural diversity (encoder-only, encoder-decoder, masked autoencoding), making consistent performance comparisons difficult. There is a need for rigorous, controlled benchmarking to guide architecture selection for multimodal geospatial reasoning.

Method: The authors standardize pretraining by using identical self-supervised learning objectives and training datasets across multiple leading FM architectures, all under consistent parameterization. Models are evaluated on the GEOBench benchmark across both classification and segmentation tasks, with a focus on flexibility across varied spectral band configurations.

Result: The controlled comparison yields new insights into how architectural choices affect model flexibility, modality alignment capability, and downstream task performance. Specific architectural strengths and limitations are identified under standardized conditions.

Conclusion: The study provides practical guidance for designing next-generation geospatial foundation models, emphasizing that architecture selection involves key trade-offs between flexibility, modality alignment, and task performance that must be carefully balanced for robust multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: Foundation models are rapidly transforming Earth observation by enabling scalable pretraining across diverse unlabeled geospatial modalities. However, their architectural diversity ranging from encoder-only to encoder-decoder and masked autoencoding paradigms makes it challenging to assess performance trade offs in a consistent manner. In this work, we present an apples-to-apples comparison of leading FM architectures designed for geospatial multimodal reasoning, with a particular focus on flexibility across varied spectral band configurations. We standardize pretraining using identical self supervised learning objectives and training datasets, and evaluate all models under consistent parameterization on the GEOBench benchmark across classification and segmentation tasks. Our results offer new insights into the design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. By highlighting architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions, this study provides practical guidance for building next generation geospatial foundation models capable of robust multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [217] [Viral Proteins Reveal Geometry of Protein Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12609)
*Arthur Bigot,Harmon Bhasin,Core Francisco Park,Eugene Shakhnovich,Dianzhuo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Protein language models (pLMs) trained on imbalanced datasets exhibit a dominant "nativeness axis" in embedding space, ordering sequences from cellular to viral to random. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families, yet viral-specific signal persists, enabling linear separability beyond what perplexity or shallow features capture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how protein language models represent underrepresented biological sequences (viral proteins) given the highly imbalanced nature of their training data.

Method: Using viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, the authors analyze embedding space structure through the lens of masked reconstruction perplexity, nativeness axis alignment, scaling behavior, and linear separability experiments comparing pLM embeddings against zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features.

Result: Key findings: (1) A dominant nativeness axis emerges in embedding space aligned with perplexity, ordering sequences from cellular → viral → shuffled/random. (2) Model scaling contracts this axis non-uniformly across viral families. (3) Viral proteins remain linearly separable from cellular proteins in embedding space, beyond what is captured by perplexity or shallow features alone.

Conclusion: pLM representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while simultaneously preserving information specific to distinct biological groups, suggesting that underrepresentation does not eliminate group-specific signal in learned embeddings.

Abstract: Protein language models are trained on highly imbalanced datasets, raising the question of how they represent underrepresented biological sequences. Using viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, we identify a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space, aligned with masked reconstruction perplexity, that orders sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins through viral proteins to shuffled and random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families. Despite this, protein language model embeddings retain viral-specific signal: viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features. Together, these results suggest that pLM representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while preserving information specific to distinct biological groups.

</details>


### [218] [The Mathematics of AI Winters: The mathematical Taxonomy of Paradigm Fragility in AI Winter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12610)
*Miquel Noguer i Alonso,David Pacheco Aznar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that the first and second AI winters were not solely caused by engineering failures and inflated expectations, but also by genuine formal mathematical barriers—including limits in representation, optimization, complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation—that aligned with central disappointments of early AI. These barriers were later mitigated, not eliminated, by subsequent breakthroughs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To complement the standard narrative that AI winters resulted from engineering shortcomings and commercial hype by demonstrating that dominant paradigms of those periods also encountered provable formal/mathematical bottlenecks, thereby offering a more structurally grounded explanation for the slowdowns in AI research.

Method: A synthetic (non-archival) analysis that maps key historical disappointments in AI onto mathematically precise bottlenecks: Minsky & Papert's perceptron impossibility results, Blum & Rivest's NP-hardness of neural network training, Stone's minimax rates for high-dimensional nonparametric estimation, vanishing-gradient analyses by Hochreiter and Bengio et al., and statistical learning theory from Vapnik-Chervonenkis, Valiant, and Blumer et al.

Result: The paper identifies that each major early-AI disappointment had a corresponding formal barrier: perceptron limitations (XOR, parity), computational intractability of training, curse of dimensionality in nonparametric learning, and vanishing gradients in deep networks. It then traces how later advances—deep architectures, ReLU activations, convolutional networks, SGD, and large datasets—mitigated rather than eliminated these barriers.

Conclusion: The AI winters cannot be fully understood without acknowledging the formal mathematical barriers that blocked progress under then-dominant paradigms. The subsequent resurgence of AI was enabled by innovations that worked around these barriers, though the barriers themselves remain theoretically relevant.

Abstract: Two major periods of reduced funding and confidence in artificial intelligence research, commonly called the first and second AI winters, are usually explained through engineering failure, commercial disappointment, and inflated expectations. This article develops a complementary thesis: that the dominant paradigms of those periods also met genuine formal barriers, including limitations of representation, optimisation, computational complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation. The contribution is synthetic rather than archival. We do not claim that particular theorems mechanically caused the winters; rather, we show that several central disappointments of early AI were aligned with mathematically precise bottlenecks. We analyse these bottlenecks through the perceptron impossibility results of Minsky and Papert, the complexity-theoretic hardness of exact neural-network training established by Blum and Rivest, minimax rates for nonparametric estimation in high dimension due to Stone, vanishing-gradient analyses by Hochreiter and by Bengio and collaborators, and classical statistical learning theory in the tradition of Vapnik and Chervonenkis, Valiant, and Blumer and collaborators. We then relate these barriers to the later breakthroughs that mitigated, rather than eliminated, them.

</details>


### [219] [Evaluation of AutoML Frameworks for IDS under Imbalanced Data Conditions of the NSL-KDD Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12611)
*Wiliane Carolina Silva,Evandro César Vilas Boas,Felipe A. P. de Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evaluates nine open-source AutoML frameworks on the NSL-KDD dataset under full five-class imbalance. PyCaret achieved best macro-F1 (66%); frameworks without native imbalance handling degraded significantly on rare attacks (R2L, U2R). Establishes a standardized benchmark and advocates for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization in AutoML pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior AutoML studies for network intrusion detection simplify the problem via binary classification or removal of minority classes, leading to unrealistic and overly optimistic assessments. Real-world IDS data exhibits severe multiclass imbalance with rare but critical attack types (R2L, U2R), necessitating a rigorous, realistic evaluation of how AutoML frameworks handle imbalance-sensitive learning.

Method: Nine open-source AutoML frameworks (including PyCaret, AutoGluon, Auto-Sklearn, TPOT, H2O, etc.) are evaluated on the NSL-KDD dataset preserving the original five-class distribution. A unified, reproducible experimental protocol is applied, analyzing architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and native imbalance-handling mechanisms. Primary metric: macro-F1 score.

Result: PyCaret achieved the highest macro-F1 (66%), followed by AutoGluon (55%). Frameworks with ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization yielded superior minority-class discrimination. Accuracy-oriented optimization proved insufficient: high accuracy coexisted with poor generalization on R2L and U2R attacks. Frameworks lacking native balancing support showed marked degradation on rare classes.

Conclusion: AutoML frameworks require native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation strategies to be viable for real-world IDS deployment. The work provides a standardized benchmark for future research and highlights current architectural limitations in automated learning pipelines under severe multiclass imbalance.

Abstract: This work investigates the impact of severe class imbalance on the performance of automated machine learning (AutoML) frameworks for multiclass network intrusion detection using the NSL-KDD dataset. Unlike previous studies that simplify the problem through binary classification or minority-class removal, we preserve the original five-class distribution, including highly underrepresented attacks such as R2L and U2R, enabling a realistic evaluation of imbalance-sensitive learning behavior. Nine open-source AutoML frameworks were analyzed under a unified and reproducible experimental protocol, considering differences in architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and imbalance-handling mechanisms. The results demonstrate that frameworks incorporating ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization achieve better minority-class discrimination. PyCaret obtained the best overall performance, reaching 66\% macro-F1, followed by AutoGluon with 55\%, whereas frameworks lacking native balancing support exhibited significant degradation in minority-class detection capability. The analysis further shows that accuracy-oriented optimization alone is insufficient for highly imbalanced IDS scenarios, since high-weighted metrics may coexist with poor generalization on rare attack categories. As a contribution, this work establishes a standardized benchmark for AutoML-based intrusion detection under severe multiclass imbalance, highlighting current architectural limitations and the need for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation strategies into automated learning pipelines. The source code is publicly available.

</details>


### [220] [Fed-FBD: Federated Functional Block Diversification for Isolation, Privacy, and Surgical Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12679)
*Weijie Chen,Alan B. McMillan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fed-FBD proposes a modular federated learning architecture that decomposes a ResNet into independently tracked functional blocks stored in a color-coded warehouse, enabling adversarial isolation, privacy-by-design, and surgical machine unlearning at sub-second cost, with only modest accuracy trade-offs compared to FedAvg.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard federated learning (FedAvg) treats clients as black boxes, lacking mechanisms for isolating adversarial contributors, auditing per-client influence, and supporting the right to be forgotten. These are critical gaps for privacy-sensitive domains like healthcare where data integrity, traceability, and regulatory compliance (e.g., GDPR) are essential.

Method: Fed-FBD decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (stem, four residual groups, classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants. Each color is assembled from independently tracked, contributor-stamped blocks, providing architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation. This design inherently limits contamination to an adversarial client's own blocks, provides privacy-by-design (membership inference advantage indistinguishable from random chance without additional privacy mechanisms), and enables surgical unlearning by removing specific blocks without retraining.

Result: On six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST at 224×224, and CIFAR-10, Fed-FBD shows a modest 0.3%–3.1% IID accuracy gap on adequately sized datasets compared to FedAvg. Under Dirichlet(α=1.0) non-IID settings, it remains within 0.8%–4.0% of FedAvg on three of four datasets. Six adversarial attacks were confined to the poisoned client's own blocks with at most ±0.01 AUC drift on clean colors.

Conclusion: Fed-FBD successfully trades a small accuracy gap for three important guarantees absent in FedAvg: block-level adversarial isolation, inherent privacy-by-design against membership inference, and sub-second surgical machine unlearning. The approach is particularly suited for medical federated learning where traceability and data governance are paramount.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw patient data, but standard approaches such as FedAvg treat each client as a black box and provide no mechanism for isolating an adversarial contributor, auditing per-client influence, or honoring a departed participant's right to be forgotten. We present Fed-FBD (Federated Functional Block Diversification), a modular federated architecture that decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (the stem, four residual groups, and the classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants, each assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks. Fed-FBD provides three capabilities absent in FedAvg: (i) architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation, so that an adversarial or mislabelled client cannot contaminate the clean colous; (ii) privacy-by-design, where membership inference advantage is already indistinguishable from chance before any privacy mechanism is applied; and (iii) surgical machine unlearning of a departed participant's contribution at sub-second cost and without retraining. Experiments on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST at 224x224, and CIFAR-10 show that Fed-FBD trades a modest 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap on the adequately sized datasets for these guarantees, remains within 0.8%-4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet alpha=1.0 on three of four datasets, and confines all six adversarial attacks we study to the poisoned client's own blocks with at most +/-0.01 AUC drift on the clean colors.

</details>


### [221] [Towards Provably Fair Machine Learning: Bayesian Approaches For Consistent and Transparent Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12615)
*Owen O'Neill,Fintan Costello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML classifiers produce inconsistent predictions across granular subgroups, disproportionately harming minorities. The paper derives a Fair Bayesian classifier that enforces determinism and statistical consistency across all subgroups, abstaining when no consistent prediction is possible. It achieves zero consistency error while exceeding baseline accuracy on three benchmark datasets, providing a principled fairness foundation grounded in frequentist inference reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes ML classifiers exhibit systematic prediction quality variation across granular subgroups (intersections of multiple features). Regularization exacerbates this by collapsing small subgroups into larger ones, disproportionately impacting demographic minorities. Predictions for small subgroups are often statistically inconsistent with observed data, yet standard fairness approaches do not directly address this inference problem rooted in the unreliability of frequentist inference at small sample sizes.

Method: The authors formalize two requirements: (1) determinism—identical individuals receive identical predictions, and (2) statistical consistency—for each subgroup, one cannot reject (at significance level α) the hypothesis that predictions were drawn from the Bayesian optimal target distribution inferred for that subgroup. From these, they derive the Fair Bayesian classifier, which uses Bayesian inference to estimate subgroup-specific target distributions and enforces both requirements simultaneously across all subgroups. The classifier abstains when no consistent deterministic prediction is possible, rather than outputting unreliable predictions.

Result: On Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing datasets, standard classifiers produced statistically inconsistent predictions for a substantial proportion of subgroups. The Fair Bayesian classifier achieved zero consistency error by construction while exceeding baseline accuracy and multicalibration on every dataset tested.

Conclusion: Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. Addressing the inference unreliability in small subgroups—where minority demographics are disproportionately concentrated—is a necessary step toward fair ML. The Fair Bayesian classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is practically achievable at the finest resolution the data supports.

Abstract: ML classifiers deployed in high-stakes domains produce predictions whose quality varies systematically across subgroups. For granular subgroups defined by intersections of multiple features, predictions are often inconsistent with the observed data: the model's outputs contradict the evidence available for that subgroup. This problem is exacerbated by regularisation, which improves aggregate performance by collapsing small subgroups into larger groups, disproportionately affecting demographic minorities. We define two requirements for consistent prediction: determinism (identical individuals receive identical predictions) and statistical consistency (we cannot reject, at significance level alpha, the hypothesis that the predictions for a subgroup were drawn from the Bayesian optimal target distribution inferred for that subgroup). From these requirements we derive the Fair Bayesian classifier, which enforces both across every group and subgroup simultaneously and abstains whenever no consistent deterministic prediction is possible. On three benchmark datasets (Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing), standard classifiers produce statistically inconsistent predictions for a substantial proportion of subgroups. Our classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction while exceeding baseline accuracy and multicalibration on every dataset tested. Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. Minority demographics are disproportionately concentrated in small subgroups, precisely where frequentist inference is least reliable; addressing this inference problem is therefore a necessary step toward fair ML. By enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest resolution the data supports, the our classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is achievable in practice.

</details>


### [222] [Bag of Dims: Training-Free Mechanistic Interpretability via Dimension-Level Sign Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12629)
*Varun Reddy Nalagatla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer hidden state dimensions function as independent binary semantic registers, where sign encodes feature presence and magnitude encodes confidence. This "Bag of Dims" property enables training-free feature discovery, reading, and interpretation across the full compute pathway (hidden states, attention projections, FFN weights) in multiple model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing assumption in mechanistic interpretability that features are represented in superposition across arbitrarily rotated directions, requiring expensive dictionary learning (e.g., sparse autoencoders) to recover. The authors hypothesize that the standard neuron basis itself may already encode semantically meaningful, independently interpretable features.

Method: Four progressive experiments across Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, and Mistral 7B: (1) Sign-only decoding — replacing all activation magnitudes with unity and measuring next-token prediction accuracy; (2) Semantic category discovery — using a single-token type cache (one forward pass per vocabulary token) with per-dimension sign consistency to discover categories from 50 anchor tokens; (3) Attention space validation — extending discovery to K and V projections; (4) FFN writer neuron linking — inspecting static FFN weights to connect features to individual output neurons, plus fully unsupervised discovery scaling to 1500 features.

Result: Sign patterns alone achieve 72–93% top-5 next-token accuracy through the LM head; pure Hamming scoring reaches 80–90% top-4096. Per-dimension sign consistency discovers 175 semantic categories (mean AUC 0.80) with zero training; adding a trained probe yields only +0.018 AUC and converges to axis-aligned weights. All 175 categories remain discoverable in K/V projections. Static FFN weight inspection links 20% of features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement vs. 0% random). Top-200 neuron coalitions achieve >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote. Unsupervised discovery scales to 1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity, with pairwise MI of 0.0014 bits confirming near-independence of dimensions.

Conclusion: The standard neuron basis already constitutes a sufficient, training-free feature basis spanning the entire transformer compute pathway. This calls into question the necessity of expensive superposition-based methods like sparse autoencoders for feature extraction, given that axis-aligned, independent binary registers appear to be a fundamental architectural property rather than an artifact of specific models or training regimes.

Abstract: We show that the standard basis of transformer hidden states already provides a training-free, architecture-general feature basis. Individual dimensions encode semantic content via their signs and confidence via their magnitudes, functioning as independent binary registers. We validate this Bag of Dims framework across three model families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B) through four progressive experiments. Sign patterns alone carry predictive content: replacing all magnitudes with unity achieves 72-93% top-5 next-token accuracy through the LM head, and pure Hamming scoring without any decoder reaches 80-90% top-4096. These sign patterns organize into semantic features: using a single-token type cache (one forward pass per vocabulary token, no context), we discover 175 categories via per-dimension sign consistency (mean AUC 0.80) from 50 anchors with zero training. A trained probe adds only +0.018 AUC and converges to axis-aligned weights, confirming negligible cross-dimension structure. This structure extends to attention: all 175 categories remain discoverable in K and V projections. On the write side, static FFN weight inspection links 20% of features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement; random controls: 0%), with top-200 neuron coalitions achieving >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote. Fully unsupervised discovery (random seeds, no labels) scales to 1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity across all three models, with pairwise MI of 0.0014 bits confirming low inter-dimension coupling. These results establish that the standard basis already suffices for feature reading throughout the transformer compute pathway, requiring no training, no optimization, and no GPU-days beyond a single forward pass per vocabulary token.

</details>


### [223] [Detecting Functional Memorization in Code Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12764)
*Matthieu Meeus,Anil Ramakrishna,Matthew Grange,Zheng Xu,Luca Melis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates functional memorization in LLMs for code — where models reproduce functional logic without verbatim textual overlap. Using a counterfactual setup with Olmo-3-32B (midtrained vs. pretrained reference), the authors prompt for Python function completions and measure both textual and functional similarity. Results demonstrate clear evidence of functional memorization, arguing for auditing metrics beyond surface-level textual overlap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on training data memorization in LLMs has focused on textual (verbatim) overlap between generated outputs and training examples. However, code presents a unique challenge: two code snippets can be functionally identical while sharing little to no textual similarity. Existing textual-overlap-based auditing methods thus systematically underestimate memorization in the code domain. This motivates the need to characterize and measure functional memorization — the extraction of functional logic that evades verbatim detection.

Method: The authors employ a counterfactual experimental design using Olmo-3-32B. One version is midtrained on target code (exposed), while a pretrained baseline serves as the unexposed reference. Both models are prompted with Python function signatures and asked to generate completions.
