Memorization is assessed along two axes: (1) textual similarity (traditional verbatim/near-verbatim metrics) and (2) functional similarity, measured via LLM-as-a-judge and execution-based evaluation (e.g., comparing outputs on identical inputs). The differential between the exposed and reference models isolates the effect of training exposure on both textual and functional reproduction.

Result: The midtrained model exhibits clear evidence of functional memorization — it reproduces functional logic from the training data even when textual overlap is low or absent. The comparison with the pretrained reference confirms that this functional leakage is attributable to training exposure rather than general code-generation capabilities. This demonstrates that verbatim metrics alone are insufficient for auditing code memorization, as functional equivalence can persist without detectable textual copying.

Conclusion: Functional memorization is a real phenomenon in LLMs for code that existing textual-overlap audits fail to capture. The findings underscore the need for auditing frameworks that incorporate functional similarity measures — such as execution-based evaluation — to more accurately assess training data leakage and its implications for copyright, privacy, and benchmark contamination in code-generating models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate code at scale. Meanwhile, prior work has investigated whether training data may be recoverable from model outputs, by auditing the textual overlap between training examples and model generations. Code, however, can be functionally equivalent while textually dissimilar. In this work, we study functional memorization: extraction of functional logic beyond what verbatim metrics detect. We construct a counterfactual setup for Olmo-3-32B, comparing a midtrained model (exposed to target code) against a pretrained reference (not exposed). We prompt both models with Python function signatures and measure both textual and functional similarity (i.e., LLM-as-a-judge, execution-based). Our results show clear evidence of functional memorization, highlighting the need for auditing metrics that go beyond textual overlap.

</details>


### [224] [Keep Policy Gradient in Charge: Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation for Long-Horizon Tool-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12634)
*Tianyu Ding,Jianhong Xin,Juan Pablo De la Cruz Weinstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGCD uses contrastive sibling rollouts and an external LLM to generate dense, stepwise credit references for tool-use RL, improving token-level advantage assignment without leaking privileged information to the deployed agent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon tool-use RL suffers from sparse trajectory-level rewards that fail to pinpoint which reasoning, API, or answer tokens deserve credit. While self-distillation could densify the signal, naive token-level distillation amplifies both useful and harmful behaviors indiscriminately because it lacks verifier-grounded credit assignment.

Method: SGCD employs four components: (1) dynamic sampling of successful and failed sibling rollouts from the same prompt; (2) an external LLM that contrasts these siblings to produce a training-only stepwise credit reference; (3) dense teacher-student divergence to drive credit reassignment; and (4) bounded detached credit weights that reshape GRPO token-level advantages. Crucially, the deployed student never sees the external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle at inference time.

Result: On AppWorld, SGCD improves TGC from 42.9→45.6 (test_normal) and 24.7→27.0 (test_challenge). On τ³-airline, pass@1 improves from 0.583→0.602, outperforming matched GRPO baselines.

Conclusion: SGCD demonstrates that distillation can be safely used for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss, yielding consistent gains across complex tool-use benchmarks without exposing the deployed agent to external dependencies.

Abstract: Long-horizon tool-use reinforcement learning can learn from outcome verification, but its
  trajectory-level advantage is broadcast across many reasoning, API, and answer tokens.
  Self-distillation promises a denser signal by reusing a policy's own rollouts or a privileged
  teacher. We show, however, that direct token-level self-distillation can silently destroy tool use:
  it rehearses teacher behavior without knowing which actions the verifier rewards, so useful skills
  and harmful shortcuts are amplified together. We introduce Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation
  (SGCD), which uses distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss.
  Dynamic sampling produces mixed successful and failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM summarizes
  their contrast into a training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence
  drives credit reassignment; and bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages. The
  deployed student sees no external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle. Across AppWorld and
  $τ^3$-airline, SGCD improves over matched GRPO comparators: AppWorld TGC $42.9 \to 45.6$ on
  test_normal and $24.7 \to 27.0$ on test_challenge, and $τ^3$-airline pass@1 $0.583 \to 0.602$.

</details>


### [225] [PolicyGuard: Towards Test-time and Step-level Adversary Defense for Reinforcement Learning Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12896)
*Junfeng Guo Heng Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolicyGuard is a test-time, step-level backdoor defense for RL agents that uses Gaussian Process posterior variance and pseudo trajectories to detect malicious actions. It achieves strong AUROC across multiple attack types without requiring access to the agent's internal parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL agents are vulnerable to backdoor attacks where triggers induce malicious behavior. Existing defenses are limited: they require internal parameters, operate at coarse granularity (model/trajectory level), or only work against specific attacks. A more general, fine-grained, black-box defense is needed.

Method: PolicyGuard operates at test time and at the individual time-step level. It employs Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance to quantify uncertainty over actions and uses adapted pseudo trajectories to enable step-level uncertainty computation. Backdoor-triggered actions are detected as those with anomalously high GP posterior variance. Theoretical foundations are provided to justify the use of GP posterior variance for detection.

Result: PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection across seven RL games. It attains an average AUROC of 0.856 against perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 against adversary-agent attacks.

Conclusion: PolicyGuard offers an effective, black-box, step-level backdoor defense for RL agents, backed by both theoretical analysis and strong empirical performance across diverse attacks and environments.

Abstract: While real-world applications of reinforcement learning (RL) are becoming increasingly popular, the security of RL systems deserve more attention and exploration. In particular, recent work has revealed that RL agents are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where a victim agent behaves normally under standard conditions but executes malicious actions when a specific trigger is activated. Existing backdoor defenses for RL either require access to the agent's internal parameters, operate only at the model or trajectory level, or are limited to specific attack types. To ensure the security of RL agents, we propose \texttt{PolicyGuard}, a \textit{test-time step-level} backdoor defense which leverages Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance and adapts pseudo trajectories to enable uncertainty computation for individual time step. Besides, we also provide theoretical foundations to explain the efficacy of GP posterior variance. Extensive experiments across seven RL games demonstrate that PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection performance in most cases, with average AUROC of 0.856 for perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 for adversary-agent attacks.

</details>


### [226] [The Metric Picks the Winner: Evaluation Choice Flips Model Rankings for Drug-Response Prediction in Unseen Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12639)
*Dhruv Agarwal,Riya Bisht*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that model rankings in drug-response prediction flip depending on the evaluation metric: under a per-gene variance proxy, simple linear regression on Morgan fingerprints wins; under the contest's true active-compound weighted MSE, deep models and fusion approaches significantly outperform simple baselines. This is the first demonstration of metric-calibration effects on held-out drug chemistry data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field of drug-response prediction struggles because complex deep learning models often fail to outperform trivial baselines when test compounds are held out by chemical structure. The authors investigate this phenomenon systematically on the VCPI THP-1 DRUG-seq benchmark, aiming to understand why model rankings are inconsistent and how evaluation metrics drive winner selection.

Method: A staged approach: (1) dumb baselines — untreated control and mean training-compound response; (2) non-parametric retrieval using Tanimoto-weighted average of nearest training compounds; (3) a fusion stage combining frozen chemistry embeddings with retrieval-support features to predict residuals over the mean, incorporating an uncertainty head and gene programs. All evaluated under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split on 14,026 training compounds from the VCPI DRUG-seq dataset, with rankings compared under both inverse-variance per-gene proxy and the contest's Mejia-weighted active-set metric.

Result: Under the inverse-variance per-gene proxy, regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints outperforms deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa. Under the official active-set metric (Mejia weights), this reverses: deep models and the fusion decoder significantly beat the linear fingerprint baseline (−0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10⁻⁴), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. Mean baseline scores: 0.535 vs organizers' 0.507 reference.

Conclusion: The metric used to evaluate drug-response prediction determines which model wins — the first demonstration of this metric-calibration effect on real held-out drug chemistry (previously shown only on genetic perturbation). Researchers must align evaluation metrics with the true biological or clinical objective, and the community should adopt standardized, contest-validated scoring to enable fair model comparisons.

Abstract: Predicting how a cell's transcriptome responds to a drug it has never seen is a core, hard problem in computational cell biology: recent benchmarks show complex models often fail to beat trivial baselines once test compounds are held out by chemistry. We study one cell line and assay, THP-1 cells profiled by DRUG-seq, scored by the active-compound weighted MSE(wMSE) of the VCPI prediction contest. We propose a staged approach: dumb baselines (untreated control and mean training-compound response) that the field keeps failing to beat; non-parametric retrieval (a Tanimoto-weighted average of a held-out compound's nearest training compounds); and a fusion stage combining a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict the residual over the mean, with an uncertainty head and gene programs. On the released VCPI THP-1 drug-seq data (14,026 training compounds), under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split, the model ranking inverts depending on the metric. Under an inverse-variance per-gene proxy, a regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints appears to win over the deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa -- the textbook "simple baselines win" result. But under the contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights, validated against the official scorer; mean baseline 0.535 vs the organizers' 0.507 reference), that reverses: the deep models win, our fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10^-4), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. Picking the metric picks the winner -- to our knowledge the first demonstration on real held-out drug chemistry of the metric-calibration effect established largely on genetic perturbation. We release a reproducible pipeline
  wired to the official scorer that emits a valid submission over the real 1064 x 12,995 grid.

</details>


### [227] [Individual Control Barrier Functions-Guided Diffusion Model for Safe Offline Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12640)
*Qingyun Guo,Junyi Shi,Jianuo Huang,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that integrates neural individual control barrier functions into diffusion models for safety-constrained trajectory generation, with policies recovered via inverse dynamics, demonstrating improved safety while maintaining competitive rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL using diffusion models has shown promise in single-agent settings for modeling complex data distributions, but existing approaches neglect safety challenges in multi-agent environments. Safe exploration is critical in multi-agent settings where agent interactions can lead to constraint violations.

Method: The algorithm embeds neural individual control barrier functions (CBFs) into the diffusion model framework for safe trajectory generation in offline multi-agent RL. Control policies are then recovered from the generated safe trajectories through inverse dynamics modeling.

Result: Evaluated across diverse benchmarks, the proposed algorithm demonstrates substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: Embedding control barrier functions into diffusion-based offline MARL effectively addresses the safety gap in multi-agent settings, enabling safe policy learning from static datasets without sacrificing task performance.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning allows control policies to be learned directly from data without online interaction, making it suitable for safety-critical tasks. Recent studies have applied diffusion models to offline reinforcement learning to leverage their strong capacity for modeling complex data distributions. However, existing approaches primarily focus on single-agent settings, leaving the safety challenges in multi-agent environments largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics. We evaluate our algorithm across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards.

</details>


### [228] [TEDD: Robust Detection of Unstable Temporal Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12643)
*Ricardo Ribeiro Pereira,Bruno Casal Laraña,Nádia Soares,Miguel Araújo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TEDD, a technique for detecting temporal distribution changes (drifts) in features that can degrade ML model performance. It uses a regression model predicting instance timestamps to identify which features contribute to instability, demonstrating effectiveness across various change patterns without requiring parameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world temporal data often exhibits distribution shifts over time, causing ML models to degrade when deployed on data that differs from the training distribution. Practitioners need automated methods to identify which features are changing so they can apply mitigations (e.g., data transformations) and maintain robust model performance.

Method: TEDD (Temporal drift detection) leverages a regression model that predicts the timestamp of each instance from the feature values. Features that strongly contribute to accurate timestamp prediction are flagged as temporally unstable / drifting. The approach quantifies the amount of change per feature and supports both numerical and categorical features, as well as multivariate drift detection.

Result: TEDD detects all basic types of temporal changes (both numerical and categorical), handles multivariate drifts, returns comparable per-feature change scores, requires no hyperparameter tuning, and scales well in both number of features and instances. It was validated on both real and synthetic datasets against other methods.

Conclusion: TEDD provides a simple, scalable, and parameter-free solution for detecting temporally unstable features, enabling practitioners to build more robust ML models that maintain performance over time by proactively identifying and addressing feature drift.

Abstract: When working with real-world temporal data, it is common to encounter features whose distribution is changing over time. The naive employment of Machine Learning models on this unstable data might lead to rapidly degrading performance, especially if the new distribution is much different from what was previously seen during training. In order to cope with this problem, it is critical to automatically identify features that are changing over time. With these features detected, data scientists and other practitioners will be able to mitigate the issue (for instance, by applying data transformations), deploying more robust models that retain high performance for longer periods of time. In this paper, we describe which temporal changes a feature should not suffer from, and propose TEDD, a technique to a) identify when a dataset might lead to an unstable Machine Learning model and b) automatically detect which features cause such lack of robustness. In order to achieve it, we leverage a regression model to highlight which features contribute to a good prediction of an instance's timestamp. We compare our approach to other methods in real and synthetic data, testing their detection capability on all simple change patterns. We show that our method: detects all types of basic changes, both for numerical and categorical features; can detect multivariate drifts; returns a comparable value measuring the amount of change of each feature; requires no parameter tuning; and is scalable both on number of features and instances of the dataset.

</details>


### [229] [Physics-Aware Auxiliary Losses Improve Out-of-Distribution Generalization of a GNN Synthesizability Filter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12651)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Using cheap physical priors (topological complexity via Bertz index and strain energy via MMFF94) as auxiliary supervision on a GNN yields modest but statistically significant improvement in out-of-distribution synthesizability prediction, but only multi-seed evaluation reveals the true effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing synthesizability filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely data-driven and degrade when molecular generative models propose structures far from the training distribution. The authors investigate whether physics-inspired auxiliary losses can improve OOD generalization without expensive computations.

Method: A GINE backbone is augmented with two auxiliary regression tasks: (1) topological complexity supervised by the Bertz index, and (2) strain energy supervised by MMFF94 force-field calculations. A 4-way ablation (baseline, +complexity, +strain, +both) is evaluated on a single-source OOD split: training on drug-like molecules (HIV+Tox21) and testing on COCONUT natural products. Five seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals ensure statistical rigor.

Result: All three physics-aware variants significantly outperform the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774). The combination (+both) yields the largest gain (Δ = +0.0066, 95% CI [+0.0038, +0.0093]), followed by +complexity (Δ = +0.0060) and +strain (Δ = +0.0032). All CIs exclude zero. No differences are observed in-distribution. A single-seed experiment produced a misleading non-monotone result, underscoring the need for multi-seed evaluation.

Conclusion: Closed-form physical priors provide a cheap, statistically significant OOD boost for synthesizability prediction. The gains are modest but real, and the study highlights a methodological warning: single-seed experiments can yield qualitatively different (and incorrect) conclusions.

Abstract: Machine-learning drug-discovery pipelines increasingly rely on generative models that propose molecules far from the data used to train downstream synthesizability filters. Existing
  filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade in exactly this out-of-distribution (OOD) regime. We ask whether cheap, closed-form physical priors, used
  as auxiliary supervision on a graph neural network (GNN), improve OOD generalization. We add two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: a topological complexity regression supervised by
  the Bertz index, and a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. On a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV, Tox21, COCONUT) labeled by SAScore thresholds we reproduce
  a strong in-distribution baseline, then evaluate a 4-way ablation (baseline / +complexity / +strain / +both) on a single-source OOD split (train on drug-like HIV+Tox21, test on
  COCONUT natural products), repeated over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. All three physics-aware variants give a small but statistically significant OOD improvement
  over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Delta = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Delta = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Delta = +0.0066 ([+0.0038,
  +0.0093]); every interval excludes zero, and the combination is best. The variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect is visible only under OOD evaluation. We are
  explicit that the effects are modest, and we report a cautionary methodological finding: a single-seed version of this experiment produced a qualitatively different (non-monotone)
  story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

</details>


### [230] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12658)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs provide a unified framework for pharmacokinetic modeling that matches standard estimators on textbook problems, exposes structural non-identifiability hidden by mis-specified closed-form methods, and naturally absorbs sparse heterogeneous tissue measurements — offering a principled alternative when analytical solutions break down.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In chemotherapy pharmacokinetics, plasma drug concentration is routinely measured but tissue concentration — which drives both tumor kill and toxicity — is typically unobserved. Standard clinical practice relies on nonlinear least-squares (NLS) fitting of an analytical biexponential solution, which fails when the model becomes nonlinear (e.g., Michaelis-Menten saturable elimination) because the closed form no longer exists and mis-specification goes undetected. The authors seek a uniform, physics-aware method that handles both linear and nonlinear compartment models without requiring analytical solutions.

Method: The authors benchmark three approaches on two pharmacokinetic problems: (1) NLS on the analytical biexponential plasma solution (clinical baseline), (2) a data-only MLP (physics-agnostic neural baseline), and (3) a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN). The first problem is a linear two-compartment model; the second is a Michaelis-Menten extension with saturable elimination that lacks a closed-form plasma solution. They evaluate plasma curve fitting, tissue concentration recovery, and structural identifiability — including experiments where two sparse tissue observations are added to the PINN to assess whether identifiability can be rescued.

Result: On the linear two-compartment problem, the PINN matches near-optimal NLS performance on plasma while recovering tissue curves in a single training pass; the data-only MLP fails on tissue by ~10x. On the Michaelis-Menten problem, NLS is mis-specified (its biexponential ansatz is invalid) and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN reveals the model is structurally non-identifiable from plasma alone, converging to a basin where k12 → 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds, k21 is recovered within 1% of truth; Vmax and Km within one standard deviation; k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 → 0.82) but remains ~2σ below truth — a recovery the closed-form NLS cannot attempt.

Conclusion: PINNs do not outperform NLS where NLS is appropriate; rather, they offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that mis-specified estimators conceal, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss function — making them a principled tool for pharmacokinetic modeling when analytical solutions are unavailable or when tissue measurements (even sparse ones) are available.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

</details>


### [231] [A Zero-shot Generalized Graph Anomaly Detection Framework via Node Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12673)
*Phan Nguyen,Dat Cao,Hien Chu,Khue Hoang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AlignGAD is a zero-shot cross-domain graph anomaly detection framework that aligns heterogeneous features, constructs cluster-aware graph views, and scores node reconstruction discrepancies to generalize anomaly detection across different graph domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GAD methods rely on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, severely limiting cross-domain generalization. A zero-shot framework is needed to detect anomalies in unseen target graphs without domain-specific adaptation.

Method: AlignGAD comprises three modules: (1) a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; (2) a Clustering Module that builds cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; (3) a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence across different graph views.

Result: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that AlignGAD is effective under the zero-shot GAD setting, outperforming baselines that lack cross-domain generalization capability.

Conclusion: AlignGAD provides a promising zero-shot solution for cross-domain graph anomaly detection by unifying feature spaces, leveraging cluster-level structural cues, and aggregating multi-view anomaly evidence, thus enabling robust generalization to unseen target graphs.

Abstract: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs, showing strong potential in real-world applications with heterogeneous graph data. However, existing methods often depend on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, which limits their ability to generalize across different domains. To address this challenge, we propose AlignGAD, a zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection framework. Our framework is built upon three key components: a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; a Clustering Module that constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

</details>


### [232] [How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12680)
*Julia Kostin,Kasra Jalaldoust,Elias Bareinboim,Samory Kpotufe,Fanny Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper theoretically analyzes when causal invariance knowledge improves supervised domain adaptation (sDA) in finite-sample settings. For linear regression, the authors derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that gains depend on target-risk margins among candidate invariant predictors and finite-source estimation error. When margins are large relative to target sample size, adaptive aggregation matches the best candidate; when small, no algorithm can reliably exploit causal structure. Results are linked to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validated empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing causality-based domain generalization work shows that shared causal structure yields invariant predictors with stable risk across shifts, but this is primarily a population-level guarantee. In practice, finite-sample settings with a few labeled target samples (supervised domain adaptation) are common, yet it is underexplored whether causal invariance provably helps in such regimes. The paper aims to close this gap.

Method: The authors study linear regression with full or partial causal knowledge specifying invariant feature subsets. Each subset yields a source-trained candidate predictor. They derive matching upper and lower bounds on target risk, characterize when an adaptive aggregation procedure (e.g., leveraging target validation data to select among candidates) can match the best candidate, and connect the risk margins separating candidates to structural shift magnitude in linear Structural Causal Models (SCMs).

Result: Finite-sample gains are governed by (1) target-risk margins among candidate predictors and (2) finite-source estimation error. When margins are sufficiently large relative to target sample size n_Q, adaptive aggregation matches the best candidate and avoids negative transfer. When margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection for faster rates. Margins are tied to the magnitude of structural shifts in linear SCMs.

Conclusion: Causal invariance knowledge is provably beneficial for supervised domain adaptation only when target-risk margins among candidate invariant predictors are sufficiently large. Otherwise, causal structure provides no finite-sample advantage. This delineates the precise conditions under which causal domain generalization translates to practical gains.

Abstract: Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

</details>


### [233] [Forecasting Is Not Attribution: Localizing Decoder Bypass in Graph-Based Neural Marketing Mix Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12687)
*Yunbo Wang,Bolbi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Marketing mix models (MMM) using graph neural networks can achieve low forecasting error while completely failing at attribution, a phenomenon termed "attribution bypass." The authors introduce DICE-MMM, a diagnostic framework that decouples graph recovery, forecasting, and attribution alignment, and demonstrate via stress tests that low MSE does not certify correct attribution—the bottleneck is graph-support selection, not forecasting capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph-based neural MMMs conflate two distinct goals: forecasting business outcomes and attributing those outcomes to marketing channels. A high-capacity decoder can achieve low forecasting error through shortcuts (target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory) without routing counterfactual sensitivity through the graph—meaning the graph used for attribution may be meaningless. No existing framework systematically diagnoses this "attribution bypass" failure mode.

Method: DICE-MMM is a three-stage bounded diagnostic and training framework. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder to recover graph structure. Stage 2 freezes the encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder where all cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. The framework is evaluated using three tests: CIG (Counterfactual Influence on Graph), AR-CIG (AutoRegression-adjusted CIG), and graph-swap tests. Experiments span controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, benchmarking against CausalMMM on both forecasting (MSE@7) and attribution alignment (nAUPRC) metrics.

Result: In sparse-target benchmarks, both no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 ≈ 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 ± 0.129 at comparable MSE. The frozen graph-swap test localizes the bottleneck: a DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 ± 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 ± 0.027 with the oracle graph. DICE also demonstrates improved stable graph recovery over CausalMMM across controlled swaps and external stress tests.

Conclusion: Low forecasting MSE can hide complete attribution bypass. The unresolved bottleneck in graph-based neural MMM is graph-support selection (recovering which edges should exist), not forecasting accuracy or decoder capacity. DICE-MMM provides a rigorous framework for stress-testing and localizing this failure, separating what have traditionally been conflated questions in MMM evaluation.

Abstract: Marketing mix models are used to forecast business outcomes and to attribute those outcomes to marketing channels, but these goals are not equivalent. We study a failure mode in graph-based neural MMM called attribution bypass: a high-capacity decoder can obtain low forecasting error through target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory while failing to route counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used as the attribution object. We introduce DICE-MMM as a bounded diagnostic and training framework. We do not claim that observational neural MMM identifies causal effects. Instead, DICE separates three questions often conflated in graph-based MMM: graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and whether the trained decoder's perturbation-induced influence is graph aligned. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder use is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. Across controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. The experiments show that forecasting accuracy is not an attribution certificate: in a sparse-target benchmark, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 +/- 0.129 at comparable MSE. Frozen graph-swap localizes the bottleneck: the same DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 +/- 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 +/- 0.027 with the oracle graph. The contribution is a stress test and failure-localization framework showing that low MSE can hide attribution bypass and that the unresolved bottleneck is graph-support selection, not forecasting or decoder capacity.

</details>


### [234] [M*: A Modular, Extensible, Serving System for Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12688)
*Atindra Jha,Naomi Sagan,Keisuke Kamahori,Irmak Sivgin,Rohan Sanda,Steven Gao,Mark Horowitz,Luke Zettlemoyer,Olivia Hsu,Jure Leskovec,Baris Kasikci,Stephanie Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M* is a universal serving system for composite AI models that represents models as dataflow graphs (Walk Graphs), enabling efficient, modular serving across diverse multimodal architectures with up to 12.5× performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emerging composite model architectures integrate heterogeneous components (vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion heads, audio codecs, etc.), but existing serving frameworks assume narrow, fixed model structures and cannot efficiently accommodate this architectural diversity.

Method: Introduces the Walk Graph abstraction, which represents composite models as dataflow graphs where requests are processed as graph traversals. This enables arbitrary composition of model components, flexible placement onto physical clusters, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime.

Result: M* achieves 20% lower end-to-end latency vs. vLLM-Omni on text-to-image (BAGEL), up to 2.9× lower real-time factor and 2.7× higher throughput vs. vLLM-Omni on text-to-speech (Qwen3-Omni), and up to 12.5× improvement over V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline on robotic planning.

Conclusion: M* demonstrates that a unified, graph-based serving abstraction can substantially improve serving efficiency across diverse composite model families with minimal developer effort, paving the way for broader adoption of complex multimodal architectures.

Abstract: We are entering a new era of composite model architectures that integrate diverse components such as vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion and flow heads, audio codecs, action generators, and world-model predictors. Such architectures underpin a broad class of multimodal models, including unified multimodal models, omni models, speech-language models, vision-language-action policies, and world models. However, existing model serving frameworks were built on narrow assumptions about model structure, making them ill-suited to accommodate this new architectural diversity. Here we present M*, a universal serving system for efficient serving of composite AI models. M* represents models as dataflow graphs, processing requests spanning diverse modalities and tasks as traversals over these graphs. The core insight is a modular abstraction that supports arbitrary composition of model components, flexible placement onto a physical cluster, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. We call this abstraction the Walk Graph and show how it can concisely capture composite models from a broad range of families. We instantiate M* on representative models and find that it achieves, on average, 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads on BAGEL, while delivering up to 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech workloads on Qwen3-Omni. M* also outperforms the V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline for robotic planning by up to 12.5x. Thus, our work paves the road towards more efficient serving of complex models with minimal developer effort.

</details>


### [235] [Two-Layer Linear Auto-Regressive Models Estimate Latent States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12691)
*Yahya Sattar,Sunmook Choi,Leo Maynard-Zhang,Yassir Jedra,Maryam Fazel,Sarah Dean*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-layer linear auto-regressive models trained via empirical risk minimization on partially observed linear dynamical systems provably learn to approximate Kalman filtering, with the hidden representation recovering optimal state estimates up to a similarity transformation, supported by benign optimization landscape and finite-sample guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how and why auto-regressive models learn latent representations from sequential data remains an open theoretical question. This work aims to provide a rigorous theoretical explanation for this phenomenon.

Method: The authors analyze two-layer linear auto-regressive models trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems. The analysis proceeds in three steps: (1) showing the Kalman filter is well-approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error; (2) proving the non-convex optimization landscape is benign — all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima; (3) providing finite-sample guarantees for prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery.

Result: The learned hidden representation coincides with Kalman filter state estimates up to a similarity transformation, despite the model having no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics. Numerical simulations corroborate the theoretical findings, showing that auto-regressive latent representations recover state estimates.

Conclusion: Auto-regressive models implicitly learn to perform Kalman filtering when trained on data from linear dynamical systems, providing a theoretical foundation for understanding representation learning in sequential models. The benign optimization landscape and finite-sample guarantees further strengthen the practical reliability of such models.

Abstract: Auto-regressive models have emerged as powerful tools for sequential data, from language to video. Understanding how and why these models learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. In this work, we demonstrate that when trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering. In particular, we show that the learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the state estimates produced by the optimal (Kalman) filter, even though the model has no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics or state. The result follows from three main insights. First, we establish that the Kalman filter is well approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. Second, we show that despite non-convexity, the two-layer optimization landscape is benign, i.e., all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima. Finally, as our main contributions, we provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery. Numerical simulations support the theoretical results and demonstrate that the latent representations of auto-regressive models recover state estimates.

</details>


### [236] [LLM-Powered Personalized Glycemic Assessment in Type 2 Diabetes with Wearable Sensor Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12699)
*Yifan Gao,Yanmin Gong,Yun Shi,Yuanxiong Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GlyLLM is an LLM-powered framework that integrates wearable sensor data (CGM, fitness trackers) with structured metadata for personalized glycemic assessment in Type 2 Diabetes, outperforming traditional ML methods by ~13% on glucose forecasting and diabetes categorization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing glycemic assessment methods rely primarily on historical blood glucose data and traditional ML, overlooking personalized individual-level context (e.g., biometrics, surveys). LLMs' capacity to integrate diverse modalities and model sequential dependencies presents an untapped opportunity for personalized diabetes care.

Method: The authors propose GlyLLM, a framework that leverages pre-trained LLMs to fuse CGM data, fitness tracker outputs, and structured metadata (diabetes surveys, biometric tests, health information) via sensor-text semantic abstraction, applied at decision time for glucose forecasting and diabetes categorization tasks on the AI-READI dataset.

Result: GlyLLM outperforms traditional ML baselines by 13.66% in RMSE for glucose forecasting and 13.08% in AUROC for diabetes categorization. Ablation reveals that diabetes surveys and biometric tests are the most critical metadata components for glycemic assessment.

Conclusion: GlyLLM represents a promising step toward using LLMs for personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care, demonstrating that integrating multimodal wearable and contextual data through pre-trained language models yields significant performance gains over conventional approaches.

Abstract: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) poses an increasing global health threat, demanding effective glycemic assessment to support personalized and improved diabetes care. Wearable sensors such as continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and fitness trackers offer many valuable insights for glycemic assessment. However, effectively analyzing these data requires integration with essential individual-level context. Existing methods are often based on traditional machine learning (ML) and rely primarily on historical blood glucose measurements and overlook personalized information, which limits their performance across diverse diabetes populations. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their ability to integrate diverse data modalities while modeling sequential dependencies, motivating the exploration of their potential for personalized glycemic assessment.
  In this paper, we propose GlyLLM, an LLM-powered framework for modeling CGM-based glycemic dynamics through the integration of wearable sensor data and structured metadata. GlyLLM can leverage the extensive prior knowledge of pre-trained LLMs and achieve sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time. Experiments on two related tasks on the AI-READI dataset demonstrate that our model outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66\% in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for glucose forecasting and 13.08\% in Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) for diabetes categorization. Additionally, our ablation study shows that diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more critical than other health information for glycemic assessment. Our work presents a promising step toward harnessing the power of LLMs to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care.

</details>


### [237] [A Stabilized Path-Space Approach to Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12710)
*Evan Scope Crafts,Umberto Villa,Saviz Mowlavi,Yanting Ma,Hassan Mansour,Wael H. Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion posterior sampling that reformulates the problem as stochastic optimal control over trajectories, introduces time reparameterization to remove initialization bias, uses trust-region optimization with log-variance objectives, and unifies guidance-based samplers while enabling importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posteriors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors, lacking principled uncertainty quantification and suffering from initialization bias. A rigorous framework connecting diffusion sampling to stochastic optimal control is needed.

Method: The authors define a likelihood-weighted target measure on diffusion trajectories (path-space), casting posterior sampling as learning a controlled process matching this measure via stochastic optimal control. Key innovations: (1) time reparameterization that removes bias from unknown initial value function without auxiliary training; (2) trust-region path-space optimization with log-variance objectives; (3) unification with existing guidance samplers; (4) importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations.

Result: The framework is evaluated on benchmark inverse problems with analytical or high-quality reference posteriors, demonstrating improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches (e.g., DPS, PiGDM), and providing principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: The path-space optimal control formulation yields a principled, stabilized approach to diffusion posterior sampling that unifies existing methods, quantifies approximation error, and enables asymptotically exact inference via importance sampling, with demonstrated empirical improvements.

Abstract: Diffusion models provide expressive data-driven priors for Bayesian inverse problems, but many diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors. In this work, we develop a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based posterior sampling. Starting from a base diffusion process whose terminal marginal represents the prior, we define a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories and cast posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process whose path measure matches this target. This formulation connects diffusion posterior sampling to stochastic optimal control while preserving the Bayesian structure needed for uncertainty quantification. We introduce a time reparameterization that makes the path-space control problem well posed by removing the bias induced by the unknown initial value function, without auxiliary training. We then learn the control via a trust-region path-space optimization method with log-variance objectives. The path-space perspective also unifies our learned control approach with existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies the sampling error induced by approximate controls, and yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. We evaluate the proposed framework on a suite of benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors, enabling principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification. These experiments provide insight into the behavior of diffusion-based posterior samplers and demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches.

</details>


### [238] [Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Detectors for Open-Set RF Fingerprinting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12718)
*Sudeepta Mondal,Ganesh Sundaramoorthi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper adapts out-of-distribution (OOD) detection methods to open-set RF fingerprinting, presenting a unified information-theoretic framework. Crucially, it demonstrates that OOD detectors can be tuned without auxiliary OOD data—a practical necessity in RF environments—and achieve performance comparable to detectors with access to true OOD tuning data on the POWDER dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RF fingerprinting systems face distribution shift in open-world environments due to unknown transmitters and temporal drift. While OOD detection is a natural fit, its adoption in RFF is limited because most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning—data that is impractical to collect in real RF environments.

Method: The authors introduce several OOD detection methods from ML literature to the open-set RFF domain, framing them within a unified information-theoretic mathematical framework. This framework enables systematic analysis and development of new methods. They also apply recent techniques for tuning OOD detectors without requiring OOD tuning data (data-free tuning). Evaluation is conducted on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset.

Result: Detectors tuned without any OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines that have access to true OOD tuning data, and significantly outperform baselines that lack true OOD tuning data. This holds on the POWDER dataset, demonstrating practical viability.

Conclusion: Data-free OOD detector tuning is practically viable for open-set RF fingerprinting, removing a key barrier to adoption. The information-theoretic framework provides a principled foundation for future method development in this domain.

Abstract: Radio-frequency (RF) fingerprinting systems must operate in open-world environments where signals from unknown transmitters and temporal drift introduce distribution shift at test time. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection provides a natural framework for this problem, yet its application to RF fingerprinting (RFF) remains limited. A key barrier to their adoption is that most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning, an assumption that is difficult to satisfy in RF environments where representative OOD data is impractical to collect. In this work, we introduce a promising set of OOD detection methods from the machine learning literature to open-set RFF domain. We present these methods within a unified mathematical framework based on information theory, which is a natural framework for communication systems. Our framework allows for the systematic analysis of methods and development of new methods. We further demonstrate the applicability of recent work on tuning OOD detectors without given OOD tuning data for open-set RFF. We evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, showing that detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines with access to true OOD tuning data and greatly out-perform baseline approaches without access to true OOD tuning data, showcasing the practical viability for the RFF problem.

</details>


### [239] [Normative Robustness as a Frontier for Non-Verifiable Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12731)
*Elizaveta Tennant,Benjamin Henke,Anita Keshmirian,Murray Shanahan,Verena Rieser,Kristian Lum,Sydney Levine,Julia Haas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "moral robustness" as a metric for evaluating LLMs' consistency in moral reasoning. Using 48,000 simulated moral deliberations, it finds that frontier LLMs exhibit "moral deliberative sycophancy" — shifting both verdicts and justifications toward users' stated views, and varying judgments based on premise order (13–22%) and conversation duration (10–24%), despite successfully ignoring irrelevant distractors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for advisory and deliberative roles in subjective domains lacking objective ground truths, traditional evaluations focused on fact-based reasoning (math, science) fail to capture whether models can handle ambiguous, value-laden problems reliably over time and across contexts.

Method: The authors propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning and define "moral robustness." They construct a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework manipulating four variables: premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations are simulated across four frontier LLMs.

Result: Models successfully ignore morally irrelevant distractors. However, they shift reasoning toward the user's stated moral view by up to 6.5% on average. Moral judgments are altered by premise order in 13–22% of cases and by conversation duration (single-turn vs. multi-turn) in 10–24% of cases. Critically, models tailor not only final verdicts but also underlying justifications to align with user views.

Conclusion: The paper characterizes this failure mode as "moral deliberative sycophancy," demonstrating that frontier LLMs lack moral robustness — they are unduly influenced by superficial contextual factors and user preferences, undermining their reliability as moral deliberation partners.

Abstract: As LLMs increasingly serve in advisory and deliberative roles, users rely on them for non-verifiable reasoning in domains lacking objective ground truths. However, traditional evaluations of LLM reasoning focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains, such as mathematics and science, leaving uncertainty over whether and to what degree models can handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time. To address this concern, we propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. We define moral robustness as a model's capacity to exhibit sound moral reasoning across time and contexts, and we introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to empirically measure this capability. We simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. We find that models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift their reasoning by up to 6.5%, on average, towards the user's stated preferred moral view, and varying their reasoning depending on factors such as order (altering moral judgments by order in 13-22% of the cases) and duration (altering moral judgments between single-turn and multi-turn in 10-24% of the cases). Our analysis indicates that models tailor not just their final verdicts but their underlying justifications to align with a user's moral viewpoint - a failure mode we characterize as moral deliberative sycophancy.

</details>


### [240] [Let's Ask Gauss: Improved One-Run Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12733)
*Adya Agrawal,Yu Wei,Jaspal Singh,Malik Magdon-Ismail,Vassilis Zikas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new one-run DP auditing framework for DP-SGD that leverages the asymptotic Gaussian distribution of canary-aligned signal sequences, yielding tighter empirical privacy lower bounds than prior threshold-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing one-run DP auditing methods binarize canary membership signals via thresholding, discarding rich distributional information contained in the full signal sequence. This leads to loose privacy lower bounds and inefficient use of audit data.

Method: The authors show that in white-box DP-SGD, canary-aligned signals across training iterations form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum converges to a Gaussian distribution. They leverage this asymptotic normality to construct a more powerful statistical test for distinguishing models trained with vs. without a given canary, yielding tighter empirical ε lower bounds from a single run.

Result: The proposed distributional auditing framework achieves tighter privacy lower bounds compared to prior one-run methods, demonstrating that the Gaussian approximation captures information otherwise lost by thresholding.

Conclusion: Exploiting the asymptotic distributional structure of canary signals in DP-SGD enables significantly more efficient and tighter empirical privacy auditing from a single training run, strengthening practical privacy assurance.

Abstract: Privacy auditing provides an important safeguard by estimating the actual information leaked by a model, thus ensuring that theoretical privacy guarantees hold in practice. We study empirical privacy auditing for differentially private (DP) machine learning, focusing on efficient one-run methods for mechanisms such as DP-SGD. Prior one-run approaches threshold training examples or "canaries" into binary membership guesses, which discards useful information. We show that, in the white-box DP-SGD setting, canary-aligned signals naturally form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian. Leveraging this distributional perspective, we develop a DP-auditing framework that leads to tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.

</details>


### [241] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Radial Basis Functions for PDEs with Dirac Delta Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12735)
*Manuel Reyna,Alexandre Tartakovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs struggle with Dirac delta functions in PDEs, requiring smooth approximations that introduce errors. This paper reframes PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods, enabling direct treatment of Dirac terms via weak-form integration. Comparing PINNs with RBF-RLS, the authors show RBF-RLS consistently outperforms PINNs for transport problems with Dirac deltas, and they explain this using Neural Tangent Kernel theory. The methods are tested on linear PDEs for groundwater flow, transport in porous media, and rivers, including inverse problems with synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PINNs cannot directly handle Dirac delta functions in PDE forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions. The common workaround — approximating deltas with smooth surrogate functions (e.g., Gaussians) — introduces significant modeling errors. The authors seek a principled approach that circumvents this limitation by leveraging the weak-form interpretation of PINNs as RLS methods.

Method: The authors reinterpret PINNs within the Residual Least Squares (RLS) framework, which allows direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating them out in the weak-form residual. They compare two RLS formulations: (1) PINNs (deep neural network basis) and (2) RBF-RLS (Radial Basis Function expansion / single-layer RBF network). They analyze both using Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory to explain why RBF-RLS converges while PINN residuals fail to converge to zero. The methods are validated on linear PDEs modeling groundwater flow, solute transport in porous media, and river transport, covering both forward and inverse problems with synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

Result: Integrating out Dirac deltas in the weak form causes PINN residuals to fail to converge to zero, whereas RBF-RLS consistently yields accurate forward and inverse solutions for transport problems. The NTK analysis provides a theoretical explanation for this discrepancy. Both methods are validated successfully against linear PDE benchmarks, with RBF-RLS demonstrating superior robustness for problems involving Dirac delta sources.

Conclusion: The RLS perspective on PINNs enables direct treatment of Dirac delta functions via weak-form integration, avoiding the modeling errors of smooth approximations. RBF-RLS emerges as a more reliable alternative to PINNs for PDEs with Dirac delta terms, supported by NTK theory. This approach is practically applicable to hydrogeological and river transport inverse problems using real-world data.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a machine learning method for solving forward and inverse Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). When applied to PDEs with Dirac delta functions in the forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, PINNs require approximating them with smooth surrogate functions, a practice that can introduce significant modeling errors. In this work, we exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods and show that this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating the weak-form equation. Among RLS formulations other than PINN, we focus on the Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (also known as a single-layer RBF Network). We show that while integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. We explain this finding using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. We test both approaches on linear PDEs that represent groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. We solve inverse problems to fit synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

</details>


### [242] [Deep Unfolded Latent Optimally Partitioned-l2/l1 Networks for Data-driven Block-Sparse Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12740)
*Takanobu Furuhashi,Hidekata Hontani,Qibin Zhao,Tatsuya Yokota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes two deep unfolding architectures for the LOP-l2/l1 method to enable automatic hyperparameter tuning, overcoming numerical instability in proximal operator differentiation. One uses implicit differentiation for stability, the other uses Deep Weight Factorization for flexibility and nonconvex data terms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LOP-l2/l1 is effective for block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions, but requires manual hyperparameter tuning. Its proximal operator suffers from numerical instability during differentiation, preventing integration with Deep Unfolding for automatic parameter learning.

Method: Two architectures are proposed: (1) a stable DU framework employing implicit differentiation to bypass direct differentiation of the proximal operator; (2) a flexible variant using Deep Weight Factorization (DWF) that also accommodates nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms.

Result: Numerical experiments show DU-LOP-l2/l1 achieves competitive performance and demonstrates high resilience against impulsive noise.

Conclusion: The proposed DU-based LOP-l2/l1 architectures successfully enable automatic parameter tuning while maintaining robustness, with the DWF variant offering additional flexibility for nonconvex fidelity terms.

Abstract: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 approach enables block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions but relies on manual hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents its automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding (DU). To address these limitations, we propose two architectures: a stable framework utilizing implicit differentiation and a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization (DWF). The DWF-based approach also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 yields competitive performance and high resilience against impulsive noise.

</details>


### [243] [Adaptive Weighted Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12763)
*Aditya Bhaskara,Ashok Cutkosky,Ravi Kumar,Manish Purohit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies selecting the maximum from n unknown values using only one noisy unbiased estimate per value, designing strategies that are both admissible (not uniformly dominated) and never worse than a baseline like uniform random selection, with applications to stochastic optimization offering no-compromise online-to-batch conversion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many decision and optimization settings, one must select the best among several options when only noisy estimates of their true values are available. Existing strategies may be dominated (there exists another strategy that is always at least as good and sometimes strictly better) or may perform worse than a naive baseline in adverse conditions. The goal is to design strategies that are simultaneously admissible and robust, guaranteeing no worse performance than a simple baseline while excelling in favorable settings.

Method: The authors consider a setting with n unknown real values x_i, each observed through a single independent unbiased estimate y_i. They design selection strategies (decision rules mapping the vector of estimates to a chosen index) that satisfy two properties: (1) admissibility — no other strategy uniformly dominates it in terms of expected value of the selected item; (2) baseline dominance — the strategy never performs worse than a prespecified baseline (e.g., uniform random selection). They analyze the structure of such strategies and characterize conditions for admissibility. The framework is applied to stochastic optimization via online-to-batch conversion, where iterates from an online algorithm are aggregated into a final model.

Result: The paper establishes that admissible strategies exist which never fall below the baseline. These strategies leverage the structure of the noise distribution to outperform the baseline when there is sufficient signal-to-noise ratio, while defaulting to baseline-level performance otherwise. In the stochastic optimization application, this yields online-to-batch conversion bounds that never underperform random iterate selection but can achieve significantly better guarantees in benign (low-noise, well-behaved) settings.

Conclusion: Admissibility and baseline dominance can be simultaneously achieved in the noisy maximum selection problem. The resulting strategies provide a principled "no-compromise" guarantee — robustness against worst-case noise while retaining upside potential in favorable conditions. This framework offers practical value for stochastic optimization, where it enables safe yet potentially improved model selection from optimization trajectories.

Abstract: We study the problem of selecting the largest among $n$ unknown values $x_1,\dots,x_n$ given only a single unbiased estimate $y_i$ for each $x_i$. We design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and also never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. We provide an application to stochastic optimization, where we obtain online-to-batch conversion bounds with a desirable "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, and yet can be significantly better in benign settings.

</details>


### [244] [ProPlay: Procedural World Models for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12780)
*Yijun Ma,Zehong Wang,Yiyang Li,Ziming Li,Xiaoguang Guo,Weixiang Sun,Chuxu Zhang,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProPlay introduces a procedural world model that enables LLM agents to rehearse future trajectories (preplay) using a procedure graph with reliability embeddings, then refine it via environment feedback, improving self-evolution in partially observable environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-evolving agents struggle in partially observable environments because existing LLM-agent methods decouple memory/planning modules and fail to close the loop between experience and internal environment understanding. Agents need to actively explore, learn from sparse feedback, and determine when to trust prior experience.

Method: ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures organized in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition carries a reliability record embedding estimating task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, the agent simulates procedural trajectories over the graph as structured soft guidance (procedure-level preplay); after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback.

Result: Experiments on public benchmarks show ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines.

Conclusion: Closing the loop between planning and memory through procedural preplay with learned reliability embeddings enables more effective self-evolution without external supervision. The approach yields consistent gains in partially observable settings.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents are expected to improve through interaction without external supervision, but this remains difficult in partially observable environments where agents must explore actively, learn from limited feedback, and decide when to trust prior experience. Existing LLM-agent methods often rely on memory or planning modules, yet they rarely close the loop between them to continually refine an internal understanding of environment dynamics. We introduce ProPlay, a procedural world model that supports procedure-level preplay, where agents can rehearse future procedural paths using the learned world knowledge. Rather than representing experience as isolated rules or low-level action constraints, ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition is associated with a reliability record embedding to estimate its task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, ProPlay simulates future procedural trajectories over known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback. Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines. Our code has been released in https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

</details>


### [245] [SymQNet: Amortized Acquisition for Low-Latency Adaptive Hamiltonian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12808)
*Yash Vardhan Tomar,Dheeraj Peddireddy,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SymQNet uses offline reinforcement learning to learn a fast acquisition policy for adaptive Hamiltonian learning, reducing decision latency by 47–72× compared to Bayesian online methods while maintaining posterior feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning requires recomputing Bayesian design rules after every posterior update, which takes seconds per step and becomes a prohibitive wall-clock cost across hundreds of shots. This latency bottleneck limits the practicality of adaptive controllers for quantum device calibration and characterization.

Method: SymQNet is an amortized reinforcement-learning approach that learns a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy offline. During online use, it employs a fast policy forward pass while retaining Bayesian posterior feedback. The method is benchmarked against bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step Bayesian active learning by disagreement (BALD) on transverse-field Ising models.

Result: At five qubits, SymQNet reduces acquisition-only decision latency by 47.1× over bounded Fisher-information search and 72.6× over bounded two-step BALD. At twelve qubits, full simulated steps take 1.02 s for SymQNet versus 13.27 s for bounded two-step BALD.

Conclusion: Learned acquisition via amortized reinforcement learning makes adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads, substantially outperforming traditional online Bayesian acquisition methods in both speed and scalability.

Abstract: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning is central to calibrating and characterizing quantum devices. In an adaptive controller, choosing the next experiment is itself a computation. Bayesian design rules are recomputed after every posterior update, and that step can take seconds. Across hundreds of shots, those seconds become a significant wall-clock cost for adaptivity. We introduce SymQNet, an amortized reinforcement-learning approach for low-latency adaptive Hamiltonian learning. SymQNet learns a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy offline, then uses a fast policy forward pass online while retaining Bayesian posterior feedback. On transverse-field Ising benchmarks, SymQNet substantially reduces acquisition latency relative to bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step Bayesian active learning by disagreement (BALD). At five qubits, it reduces acquisition-only decision latency by $47.1\times$ and $72.6\times$ relative to these online baselines; at twelve qubits, full simulated steps take $1.02$ s for SymQNet versus $13.27$ s for bounded two-step BALD. Overall, we show that learned acquisition can make adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads.

</details>


### [246] [CLARITree: Cholesky and Lookahead Accelerations for Regression with Interpretable Piecewise Linear Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12840)
*Yixiao Wang,Hayden McTavish,Varun Babbar,Margo Seltzer,Cynthia Rudin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel algorithm for near-optimal sparse piecewise linear regression trees that combines lookahead search with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates, achieving superior scalability and a favorable accuracy-efficiency-sparsity trade-off over existing optimal methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimal regression tree methods (dynamic programming, branch-and-bound) yield better performance than greedy induction but are computationally prohibitive for general linear regression trees. Recent lookahead strategies have improved runtime in classification, but adapting them to achieve near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees with better scalability remains an open challenge.
