Method: The authors propose a lookahead-style search algorithm for building piecewise linear regression trees, augmented with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates to the Gram matrix for fast model refitting during the search process.

Result: The method demonstrates a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity. It scales significantly better than the current state of the art, with both theoretical and empirical support.

Conclusion: Combining lookahead search with rank-one Cholesky updates enables near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees at substantially reduced computational cost, advancing the practical applicability of optimal tree methods beyond classification into the linear regression domain.

Abstract: Regression trees are among the most interpretable yet expressive model classes in machine learning. Historically, greedy induction has been the dominant approach for constructing well-performing regression trees. While optimal methods based on dynamic programming and branch-and-bound exist, they are computationally prohibitive for general linear regression trees, despite often achieving substantially better performance than greedy approaches. Recent work has shown that specialized lookahead strategies can dramatically improve runtime while maintaining near-optimal performance, primarily in classification settings. In this work, we develop a novel algorithm for near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees that combines a lookahead-style search strategy with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix. We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that our method achieves a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity, and scales significantly better than the current state of the art.

</details>


### [247] [TimeROME-DLM: Temporal Causal Tracing and Low-Rank Inference-Time Knowledge Editing for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12841)
*Zhengtao Yao,Liuyang Song,Hongbo Zhang,Chenhao Wei,Haoyan Xu,Guang Yang,Siheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeROME-DLM introduces the first training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge editing framework for masked diffusion language models, using causal tracing at specific denoising timesteps and a closed-form low-rank residual edit memory, achieving strong forgetting performance while preserving retain-set utility across multiple MDLM architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge-editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) are designed exclusively for autoregressive transformers and fail on masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) because iterative denoising violates their assumptions, and gradient-based alternatives either consume excessive VRAM or cause model collapse at standard learning rates.

Method: TimeROME-DLM combines two components: (1) Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal tracing, which identifies the most causal coordinate for a fact's object prediction across denoising steps via interventions; and (2) a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject-key/target-delta pairs for all forget facts, applying a single ridge-regularized update with sparsification at the traced coordinate during every diffusion forward pass, with frozen backbone weights and only three tuned hyperparameters (α, λ, q).

Result: On TOFU forget01 with LLaDA-8B-Base, forget-set log-probability drops ~83 nats while retain-set log-probability stays within ~1 nat. The method transfers zero-shot to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B, handles 50 sequential facts with flat retain performance, scales sub-linearly to 400 facts, and achieves 4–14× wall-clock speedup with zero extra VRAM over the best training-time baseline.

Conclusion: TimeROME-DLM successfully closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs at a fraction of the computational cost, establishing the first practical inference-time knowledge editing framework for masked diffusion language models.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) such as LLaDA now rival autoregressive (AR) LLMs, but every existing knowledge-editing and unlearning method (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) targets AR transformers and either makes assumptions that fail under iterative denoising, or requires gradient updates whose backward-pass activations cost tens of GB of extra VRAM and which collapse MDLMs at standard learning rates. We introduce TimeROME-DLM, the first training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge-editing framework for MDLMs. It couples two components: a Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal-tracing protocol that identifies, for each fact, the coordinate whose intervention most strongly drives the object prediction at later denoising steps; and a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas across all forget facts and applies a single ridge-regularised update at that coordinate at every diffusion forward, with sparsification to limit utility spillover. Backbone weights stay frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned on a small validation split. On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM cuts forget-set log-probability by roughly 83 nats. The same configuration transfers to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. It keeps retain-set log-probability nearly flat (within ~1 nat at the utility-safe operating point) across 50 sequentially inserted facts, delivers a four- to fourteen-fold wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM over the strongest converged training-time baseline, and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts. TimeROME-DLM closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs at a fraction of the computational cost.

</details>


### [248] [Interpretable Factor Decomposition for Decision Intelligence in Large-Scale Financial Markets: Evidence from China's A-Share Market](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12843)
*Xiao Han,Yao Xiao,Zhen Zhang,Moxuan Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An interpretable XGBoost pipeline with TreeSHAP attribution decomposes Chinese A-share equity return predictability, revealing behavioral signals dominate (58.2%) over valuation ratios (10.7%). SHAP and ablation analyses diverge in ways that reveal feature substitutability structures invisible to either method alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the black-box nature of machine learning models in asset pricing by creating an interpretable framework that audibly decomposes cross-sectional return predictability into factor contributions, and to understand the relative importance of different signal categories (behavioral vs. fundamental) in the Chinese equity market.

Method: XGBoost model trained on 60-month rolling windows with 55-month out-of-sample periods applied to 3,632 Chinese A-share stocks (2009–2019). TreeSHAP is used for model attribution and factor contribution decomposition across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis is conducted to cross-validate SHAP rankings, with divergence analysis revealing feature substitutability patterns.

Result: XGBoost achieves mean AUC of 0.547 and a top-vs-bottom quintile long-short spread of +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; annualized Sharpe 2.23). Alpha persists after Carhart four-factor adjustment (+2.31%/month; t = 7.48). SHAP decomposition shows behavioral signals (turnover, momentum) contribute 58.2% of predictive attribution versus 10.7% for valuation ratios. Ablation cross-validates rankings but diverges from SHAP, exposing feature substitutability invisible to either method alone.

Conclusion: Machine learning models can be made interpretable and auditable for cross-sectional return prediction. Behavioral factors overwhelmingly dominate valuation ratios in Chinese A-share markets. SHAP and ablation analyses should be used jointly, as their divergence reveals meaningful feature interaction and substitutability structures that single-method approaches would miss.

Abstract: We present an interpretable machine learning pipeline to decompose Cross-Sectional Equity Return Predictability into auditable factor contribution. We apply an XGBoost model with TreeSHAP attribution and conduct stress testing on 3632 Chinese A-share stocks from 2009 until 2019. Using 60-month, rolling windows over 55 months of out-of-sample data, XGBoost obtains a mean AUC of 0.547 and +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; Annualized Sharpe 2.23) long-short spread for the top vs bottom quintiles. This alpha is persistent after adjusting for the Carhart four-factor model (+2.31%/month; t = 7.48). SHAP Decomposition indicates that behavioral signals (turnover and momentum) account for 58.2% of predictive attribution compared to 10.7% for valuation ratios, on average, across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis serves to cross-validate this ranking and provides evidence that SHAP and ablation diverge in a manner that highlights feature substitutability structure that is largely invisible to either method used in isolation.

</details>


### [249] [Multimodal Graph Negative Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12863)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphMNL addresses node-level branch semantic imbalance in multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) by using negative learning as cross-branch guidance — teaching inferior branches which classes are unlikely — rather than forcing them to imitate potentially biased dominant predictions. It achieves SOTA performance (72.47% acc, 76.60 F1) on Grocery/Reddit M datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In MAGs, different modality branches (topology, text, images) vary in semantic reliability across nodes. Existing methods rely on cross-branch agreement or alignment, using the dominant branch as supervision — but when the dominant branch is biased, this forced imitation propagates errors and suppresses useful semantics in other branches.

Method: GraphMNL introduces: (1) a branch library to characterize each branch; (2) graph-aware reliability arbitration to distinguish dominant vs. inferior branches per node; (3) gating of unstable transfer; and (4) target-preserving negative learning, where supervised loss learns the correct class while negative loss suppresses unlikely non-target classes when branch agreement is unreliable.

Result: GraphMNL achieves best performance with 72.47% accuracy and 76.60 F1 score on Grocery datasets and Reddit M datasets.

Conclusion: Decoupling target supervision from branch guidance via negative learning effectively mitigates branch semantic imbalance in MAGs, avoiding bias propagation while preserving useful modality-specific semantics.

Abstract: Multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) integrate graph topology with heterogeneous modality attributes, such as text and images, thereby enabling richer modeling of complex relational systems. However, such expressiveness also makes learning on MAGs depend on multiple semantic sources, including structural topology, textual and visual attributes, each of which can be regarded as a branch for node representation. Node-level branch semantic imbalance arises when these branches differ across nodes in semantic informativeness and reliability: a branch that provides discriminative semantics for one node may mislead another due to bias in modality quality or structural context. Existing methods often mitigate such heterogeneity through cross-branch agreement or alignment, implicitly treating the dominant prediction as reliable supervision. When the dominant branch is biased, forced imitation may propagate its bias to other branches and suppress original semantics that are useful for classification. We propose GraphMNL, a graph-aware multimodal negative learning framework that addresses this issue by using Negative Learning as cross-branch guidance. Instead of forcing inferior branches to imitate a teacher prediction, the model teaches them which classes a node is unlikely to belong to. GraphMNL builds a branch library, identifies dominant and inferior branches via graph-aware reliability arbitration, gates unstable transfer, and applies target-preserving negative learning over non-target classes. This design decouples target supervision from branch guidance so that supervised losses learn the correct class, while Negative Learning suppresses unlikely alternatives when branch agreement is unreliable. Through the comprehensive experimental evaluation, GraphMNL achieves the best performance on Grocery datasets with 72.47% accuracy and 76.60 F1 score on Reddit M datasets.

</details>


### [250] [SMGFM: Spectral Multimodal Graph Pretraining for Multimodal-Attributed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12867)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMGFM is a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes node signals into graph-frequency bands, assigns semantic roles via topology-conditioned routing, and performs band-level cross-modal interaction to disentangle structure-induced and modality-intrinsic semantics in multimodal-attributed graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs), structure-induced semantics (topology-consistent, low-frequency) and modality-intrinsic semantics (fine-grained, high-frequency) contribute differently to downstream tasks. Traditional coupling of topology with node features forces uniform smoothing, which erases modality-specific distinctions. The key challenge is identifying semantic roles before cross-modal fusion.

Method: SMGFM leverages graph-frequency variation as a prior: low-frequency components capture topology-consistent semantics, high-frequency preserves modality-specific semantics. It (1) constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens using scalable Chebyshev filters, (2) estimates coupling reliability via topology-conditioned routing, (3) performs band-modality interaction before fusion, and (4) employs frequency-routed objectives that align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes.

Result: Extensive experiments on MAG datasets show SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across both graph-level and modality-level tasks.

Conclusion: Disentangling semantics by frequency band before cross-modal fusion effectively resolves the tension between structure-induced smoothness and modality-specific distinctiveness, and the spectral routing strategy provides a principled prior for semantic role assignment in multimodal graph learning.

Abstract: Multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs) couple graph topology with node semantics from text, images, and other modalities. Traditional graph learning contextualizes node semantics by coupling topology with node features. However, this coupling design becomes troublesome in MAGs, where structure-induced and modality-intrinsic semantics may contribute differently to downstream tasks. Structure-induced semantics promote relational consistency through smooth topological variation, whereas modality-intrinsic semantics often encode local, fine-grained distinctions that should not be uniformly smoothed or aligned. Therefore, the key challenge is to identify semantic roles before cross-modal fusion. To this end, we leverage graph-frequency variation as a prior, where low-frequency components capture topology-consistent semantics and high-frequency components preserve modality-specific semantics. Based on this intuition, we propose SMGFM, a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes each modality-specific node signal into graph-frequency bands and assigns band-level semantic roles before cross-modal interaction. Concretely, SMGFM constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens with scalable Chebyshev filters, estimates their coupling reliability through topology-conditioned routing, and performs band-modality interaction before fusion. Its frequency-routed objectives align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes, mitigating spatial-domain entanglement and uniform cross-modal alignment. Extensive experiments conducted on the MAG datasets demonstrate that SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across graph-level and modality-level tasks.

</details>


### [251] [Multi-Bitwidth Quantization for LLMs Using Additive Codebooks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12876)
*Liza Babaoglu,Shuangyi Chen,Ashish Khisti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Drop-by-Drop is a post-training quantization framework that produces a single LLM checkpoint supporting multiple inference-time bitwidths, using Matryoshka-style supervision over additive codebooks grounded in successive refinement theory, achieving competitive perplexity with reduced storage overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are deployed across diverse hardware with varying resource constraints, yet current quantization methods typically produce fixed-bitwidth models, requiring separate checkpoints for each precision level. There is a critical need for a single model that can adaptively trade off performance and efficiency at inference time without retraining.

Method: Drop-by-Drop frames multi-bitwidth quantization through the lens of successive refinement from information theory, establishing that Gaussian-distributed LLM weights can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are added under a weighted MSE distortion. The method employs additive codebooks with Matryoshka-style supervision in the loss function, where ordered subsets of codebooks yield accurate partial reconstructions at each precision level. A single checkpoint serves multiple bitwidths.

Result: Drop-by-Drop significantly reduces storage and memory overhead by requiring only one checkpoint for multiple bitwidths. It maintains competitive perplexity and accuracy across major LLM architectures including Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

Conclusion: Drop-by-Drop provides a theoretically grounded and practically effective solution for adaptive multi-bitwidth LLM deployment, enabling flexible precision control from a single post-training quantized model without compromising performance.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across heterogeneous hardware with varying resource constraints, the ability to adaptively manage the trade-off between performance and efficiency without retraining is critical. We propose Drop-by-Drop, a novel multi-bitwidth post-training quantization framework that enables inference-time precision control over LLM weights from a single trained model. Our method is theoretically grounded in information theory and successive refinement. We establish that LLM weights, which commonly follow a Gaussian distribution, can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are incorporated, under a weighted mean squared error distortion motivated by LLM loss functions. To realize this in practice, Drop-by-Drop incorporates Matryoshka-style supervision into the loss function, exploiting the structure of additive codebooks. Drop-by-Drop produces a single model where ordered subsets of codebooks yield accurate partial reconstructions at each precision level. This approach significantly reduces storage and memory overhead by allowing a single checkpoint to serve multiple bitwidths, while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures, such as Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

</details>


### [252] [LongSpike: Fractional Order Spiking State Space Models for Efficient Long Sequence Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12895)
*Xinrui He,Qiyu Kang,Xuhao Li,Zheng-Jun Zha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongSpike introduces fractional-order state-space modeling (f-SSM) into spiking neural networks, breaking the "memoryless" bottleneck of first-order ODE-based SNNs to better capture long-range dependencies, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on long-sequence benchmarks while maintaining sparse, energy-efficient computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dominant SNN architectures rely on first-order ODEs for neuronal dynamics, which impose a "memoryless" bottleneck that severely limits their ability to capture complex, long-range dependencies in long-sequence tasks. The authors aim to overcome this fundamental limitation by incorporating richer temporal memory mechanisms.

Method: The authors propose LongSpike, which integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling (f-SSM) from control theory into SNNs. By extending integer-order SSMs to the fractional-calculus regime, the framework enables hierarchical integration of neuronal dynamics with long-memory kernels. A state-space formulation is leveraged to support efficient, parallel training, mitigating the computational overhead and parallelization challenges of fractional operators.

Result: LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy on the Long Range Arena (LRA) benchmark, large-scale WikiText-103 language modeling, and Speech Commands, while preserving sparse synaptic computation.

Conclusion: Fractional-order SSMs provide a principled and effective way to equip SNNs with long-memory capabilities, overcoming a core architectural bottleneck. The approach is both accurate and computationally practical, with code publicly released.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well-regarded for their biological plausibility and energy efficiency in processing sequential data. However, dominant SNN architectures typically rely on first-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) to govern neuronal state transitions. This first-order assumption imposes a "memoryless" bottleneck, limiting the model's capacity to capture the complex, long-range dependencies inherent in long-sequence tasks. In this work, we propose LongSpike, a novel SNN framework that integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling, or f-SSM, from control theory into the spiking domain. By extending traditional integer-order SSMs to the fractional-calculus regime, LongSpike enables the hierarchical integration of neuronal dynamics with long-memory kernels. To mitigate the computational overhead and parallelization challenges typically associated with fractional operators, we leverage a state-space formulation that supports efficient, parallel training. Empirical evaluations on challenging benchmarks, including Long Range Arena (LRA), large-scale WikiText-103, and Speech Commands, demonstrate that LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy while preserving sparse synaptic computation. The code is available at https://github.com/xinruihe389-commits/LongSpike.

</details>


### [253] [Selecting Samples on Graphs: A Unified Dataset Pruning Framework for Lossless Training Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12913)
*Dongyue Wu,Zilin Guo,Xiaoyu Li,Jiajia Liu,Jingdong Chen,Nong Sang,Changxin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a unified graph-based dataset pruning framework that models datasets as weighted graphs, casting pruning as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP). A greedy solution with formal approximation guarantees outperforms existing methods, reducing training time by over 40% on ImageNet-1k without accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dataset pruning criteria fall into two categories — intrinsic signals (sample-independent assessment) and extrinsic signals (diversity via pairwise relations) — each capturing only one aspect of sample utility. These methods lack robustness across varying pruning ratios and data distributions. A unified framework that jointly models both intrinsic and extrinsic sample value is needed.

Method: The dataset is modeled as a weighted graph where node weights encode intrinsic value and edge weights encode extrinsic (pairwise) value. Dataset pruning is then formulated as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP). Although MWCP is NP-hard, the authors derive a principled greedy algorithm based on sample-wise marginal gains. Under mild conditions, they prove a formal approximation guarantee that applies broadly across importance metrics.

Result: The proposed method outperforms existing dataset pruning approaches across different pruning ratios. On ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50, it reduces training time by over 40% without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: A unified graph-based formulation that jointly considers intrinsic and extrinsic sample values, with theoretical approximation guarantees, yields a robust and effective dataset pruning method that significantly reduces computational training cost.

Abstract: The rapid growth of modern training datasets has significantly increased computational cost, motivating dataset pruning~(DP) methods which retain only a subset of informative samples to reduce training cost.
  Existing pruning criteria typically rely on either intrinsic signals that assess samples independently or extrinsic signals that promote diversity via pairwise relations.
  While effective in their own specific regimes, each captures only one aspect of sample utility and lacks robustness across different pruning ratios or data distribution.
  In this work, we present a unified graph-based DP framework.
  By modeling the dataset as a weighted graph, where node weights encode intrinsic value and edge weights encode extrinsic value, DP can be cast as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP).
  Although MWCP is NP-hard, its structure admits a principled greedy solution based on sample-wise marginal gains.
  Under a few mild conditions, we further prove that this unified objective enjoys a formal approximation guarantee, which applies to a broad family of importance metrics and provides practical design guidelines.
  Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing DP methods while substantially reducing training cost, reducing training time by over 40\% without sacrificing accuracy on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50.

</details>


### [254] [Where Computation Lives Inside TabPFN: Causal Localisation of Attention Head Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12917)
*Atharva Gupta,Dhruv Kumar,Murari Mandal,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First mechanistic analysis of TabPFN 2.5 reveals temporal specialization in attention heads: one head dominates causal necessity by 2-5× at its peak layer, with the peak shifting by task complexity. Contrastive activation steering fails to transfer across samples, attributed to TabPFN's context-dependent attention mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular foundation models like TabPFN remain mechanistic black boxes. While mechanistic interpretability has advanced in language and vision models, tabular models lack similar causal analyses. The authors aim to open the black box by investigating how TabPFN's attention heads distribute computation across layers and whether interpretability tools from language models (e.g., activation steering) transfer to this domain.

Method: The authors apply three core techniques: (1) activation patching to localize causally necessary computations to specific attention heads and layers; (2) ablation studies to measure each head's causal necessity; (3) attention entropy to track information concentration across layers. Experiments use two synthetic regression datasets of varying complexity. They also test contrastive activation steering for inference-time control and analyze why it fails to transfer.

Result: A single attention head dominates causal necessity, exceeding others by 2–5× at its peak layer. The dominant head's peak layer shifts depending on task complexity. Remaining heads show symmetric late-layer activation profiles. Attention entropy and patching converge on identifying the dominant head's active layers. Contrastive activation steering successfully steers within-sample but fails to generalize across samples.

Conclusion: TabPFN exhibits temporal specialization where one head carries most computational burden, with its active layer adapting to task complexity — a form of dynamic resource allocation. The failure of cross-sample activation steering suggests TabPFN relies on in-context learning via context-dependent attention patterns rather than stable parametric directions, fundamentally distinguishing its mechanism from language models and posing challenges for direct transfer of LLM interpretability techniques.

Abstract: We present the first causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model, investigating how TabPFN 2.5's feature wise attention heads distribute computation across layers. Using activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy across two synthetic regression datasets, we find clear temporal specialisation: one head's causal necessity dominates that of the others by 2 to 5 times at peak layer, with its dominant layer shifting across tasks of different complexity, while the remaining heads exhibit symmetric late layer profiles. Attention entropy and patching provide convergent evidence for the computationally active layers of the dominant head. We additionally investigate inference time steerability via contrastive activation steering, which fails to transfer across samples. We attribute this result to TabPFN's in context learning mechanism, which encodes task structure through context dependent attention rather than the stable parametric directions that make steering tractable in language models.

</details>


### [255] [LoRA-Muon: Spectral Steepest Descent on the Low-Rank Manifold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12921)
*Franz Louis Cesista,Katherine Crowson,Cédric Simal,Stella Biderman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA-Muon adapts the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank setting, creating a LoRA variant that transfers optimal learning rates across ranks, width, depth, and factor rescaling. A rank-2 proxy recovers the dense best learning rate, and rank-32 beats dense baselines in TinyShakespeare.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA with AdamW is sensitive to initialization choices, has poor learning rate transfer across ranks, and often underperforms dense baselines. The authors aim to develop a low-rank finetuning method that better proxies full-rank Muon/Shampoo optimizers and resolves these tuning difficulties.

Method: The authors derive LoRA-Muon by applying Muon's spectral steepest-descent update rule to the low-rank setting, paired with a split weight-decay rule. They also analyze Spectron (showing its dependence on arbitrary factor scaling) and LoRA-RITE (showing its QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update, but LoRA-Muon avoids QR decomposition and second-moment storage).

Result: In compute-matched TinyShakespeare experiments, a rank-2 LoRA-Muon proxy recovers the dense optimal learning rate, and a rank-32 LoRA-Muon run achieves lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in seed-averaged sweeps. Optimal learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor rescaling.

Conclusion: LoRA-Muon serves as an effective low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers, with robust hyperparameter transfer properties. It is more accelerator-friendly and memory-efficient than LoRA-RITE by avoiding QR decomposition and second-moment storage.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) significantly reduces compute and memory costs for finetuning Deep Learning models but is often harder to tune than dense training: when using factor-wise optimizers such as AdamW, it is sensitive to initialization choices, its optimal learning rates transfer poorly across ranks, and it often fails to beat dense baselines. We derive LoRA-Muon by applying the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank setting. Along with our split weight-decay rule, our main claim is that LoRA-Muon is a good low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers. Its optimal learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor-rescaling. In our compute-matched TinyShakespeare study, a rank-$2$ proxy recovers the dense best tested learning rate, and a rank-$32$ LoRA-Muon run attains lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in the seed-averaged sweep. We further show that the Spectron optimizer depends on arbitrary factor scaling, so it would likely be a poor fit when finetuning starts from badly imbalanced factors, and that LoRA-RITE's simplified QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update. LoRA-Muon computes that update without QR-decomposition and avoids storing second moments, making it more accelerator-friendly and memory-efficient.

</details>


### [256] [Order Is Not Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12923)
*Gareth Seneque,Lap-Hang Ho,Nafise Erfanian Saeedi,Jeffrey Molendijk,Tim Elson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that current AI alignment approaches conflate "order" with "control." True control requires a receiver-gated response law—a denominator-indexed operator mapping material state, action, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. Evidence from biological systems (mouse, C. elegans, zebrafish), LLM output generation, adapter-based steering, and stochastic operators supports local admitted control but does not establish deployable pre-generation control, hidden causal sufficiency, or cross-domain coordinate identity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify the distinction between merely identifying order-inducing objects in neural systems (as done in alignment, interpretability, and steering work) and demonstrating genuine control. The authors contend that control requires a specific structural condition—a receiver-gated response law—that has not been adequately established in existing literature, leading to overclaims about what perturbation and steering studies actually demonstrate.

Method: The paper proposes a formal framework centered on a denominator-indexed response operator and tests it across four empirical panels: (1) biological neural systems (mouse ALM, C. elegans, zebrafish) providing physical response-operator evidence; (2) LLM panels measuring generated-output response laws across four material conditions, with component-sign prediction accuracy; (3) constitution-conditioned adapters as prepared media reshaping susceptibility; and (4) stochastic-operator panels separating measured opportunity from deployable action policies. The framework defines control as occurring when finite effort moves a target class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive responses, invalid formats, overdrive, and unnecessary effort remain bounded.

Result: LLM response vectors were predictable at 72.8–73.7% component-sign accuracy across four material conditions, rising to 84.3–84.8% on nonzero components. Held-out observers predicted system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy respectively. Biological panels excluded coordinate identity and controller conclusions while providing response-operator evidence. Constitution-conditioned adapters demonstrated reshaped susceptibility as prepared media. Stochastic-operator panels successfully separated measured opportunity from deployable action policies.

Conclusion: The evidence supports a driven-dissipative response-system account at the mesoscopic control level: drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers, producing admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. Local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators are supported. However, deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities remain outside the demonstrated scope.

Abstract: AI alignment, interpretability, steering, and neural perturbation studies identify order-inducing objects. We argue that order is not control. Control requires a receiver-gated response law: a denominator-indexed operator mapping material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. We identify it across biological, LLM, adapter, and stochastic-operator panels. The laws are local: an intervention can be admitted, saturated, sign-changing, leaky, or overdriven depending on medium, bath, receiver state, action port, and comparator. Control is assigned when finite effort moves a target or outcome-readout class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive, invalid format, overdrive, and unnecessary effort stay bounded. Mouse ALM, C. elegans, and zebrafish panels provide physical response-operator evidence while excluding coordinate identity and controller conclusions. LLM panels show generated-output response laws: across four material conditions, response vectors are predictable at 72.8-73.7% component-sign accuracy, rising to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy. Constitution-conditioned adapters reshape susceptibility as prepared media, and stochastic-operator panels separate measured opportunity from deployable action policies. This gives a driven-dissipative response-system account at the mesoscopic control level: drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers, producing admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. The evidence supports local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators, while leaving deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities outside scope.

</details>


### [257] [Is Spurious Correlation Removal Always Learnable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12930)
*Yibo Zhou,Bo Li,Hai-Miao Hu,Hanzi Wang,Xiaokang Zhang,Ruifan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a computational barrier in invariant learning: even when the invariant subspace is statistically identifiable, polynomial-time algorithms can fail while exhaustive search succeeds with polynomial samples. The authors quantify the role of environment diversity via a separation parameter γ, derive minimax risk bounds, characterize a phase transition under label shifts, and validate predictions on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Invariant learning aims to learn representations that are stable across environments, but existing theory largely focuses on identifiability rather than computational feasibility. The authors seek to understand when invariant learning is computationally hard despite being statistically possible, and to formally characterize how environment diversity affects learnability and estimation error.

Method: The authors construct a reduction from a hypothesized hard primitive (black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery) to show a conditional computational barrier: multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional invariant subspace (k=1) are polynomial-sample learnable by exhaustive search but not by any constant-accuracy polynomial-time algorithm. They introduce a separation parameter γ to measure environment diversity, derive minimax lower bounds under local Gaussian regularity, and characterize a phase transition at sample size n* ∝ k(d-k)/(|E|γ²) under label-induced distribution shifts.

Result: A conditional computational hardness result is established for invariant learning. The minimax risk for subspace recovery is derived as Θ(k(d-k)/(n|E|)), and a phase transition is identified at n* proportional to k(d-k)/(|E|γ²), with error scaling as 1/γ² beyond the transition. Synthetic and real-data experiments confirm the predicted theoretical gaps and transitions, and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

Conclusion: Invariant learning exhibits a fundamental computational-statistical gap: polynomial-sample learnability does not imply polynomial-time learnability. Environment diversity, captured by γ, is a key quantity governing both identifiability and the sharpness of estimation. The phase transition and diversity diagnostics provide practical guidance for assessing when invariant learning can succeed in finite samples.

Abstract: Invariant learning can fail even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable. We show a conditional computational barrier: under a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive motivated by average-case sparse-recovery reductions, there exist \emph{samplable} multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional predictive invariant subspace ($k=1$) that are learnable with polynomial samples by exhaustive search, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery algorithm would contradict the primitive. We further quantify environment diversity by a separation parameter $γ$, which controls identifiability and the curvature of invariance objectives. Under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity, the minimax risk is $\mathbb{E}[\dist(\hat{V},V_{\mathrm{inv}})^2]=Θ(k(d-k)/(n|\mathcal{E}|))$, and under label-induced shifts a phase transition occurs at $n^*\propto k(d-k)/(|\mathcal{E}|γ^2)$ with refined estimation error scaling proportional to $1/γ^2$. Synthetic and real datasets illustrate the predicted gaps and transitions and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

</details>


### [258] [Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12966)
*Achyuthan Sivasankar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSD (Frequency Synchronization Degree) is a novel, circuit-agnostic metric that detects Fourier circuit synchronization in transformers on modular arithmetic 500–3,000 steps before grokking occurs, making it the earliest known predictor. The inter-phase gap is causally shown to be a regularization phenomenon governed by Δt ∝ 1/λ, and architecture ablations confirm the precursor is a multi-block circuit property absent in MLP-only models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grokking in transformers on modular arithmetic is attributed to Fourier circuit formation, but the timing, causal structure, and controllability of this transition remain poorly understood. Existing metrics like excluded loss detect the phenomenon only after it begins, leaving a need for earlier, causal predictors.

Method: The authors introduce the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalized, permutation-tested metric quantifying Fourier circuit synchronization without requiring prior circuit knowledge. They test FSD across nine modular addition configurations (primes p ∈ {53, 71, 97, 113, 131}, three seeds each). Causal intervention involves forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay λ, measuring Δt to grokking. Architecture ablations compare attention-only, MLP-only, and single-layer transformer variants.

Result: FSD synchronizes 500–3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps, sign-test p ≈ 0.004) and precedes excluded loss in all nine cases. Forking experiments reveal Δt ∝ 1/λ (R² = 1.00 and 0.99 in two clean cases), consistent with (1/λ)·log(||W_mem||/τ). Attention-only models grok with strong FSD precursors; MLP-only models never grok; single-layer models show lagging FSD, confirming the precursor as a multi-block circuit property.

Conclusion: FSD is the earliest available predictor of grokking, providing direct causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is controlled by weight decay regularization. The scaling law Δt ∝ 1/λ offers both predictive and mechanistic insight, and architecture ablations establish that grokking requires multi-block attention circuits rather than MLP-only structures.

Abstract: Grokking -- where a transformer on modular arithmetic suddenly transitions from near-chance to near-perfect validation accuracy -- is attributed to a Fourier circuit, but its timing, causal structure, and controllability remain poorly understood. We introduce the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalised, permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronisation requiring no prior circuit knowledge. Across nine modular addition configurations (primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131}, three seeds), FSD synchronises 500-3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; all nine positive, sign-test p~0.004), and precedes a restricted-logit loss baseline (Nanda et al.'s excluded loss) in all nine cases, making it the earliest available predictor. We provide direct causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is a regularisation phenomenon: forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay lambda produces strictly monotone earlier grokking, with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda. This law replicates across three primes (p in {53,97,131}; R^2=1.00 and R^2=0.99 for two clean cases), captured as Delta_t ~ C/lambda, consistent with (1/lambda)*log(||W_mem||/tau). Architecture ablations show an attention-only model groks with a strong FSD precursor; an MLP-only model never groks; a single-layer model's FSD lags, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

</details>


### [259] [Predicting Cognitive Load from Speech and Interaction Dynamics in Dyadic Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12971)
*Tahiya Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study examines whether speech and interaction dynamics in natural dyadic conversations can predict perceived cognitive load, using audio from 53 dyads performing collaborative tasks. A two-head GRU encoder trained on static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features reveals that conversational interaction signals—particularly turn-taking dynamics and participation balance—are predictive of temporal and mental demand, emphasizing the role of task structure and interaction in modeling cognitive load beyond controlled lab settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on estimating cognitive load from speech has been confined to controlled laboratory environments, leaving a gap in understanding how reliably these methods extend to natural, collaborative conversations where spontaneous interaction dynamics may provide additional predictive signals.

Method: Audio recordings from 53 dyads engaged in nine distinct collaborative tasks were analyzed. Features were extracted across three categories: static acoustic features, dynamic speech features, and conversational interaction features. These were used to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) encoder to predict cognitive load scores across multiple dimensions.

Result: Conversational interaction features proved informative for predicting cognitive load. Temporal demand was significantly associated with turn-taking dynamics such as speech overlap and speaker switches, while mental demand correlated with imbalanced participation between speakers. Interaction-derived signals complemented traditional acoustic features.

Conclusion: Cognitive load modeling in natural collaborative settings benefits substantially from incorporating conversational interaction features alongside acoustic and dynamic speech features. Task structure and turn-taking behavior are critical for accurately estimating perceived cognitive load dimensions like time pressure and mental effort.

Abstract: Estimating cognitive load from speech has largely been studied in controlled laboratory settings, with limited understanding of its reliability in natural collaborative conversations. We investigate whether speech and interaction dynamics predict perceived cognitive load during dyadic conversations. We analyze audio from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks and extract static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit encoder to predict cognitive load scores. Results show conversational interaction provides useful signals for predicting cognitive load related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand is associated with turn-taking dynamics such as overlap and speaker switch, while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers. These findings highlight the importance of task structure and conversational interaction for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings.

</details>


### [260] [EPM-JEPA: Operator-Side Experience Modulation in JEPA-Family World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12979)
*Vedant Pandya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compares two mechanisms for injecting experience into JEPA world models under distribution shift: operand-side (EI-JEPA) vs. operator-side LoRA modulation (EPM-JEPA). EPM-JEPA's advantage over the no-memory baseline is ~1.90% while EI-JEPA underperforms it. However, the pre-registered comparison between EPM-JEPA and EI-JEPA yields a null result. The core contribution is a dynamical analysis revealing three independent processes driving the D_shift trajectory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard JEPA-family world models employ static predictors that cannot adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training. The authors aim to investigate how accumulated experience can be incorporated into a JEPA predictor under distribution shift, and which mechanism — operand-side injection or operator-side modulation — is more effective.

Method: Two architectures are compared: EI-JEPA (operand-side), which adds a compressed experience representation as a residual to the predictor's hidden state, and EPM-JEPA (operator-side), which uses the same representation to generate low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights. Evaluation uses Moving MNIST under a gravity shift distribution, with pre-registered comparison at n=50 steps using three seeds. The D_shift trajectory is decomposed into three dynamical processes.

Result: Pre-registered comparison: EPM-JEPA (0.7848 ± 0.0078) vs. EI-JEPA (0.8238), Δ = 4.74% — a null result by stated criterion. Secondary observation: EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over no-memory baseline (0.8000) consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline. D_shift trajectory analysis reveals three independent processes: buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021, rather than convergence to equilibrium.

Conclusion: Weight-level modulation (EPM-JEPA) shows a specific and consistent benefit over the baseline, unlike operand-side injection. However, the primary contribution is mechanistic: the D_shift trajectory is governed by three distinct dynamical processes, not equilibrium convergence. This motivates PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing the identified dynamical-peak limitation.

Abstract: JEPA-family world models use a static predictor whose weights do not adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training. We compare two mechanisms for incorporating accumulated experience into a JEPA predictor under distribution shift: operand-side injection, where a compressed experience representation is added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state (EI-JEPA), and operator-side modulation, where the same representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights (EPM-JEPA). On a pre-registered comparison (Moving MNIST, gravity shift), EPM-JEPA (D_shift^{n=50} = 0.7848 +/- 0.0078, three seeds) differs from EI-JEPA (0.8238) by delta = 4.74% - Outcome C: a null result - by our stated criterion, a valid outcome. As a secondary, non-pre-registered observation, EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over a no-memory baseline (0.8000), consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline, indicating the benefit is specific to weight-level modulation. Our primary contribution is a mechanism analysis: the D_shift^{n=50} trajectory reflects three independent dynamical processes - buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021 - rather than convergence to equilibrium. These findings motivate PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing this dynamical-peak limitation.

</details>


### [261] [Exposure Bias as Epistemic Underidentification in Recursive Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12990)
*Riku Green,Zahraa S. Abdallah,Telmo M Silva Filho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recursive forecasting suffers not only from distribution shift but also from epistemic underidentification: one-step supervision fails to determine correct behavior on self-generated induced states. The authors formalize this via induced states and provenance variables, decomposing error into mismatch, approximation, and provenance gaps, and show provenance-aware correction can improve closed-loop performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard framing of recursive multi-step forecasting error as "distribution shift" (train on observed histories, deploy on own predictions) is incomplete. The authors argue there is a deeper, underexplored problem: even with deterministic latent dynamics, one-step Bayes-optimal supervision may not identify the correct recursive predictor because self-generated states during rollout lack the provenance information needed to determine correct local targets.

Method: The authors introduce a formalism based on induced states Z and provenance variables P. They prove that under partial observability or state truncation, one-step supervision identifies behavior only on observed contexts, not on self-generated induced states. They derive an error decomposition for induced states into three components: (1) teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, (2) representation–class approximation error, and (3) provenance information gap. Empirically, they demonstrate the induced-state regime, define a local corrective task on fixed induced states, and explore provenance-aware correction using a simple binary provenance encoding.

Result: Rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime where fixed induced states define a local corrective task. Closed-loop gains arise both from local adaptation and from changing the induced states visited. Provenance-aware correction yields conditional (not uniform) improvements, demonstrating that reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty can be operationally addressed.

Conclusion: Exposure bias in recursive forecasting should be recast as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty, not merely distribution shift. The induced-state and provenance framework provides a more complete diagnostic and corrective lens, with provenance-aware methods offering conditional but tangible gains.

Abstract: Recursive multi-step forecasting is usually framed as distribution shift: models are trained on observed histories but deployed on their own predictions. We show this framing is incomplete by proving that, under partial observability or state truncation, recursive rollout is also an epistemic underidentification problem. Even with deterministic latent dynamics, one-step Bayes supervision identifies behavior only on observed contexts and need not identify the deployed recursive predictor once rollout queries self-generated induced states whose correct local targets are not determined by numeric state alone. We formalize this with induced states $Z$ and provenance variables $P$, and derive a decomposition of induced-state error into teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation--class approximation, and provenance information gaps. Empirically, we show that rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime, that fixed induced states define a distinct local corrective task, and that closed-loop gains arise not only from local adaptation but also from changing the induced states visited during rollout. Using a simple binary provenance encoding, provenance-aware correction can further improve performance, though gains are conditional rather than uniform. These results recast exposure bias as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty.

</details>


### [262] [DeepJEB++: Foundation Model-Driven Large-Scale 3D Engineering Dataset via 2D Latent Space Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12994)
*Soyoung Yoo,Leekyo Jeong,Jinsu Ra,Dongeon Lee,Sunwoong Yang,Hyogu Jeong,Namwoo Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeepJEB++ is a foundation-model-driven data augmentation framework that expands a small set of jet engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset by augmenting in 2D latent space, lifting to 3D, and automating FEA labeling — achieving a 40× expansion from <400 to 15,360 designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven engineering design is constrained by the scarcity of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. Existing 3D augmentation methods struggle to preserve subtle geometric variations, and automating simulation labeling is difficult because boundary conditions vary with geometry.

Method: A three-stage framework: (1) Fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders, synthesize novel views via latent interpolation, and filter with a VLM quality filter; (2) Lift validated 2D images to 3D meshes using a domain-adapted generative foundation model; (3) An automated pipeline recognizes load/bolt interfaces on each mesh and assigns finite-element labels (mass, stress, displacement) without manual intervention.

Result: Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ generates 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets — a 40× expansion — using only a single GPU per stage. Quality is evaluated on manufacturability, label fidelity against SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency.

Conclusion: DeepJEB++ demonstrates that foundation-model-driven augmentation in 2D latent space, followed by 3D lifting and automated labeling, can overcome data scarcity in engineering design. The resulting dataset will be publicly released to advance reproducible engineering-AI research.

Abstract: Data-driven engineering design is constrained by the lack of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. In particular, existing 3D data augmentation techniques have limitations in preserving subtle and diverse geometric variations, and it remains difficult to automate the subsequent simulation-labeling process, where boundary conditions vary depending on the generated geometry. We present DeepJEB++, a foundation-model-driven data-augmentation framework that expands a small seed set of jet engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset under constrained resources. Our key idea is to augment in the data-rich 2D latent space, then transfer to 3D. In Stage 1, we fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders and synthesize novel views by latent interpolation, retaining manufacturable designs through a vision-language-model (VLM) quality filter. In Stage 2, the validated images are lifted to 3D meshes by a domain-adapted generative foundation model. In Stage 3, an automated pipeline recognizes the load and bolt interfaces on each mesh and assigns finite-element labels -- mass, stress, and displacement -- without manual intervention. We assess augmentation quality along three intrinsic axes: manufacturability, label fidelity against the SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency. Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets -- a 40x expansion -- using a single GPU per stage. The dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

</details>


### [263] [Reliability of Probabilistic Emulation of Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12997)
*Sam F. Greenbury,Radka Jersakova,Paolo Conti,Marjan Famili,Christopher Iliffe Sprague,Edwin Brown,Jason D. McEwen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Comparison of probabilistic forecasting approaches for physical systems: CRPS-trained ensembles provide more reliable uncertainty quantification and faster inference than latent-space generative models, while ambient-space generative models match CRPS coverage but with higher latency. The authors release AutoCast and AutoSim frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While both generative models (diffusion/flow matching) and CRPS-trained ensembles have shown strong predictive accuracy for physical systems, the reliability of their uncertainty estimates has not been systematically evaluated. This gap motivates a rigorous, matched-budget comparison.

Method: The authors develop an evaluation framework comparing generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems under matched model size and computational budget. They assess uncertainty reliability via empirical coverage of predictive intervals, alongside accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. Both ambient-space and latent-space training regimes are examined for both approaches.

Result: CRPS-trained ensembles achieve more reliable uncertainties (better empirical coverage) than latent-space generative models on both single-step and autoregressive rollouts, with significantly faster inference. Ambient-space generative models exhibit comparable coverage to CRPS ensembles but with much higher inference latency. CRPS ensembles trained in latent space show minimal coverage degradation versus ambient space.

Conclusion: CRPS-trained ensembles offer an advantageous balance of reliability, accuracy, and computational efficiency for probabilistic emulation of physical systems. The release of AutoCast and AutoSim aims to standardize future benchmarking and accelerate research in this area.

Abstract: Two dominant approaches have emerged for generating probabilistic forecasts of physical systems: generative models, such as diffusion or flow matching; and ensembles of deterministic models with stochasticity injected, trained using the continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) loss. While both approaches have demonstrated strong predictive accuracy, the reliability of their uncertainties has not been systematically assessed. We address this gap by developing a framework to evaluate both approaches across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, under matched model size and computational budget. We assess the reliability of probabilistic emulation by inspecting the empirical coverage of predictive intervals, while also considering accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. CRPS-trained ensembles typically achieve more reliable uncertainties on both single-step prediction and autoregressive rollouts, demonstrating better coverage than the standard alternative of training generative models in a latent space. Moreover, the CRPS approach offers significantly faster inference. When generative models are trained in ambient rather than a compressed latent space, which is often infeasible for high-dimensional problems, they exhibit comparable coverage to CRPS-trained ensembles, though with substantially larger inference latency. In contrast, when CRPS-trained ensembles are trained in latent space they do not show a marked degradation in coverage with respect to ambient space. Both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles demonstrate good predictive accuracy. To facilitate future research and application, we release AutoCast, a modular framework implementing both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles, alongside AutoSim, a flexible dataset generation package for rapid prototyping.

</details>


### [264] [scLLM-DSC: LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13007)
*Ping Xu,Pengjiang Li,Tian Du,Zaitian Wang,Jiawei Gu,Ziyue Qiao,Pengfei Wang,Yuanchun Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: scLLM-DSC is a cross-modal deep structural clustering framework that integrates LLM-derived gene semantics with transcriptomic topological structure via contrastive alignment, significantly outperforming 11 SOTA methods on scRNA-seq clustering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scRNA-seq clustering methods are semantically agnostic—they rely purely on numerical statistical patterns and ignore the biological functions encoded by genes. While LLMs possess rich semantic capabilities, a structural mismatch exists between their generative pre-training objectives and the discriminative nature of cell clustering tasks, preventing direct adaptation.

Method: scLLM-DSC combines two complementary views: (1) a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View using NCBI gene priors and Cell2Sentence contextual embeddings, and (2) a Structure-Aware Topological View from a graph-guided encoder. These are integrated via a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism that enforces consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features in a unified latent space.

Result: Extensive benchmarks show scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

Conclusion: Injecting LLM-derived gene-level semantic knowledge into deep structural clustering effectively resolves semantic agnosticism in scRNA-seq analysis, and cross-modal contrastive alignment is a viable strategy to bridge generative LLMs with discriminative downstream tasks.

Abstract: Clustering is fundamental to scRNA-seq analysis, serving as a cornerstone for identifying cell populations and resolving tissue heterogeneity. However, existing methods focus on mining numerical statistical patterns, suffering from semantic agnosticism by neglecting the intrinsic biological functions encoded by genes. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer promising semantic capabilities, their direct adaptation to cell clustering is hindered by the structural mismatch between generative pre-training objectives and discriminative downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose scLLM-DSC, a novel LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering framework. Diverging from data-driven paradigms, scLLM-DSC establishes a semantically-grounded representation by synergizing two views: a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View derived from NCBI gene priors and contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings, and a Structure-Aware Topological View extracted via a graph-guided encoder. Crucially, we introduce a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism to enforce consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features within a unified latent space. Extensive benchmarks demonstrate that scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

</details>


### [265] [CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13024)
*Bo Liu,Di Dai,Jingwei Liu,Jiarui Jin,Xiaocheng Fang,Guangkun Nie,Hongyan Li,Shenda Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalMoE is a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that uses a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts to handle distribution shifts in time series, combined with Causality-Aware Self-Attention for sparse graph recovery and multimodal alignment via LLMs/VLMs, achieving SOTA on causal discovery benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural Granger Causal Discovery methods use a one-size-fits-all paradigm that fails to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes in real-world time series, resulting in entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. There is a need for a model that explicitly addresses patch-level heterogeneity and regime-specific dynamics.

Method: CausalMoE introduces: (1) Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts — dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics; (2) Causality-Aware Self-Attention — operates across variables with proximal optimization to yield sparse Granger causal graphs; (3) First integration of LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors for regularizing causal estimation.

Result: CausalMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance on fully supervised Granger causal discovery benchmarks and effectively generalizes to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

Conclusion: Modeling patch-level heterogeneity via a mixture-of-experts architecture, combined with causality-aware attention and multimodal priors, significantly improves Granger causal discovery, particularly under distribution shifts and data-scarce regimes.

Abstract: Granger Causal Discovery (GCD) is fundamental for analyzing temporal dependencies in complex systems. However, existing neural GCD methods predominantly rely on a "one-size-fits-all" paradigm, struggling to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes inherent in real-world time series. This often leads to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. In this paper, we propose CausalMoE, a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly models patch-level heterogeneity. CausalMoE introduces a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts, which dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, effectively decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics. To ensure interpretable graph recovery, we design a Causality-Aware Self-Attention mechanism operating across variables, yielding sparse Granger causal graphs via proximal optimization. Furthermore, CausalMoE is the first to integrate LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors, regularizing causal estimation in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CausalMoE establishes a new state-of-the-art on fully supervised benchmarks, while effectively generalizing to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

</details>


### [266] [TWLA: Achieving Ternary Weights and Low-Bit Activations for LLMs via Post-Training Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13054)
*Zhixiong Zhao,Zukang Xu,Zhixuan Chen,Xing Hu,Zhe Jiang,Dawei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TWLA is a post-training quantization framework that achieves 1.58-bit ternary weight compression and 4-bit activation quantization for LLMs. It combines asymmetric ternary quantization via manifold optimization, Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weight/activation distributions, and inter-layer-aware mixed-precision allocation to prevent cascading accuracy degradation, enabling end-to-end inference acceleration while maintaining high accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM ternarization methods keep activations at high precision due to heavy-tailed activation distributions, which fundamentally limits end-to-end inference acceleration. The goal is to simultaneously quantize both weights to ternary (1.58-bit) and activations to 4-bit without severe accuracy loss.

Method: TWLA integrates three components: (1) E2M-ATQ — a two-stage asymmetric ternary quantizer that first solves a Euclidean-space optimization then refines on the ternary manifold to minimize layer-output error; (2) KOTMS — applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into tri-modal distributions suitable for ternarization while statistically suppressing activation outliers; (3) ILA-AMP — uses adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs to guide bit allocation, jointly optimizing for layer-wise disparities in activation quantization gain caused by the shared orthogonal transform, preventing error cascades from weak layers.

Result: Extensive experiments show TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4 settings and delivers significant end-to-end inference acceleration compared to baselines.

Conclusion: TWLA enables practical end-to-end low-bit inference for LLMs by simultaneously addressing weight ternarization and activation quantization, overcoming the bottleneck of activation precision that limited prior ternarization methods.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit exceptional general language processing capabilities, but their memory and compute costs hinder deployment. Ternarization has emerged as a promising compression technique, offering significant reductions in model size and inference complexity. However, existing methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions and therefore keep activations in high precision, fundamentally limiting end-to-end inference acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose TWLA, a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight compression and 4-bit activation quantization while maintaining high accuracy. TWLA comprises three components: (1) Euclidean-to-Manifold Asymmetric Ternary Quantizer (E2M-ATQ) minimizes layer-output error under weight ternarization via a two-stage optimization from Euclidean initialization to manifold relocation; (2) Kronecker Orthogonal Tri-Modal Shaping (KOTMS) applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into ternary-friendly tri-modal distributions, while the shared rotation statistically suppresses activation outliers; and (3) Inter-Layer Aware Activation Mixed Precision (ILA-AMP) explicitly introduces adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs in bit allocation and jointly optimizes for the layer-wise disparity of activation quantization gains induced by the shared orthogonal transform, preventing cascades triggered by a few weak layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4, while delivering significant inference acceleration. The code is available at <https://github.com/Kishon-zzx/TWLA>.

</details>


### [267] [A green solvent screening tool for emerging materials via uncertainty aware, transformer enhanced transfer learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13060)
*Ioannis Kouroudis,Simon Ternes,Zhaosu Gu,Gohar Ali Siddiqui,Marina Ustinova,Angelo Lembo,Alessio Gagliardi,Aldo Di Carlo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a transfer learning approach using a pre-trained foundational model (trained on QM9) to predict solubility descriptors like Hansen solubility parameters, dielectric constant, and Gutmann donor/acceptor numbers, even with limited data. Uncertainty quantification is integrated, and a deployable screening tool for green solvent substitution is provided, successfully rediscovering known alternatives and proposing new eco-friendly candidates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solvent usage is projected to rise due to emerging technologies (organic/hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, catalysis), making green solvent substitution critical. However, machine learning for solubility prediction is severely constrained by limited data on key parameters, necessitating methods that perform well under data scarcity.

Method: The authors transfer a foundational model pre-trained on QM9 targets to predict solubility descriptors. The pipeline incorporates uncertainty quantification for confidence assessment. This transfer learning strategy enables high performance even when target-specific training data is extremely sparse (e.g., Gutmann Donor/Acceptor numbers).

Result: High predictive performance was achieved on data-rich targets (Hansen solubility parameters, dielectric constant) and, critically, on data-scarce targets (Gutmann Donor/Acceptor numbers). The model augmented solubility descriptor data by orders of magnitude. The deployed screening tool successfully rediscovered known green solvents and identified new candidate substitutes.

Conclusion: Transfer learning from QM9-pretrained models effectively overcomes data limitations in solubility prediction. The integration of uncertainty quantification and a user-friendly screening tool enables practical, high-throughput identification of green solvent alternatives, with demonstrated success in both validating known substitutes and discovering novel ones.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of solubility remains a central challenge across materials science and sustainable chemistry. In particular due to emerging technologies like organic and hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, and catalysis, solvent usage is expected to increase significantly within the coming years. Therefore, substituting solvents with greener alternatives is vital. This is where machine learning can have substantial impact. However, the limited data on critical parameters of solubility significantly constraints machine learning efficacy. In this work, we transfer a pre-trained foundational model on QM9 targets to our application with minimal data requirements. Additionally, the pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification, allowing the user to gauge the confidence of the predictions. As baseline, we succeed in predicting the Hansen solubility parameters and Dielectric Constant for which extensive databases exist. Importantly, we achieve high model performance on additional targets, such as Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers, where the available data is extremely limited. Overall, we augment data on solubility descriptors by orders of magnitude with high quality predictions. For effective dissemination, we deploy easy-to-use, easily integrateable with high throughput labs, customizable tool for ranking and screening possible solvent substitutes. Finally, we rediscovered known green solvent alternatives and proposed new candidates proving its relevance for finding eco-friendly solvents.

</details>


### [268] [Limits of spectral learning under noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13067)
*Sabin Roman,Ljupco Todorovski,Saso Dzeroski,Marta Sales-Pardo,Roger Guimera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Noise in sparse spectral regression induces a predictable drift in learned coefficients governed by the effective number of active modes; a universal degradation curve emerges, revealing a fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spectral methods for function approximation lack a rigorous understanding of how noise affects the stability of estimated coefficients. The paper aims to characterize this stability theoretically and derive predictable degradation behavior.

Method: Supervised regression with additive label noise using sparse spectral representations across multiple bases (Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, Haar) and dimensions. After whitening the empirical feature geometry, a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors is derived analytically and validated numerically.

Result: A closed-form overlap expression reveals a universal degradation curve parameterized by a single intrinsic noise scale. Numerical experiments across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases confirm the theory. A fundamental noise threshold is identified beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable.

Conclusion: Spectral learning exhibits intrinsic limits for recovering functional structure from noisy data, characterized by a universal noise threshold that places fundamental constraints on scientific inference via spectral methods.

Abstract: Learning functional relationships from noisy data is a central problem in scientific inference.
