We first demonstrate Jeffrey guidance by targeting a prescribed embedding distribution. With Inception embeddings as the target, this leads to substantial reductions in FID on both CIFAR-10 and FFHQ. We further apply Jeffrey guidance to fairness on CelebA-HQ, updating an unconditional diffusion model to enforce independence between attributes.

</details>


### [292] [To GAN or Not To GAN: Segmentation Analysis on Mars DEM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13252)
*Douglas Dziedzorm Agbeve,Aditya V. Handrale,Salim Fares,Seif E. Idani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses automated detection of mounds on Mars using neural network-based semantic segmentation. The authors compare a supervised semantic segmentation model against a generative adversarial approach, finding that augmenting training data with artificially generated samples does not improve detection performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual mapping of Martian mounds onto Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) is labor-intensive. Automating mound detection is essential for enabling smoother rover navigation and for identifying morphological evidence of past water or life-conducive environments on Mars.

Method: The authors employ two neural network-based semantic segmentation methodologies: (1) a standard supervised semantic segmentation model, and (2) a generative adversarial network (GAN) approach that synthetically generates additional training data. Performance is compared to assess whether artificial data augmentation yields improvements over purely supervised learning.

Result: The generative adversarial approach, which adds artificially generated data, did not improve detection results compared to the supervised semantic segmentation model alone.

Conclusion: Automated semantic segmentation can effectively detect Martian mounds from DEMs. However, naive data augmentation via GAN-generated samples does not enhance model performance, suggesting that real labeled data quality and quantity remain the primary drivers of accuracy in this domain.

Abstract: To better understand Martian Surface, which is needed to enable Rovers navigate Mars with ease, it is necessary to be able to determine the location of mounds. Detecting and studying these morphologies can also help us find evidence of extraterrestrial life, in this case, more specifically, water or signs of life conducive environments. Detection of mounds was done by manually mapping morphological parameters onto Digital Elevation Models. This paper solves the problem by automatically detecting and or predicting mounds on Mars using Neural Network based Semantic Segmentation methodologies. This is done by using supervised semantic segmentation model and generative adversarial approach. A comparison of the approaches shows that adding extra artificially generated data did not improve the result.

</details>


### [293] [Extracting Governing Equations from Latent Dynamics via Multi-View Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13260)
*Paolo Muratore,Mackenzie Weygandt Mathis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DYSCO is a multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that recovers latent dynamical systems from noisy high-dimensional observations by leveraging multiple independent views of the same underlying process. It jointly recovers latent trajectories and governing dynamics, supports symbolic equation discovery, and provides theoretical identifiability guarantees under noisy nonlinear observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recovering latent dynamical systems from noisy high-dimensional measurements is a fundamental challenge spanning representation learning, system identification, and scientific discovery. Prior methods lack theoretical guarantees under realistic noisy nonlinear observation settings, and cannot simultaneously recover latent trajectories, governing dynamics, and symbolic equations in a principled manner.

Method: DYSCO employs multi-view temporal contrastive learning: it assumes multiple independent noisy views of the same underlying latent process. By contrasting views, it disentangles signal from noise. The dynamics are parameterized in a structured functional basis, enabling symbolic recovery of governing equations within an affine gauge. The approach builds on and extends nonlinear independent component analysis (ICA) ideas to temporal dynamics.

Result: The method demonstrates accurate recovery of latent trajectories and flow fields across diverse dynamical regimes (chaotic, oscillatory, metastable) under both Gaussian and Poisson observation noise. It provides theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to an affine indeterminacy, extending prior identifiability results to noisy nonlinear observations.

Conclusion: DYSCO offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective framework for jointly recovering latent dynamics and symbolic governing equations from noisy multi-view observations, with particular relevance to neuroscience applications involving Poisson noise in neural recordings.

Abstract: Identifying latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements is a central problem at the intersection of representation learning, system identification, and scientific discovery. We present DYSCO, a multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that jointly recovers latent trajectories and the governing dynamics from such observations, by leveraging multiple independent noisy views of the same underlying process to disentangle signal from noise. By parameterizing the dynamics in a structured functional basis, our framework further enables symbolic recovery of the governing equations within an affine gauge. We offer theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to an affine indeterminacy, extending prior identifiability results to the realistic setting of noisy nonlinear observations. Empirically, we demonstrate accurate recovery of both latent trajectories and flow fields across a diverse set of dynamical regimes (e.g., chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable) under both Gaussian and Poisson observation noise, the latter being particularly relevant for neural recordings.

</details>


### [294] [Different Layers, Different Manifolds: Module-Wise Weight-Space Geometry in Transformer Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13276)
*Kirato Yoshihara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether different transformer modules benefit from distinct manifold constraints during optimization. Testing layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram (Differential Gram) geometries on GPT-2 pretraining, it finds that Stiefel-constrained attention + DGram-constrained MLP yields optimal performance, while the reverse or all-DGram configurations destabilize due to singular value growth and attention softmax saturation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing manifold optimization methods typically apply the same geometric constraint uniformly across all weight matrices in a neural network. The authors challenge this one-size-fits-all assumption, hypothesizing that functionally distinct transformer submodules (attention vs. MLP) may have different geometric preferences due to their different roles in forward computation.

Method: The authors study Manifold Muon — a manifold-constrained optimizer — for GPT-2 pretraining. They systematically compare layer-wise assignments of two manifold constraints: the Stiefel manifold (orthonormality constraint, W^T W = I) and the DGram manifold (differential Gram constraint on singular values). They evaluate four configurations: all-Stiefel, all-DGram, Stiefel-attention + DGram-MLP, and DGram-attention + Stiefel-MLP, under a shared hyperparameter setting, and analyze the failure modes via singular value dynamics and attention logit statistics.

Result: A clear asymmetry emerges: Stiefel on attention layers + DGram on MLP layers achieves the best performance. The inverted assignment (DGram-attention + Stiefel-MLP) and the all-DGram configuration become training-unstable. The failure is attributed to uncontrolled singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which inflates attention logit magnitudes, leading to softmax saturation — where attention distributions collapse toward one-hot, degrading gradient flow and training stability.

Conclusion: Symmetry-aware and geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform. Attention layers favor the isometric constraints of the Stiefel manifold, while MLP layers tolerate or benefit from the non-isometric DGram geometry. This calls for heterogeneous manifold assignment strategies in future optimizer design.

Abstract: Weight-space geometry plays a central role in neural network optimization, yet manifold constraints are often applied uniformly across all weight matrices. In this work, we ask whether different transformer modules prefer different manifold geometries. We study Manifold Muon for GPT-2 pretraining and compare layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram constraints across attention and MLP blocks. Our results show a clear asymmetry: constraining attention layers with Stiefel geometry while assigning DGram geometry to MLP layers gives the best performance among the tested configurations, whereas the inverted assignment and all-DGram configuration become unstable under the shared hyperparameter setting. We trace this failure to singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which can amplify attention logits and induce softmax saturation. These findings suggest that symmetry-aware and geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform.

</details>


### [295] [Once-for-All: Scalable Simultaneous Forecasting via Equilibrium State Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13285)
*Beinan Xu,Andy Song,Jiti Gao,Feng Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ESE is a novel multi-system forecasting paradigm that first estimates a global equilibrium state, then predicts all systems simultaneously based on deviations from equilibrium, achieving SOTA accuracy with 10-70x speedup and linear-time complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world scenarios (economics, healthcare) often involve multiple interacting systems requiring coordinated forecasts, but existing methods predict one system at a time, which is inefficient and fails to exploit inter-system dependencies.

Method: ESE operates in a single pass: it first estimates the equilibrium state across all interacting systems, then generates holistic forecasts for all systems based on the discrepancy between each system's current state and the estimated equilibrium.

Result: ESE matches or exceeds SOTA accuracy on synthetic and real-world datasets (currency exchange, COVID-19 spread). It achieves 10-70x speedup over conventional predictors, scales with linear-time complexity as the number of systems grows, and remains robust under diverse perturbations.

Conclusion: ESE is a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method that can seamlessly integrate with conventional predictors, offering a compelling trade-off between accuracy and efficiency for simultaneous multi-system forecasting.

Abstract: We introduce Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE), a novel paradigm for simultaneous prediction, where multiple interacting systems require separate yet coordinated forecasts. Such scenarios often arise in real-world settings such as economics and healthcare modeling. Unlike existing approaches that predict one system at a time, ESE forecasts all systems in a single pass. It first estimates the equilibrium state across systems, then generates holistic forecasts based on the difference between the current state and the estimated equilibrium. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, including currency exchange and COVID-19 spread modeling, demonstrate that ESE is at least as accurate as state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods while being significantly faster. In addition, ESE integrates seamlessly with conventional predictors, combining their accuracy with its exceptional efficiency and delivering a 10-70x speedup. With linear-time complexity, ESE scales far better than SOTA methods as the number of systems increases. Moreover, it remains accurate under diverse perturbations, establishing ESE as a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method.

</details>


### [296] [Clipping Makes Distributed and Federated Asynchronous SGD Robust to Stragglers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13287)
*Samuel Erickson,Mikael Johansson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides theoretical justification for why gradient clipping stabilizes asynchronous SGD (ASGD) training. Using a sub-Weibull noise model that captures heavy-tailed gradient noise in deep learning, the authors prove that clipping removes dependence on maximum delay in oracle complexity and establish both convergence in expectation and, for the first time in async optimization, high-probability convergence guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ASGD maximizes hardware utilization by eliminating idle time waiting for stragglers, but slow workers cause large update delays that degrade convergence under constant step sizes. Practitioners have empirically observed that gradient clipping stabilizes asynchronous deep learning training, yet a rigorous theoretical explanation was lacking. The authors aim to fill this gap by formally proving that clipping mitigates the negative impact of delayed gradients.

Method: The authors model gradient noise using a sub-Weibull distribution, which generalizes sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential assumptions to accommodate the empirically observed heavy-tailed noise in deep learning. Under this model, they analyze ASGD with gradient clipping and prove that the oracle complexity becomes independent of the maximum delay (a key pain point in vanilla ASGD). They derive convergence guarantees both in expectation and, notably, with high probability—the latter being a first in the asynchronous optimization literature.

Result: Clipping eliminates the dependence on maximum delay in the oracle complexity of ASGD under sub-Weibull gradient noise. The paper establishes convergence in expectation and provides the first high-probability convergence result for asynchronous optimization.

Conclusion: Gradient clipping provides a theoretically sound remedy for the instability caused by delayed updates from slow workers in ASGD. The sub-Weibull noise model proves to be an effective framework for analyzing heavy-tailed gradient behavior, and the high-probability convergence result marks a significant theoretical advance in asynchronous optimization.

Abstract: In modern machine learning, parallelization of training is an important strategy for increasing scale. Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (ASGD), which maximizes the utilization of available hardware by avoiding waiting for slow workers. However, with constant step sizes, the convergence of ASGD is nonetheless affected negatively by slow workers due to large delays in updates. At the same time, it has been empirically observed in asynchronous training of deep learning models that gradient clipping "stabilizes" training. In this work, we provide a theoretical justification for this behavior, as we show that clipping removes the dependence of the maximum delay in the oracle complexity. We employ a sub-Weibull model of gradient noise which generalizes sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential distributions to more heavy-tailed distributions, motivated by empirical observations in deep learning. We show convergence in expectation, and the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.

</details>


### [297] [Quantizing Time-Series Models As Dynamical Systems: Trajectory-Based Quantization Sensitivity Score](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13300)
*Mariya Pavlova,Harrison Bo Hua Zhu,Elizsveta Semenova,Yingzhen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TQS introduces a dynamical-systems stability perspective to post-training quantization, enabling a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from quantizer selection, and TQS-PTQ provides a calibration-free mixed-precision framework for low-precision deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional PTQ methods couple sensitivity analysis to the quantization procedure itself, preventing decoupled sensitivity estimation and making quantization budget planning difficult for black-box or compiled networks. A stability-based perspective is needed to analyze error propagation independently.

Method: The authors model network forward propagation as a discrete-time dynamical system and propose the Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), which quantifies how quantization errors amplify over the rollout horizon. TQS enables a priori sensitivity estimation without calibration data or second-order approximations. They then build TQS-PTQ, a mixed-precision framework that assigns bit-widths based on these stability-derived sensitivities.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that the dynamical-systems perspective yields robust, high-performing mixed-precision quantization, suitable for resource-constrained deployment, without requiring calibration data or costly second-order approximations.

Conclusion: A dynamical-systems framing of quantization sensitivity provides an effective, decoupled pathway for mixed-precision PTQ, enabling flexible low-precision deployment even for black-box models.

Abstract: We introduce the Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), a metric that reframes post-training quantization (PTQ) through the lens of dynamical-systems stability. By modeling the network's rollout as a discrete-time dynamical system, TQS characterizes how quantization-induced errors propagate and amplify over the rollout horizon. Unlike conventional PTQ methods, where sensitivity analysis is often coupled to the quantization procedure, TQS enables a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from quantizer selection and bit-width assignment. This separation allows for quantization budget planning even for black-box or compiled networks with fused operators. Building on this, we present TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision framework that requires no calibration data or costly second-order approximations. Our experiments show that a dynamical-systems perspective provides a robust, high-performing pathway for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained settings.

</details>


### [298] [Rarity-Gated Context Conditioning for Offline Imitation Learning-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13311)
*Yongmin Kim,ByeongHoon Jeon,Sungil Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGFiLM addresses contextually-biased anomaly detection by modulating feature representations with a rarity-gated conditioning module, where context rarity (estimated from empirical distributions) controls how strongly context influences model decisions, achieving superior F1-FPR trade-off on maritime trajectory anomaly detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical anomaly detection deployments suffer from highly imbalanced context distributions, where rare but critical contexts cause context-conditioned models to produce unstable predictions and excessive false alarms. Standard conditioning mechanisms fail to account for this frequency bias.

Method: RGFiLM (Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation) integrates Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) — context-conditioned scaling and shifting of hidden features — with a learned gating mechanism driven by a data-driven rarity score. The rarity score is estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables. Under rare contexts, the gate amplifies contextual modulation (more decisive); under frequent contexts, it remains conservative, mitigating over-sensitivity to common regimes.

Result: Evaluated on maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario, RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1-FPR trade-off compared to both context-agnostic and context-conditioned baselines.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling context rarity via a gated conditioning mechanism is an effective strategy for reducing false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection under imbalanced context distributions.

Abstract: Contextual anomaly detection aims to identify abnormal behavior conditional on context variables, but practical deployments often face highly imbalanced context distributions where rare regimes can be critical information. Under such frequency bias, context-conditioned models can produce unstable decisions and excessive false alarms in rare contexts. We propose Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation (RGFiLM), a rarity-aware conditioning module that combines feature-wise modulation (i.e., context-conditioned scaling and shifting of hidden features) with a gate controlled by a data-driven rarity score. The rarity score is estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables and regulates how strongly context modulates intermediate representations: the gate becomes more decisive under rare contexts while remaining conservative under frequent contexts. We evaluate RGFiLM on maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario. When instantiated in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline, RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1--False Positive Rate (FPR) trade-off among the compared context-agnostic and context-conditioned methods. These results suggest that explicitly accounting for context rarity is an effective approach for reducing false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection.

</details>


### [299] [Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13338)
*Kaijie Xu,Anqi Wang,Xilin Dai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PowerPhase is a large-scale probabilistic forecasting benchmark using AC power-flow data from transmission grids with up to 36,964 channels, introducing constraint-aware metrics that reveal a safety-fidelity trade-off. PowerForge, a proposed scenario-based quantile forecaster with causal bridging, achieves top average rank across all grids.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multivariate forecasting benchmarks are too small (≤2,000 channels) and lack domain-specific evaluation: power-system benchmarks miss either temporal structure or probabilistic assessment. There is a need to evaluate probabilistic forecasters at scale under real operational constraints like AC power-flow physics.

Method: The authors construct PowerPhase from six transmission grids (2,000–36,964 channels) where target trajectories are outputs of AC power-flow solves. They propose constraint-aware metrics (Safety_mBrier, NECV, CVaR-alpha) alongside standard probabilistic scores (CRPS, Distortion). They also introduce PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster featuring type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge linking variable groups.

Result: Across eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction metrics rank models differently — a trade-off termed safety-fidelity. PowerForge achieves the best average rank on every grid, demonstrating effective balance between forecast accuracy and operational constraint adherence.

Conclusion: Probabilistic forecasting evaluation must account for domain-specific operational constraints at realistic scale. PowerPhase enables this at unprecedented channel counts, and the safety-fidelity trade-off suggests that model selection should be guided by both distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction jointly.

Abstract: Probabilistic forecasting models are increasingly deployed on multivariate systems with distinct channel physics and operational constraints, but existing benchmarks evaluate neither property at scale. Public canonical multivariate benchmarks cap out at 2,000 channels, while power-system benchmarks either lack temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation. We introduce PowerPhase, a probabilistic forecasting benchmark built on six transmission grids ranging from 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels, more than an order of magnitude beyond popular canonical multivariate benchmarks. Each target trajectory is the output of an AC power-flow solve, and PowerPhase ships with constraint-aware metrics, including Safety_mBrier, NECV, and CVaR-alpha, that complement CRPS and Distortion. Across eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction rank models differently, a trade-off we term safety-fidelity. We further propose PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups, which achieves the best average rank on every grid.

</details>


### [300] [Enhanced Low-Density Region Exploration in Classifier-Guided Diffusion Models Through Modified Reverse Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13347)
*Jagriti Singh,Shekhar Verma,Muneendra Ojha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a training-free, sampling-time method to improve tail coverage in classifier-guided diffusion models by steering reverse trajectories toward low-confidence regions using modified classifier gradients, combined with guidance toward predicted real images to maintain data manifold fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard classifier guidance in diffusion models concentrates probability mass around high-density class means, resulting in poor coverage of rare/tail samples. Existing solutions require training additional networks (e.g., low-density-seeking classifiers with discriminators), while acceleration and distillation techniques do not address long-tail coverage. The authors aim to achieve improved tail coverage purely at sampling time without any additional training.

Method: The method operates in two parts: (1) A density-aware modification of classifier-guided reverse diffusion, where trajectories are steered toward low-confidence (low-density) regions by modifying the classifier gradient — this guidance is applied at the noisy image level rather than on predicted noise as in most diffusion models. (2) A second guidance term that steers sampling toward the predicted real image at each timestep, ensuring generated samples remain close to the real data manifold. Both guidance mechanisms are applied on top of a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, requiring no additional training.

Result: The proposed sampler consistently improves recall for ADM models at 64×64 resolution while maintaining comparable FID. At 256×256 resolution, visual results demonstrate the effects of different combinations of both guidance types. Additionally, standard ADM classifier guidance combined with predicted real image guidance produces high perceptual quality samples at 256×256 on ImageNet.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that training-free, density-aware sampling-time modifications to classifier guidance can effectively improve long-tail coverage in diffusion models. The dual-guidance approach (low-density exploration + real-image manifold guidance) offers a practical alternative to methods requiring additional network training, improving recall without sacrificing FID or perceptual quality.

Abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for high-fidelity image synthesis, particularly in their classifier-free guided and classifier-guided forms. However, standard classifier guidance concentrates probability mass around high-density class mean, leading to poor coverage of rare samples in the tails of the class-conditional distributions. Recent work on diffusion-based tail sampling mitigates this by training an additional low-density-seeking classifier with a synthetic-vs-real discriminator, at the cost of additional networks and training. In parallel, a number of samplers and distillation techniques accelerate or refine diffusion sampling, but do not explicitly address long-tail coverage. We propose a purely sampling-time, density-aware extension of classifier-guided conditional diffusion model that targets low-density regions without any additional training. We have applied guidance at noisy images not on predicted noise like most diffusion models. Starting from a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, we modify the guided reverse dynamics by steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via the modified classifier gradient, and at each time step, we also guide the sampling process toward the predicted real image. 1st guidance helps explore low-probability samples, and 2nd guidance helps to generate samples to be close to the real data manifold. The proposed sampler consistently improves ADM model recall at 64x64 resolution while maintaining a comparable FID, and with a 256x256 ADM model, we showed the results visually with different combinations of both guidance. We also showed that standard ADM classifier guidance, combined with predicted real image guidance, helps generate high perceptual quality samples with a 256x256 ADM model on ImageNet.

</details>


### [301] [VideoMDM: Towards 3D Human Motion Generation From 2D Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13364)
*Amir Mann,Gal Michael Harari,Merav Keidar,Or Litany*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VideoMDM trains a diffusion-based 3D human motion prior using only 2D pose supervision from monocular videos. A noisy 2D-to-3D lifter initializes 3D poses, which are diffused and denoised, then supervised via depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss — shown to be equivalent in expectation to 3D supervision. The method learns a coherent 3D motion manifold during training, nearly matching fully 3D-supervised methods on HumanML3D and generating human-preferred motions on real video datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth 3D human motion capture is expensive and restricted to controlled environments, limiting scalability. The authors aim to train high-quality 3D motion priors directly from abundant monocular video data using only 2D keypoint annotations, eliminating dependency on scarce 3D ground truth.

Method: A pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter generates approximate (noisy) 3D poses from 2D keypoints. These pseudo-3D sequences are corrupted via a forward diffusion process and denoised by the model in 3D. Crucially, the model is supervised entirely in 2D: predicted 3D poses are reprojected and compared against accurate 2D keypoints using a depth-weighted reprojection loss, proven equivalent in expectation to direct 3D supervision under mild assumptions. Standard 3D motion regularizers — velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment — are adapted to the 2D reprojection setting.

Result: On HumanML3D, VideoMDM achieves FID of 0.88 vs 0.54 for fully 3D-supervised MDM, nearly closing the gap. On real video datasets Fit3D and NBA, generated motions are consistently preferred by human evaluators, with strong quantitative metrics.

Conclusion: Training 3D motion diffusion models without 3D ground truth is viable: a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss combined with adapted motion regularizers suffices to learn a coherent 3D motion manifold, enabling scalable motion prior learning from in-the-wild monocular videos.

Abstract: We introduce VideoMDM, a diffusion-based framework that trains 3D human motion priors directly from accurate 2D poses extracted from monocular videos, without any 3D ground truth. A pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter provides approximate 3D pose sequences that serve as a noisy teacher: these are diffused, denoised by the model in 3D, and supervised in 2D by reprojecting the prediction and comparing against accurate keypoints. We show that, under mild assumptions, a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss is equivalent in expectation to direct 3D supervision, and we adapt standard 3D motion regularizers - velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment - to this 2D setting. Unlike methods that lift 2D to 3D only at inference, VideoMDM learns a coherent 3D motion manifold during training. On HumanML3D it nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54); On real video datasets Fit3D and NBA the method learns to generate motions consistently preferred by humans, with strong quantitative results.

</details>


### [302] [Positional Encoding in the Context of Memristor-Based Analog Computation for Automatic Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13379)
*Benedikt Hilmes,Nick Rossenbach,Ralf Schlüter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memristor-based NLP models suffer from ADC distortion caused by large positional encoding values. The authors mitigate this by adjusting ADC weight/precision bit allocation (~50% reduction) or removing encoding-related linear transformations (~30% reduction), without increasing energy consumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memristors enable energy-efficient analog vector-matrix multiplication for neural NLP models, but computations on these devices experience significant distortion during both weight programming and execution. The paper aims to identify and mitigate a specific source of degradation — large output values from transformed positional encodings that cause errors during analog-to-digital conversion (ADC).

Method: Two mitigation strategies are proposed: (1) Adjusting the proportion of weight bits and precision bits in the ADC of specific memristor layers to better accommodate large positional encoding values, reducing execution degradation while maintaining energy consumption. (2) For scenarios where ADC modification is infeasible, removing encoding-related linear transformations to reduce the magnitude of values entering the ADC.

Result: The first method (ADC bit allocation adjustment) achieves approximately 50% relative reduction in execution degradation. The second method (removing linear transformations) yields approximately 30% relative reduction when ADC modification is not possible.

Conclusion: Large output values from transformed positional encodings are a critical bottleneck in memristor-based NLP computation, specifically within the ADC stage. Targeted adjustments to ADC configuration or architectural modifications to the encoding pipeline can substantially mitigate this degradation without energy penalties.

Abstract: Memristors provide a new chance for resource-efficient computation of neural models for natural language processing by enabling analog execution of vector-matrix-multiplication. Yet, computations on these devices are currently subject to larger distortion, both in weight programming and execution. In this work, we identify large output values of transformed positional encodings to cause major degradation within analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) as part of memristor-based computation. By adjusting the proportion of weight and precision bits of the ADC of specific memristor layers, we reduce the degradation of the execution by ~50% relative, while keeping the estimated energy consumption stable. Additionally, we investigate scenarios where the ADC cannot be modified. In that case the degradation can be reduced by ~30% relative after removing encoding-related linear transformations.

</details>


### [303] [Hölder++: Improving the Quality-Coherence Trade-off in Multimodal VAEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13381)
*Huyen Vo,María Martínez-García,Isabel Valera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hölder++ is a multimodal VAE that implements exact Hölder pooling with shared/private latent representations and hierarchical inference, achieving an improved trade-off between generative quality and cross-modal coherence compared to prior methods like MMVAE+.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior multimodal VAEs suffer from a trade-off between generative quality (realism, diversity) and cross-modal coherence (semantic consistency). A recent approach using approximate Hölder pooling improved coherence but slightly hurt diversity. The authors aim to resolve this trade-off by implementing exact Hölder pooling and introducing architectural improvements.

Method: The paper proposes Hölder++ with three innovations: (1) the first exact (non-approximate) implementation of Hölder pooling for multimodal VAEs; (2) Hölder+, an extended architecture that models both shared representations and modality-specific private representations; and (3) Hölder++, which adds hierarchical inference to improve disentanglement between shared and private latent representations.

Result: Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, produces more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

Conclusion: Exact Hölder pooling combined with shared-private latent structure and hierarchical inference effectively addresses the generative quality-coherence trade-off in multimodal VAEs, yielding better generation and more useful latent representations.

Abstract: Existing approaches for multimodal variational autoencoders (VAEs) face a trade-off between generative quality and coherence-i.e., they struggle to generate realistic and diverse samples that, at the same time, are semantically consistent across modalities. A recent work shows that using a simple approximation to Hölder pooling as an aggregation method improves coherence over the SOTA MMVAE+, despite assuming a single shared representation across all modalities. Yet, it slightly compromises sample diversity. Inspired by this insight, we propose Hölder++, a novel multimodal VAE that improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off through: (i) the first implementation of Hölder pooling without any approximation for multimodal VAEs; (ii) an extended architecture that models distinct shared and private (i.e., modality-specific) representations (Hölder+); and (iii) hierarchical inference that further enhances the disentanglement between the shared and private representations (Hölder++). Our experiments corroborate that Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

</details>


### [304] [PolyFlow: Safe and Efficient Polytope-Constrained Flow Matching with Constraint Embedding and Projection-free Update](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13400)
*Jianming Ma,Qiyue Yang,Yang Zhang,Liyun Yan,Zhanxiang Cao,Yazhou Zhang,Yue Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolyFlow is a constrained flow matching framework that enforces polyhedral constraints directly into generative model dynamics using a discrete-time, projection-free architecture, achieving zero constraint violation with high distributional fidelity and reduced inference latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow-based generative models are powerful but struggle in safety-critical physical systems due to strict constraints. Existing post-hoc correction methods are computationally expensive and distort the learned distribution, motivating a need for models with built-in constraint satisfaction.

Method: PolyFlow introduces a discrete-time flow formulation combined with a projection-free architecture. Constraints are embedded directly into the flow dynamics, guaranteeing strict satisfaction of arbitrary polyhedral constraints without iterative solvers, and eliminating discretization errors.

Result: PolyFlow achieves zero constraint violation while maintaining high distributional fidelity across planning and control tasks. Compared to SOTA constrained generation baselines, it significantly reduces inference latency and offers an improved trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality.

Conclusion: PolyFlow demonstrates that embedding polytope constraints directly into flow matching is an effective approach for safety-critical applications, outperforming post-hoc correction methods in both speed and constraint satisfaction without sacrificing generative fidelity.

Abstract: While flow-based generative models have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of domains, deploying them in safety-critical physical systems remains challenging due to strict constraint requirements. Existing approaches typically enforce safety through post-hoc corrections, which incur substantial computational overhead and may distort the learned distribution. We propose PolyFlow, a polytope-constrained flow matching framework that embeds constraints directly into the model and flow dynamics. PolyFlow introduces a discrete-time flow formulation and a projection-free architecture, which eliminate the discretization error and guarantee strict satisfaction of arbitrary polyhedral constraints, without the need for expensive iterative solvers. Experimental results show that PolyFlow achieves zero constraint violation while maintaining high distributional fidelity across a range of planning and control tasks. Compared to state-of-the-art constrained generation baselines, PolyFlow significantly reduces inference latency and demonstrates a favorable trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality. Code is available on https://github.com/MJianM/PolyFlow.

</details>


### [305] [Accelerating Speculative Diffusions via Block Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13426)
*Alexander Soen,Hisham Husain,Valentin De Bortoli,Arnaud Doucet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new method enables efficient speculative sampling for diffusion models by adapting block verification from LLMs, achieving up to 6.3% speedup over existing speculative diffusion methods with a training-free drafter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference but is hard to adapt to continuous diffusions because efficiently sampling the residual distribution — required by the original acceptance-rejection scheme — is non-trivial in continuous space. Existing diffusion adaptations either use inefficient sampling or abandon the original mechanism.

Method: The authors introduce a scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models, crucially enabling block verification (adapted from LLMs) to provably improve draft acceptance rates. They also formalize the Free Drafter, a training-free, heuristic self-speculative drafter.

Result: The proposed Free Drafter with block verification yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative diffusion methods, with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

Conclusion: Efficient speculative sampling with block verification is viable for diffusion models, and the training-free Free Drafter provides meaningful acceleration without extra training cost.

Abstract: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by using a draft model to generate tokens, with an acceptance-rejection scheme that ensures that the output matches the target distribution. Adapting this to continuous diffusions is difficult because speculative sampling requires drawing from a residual distribution. While straightforward in discrete spaces, efficiently sampling this residual in continuous space is non-trivial. Consequently, existing diffusion adaptations either use computationally inefficient sampling techniques or rely on an alternative scheme. In this work, we introduce a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models. Our approach offers a critical advantage over current methods: it enables us to adapt block verification from LLMs to diffusions -- which provably improves the acceptance rate of drafts. Furthermore, we formalize and analyze the Free Drafter, a heuristic self-speculative drafter for diffusions that requires no training. By enabling block verification, our Free Drafter yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

</details>


### [306] [How Much Memory Do We Need? Adaptive Memory Gate for Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13443)
*Jihyeon Hur,Yongseok Kwon,Min-Gi Jo,Jeongwhan Choi,Noseong Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AMGFNO dynamically adjusts memory weight via a learnable gate in neural operators, achieving 55-79% nRMSE reduction at low resolution compared to fixed-weight memory-augmented neural operators, with the gate value automatically decreasing as resolution increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory-augmented neural operators use a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions (resolution, physical parameters like viscosity), which is suboptimal. The authors' preliminary experiments show optimal memory weight varies significantly with resolution and viscosity, motivating an adaptive gating mechanism.

Method: AMGFNO (Adaptive Memory Gated Fourier Neural Operator) extends memory-augmented neural operators by introducing a learnable gate that dynamically modulates memory weight based on input conditions. The architecture is evaluated on Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations.

Result: AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over fixed-weight baselines at low resolution. The learned gate value automatically decays from approximately 0.7 to near-zero as resolution increases, confirming that memory contribution should diminish with higher-resolution observations.

Conclusion: Adaptive memory gating provides a simple yet effective mechanism for making memory-augmented neural operators robust across varying observation conditions, eliminating the need to hand-tune memory weight for each setting.

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful data-driven approach for solving time-dependent PDEs. Among recent advances, memory-augmented neural operators explicitly incorporate past states and have achieved remarkable performance under low-resolution observation settings. However, existing approaches apply a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions, such as resolution or physical parameters, limiting their adaptability. Our preliminary experiments reveal that optimal memory weight varies with resolution and viscosity, implying that a fixed memory weight cannot simultaneously optimize performance across diverse settings. We propose AMGFNO, which dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate. On the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over at low resolution, with the learned gate value automatically decreasing from $\bar{g} \approx 0.7$ to near-zero as resolution increases.

</details>


### [307] [Clustering Node Attributed Networks with Graph Neural Networks and Self Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13444)
*Rodrigo de Sapienza Luna,Daniel Ratton Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-learning GNN framework for attributed graph clustering that iteratively refines both node representations and the graph structure across multiple rounds, outperforming single-round GNN clustering and single-modality methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional graph clustering uses only network topology, but real-world graphs have informative node attributes. Existing GNN-based clustering methods typically train in a single round, leaving potential improvements from iterative refinement unexplored. The authors aim to jointly leverage edges and attributes in a multi-round, fully unsupervised self-learning framework.

Method: A multi-round self-learning framework where each round: (1) a GNN generates node representations, (2) these representations are used to cluster nodes, (3) the resulting clustering influences the graph structure for the next round's representation learning, and (4) a context graph (derived from the original graph) is constructed per round to guide representation generation. The process is fully unsupervised.

Result: On synthetic data, the method extracts information from both edges and attributes, outperforming algorithms using only one modality when neither is highly informative. Multiple rounds consistently improve performance over a single long training round. On real datasets, the method is competitive with state-of-the-art when cluster sizes are balanced.

Conclusion: Iterative self-learning with alternating representation updates and clustering refinement provides a meaningful performance gain over single-round GNN clustering. The framework effectively fuses network and attribute information, particularly in low-information regimes.

Abstract: Graph clustering - partitioning the node set of a graph into disjoint subsets that reflect some latent information - is a fundamental problem as it finds applications in a myriad of different scenarios. While this classic problem has been tackled for decades by different communities, a recent variation of the problem driven by real data considers the scenario where nodes have attributes that are also informative. This has triggered novel methods that simultaneously leverage network information (edges) and node information (attributed) in the design of novel clustering algorithms. This work proposes a novel framework that builds on prior works that have applied graph neural networks (GNN) to graph clustering. The proposed framework operates in rounds of self learning in a fully unsupervised setting. In each round, a GNN generates representations for nodes that are used to cluster the nodes. This clustering influences the graph used to generate the node representation in the next round. Moreover, a context graph built in each round using the original graph is used to generate the node representations. Empirical results show that the proposed methodology extracts information from both network edges and node attributes in synthetic data, outperforming algorithms focused solely on the network or attributes when neither are very informative. Multiple rounds of learning also improve the performance and always outperforms a long single round of training (i.e., classic GNN graph clustering). When considering real datasets, empirical results indicate that the proposed methodology is competitive to state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

</details>


### [308] [Uncertainty Estimation for Molecular Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13451)
*Paul Seij,Christian A. Naesseth,Stephan Mandt,Metod Jazbec*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a post-hoc method for estimating per-sample uncertainty in pretrained molecular diffusion models by applying a Laplace approximation to the denoising network, measuring noise prediction variability across the generation trajectory. The resulting uncertainty score negatively correlates with sample quality and can be used for sample filtering to improve performance at test time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models for 3D molecular generation lack a principled mechanism to signal when generated molecules are likely to be of low quality. This absence hinders reliable deployment and sample selection.

Method: The authors apply a Laplace approximation to the denoising network of a pretrained molecular diffusion model. They then measure the variability (variance) of the noise prediction across the full generation trajectory to derive a per-sample uncertainty score.

Result: The uncertainty score is empirically shown to negatively correlate with established sample-level quality metrics, indicating it is informative of molecular quality. Using this score to filter generated samples improves model performance through test-time scaling.

Conclusion: The proposed post-hoc uncertainty estimation provides a practical, principled signal for identifying low-quality molecular samples, enabling test-time filtering that enhances the utility of pretrained molecular diffusion models without retraining.

Abstract: Diffusion models have seen wide adoption for 3D molecular generation, yet they offer no principled signal of when a generated molecule is likely to be of low quality. We propose a post-hoc method for estimating per-sample uncertainty in pretrained molecular diffusion models. Building on a Laplace approximation of the denoising network, we measure the variability of the noise prediction across the generation trajectory. Empirically, we show that the resulting uncertainty score is informative of sample quality, exhibiting a negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics. We further study how the proposed uncertainty score can be used to filter generated samples, improving model performance via test-time scaling.

</details>


### [309] [Reinforcement Learning for Neural Model Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13461)
*Shaivi Malik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper frames neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem, introducing two environments (MaskWorld and ShiftWorld) where agents learn to modify pretrained networks via reward feedback. The approach is evaluated on bias mitigation and machine unlearning, demonstrating that learned editing policies can match or outperform hand-crafted algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Editing pretrained neural networks typically requires designing task-specific algorithms, which is labor-intensive and time-consuming. The authors aim to replace this manual engineering with a general framework where editing policies are learned automatically through reinforcement learning.

Method: The framework casts neural model editing as an RL problem with two environments: MaskWorld (multiplicative weight scaling via continuous masks) and ShiftWorld (additive weight updates). The reward function balances two objectives: preserving utility on the original task and achieving the editing goal. Agents learn to apply targeted weight modifications using this composite reward signal, eliminating the need for manually designed editing algorithms.

Result: On machine unlearning, learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while retaining over 90% accuracy on the retain set. On bias mitigation, policies improve bias-related metrics by more than 5% without degrading general classification performance.

Conclusion: Neural model editing can be effectively reformulated as a reinforcement learning problem, enabling editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than engineered for each specific task. This opens a general-purpose path for model modification across diverse objectives.

Abstract: Editing pretrained neural networks requires specialized algorithms tailored to specific objectives. Designing such algorithms is often time-consuming and demands significant effort. We present an exploratory framework that formulates neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem, where agents modify models using reward feedback. We introduce two environments: MaskWorld, where agents scale weights multiplicatively, and ShiftWorld, where agents apply additive weight updates. The reward function combines a utility-preservation objective with a task-specific editing objective, enabling agents to learn targeted modifications while maintaining overall model performance. We evaluate the framework on bias mitigation in text classification and machine unlearning in image classification, both of which traditionally rely on specialized algorithms. Our results show that the learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while preserving over 90% retain set accuracy on the unlearning task. In the bias mitigation setting, the learned policies improve bias-related performance by more than 5% while maintaining general classification utility. Our findings show that neural model editing can be cast as a reinforcement learning problem, allowing editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than manually engineered for each task.

</details>


### [310] [CRAFTIIF: Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13486)
*William Smits*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFTIIF is an unsupervised anomaly detection framework for multivariate time series that uses 500 random wavelet features and five Isolation Forests to detect four distinct anomaly types (point, distributional, temporal, collective) with built-in interpretability via branch firing attribution, achieving state-of-the-art VUS-PR performance on the mTSBench benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unsupervised anomaly detection methods for multivariate time series typically target only one or two of the four structurally distinct anomaly types (point, distributional, temporal, collective), lack interpretability, and require dataset-specific tuning. A unified, fully unsupervised framework capable of detecting all four types with automatic thresholding and inherent explainability is needed.

Method: CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws from four wavelet families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each designed to target a specific anomaly type. These type-specific features feed five structured Isolation Forests: one per anomaly type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. An adaptive dual-thresholding mechanism (Otsu/MAD) automatically calibrates detection across wide anomaly rate ranges (0.1%–69.2%). Interpretability is inherent: because each IF is trained exclusively on type-specific features, branch firing directly provides anomaly-type attribution without post-hoc explanation.

Result: On the mTSBench benchmark (19 datasets), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), ranking first among 25 methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, a +40.7% improvement). A diagnostic framework identifies 6 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), the four-branch structure (+20%), and the meta-IF (+23%) are each critical components.

Conclusion: CRAFTIIF demonstrates that a wavelet-based, type-specific Isolation Forest architecture with adaptive thresholding can unify detection of all four anomaly types in a fully unsupervised setting, achieving leading benchmark performance while providing inherent interpretability. The diagnostic framework further reveals fundamental detectability limits that explain performance ceilings across datasets.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is challenged by four structurally distinct anomaly types -- point (isolated spikes), distributional (level shifts), temporal (rhythm changes), and collective (inter-sensor correlation breakdowns) -- each requiring different feature representations. Most unsupervised methods target only one or two types and provide limited interpretability. We present CRAFTIIF (Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest), a fully unsupervised framework targeting all four types without dataset-specific tuning. CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type, feeding five structured Isolation Forests -- one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. An adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibrates detection automatically across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%. Because each IF is trained exclusively on type-specific features, branch firing provides direct anomaly-type attribution by construction, without post-hoc explanation. Evaluated on all 19 datasets of the mTSBench benchmark (Zhou et al., TMLR 2026), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all 19 datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), ranking first among all 25 evaluated methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). A diagnostic framework -- oracle F1, detectability limits, and branch separation ratios -- identifies 6 of 19 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation over 11 conditions confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each essential. Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif

</details>


### [311] [A2D2: Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13565)
*Sophia Tang,Yuchen Zhu,Molei Tao,Pranam Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A2D2 is a reward-guided fine-tuning framework for any-length discrete diffusion models that jointly optimizes insertion and unmasking policies with an adaptive decoding schedule, providing theoretical guarantees for converging to the reward-tilted distribution without target samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While discrete diffusion models have been extended to any-length generation via token insertion, principled reward-guided fine-tuning for such models remains unexplored. Existing methods are limited to fixed-length settings or inference-time guidance, lacking a unified framework with theoretical guarantees.

Method: The authors derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for joint insertion-unmasking path measures to enable convergence to the reward-tilted distribution. They establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable proxies for minimizing decoding error and propose the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure under the reward-tilted target.

Result: A2D2 empirically improves reward optimization, generation flexibility, and accuracy compared to prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

Conclusion: A2D2 provides the first principled framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models, combining theoretical guarantees with practical performance gains in controlled sequence generation.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models offer a simple and stable likelihood-based framework for sequence generation, recently extended to any-length settings via token insertion. Principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion, however, remains largely unexplored. We introduce Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding (A2D2), a unified framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models via joint optimization of the insertion and unmasking policies together with a quality-based inference schedule. We derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for the joint insertion-unmasking path measures, enabling theoretically guaranteed convergence to the intractable reward-tilted sequence distribution without requiring target samples. Building on this, we establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable approaches for minimizing decoding error and introduce the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure that generates the reward-tilted distribution. Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

</details>


### [312] [Adjusted Cup-Product Neural Layer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13568)
*Snigdha Chandan Khilar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the adjusted cup product neural layer — a neural primitive that hardwires the cup product with a higher gauge theory adjustment term, yielding a readout that is gauge invariant by design. The theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle, output depends entirely on the adjustment coefficient; setting it to zero eliminates all output, making the adjustment the sole source of gauge-invariant signal. The observable is proven to be a nonzero quadratic form, exactly invariant under 1- and 2-gauge transformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains. The authors aim to design a neural network layer that respects gauge invariance structurally, rather than learning it from data, ensuring the readout is physically/geometrically meaningful by construction.

Method: The authors propose the adjusted cup product neural layer, which hardwires the standard cup product on cochains together with an adjustment term derived from higher gauge theory. They analyze the layer's behavior on closed cycles and characterize its gauge transformation properties under 1- and 2-gauge symmetries.

Result: On a closed cycle, the layer's output is shown to depend entirely on the adjustment coefficient; setting the coefficient to zero annihilates the output irrespective of all other parameters. The adjustment is thus the unique source of gauge-invariant signal. The resulting observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is proven exactly invariant under both 1-gauge and 2-gauge transformations.

Conclusion: The adjusted cup product neural layer provides a principled, gauge-invariant neural primitive grounded in higher gauge theory, with the adjustment term being both necessary and sufficient for producing any gauge-invariant output on closed cycles.

Abstract: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains. The adjusted cup product neural layer has been introduced in this paper. It is a neural primitive that hard wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory. This creates a readout that is gauge invariant by design. Their main theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely regardless of other parameters. Thus the adjustment is the only source of gauge invariant signal. They prove this observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.

</details>


### [313] [Existence Precedes Value: Joint Modeling of Observational Existence and Evolving States in Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13571)
*Yifan Hu,Hongzhou Chen,Peiyuan Liu,Yiding Liu,Zewei Dong,Jiang-Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Timeflies reformulates time series forecasting under irregular/missing data as a joint problem of future observability inference and value estimation, using dual observation-value streams with three coupling modules, and introduces the Shadow benchmark and OVJE metric for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continuous-time forecasting models (Neural ODEs, continuous-time GNNs) assume future observation timestamps are known in advance (oracle assumption), which is unrealistic when sensors may fail, delay, or sample irregularly. The fundamental question is not just "what will the value be?" but also "will there even be a valid observation?"

Method: Timeflies uses two parallel streams — an observation stream and a value stream — coupled via three modules: (1) reliability-aware embedding, (2) observation-guided dependency modeling, and (3) joint prediction. It also introduces Shadow, a benchmark mixing public datasets with industrial data featuring natural missingness, and OVJE (Observation-Value Joint Entropy) as a new evaluation metric.

Result: Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods across extensive experiments, demonstrating that explicitly modeling future observability significantly improves forecasting under missing data regimes.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling the coupling between observation dynamics and state evolution is crucial for practical time series forecasting with irregular/missing data, and the joint observability-value formulation represents a more realistic and effective paradigm than the traditional oracle assumption.

Abstract: Real-world time series are often highly incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling, making reliable forecasting fundamentally challenging. Existing methods have evolved from impute-then-forecast pipelines to continuous-time models such as Neural ODEs and continuous-time graph networks. While these approaches improve the modeling of historical irregularity, they still rely on an implicit oracle assumption at inference time: the timestamps of future valid observations are presumed to be known in advance. This assumption limits practical relevance, since in many real systems the more fundamental question is not only what the future value will be, but also whether a valid observation will occur at all. In this paper, we propose Timeflies, a unified framework that reformulates forecasting as a joint problem of future observability inference and value estimation. To explicitly model the interaction between observation dynamics and state evolution, Timeflies adopts an observation stream and a value stream, coupled through three dedicated modules for reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. We further construct Shadow, a benchmark that combines natural missingness from public datasets with real-world industrial data, and introduce the Observation-Value Joint Entropy (OVJE) metric to comprehensively evaluate this coupled predictability. Extensive experiments show that Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling future observability in time series forecasting with missing values. Code and dataset are available in https://github.com/ant-intl/Timeflies.

</details>


### [314] [Learning with Simulators: No Regret in a Computationally Bounded World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13576)
*Sasha Voitovych,Abhishek Shetty,Noah Golowich,Alexander Rakhlin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces simulatable processes, where a learner can query a simulator approximating any (potentially complex/dependent) data-generating process. Shows that VC-dimension-based learning guarantees, previously limited to independent data, extend to this setting. Demonstrates advantages of conditional sampling and exhibits a universal algorithm achieving regret bounded by time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity, broadening the PAC model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical learning theory heavily relies on independence assumptions, leaving strongly dependent data processes poorly understood. The authors aim to bridge this gap by formalizing a model where dependence can be arbitrary but the learner has simulator access.

Method: Introduces the simulatable process framework: the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the target distribution (which can be an arbitrarily dependent stochastic process). Under this framework, the authors derive error bounds based on VC dimension, study conditional sampling, and construct a universal algorithm whose regret scales with the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the data-generating process.

Result: VC-dimension-based learning guarantees are recovered under simulatable processes, matching classical independent-data bounds. Conditional sampling yields strict statistical and computational benefits. A single universal algorithm learns any VC class under all polynomial-time-samplable processes, with regret controlled by the process's time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.

Conclusion: The simulatable process framework significantly broadens the classical PAC model by decoupling learning guarantees from independence assumptions, instead requiring only simulator access. This unifies and extends prior results while revealing fundamental connections between learning, simulation, and Kolmogorov complexity.

Abstract: Understanding the minimal assumptions necessary for generalization is the fundamental question in learning theory. Unfortunately, most results rely heavily on independence (or some proxy thereof) of the data-generating process, while results for strongly dependent data are far more limited. Towards addressing this gap, we introduce the framework of simulatable processes, where the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the distribution generating the data (which may be an arbitrarily complex and dependent process). Surprisingly, given access to such a simulator, we show that we can recover the same learning guarantees as in the classical setting with independent data, namely, error bounds that depend on the VC dimension. Further, we use this framework to study the power of conditional sampling and show strict statistical and computational advantages in this setting. As a highlight of our framework, we exhibit a single algorithm that simultaneously learns any given VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the process. This provides a significant conceptual broadening of the classical PAC model.

</details>


### [315] [Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging: Transitioning from Uniform Priors to Sparse Posteriors in Ensemble Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13589)
*Meher Sai Preetam,Meher Bhaskar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCSB is a post-training framework that jointly optimizes ensemble pruning and probability calibration by solving a simplex-constrained sparse optimization problem using a concave quadratic penalty, achieving up to 96% compression with improved calibration and maintained accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard bagging ensembles use uniform voting weights, ignoring base estimators' varying local competence, which leads to model overconfidence. Existing pruning methods lack a rigorous simplex-constrained formulation, and the L1 norm fails to induce sparsity on the simplex (the "L1-simplex paradox").

Method: Formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex minimizing OOB loss. Address the L1-simplex paradox by introducing a concave quadratic penalty to induce sparsity (since L1 is constant on the simplex). The method is model-agnostic and applies post-training.

Result: SCSB achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups. It lowers Expected Calibration Error (superior probability calibration) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

Conclusion: SCSB provides a mathematically rigorous, model-agnostic solution for simultaneous ensemble compression and calibration, overcoming the fundamental limitation of L1 sparsity on the simplex through a concave quadratic penalty.

Abstract: We present Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB), a mathematically rigorous framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap-based bagging ensembles. Standard bagging ensembles (such as Random Forests, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks) assign uniform voting power to all constituent estimators. However, this naive uniform prior ignores the varying local competence of base estimators and contributes to model overconfidence. We formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex by minimizing the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) loss. To induce sparsity, we address the theoretical "L1-simplex paradox" -- the mathematical reality that the L1 norm is constant on the simplex and fails to prune -- by introducing a concave quadratic penalty. SCSB is model-agnostic and achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

</details>


### [316] [The Stable Recovery Manifold: Geometric Principles Governing Recoverability in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13637)
*Ayushman Trivedi,Bhavika Melwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper challenges the view of catastrophic forgetting as knowledge destruction, showing instead that forgotten information remains compactly decodable. Analyzing a sequentially trained ResNet-18 on Split CIFAR-100, the authors find that recovery dimensionality stays stable (~8 principal directions) despite representational drift, and principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862). The results support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis: forgetting is an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem, not information loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether catastrophic forgetting in continual learning represents genuine destruction of knowledge or merely a loss of accessibility to preserved information. The authors seek to test two competing hypotheses: the Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis (forgotten knowledge becomes progressively harder to recover) versus the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis (forgotten knowledge remains compactly structured and decodable despite representational drift).

Method: The authors use Split CIFAR-100 with a sequentially trained ResNet-18 across ten tasks. They introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), defined as the minimum number of singular directions needed to preserve 90% of full probe performance when decoding previous-task knowledge. They measure representational drift via principal-angle analysis between task representations and model recovery complexity through linear probing. A geometric model is constructed to predict recoverability from principal-angle drift.

Result: Key findings: (1) Recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout sequential training (mean k_t = 8.0), contrary to the Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis. (2) Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862). (3) A simple geometric model based on manifold alignment explains 82.2% of recoverability variance. (4) Substantial representational drift occurs, yet forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable.

Conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem, not information destruction. The Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis is supported: previously learned knowledge persists in a low-dimensional, stable subspace even as representations shift. This reframes continual learning research toward maintaining manifold accessibility rather than preventing knowledge loss.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is often viewed as the destruction of previously learned knowledge during sequential learning. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, we investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning. Using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18, we analyze recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. We introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), a measure of the minimum number of singular directions required to preserve 90 percent of full probe performance. Contrary to our Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis, recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2 percent of recoverability variance. These findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis, suggesting that forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization.
