The results indicate that catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction.

</details>


### [317] [Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13657)
*Guo Yu,Wenlin Liu,Yulan Hu,Hao-Xuan Ma,Jun-Peng Jiang,Han-Jia Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: On-policy distillation (OPD) produces small, coordinate-sparse, FFN-heavy updates that are spectrally concentrated and lie away from principal singular subspaces of source weights. Training only the discovered sparse subnetwork recovers near-full OPD performance, but AdamW remains necessary due to heterogeneous gradient scales from dense supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD combines on-policy student trajectories with dense teacher supervision, yet the mechanism by which this hybrid recipe modifies model parameters is poorly understood. The authors aim to characterize the structural and geometric properties of OPD-induced weight updates.

Method: The authors analyze parameter updates from OPD across multiple language and vision-language model pairs. They examine sparsity patterns (coordinate-level, layer-wise distribution, FFN vs. attention), conduct subnetwork training experiments to validate functional importance of sparse structure, perform optimizer ablations (SGD vs. AdamW), and study geometric properties including rank, spectral concentration, alignment with principal singular subspaces, and correlation with source weight magnitudes.

Result: OPD updates are small, coordinate-sparse, distributed across layers, and FFN-heavy. Training only the discovered sparse subnetwork recovers near-identical performance to full OPD. SGD underperforms AdamW due to heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales preserved by dense supervision. Geometrically, updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lie mostly away from principal singular subspaces of source weights, and fall disproportionately on coordinates where source weights are near zero.

Conclusion: Dense teacher supervision does not turn OPD into ordinary dense parameter rewriting. Instead, OPD retains important geometric signatures characteristic of on-policy post-training, suggesting a distinct update regime that blends sparsity and structured low-rank-like geometry without collapsing to trivial dense updates.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe as it combines two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision, yet how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD}-style updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers and are usually FFN-heavy. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full \textsc{OPD}. However, the sparsity-inducing SGD optimizer underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation, likely because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where AdamW's adaptive scaling remains useful. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

</details>


### [318] [Understanding Truncated Positional Encodings for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13671)
*James Flora,Mitchell Black,Weng-Keen Wong,Amir Nayyeri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the theoretical properties of truncated positional encodings (PEs) in GNNs, revealing that under truncation, spectral and walk-based PEs — previously known to be expressively equivalent in their complete form — become fundamentally different in power, with truncated spectral PEs no longer surpassing 1-WL expressivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Complete positional encodings (full eigenspaces, full adjacency powers) are theoretically equivalent (between 1-WL and 3-WL) but require O(n³) complexity. Practitioners use truncated variants (e.g., first k eigenspaces), yet their theoretical properties remain unstudied, leaving a gap between practice and theory.

Method: The authors theoretically analyze the expressive power of truncated PEs by comparing different families (spectral vs. walk-based) under truncation, proving separation results. They introduce k-harmonic distances as a case study to illustrate expressivity differences among closely related truncated spectral PEs, and validate findings with experiments on real-world datasets.

Result: Under truncation, spectral and walk-based PE families are provably different in expressive power. Truncated spectral PEs are shown to be no stronger than 1-WL. The k-harmonic distances reveal nuanced expressivity distinctions even among related spectral PEs. Empirically, mixing truncated PEs outperforms using any single family.

Conclusion: Truncation fundamentally alters the theoretical landscape of positional encodings, breaking the expressive equivalence observed in their complete forms. Practitioners should consider hybrid PE strategies rather than relying on a single truncated family.

Abstract: Positional encodings (PEs) enhance the power of graph neural networks (GNNs), both theoretically and empirically. Two of the most popular families of PEs - spectral (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (polynomials of the adjacency matrix) - are theoretically equivalent in expressive power, with expressivity between the 1-WL and 3-WL tests. However, this equivalence assumes the GNN uses the "complete" version of these PEs, which requires $O(n^3)$ time and space complexity. Instead, practitioners commonly use truncated variants of these encodings, such as the first $k$ eigenspaces or powers of the adjacency matrix. However, the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown. In this work, we initiate the study of these truncated PEs. Theoretically, we show that, under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. As a corollary, we show that truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. We also study a family of spectral PEs, the $k$-harmonic distances, to highlight the differences in expressive power of even closely related truncated PEs. Finally, we experimentally show that a mix of truncated PEs is preferable to any single family on real-world datasets.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [319] [OpenRoundup: Multi-Table Data Wrangling Through Interactive Visualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12648)
*Stephen Kasica,Charles Berret,Tamara Munzner*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: OpenRoundup is a browser-based, no-code tool that enables data journalists to consolidate multiple tables into a single analysis-ready dataset using a schema-first paradigm with two novel operations (Stack and Pack). It introduces "eager table consolidation" and is validated through replication of 17 real-world journalist workflows and a deployment study with four professionals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data journalists frequently need to integrate records across multiple independently published tables for accountability reporting, but existing interactive data wrangling tools treat the single table—not the collection of tables—as the primary unit of work. This gap leaves journalists who lack programming skills unable to perform multi-table consolidation without writing code.

Method: OpenRoundup is implemented as an open-source, browser-based system with five coordinated panels featuring schema previews, data quality alerts, and a recursive treemap. It runs entirely client-side via DuckDB-WASM for privacy. The system introduces (1) "eager table consolidation"—assembling a composite table early via interactive, incremental assembly—and (2) a declarative vocabulary of two operations: Stack (union-like vertical concatenation) and Pack (join-like horizontal combination). Evaluation combines a replication study (reproducing 17 published journalist programming workflows) and a deployment study (four professional data journalists).

Result: The replication study demonstrates that OpenRoundup's interface expressively covers real-world consolidation tasks from published journalism. The deployment study confirms utility for practitioners who conceptually understand joins but lack programming skills, and reveals an unanticipated secondary value in data journalism education.

Conclusion: OpenRoundup fills a critical tooling gap by elevating the collection of tables to the primary unit of interactive wrangling. Its eager consolidation paradigm and minimal declarative operation set (Stack/Pack) prove sufficient for real-world journalism tasks, while the client-only architecture addresses privacy concerns inherent to sensitive data. The system also shows promise as a pedagogical tool for data journalism.

Abstract: Data journalists routinely integrate records across multiple independently published sources to support accountability reporting, yet no existing interactive wrangling tool treats the collection of tables -- rather than the single table -- as its primary unit of work. We present OpenRoundup, an open-source, browser-based system that enables data journalists to consolidate multiple tables into a single analysis-ready output without writing code. The interface comprises five coordinated panels that implement a schema-first, values-on-demand paradigm with live schema previews, ambient data quality alerts, and a recursive treemap visualization of the evolving operation tree. A client-only architecture powered by DuckDB-WASM runs in the browser, providing strong data privacy guarantees suited to sensitive journalism data. The system introduces two conceptual contributions: eager table consolidation, in which a composite table is assembled early in the wrangling phase via interactive, incremental assembly of multiple source tables; and a declarative vocabulary for table consolidation consisting of two operations, Stack and Pack. We evaluate the system through a replication study in which the authors reproduce 17 published journalist programming workflows using only the interface, and a deployment study with four professional data journalists. The replication study demonstrates expressive coverage of real-world consolidation tasks. The deployment study confirms utility for practitioners who understand joins conceptually but lack the programming skills to execute them, and surfaces an unanticipated secondary value for data journalism education.

</details>


### [320] [A Multiplexing Design Space: Theory, Method, and Application](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12719)
*Yiwen Xing,Afrah Farea,Saiful Khan,Min Chen*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper proposes a systematic design method for exploring visual multiplexing (simultaneously conveying multiple data values per data point) in a constrained application context. Using machine learning workflows for PDE-approximating models as a case study, the authors develop a three-step design process—preceded by domain grounding—to identify optimal multiplexing designs for comparing multiple 2D scalar fields, involving domain experts in co-design and evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual multiplexing is common in visualization design but is rarely explored systematically as design patterns. The design space is vast, making exhaustive exploration impractical. The authors address the need for a principled method to narrow this space within a specific application domain—here, ML researchers analyzing inter-relationships among multiple 2D scalar fields in PDE-modeling workflows, where simple superposition of heatmaps fails.

Method: The method comprises a pre-design step for domain grounding and theoretical analysis, followed by three design steps that systematically explore the visual multiplexing design space for multiple 2D scalar fields. The approach incorporates domain experts through co-design and evaluation activities to identify optimal default multiplexing designs and user-controllable variations via a UI.

Result: The design process yields relatively optimal default multiplexing designs for comparing multiple 2D scalar fields and identifies the need for small, user-controllable variations accessible through a user interface, validated through expert involvement.

Conclusion: A structured, domain-grounded design method can effectively constrain and explore the visual multiplexing design space, producing actionable design patterns and UI controls tailored to specific analytical tasks such as ML-driven PDE model development.

Abstract: Many visualization designs feature phenomena referred to as ``visual multiplexing'', where multiple pieces of information associated with the same data point are conveyed simultaneously. Although visualization designers are able to bring such phenomena, often unconsciously, into their designs, the design space of visual multiplexing is huge, and it is uncommon to explore visual multiplexing systematically as design patterns. In this paper, we propose a design method for exploring a smaller design space constrained by an application. As an illustrative case study, we focus on machine learning (ML) workflows for developing ML models that approximate partial differential equations (PDEs). In these workflows, ML researchers need to analyze the inter-relationships among multiple 2D scalar fields frequently. Since superimposing one heatmap on top of another is not an effective design, we formulate three design steps to explore the design space of visual multiplexing in the context of multiple 2D scalar fields. Our design method also includes a pre-design step for domain grounding and theoretical analysis, and involves domain experts in both co-design and evaluation activities. The design process enables us to identify relatively optimal default multiplexing designs as well as the need for small variations that domain experts can control through a user interface.

</details>


### [321] [Exploring How Agent Voice Accents Shape Human-AI Collaboration in K-12 Group Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12805)
*Prerna Ravi,Carúmey Stevens,Ben Hurt,Brandon Hanks,Grace Lin,Emma Anderson*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A study with 33 teachers found that LLM voice agent accents (British, Indian, African American) significantly shaped group collaboration dynamics: the British-accented agent was treated as a tool, while Indian- and African American-accented agents were anthropomorphized and integrated as peers, affecting trust, engagement, and reliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While collaboration is essential in 21st-century education, teachers struggle to facilitate productive peer interaction. LLM conversational agents offer new mediation possibilities, but it is unknown how voice characteristics (specifically accent) affect learner perceptions, trust, and group dynamics — prior work only examined accent effects in one-to-one settings, not in groups.

Method: Between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers, manipulating a GenAI voice agent's accent across three conditions: British, Indian, and African American. Data collected through surveys, group interaction analyses, and artifacts.

Result: Accent shaped participants' mental models and the agent's assumed role: the British-accented agent was treated instrumentally as a detached tool, while Indian and African American-accented agents were anthropomorphized and integrated as peer collaborators. These role expectations cascaded into differences in trust, engagement, and reliance over time.

Conclusion: Sociolinguistic design features like accent are consequential for shaping group dynamics in CSCL. Findings carry implications for designing culturally inclusive AI partners in group learning contexts.

Abstract: Collaboration is widely recognized as a cornerstone of 21st-century education, yet teachers still encounter persistent challenges in fostering productive peer interaction. LLM conversational peer agents introduce new possibilities for mediating in-person group work, raising questions about how persona design, particularly their voice characteristics, shapes learners' perceptions, trust, and interactional dynamics. While prior work has examined agent accent effects in one-to-one settings, little is known about how these effects manifest in groups. We conducted a between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers examining how a GenAI voice agent with different accents (British, Indian, and African American) influenced collaboration and agent perception. Across surveys, group interaction analyses, and artifacts, we find that accent shaped participants' mental models and the roles the agent assumed in group interaction. The British-accented agent was largely treated as a tool and engaged in detached, utility-based ways, whereas Indian- and African American-accented agents were more readily anthropomorphized and integrated as peers. These role expectations influenced trust, engagement, and reliance over time. This work advances understanding of how GenAI's sociolinguistic design features shape group dynamics in CSCL, with implications for designing culturally inclusive AI partners in group learning.

</details>


### [322] [From Prompts to Preferences: An Open-Source Platform for Generative AI-Enhanced Conjoint Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12972)
*Philipp Brauner*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: An open-source, self-hosted web platform for conjoint surveys that leverages generative AI (LLMs and text-to-image models) to create integrated textual and visual stimuli, lowering technical barriers and enhancing reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conjoint analysis is widely used across disciplines, but researchers without commercial platform access face high costs and lack end-to-end infrastructure. Existing tools do not support AI-generated integrated stimuli formats.

Method: The authors developed an open-source web application featuring: a setup wizard, AI-assisted attribute suggestion, parameterised base prompts for LLM-generated textual scenarios and text-to-image visual stimuli (with optional LLM-facing level annotations), live data analysis, and full export bundles (stimuli, prompts, responses). The platform is demonstrated via a proof-of-concept study (N=55) on care robot preferences for ambient assisted living.

Result: The platform successfully enables end-to-end conjoint survey design, deployment, and analysis using both tabular and AI-generated integrated stimuli. The proof-of-concept study on care robot preferences demonstrates feasibility and practical utility with AI-generated visual stimuli.

Conclusion: GenAI-generated stimuli can broaden conjoint methodology in HCI and related fields, but theoretical grounding must remain the researcher's responsibility. The platform promotes transparency and reproducibility through comprehensive export bundles.

Abstract: Conjoint analysis is a widely used preference measurement method in marketing research, political science, healthcare, and human-computer interaction. Despite broad adoption, researchers without access to commercial platforms face significant barriers, as existing tools are either expensive or lack end-to-end survey infrastructure. This paper presents an open-source, self-hosted web application for designing, deploying, and analysing conjoint surveys. Beyond conventional tabular stimuli, the platform uses generative AI to produce integrated stimuli formats: textual scenario descriptions generated by a large language model, and visual stimuli by a text-to-image model. A researcher-defined base prompt is parameterised with the conjoint profile, and optional LLM-facing level annotations enrich the generation. A structured setup wizard, AI-assisted attribute suggestion, and live data analysis lower the technical barriers for researchers new to conjoint methodology. A full export bundle including all stimuli, their generating prompts, and response data facilitates transparency and reproducibility. The platform is demonstrated through a proof-of-concept study on care robot preferences for ambient assisted living (AAL, N=55) using AI-generated visual stimuli. The paper discusses the role of AI assistance in conjoint design, arguing that theoretical grounding must remain the researcher's responsibility, and outlining how genAI-generated stimuli can broaden the methodological repertoire for HCI and related fields.

</details>


### [323] [Mod-Guide: An LLM-based Content Moderation Feedback System to Address Insensitive Speech toward Indigenous Ethnic and Religious Minority Communities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13397)
*Dipto Das,Achhiya Sultana,Ankit Singh Chauhan,Saadia Binte Alam,Mohammad Shidujaman,Shion Guha,Sunandan Chakraborty,Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper examines how LLM-based content moderation systems often fail to recognize culturally insensitive speech against minority communities. Focusing on Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities, the authors co-created a culturally grounded corpus and developed Mod-Guide, a RAG-enhanced moderation tool that incorporates minority lived experiences, demonstrating improved contextual accuracy and differential perception across ethnic lines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based content moderation systems are increasingly deployed to handle online harmful speech, but they exhibit epistemic limits in recognizing culturally insensitive speech — language that marginalizes historically underrepresented communities through implicit erasure, misrepresentation, or normative framing rather than overt hostility. The authors argue that minority voices and hermeneutical perspectives are systematically excluded from moderation pipeline design, creating a gap between algorithmic judgment and lived community experience.

Method: The authors co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech in collaboration with members of Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities. They then integrated these community narratives into moderation pipelines using Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), building a tool called Mod-Guide. The system leverages contextual cues from lived experience to enhance LLM sensitivity to minority viewpoints. Mixed-method evaluations were conducted with both minority and majority participants to assess contextual accuracy and perceptual differences.

Result: RAG-enhanced moderation responses demonstrated superior contextual accuracy compared to baseline LLM moderation. Crucially, minority and majority participants perceived the moderation responses differently, confirming that cultural positionality shapes how moderation is experienced. The integration of community narratives via RAG improved the epistemic grounding of moderation decisions.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion should be central to content moderation system design. Mod-Guide demonstrates that incorporating minority community perspectives through RAG can bridge the gap between automated moderation and culturally sensitive judgment, advancing HCI, AI ethics, and social computing research.

Abstract: Language operates as a mechanism of both marginalization and resistance, especially for minority communities navigating insensitive and harmful speech online. As content moderation increasingly depends on large language models (LLMs), concerns arise about whether these systems can recognize culturally insensitive speech-language that disregards or marginalizes the cultural and religious perspectives of historically underrepresented communities, often through implicit erasure, misrepresentation, or normative framing, rather than overt hostility. Focusing on Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities -- the country's largest religious and Indigenous ethnic minorities, respectively -- this paper investigates the epistemic limits of LLM-based moderation systems and explores methods for incorporating minority perspectives. We co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech with community members and integrated their narratives into moderation pipelines using retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Our tool, Mod-Guide, improves LLM sensitivity to minority viewpoints by leveraging contextual cues derived from lived experience. Through mixed-method evaluations involving both minority and majority participants, we demonstrate that RAG-enhanced moderation responses are more contextually accurate and perceived differently across ethnic lines. This work advances research in human-computer interaction, AI ethics, and social computing by foregrounding restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion in the design of content moderation systems.

</details>


### [324] [Ride, Track, and Recover: Pilot Randomized Trial of a Wearable Digital Self-Management Intervention During a Veteran Endurance-Cycling Program](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13529)
*Alan Ta,Nilsu Salgin,Caleb Armstrong,Kala Phillips Reindel,Farzan Sasangohar*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A pilot study with veterans with PTSD compared digital intervention plus physical activity, physical activity only, and at-home monitoring. The digital intervention group showed hyperarousal stabilization and better symptom maintenance, suggesting wearable-based digital self-management tools may aid PTSD symptom regulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PTSD in veterans involves persistent hyperarousal and comorbid anxiety/depression that are difficult to monitor and manage outside clinical settings, creating a need for scalable, real-world approaches combining wearable sensing with digital interventions.

Method: Thirteen veterans in a Project Hero cycling event were randomized to digital intervention plus physical activity (n=7) or physical activity only (n=3), with a third at-home control cohort (n=7, 4 analyzed). Continuous smartwatch sensing (heart rate + accelerometry) detected hyperarousal events with real-time participant confirmation. Weekly self-reports captured anxiety, depression, and PTSD severity. Generalized additive mixed models modeled nonlinear symptom and hyperarousal trajectories.

Result: Baseline-normalized hyperarousal trajectories differed significantly across conditions: the digital intervention group showed structured stabilization vs. late-study escalation in the physical-only group. Both cycling groups improved acutely during the event, but the digital group maintained gains better. The at-home control group showed gradual symptom decline. ML detection precision varied across individuals and correlated positively with symptom severity.

Conclusion: Coupling wearable hyperarousal detection with digital self-management tools may support hyperarousal stabilization and sustained symptom improvement in veterans with PTSD. Personalization and human-centered design are critical for wearable mental health systems.

Abstract: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in veterans is characterized by persistent hyperarousal and comorbid anxiety and depressive symptoms that are difficult to monitor and manage outside clinical settings. Thirteen veterans participating in a Project Hero cycling event in Texas were randomized by computer-generated sequence in a naturalistic setting to two arms: (1) digital intervention plus physical activity, or (2) physical activity only, plus a third at-home monitoring control cohort consisting of 7 veterans selected from the broader Project Hero veteran community. Continuous smartwatch sensing combined heart rate and accelerometer features to detect hyperarousal events, which were confirmed in real time by participants. Weekly self-report measures of anxiety, depression, and PTSD severity were collected. Generalized additive mixed models characterized nonlinear trajectories over time. Baseline-normalized hyperarousal trajectories differed significantly across conditions, with the digital intervention group (n=7) showing structured stabilization compared to late-study escalation in the physical-only group (n=3). Both cycling groups exhibited acute symptom improvements during the endurance event; however, the digital intervention group demonstrated a higher overall maintenance of gains. The at-home control group (n=4) showed gradual symptom declines. Perceived precision of ML detections varied substantially across individuals and was positively associated with symptom severity, with higher-severity participants confirming a greater proportion of detected events. These results suggest that coupling wearable detection with digital self-management tools may support stabilization of hyperarousal and symptom improvement while emphasizing the importance of personalization and human-centered design in wearable mental health systems.

</details>
